On the other hand, it was expected to observe a disagreement between serological tests and qPCR when serological tests are positive and qPCR is negative, since it is known in the literature that serological tests tend to show negative results later than parasitological tests.
23.Rationale: The Passage Body documents that qPCR positivity and parasite load decreased substantially after treatment and that serological markers decline more slowly. However, the specific claim that residual qPCR-detected DNA post-treatment originates from lysed (nonviable) parasites rather than from viable infection is not stated or tested in the study. The passage gives no direct evidence (e.g., viability assays, culture-confirmed parasites concurrent with qPCR positivity) to distinguish DNA from lysed organisms versus DNA from live parasites. Therefore this causal inference is speculative and cannot be derived solely from the presented data.

23.Option: The molecular characterization of T. cruzi DTUs suggests that the persistence of T. cruzi DNA post-treatment is due to the presence of genetically resistant strains, particularly Tc II–VI, which are less susceptible to Nifurtimox.
Evidence: For the amplification of the mini-exon gene, we were able to characterize 46% (25/54) of qPCR positive samples from the 62 patients in pre-treatment. Of these 25 samples: 22 were identified as TcI, 2 were identified as being one or more of DTUs Tc II to VI (TcII-TcVI), and 1 being from both the group Tc I and from the group of Tc II to Tc VI (mixed).
At 24 months post-treatment of the 5 patients with positive qPCR samples, 60% (3) amplified, and all were Tc II-Tc VI.

The observed efficacy of these treatments varies widely (15–80%) depending on the region, the genotype of the parasite, the age of the patients, the time between infection and start of treatment and the clinical stage of the disease [10–12].
23.Rationale: The Passage Body reports a shift in the DTU composition among the small number of qPCR-positive samples at late follow-up (several late positives were Tc II–VI). It also notes that treatment efficacy can vary by parasite genotype. However, the study did not measure or demonstrate genetic resistance to Nifurtimox, nor did it perform susceptibility testing or present data showing that Tc II–VI are less susceptible in this cohort. Inferring that persistence is due to genetically resistant strains is an untested causal claim beyond the presented observational data. Therefore this conclusion cannot be reliably derived from the passage alone.

23.Option: The persistence of T. cruzi DNA detected by qPCR after treatment indicates that Nifurtimox is ineffective in reducing parasite load and that treatment failure is likely in most patients.
Evidence: The proportion of patients with positive qPCR also presented a significant decrease from 88.4% (38/43) in pre-treatment period to 12.1% (4/33) after 30 months of follow-up (Fig. 2).

A statically significant decrease of parasitemia at 6 months after treatment was observed, from a median of 6.2 (range 0–36,520) to 0.2 (range 0 to 124.5) parasite equivalents/mL (Friedman test, p <0.001) (Fig. 5).

Simultaneous negative seroconversion of both tests (ELISA and IFAT) only started at 12 months 12.5% (95%CI: 2.36–22.64) and reached 41.9% (26.5–57.3) of patients at 30 months after treatment (Fig. 3).
23.Rationale: The data in the passage demonstrate that Nifurtimox treatment substantially reduced both qPCR positivity and median parasite load in the cohort. A large majority of patients became qPCR-negative over time (from 88.4% positive pre-treatment to 12.1% positive at 30 months). Serological markers also declined over follow-up. These findings indicate that the drug had a significant effect on parasite burden for most patients, contradicting the blanket statement that Nifurtimox is ineffective and that treatment failure is likely in most patients. Thus the option overstates and contradicts the presented evidence.

23.Option: The detection of T. cruzi DNA by qPCR in patients post-treatment is most likely due to reinfection, given the high prevalence of triatomine vectors in the study area and the persistence of positive results over time.
Evidence: The etiologic agent is the parasite Trypanosoma cruzi, which is transmitted primarily through infected feces of triatomine insect vectors.
The houses of the persons included in this study were sprayed with residual pyrethroid insecticide before and after initiating etiological treatment.

For the survival analysis, several variables were evaluated, whereas the presence of bad housing characteristics (thatched roof, dirt floor and mud walls) represented shorter survival time (time to positive test) with a cumulative survival of 50% (95%CI: 32.3–67.7) versus only 17.2% (95%CI: 16.5–17.9) for children living in good-housing conditions. This difference was statistically significant (log-rank test, p = 0.03) (Fig. 6).
The proportion of patients with positive qPCR also presented a significant decrease from 88.4% (38/43) in pre-treatment period to 12.1% (4/33) after 30 months of follow-up (Fig. 2).
23.Rationale: While triatomine vectors are the known transmission route and housing conditions were associated with time to qPCR positivity, the study implemented spraying of houses before and after treatment, which reduces the plausibility of widespread reinfection as the primary explanation. The passage does not provide direct evidence of reinfection events (e.g., documented new exposures, vector infection status, or genotyping demonstrating different strains pre- and post-treatment) and does not quantify local vector prevalence contemporaneous with follow-up. Therefore attributing post-treatment qPCR positivity 'most likely' to reinfection is not supported by the data presented and is speculative.

24.Option: The phage-displayed antigens developed in this study are not suitable for large-scale screening due to the need for specialized equipment and mammalian cell cultures, which are not available in simple laboratory settings.
Evidence: Recently, serodiagnosis of strongyloidiasis has been achieved with excellent sensitivity and specificity using a 31-kDa recombinant antigen termed NIE and a luciferase immunoprecipitation system; however, these recombinant antigens require specific luciferase-antigen fusion construct and mammalian cell cultures, which is not easy to be attained, and is not available for simple laboratory settings.

Due to difficulties to product crude extracts and some other recombinant antigens our aim was to develop a strategy for simpler antigen production by Phage Display to detect IgG in large population screenings.
Selected mimotopes may be used in a simple, specific and low cost phage ELISA as described by several authors
24.Rationale: The passage explicitly contrasts the NIE recombinant antigen approach (which requires luciferase fusion constructs and mammalian cell cultures that are not available in simple labs) with the phage display approach developed in this study. The authors state their aim was to develop a strategy for simpler antigen production by Phage Display to detect IgG in large population screenings and note that selected mimotopes may be used in a simple, specific and low cost phage ELISA. Therefore the claim that phage-displayed antigens are unsuitable for large-scale screening due to need for specialized equipment and mammalian cultures is not supported; in fact the passage argues the opposite (phage ELISA being simpler and lower cost).

24.Option: The C9 phage clone demonstrated high diagnostic performance with 87.5% sensitivity, 80% specificity, and 82.5% diagnostic efficiency, and exhibited low cross-reactivity with sera from patients with other parasitic infections, making it a promising candidate for large-scale screening programs.
Evidence: Precisely 87.5% of the serum samples from Group I were positive for phage clones C9, whereas for the phage clones C10, B4, D3 and B2, the positivity in this group was respectively, 85%, 82.5%, 80%, and 80%.
C9 demonstrated the greatest diagnostic performance considering Sp, Se and DE of 87.5%, 80%, and 82.5%, respectively.
C9 was the only clone that did not cross-react with hookworm patient sera and recognized few other species of parasites sampled.
24.Rationale: The passage supports that clone C9 had the best overall diagnostic performance among the tested clones and that it showed relatively low cross-reactivity (e.g., did not cross-react with hookworm sera). However, the numeric values in the option are presented in an order (87.5% sensitivity, 80% specificity) that conflicts with the explicit phrasing in the passage, which states "Sp, Se and DE of 87.5%, 80%, and 82.5%, respectively." That sentence indicates specificity = 87.5% and sensitivity = 80% (with DE = 82.5%). Although an earlier sentence reports 87.5% of Group I samples were positive for C9 (which could be interpreted as a sensitivity measure), the passage's ROC-summary sentence explicitly assigns 87.5% to specificity and 80% to sensitivity. Because the option gives the sensitivity and specificity values in the reversed order relative to the clearly stated ROC results, the exact numerical claim as written cannot be reliably derived from the Passage Body.

24.Option: The use of a 31-kDa recombinant antigen (NIE) in serodiagnosis of strongyloidiasis has been shown to have high sensitivity and specificity, and is therefore a superior alternative to the phage-displayed antigens developed in this study.
Evidence: Recently, serodiagnosis of strongyloidiasis has been achieved with excellent sensitivity and specificity using a 31-kDa recombinant antigen termed NIE and a luciferase immunoprecipitation system; however, these recombinant antigens require specific luciferase-antigen fusion construct and mammalian cell cultures, which is not easy to be attained, and is not available for simple laboratory settings.

Due to difficulties to product crude extracts and some other recombinant antigens our aim was to develop a strategy for simpler antigen production by Phage Display to detect IgG in large population screenings.
C9 demonstrated the greatest diagnostic performance considering Sp, Se and DE of 87.5%, 80%, and 82.5%, respectively.
24.Rationale: While the passage acknowledges that the 31-kDa recombinant NIE antigen achieves excellent sensitivity and specificity, it also notes practical limitations (need for luciferase fusion constructs and mammalian cell cultures) that reduce its accessibility for simple laboratories and large population screenings. The study presents phage-displayed clones (notably C9) with good diagnostic performance (ROC-derived Se/Sp/DE values). The passage does not present direct comparative performance data demonstrating NIE is superior to the phage clones, nor does it conclude that NIE is the better alternative for population-level screening given its practical constraints. Thus the strong conclusion that NIE is a superior alternative cannot be derived solely from the Passage Body.

24.Option: The low cross-reactivity observed in the C9 clone is likely due to the absence of prior exposure to S. stercoralis in the healthy individuals and patients with other parasites, suggesting that the test results are not influenced by previous infections.
Evidence: C9 was the only clone that did not cross-react with hookworm patient sera and recognized few other species of parasites sampled.

Group III contained 40 apparently healthy individuals based on their clinical observation, without evidence of contact with S. stercoralis infection or previous history of strongyloidiasis and three fecal samples tested negative.
24.Rationale: The passage documents that C9 showed relatively low cross-reactivity and describes Group III as apparently healthy without evidence of contact with S. stercoralis. However, there is no evidence in the Passage Body that attributes low cross-reactivity specifically to absence of prior exposure in controls or other-parasite patients; nor does it state that prior infections do not influence test results. The passage does not provide data on individuals' past exposures beyond the descriptions of groups, and it does not claim causation between absence of prior exposure and low cross-reactivity. Therefore the causal inference in this option is not supported by the text.

24.Option: The competitive Phage-ELISA results confirm that the phage clones bind to the same epitopes as the crude antigen from S. venezuelensis, indicating that they are functionally equivalent and can fully replace traditional antigen preparations.
Evidence: each phage clone was partially inhibited in a dose dependent manner when serum of S. stercoralis patient was pre-incubated with saline extract from S. venezuelensis (heterologous antigen).
In contrast, no inhibition of this interaction was observed by the addition of a wild-type phage.

These data showed that phage displayed peptides reacted specifically with antibody from patients with Strongyloidiasis demonstrating that there was a competition for IgG between mimotopes and the antigenic extract.
24.Rationale: The competitive Phage-ELISA data show dose-dependent partial inhibition when sera were pre-incubated with saline extract, and the authors interpret this as evidence the phage-displayed peptides react specifically with patient antibodies and compete with the crude antigen for IgG binding. However, the inhibition is described as partial (not complete), and the passage does not claim that the phage clones are fully functionally equivalent to traditional antigen preparations or that they can fully replace them. The evidence supports that the mimotopes bind relevant antibodies and mimic antigenic determinants to some extent, but it does not justify the stronger claim of full equivalence and outright replacement of traditional antigens.

25.Option: Patients with severe neutropenia due to benzylpenicillin should routinely receive granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) or prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics to prevent life-threatening complications.
Evidence: They were given symptomatic relief and supportive treatment rather than human granulocyte colony-stimulating factor, glucocorticosteroid or other prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics.
Both patients' CBCs returned to normal within four and five days, respectively, after withdrawing benzylpenicillin.
None experienced any severe complication of therapy and all had recovery of a normal ANC within seven days.
25.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the two patients with severe neutropenia were managed with symptomatic relief and supportive care rather than G-CSF or prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics, and both recovered rapidly after stopping benzylpenicillin. There is no evidence in the Passage Body that routine use of G-CSF or prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics is necessary or superior; thus the recommendation in the option is not supported by the presented data.

25.Option: The presence of fever and chills in patients with benzylpenicillin-induced neutropenia is a reliable indicator of underlying bacterial infection, necessitating immediate broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy.
Evidence: The accompanying symptoms were chills and fever (38.5–39.7℃, 5 patients), fatigue (2 patients), cough (1 patient), sore throat (1 patient), diarrhea (1 patient) and erythematous rash (1 patient).
Blood, sputum and throat swab cultures did not reveal an infectious etiology (e.g.: bacterium and fungus) among the febrile patients.
They were given symptomatic relief and supportive treatment rather than human granulocyte colony-stimulating factor, glucocorticosteroid or other prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics.
25.Rationale: Although some patients with neutropenia had fever and chills, cultures did not identify a bacterial or fungal etiology among the febrile patients. The clinicians managed these cases supportively rather than initiating immediate broad-spectrum antibiotics. Therefore, fever and chills in this context were not shown in the Passage Body to be a reliable indicator of underlying bacterial infection requiring immediate broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy.

25.Option: Benzylpenicillin-induced neutropenia was well tolerated in the cohort of patients with neurosyphilis, with rapid normalization of neutrophil counts and no life-threatening complications, even in cases of severe neutropenia, after discontinuation of the drug.
Evidence: The total incidence of benzylpenicillin-induced neutropenia was 2.42% (95% CI: 1.38–4.13%, 14/578) among this cohort of patients with NS.
For the majority (13/14) of patients, the duration of treatment before onset of neutropenia ranged from 10 to 14 days, and the cumulative dose of benzylpenicillin varied from 240 to 324 megaunits.
The range of nadir total white blood cell (WBC) counts was 0.60 to 3.69×109/L, with nadir ANC from 0.04 to 1.49×109/L.
They were given symptomatic relief and supportive treatment rather than human granulocyte colony-stimulating factor, glucocorticosteroid or other prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics.
Both patients' CBCs returned to normal within four and five days, respectively, after withdrawing benzylpenicillin.
None experienced any severe complication of therapy and all had recovery of a normal ANC within seven days.
25.Rationale: The Passage Body documents that benzylpenicillin-induced neutropenia occurred in a small proportion of neurosyphilis patients, typically after prolonged high-dose therapy, and that even the two patients with severe neutropenia recovered their CBCs within days after stopping benzylpenicillin without life-threatening complications. Management was supportive rather than aggressive (no routine G-CSF or prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics), and all affected patients recovered rapidly, supporting the inference in the option.

25.Option: The incidence of benzylpenicillin-induced neutropenia among neurosyphilis patients is significantly higher than in patients receiving benzylpenicillin for other conditions, suggesting a unique susceptibility in this population.
Evidence: Benzylpenicillin-induced neutropenia, a complication of prolonged therapy with high doses, has been well documented when treating infective endocarditis, leading some patients to withdraw necessary treatment and even undergo insidious life-threatening sepsis [4–7].

A total of 1,367 NS patients were treated with a standard regimen of benzylpenicillin during the study period, 613 of whom received treatment for the first time. Of these, 578 patients underwent repeat CBC during the treatment and were included according to the study criteria. Fourteen patients, all of whom had prior normal CBCs, had a repeat ANC below 1.5×109/L.
The total incidence of benzylpenicillin-induced neutropenia was 2.42% (95% CI: 1.38–4.13%, 14/578) among this cohort of patients with NS.
25.Rationale: The Passage Body reports the incidence of neutropenia in this neurosyphilis cohort but provides no comparative incidence data for patients receiving benzylpenicillin for other conditions. Although neutropenia with high-dose benzylpenicillin has been documented in other settings (e.g., infective endocarditis), there is no evidence in the Passage Body to support a claim of a significantly higher incidence or unique susceptibility in neurosyphilis patients. Therefore the option cannot be derived from the presented data.

25.Option: The severity of benzylpenicillin-induced neutropenia in neurosyphilis patients is strongly associated with older age and the presence of neurologic complications, requiring more intensive monitoring.
Evidence: The median age of these patients was 55 years (range: 27 to 79).
The severity of neutropenia had no association with age, gender or type of neurosyphilis (p>0.05).
The multivariate logistic regression was not carried out since no significant factors were found by chi-square test.
25.Rationale: The Passage Body explicitly states that severity of neutropenia had no association with age, gender, or type of neurosyphilis (p>0.05), and no significant factors were identified by chi-square testing. Thus the claim that severity is strongly associated with older age and neurologic complications is contradicted by the presented data and cannot be inferred.

27.Option: WASH improvements in Kalemie had a greater impact on cholera transmission than vaccination, as they directly reduced environmental contamination and exposure over time.
Evidence: The WASH intervention decreased the environmental exposure rate βe to βe−βWASH by the end of the study period.

Over this 118 week period, we estimated: 3,702 (mean: 3,702.3, 95% CrI: 1,302.5–7,542.0) additional cases, a 2.56% increase (mean: 2.56%, 95% CrI: 1.79%-3.30%), when removing vaccination alone (scenario with WASH only), 1,585 (mean: 1,585.5, 95% CrI: 1,321.9–5,108.8) cases avoided, 1.03% (mean: 1.03%, 95% CrI: 0.01%-2.85%), by WASH alone (scenario with vaccination only), and 5,259 (mean: 5,258.6, 95% CrI: 1,576.6–11,337.8) cases avoided, 3.57% (mean: 3.57%, 95% CrI: 2.02%-5.72%), by implementing both vaccination and WASH (scenario with no vaccination and no WASH improvements) (Fig 3B).
27.Rationale: The passage states that WASH reduced the environmental exposure parameter (βe), but the modelled scenario results show vaccination prevented more additional cases (3,702) than WASH alone (1,585). Thus the evidence does not support the claim that WASH had a greater impact than vaccination; the quantitative model outputs indicate the opposite in this setting.

27.Option: The high immunity observed in Kalemie is primarily due to repeated vaccination campaigns rather than environmental exposure, indicating that vaccination has been a key driver of population-level protection.
Evidence: The high immunity inferred by the model was maintained through annual flare-ups and constant environmental exposure.

Based on our model, the targeted vaccinations occurred when population immunity was high: 97.8% (95% CrI: 96.7–98.6) in November 2013, 89. 0% (95% CrI: 76.7–96.7) in July 2014, and 89.1% (95% CrI: 77.2–96.6) during the catch-up in August 2014.
27.Rationale: The passage explicitly attributes the high inferred immunity to repeated annual outbreaks and ongoing environmental exposure, and notes that vaccination occurred when population immunity was already high. Therefore the high immunity cannot be said to be primarily due to vaccination based on the presented evidence.

27.Option: The impact of vaccination in endemic cholera settings may be limited when high population immunity is maintained through constant environmental exposure, even with high vaccine coverage.
Evidence: Our model suggested that vaccination campaigns with small target population sizes would have a limited impact in populations with high immunity (Fig 3C).
However, the timing of a pulse of vaccination could substantially influence the impact of vaccination campaigns.

The best performing vaccination scenario (darkest cell of the heatmap in Fig 3C) avoided 12,777 cases (mean: 12,776.7, 95%CrI: 4,681.0.7–26,019.5) over 118 weeks for 200,000 vaccinated people. However, the high level of local immunity would result in vaccinating a large proportion of immune individuals, reducing the impact of the vaccination.
The high immunity inferred by the model was maintained through annual flare-ups and constant environmental exposure.
27.Rationale: The passage provides multiple model-based findings that directly support this inference: when background immunity is high because of repeated exposure from the environmental reservoir, vaccination—especially small or targeted campaigns—has limited additional impact because many vaccine recipients are already immune. The authors explicitly state vaccination impact is reduced by high local immunity maintained through environmental exposure and that large-scale or well-timed campaigns are required to achieve larger gains.

27.Option: Vaccination campaigns in endemic cholera settings are most effective when timed to coincide with the peak of environmental bacterial growth, as this maximizes the number of susceptible individuals exposed to the vaccine.
Evidence: Our model suggested that vaccination campaigns with small target population sizes would have a limited impact in populations with high immunity (Fig 3C). However, the timing of a pulse of vaccination could substantially influence the impact of vaccination campaigns.
Specifically, timing the vaccination to occur at the lowest point of population immunity and before an outbreak began increased its impact.
27.Rationale: The passage states that optimal timing is at the lowest point of population immunity and before an outbreak starts, not at the peak of environmental bacterial growth. Timing to coincide with peak environmental growth would likely be during or just before outbreaks, whereas the authors emphasize pre-outbreak timing when susceptibility is greatest. Therefore the option's claim is not supported by the text.

27.Option: The modest impact of the intervention in Kalemie was primarily due to the limited scale and incremental implementation of the WASH improvements, which prevented a significant reduction in environmental exposure.
Evidence: The WASH component of the intervention focused on improving access to clean water. Although it was not acting on every dimension of WASH, we simply refer to it as “WASH intervention” below.

The vaccination campaign originally targeted about 120,000 people ... but was interrupted ... resumed ... reducing the target population to about 52,000 people ... Ultimately, 81.2% of the target population received at least one dose [24].

The WASH intervention incurred delays in the aftermath of the security issue that delayed the OCV intervention. Its first milestone, extending access to tap water was achieved in October 2014 and the remaining components were completed incrementally until early 2016.
The model did not allow the environmental contamination to vary because the intervention did not target waste management.

Over this 118 week period, we estimated: 3,702 ... additional cases ... when removing vaccination alone (scenario with WASH only), 1,585 ... cases avoided ... by WASH alone ... and 5,259 ... cases avoided ... by implementing both vaccination and WASH ...

We estimated that removing environmental exposure or contamination would have a critical impact on cholera dynamics. These strategies avoided 142,518 cases ... and 134,373 cases ... respectively. In each of these scenarios local cholera transmission was virtually interrupted (Fig 4A and 4B).
27.Rationale: The passage documents that the WASH intervention was limited in scope (focused on water access, did not target waste management), implemented incrementally after delays, and that the model did not treat environmental contamination as reduced by the intervention. Model results show relatively small reductions in cases from the actual interventions but indicate that removing environmental exposure/contamination would have a very large impact. Together these points support the inference that the modest observed impact was largely attributable to the limited scale and incremental implementation of WASH measures that failed to substantially reduce environmental transmission.

28.Option: The environmental niche models predicted that regions in the Blue Ridge, Mississippi Alluvial Plain, and Southern Florida Coastal Plain are least suitable for human-tick encounters, which aligns with the observed absence of ticks in these areas.
Evidence: Maps indicating unsuitable habitat for sites used as active surveillance with dry ice traps for tick species (a) Amblyomma americanum, (b) Dermacentor variabilis, and (c) Ixodes scapularis in Coweeta, Blue Valley, and Bent Creek experimental forests in western North Carolina and northern Georgia in southern Appalachian Mountains (2021).

Only one model for I. scapularis had an AUC value greater than 0.8 (Table 2). The high contribution environmental variables were evapotranspiration, gross primary productivity, maximum temperature, vegetation indices, and soil organic matter (Table 3). The area of potential suitability for I. scapularis included all ecoregions in the southeastern US; however, there was a reduced risk to encounter I. scapularis ticks in southern Florida, eastern Tennessee and Mississippi, and western Arkansas (Fig 4).
There were no ticks collected in any of the 15 traps that were placed in the three experimental research forests, nor were ticks encountered on the individual walking to and from each site.

The CDC tick surveillance datasets indicated D. variabilis to be reported but there were no county records for A. americanum and I. scapularis in Macon County North Carolina where The Coweeta and Blue Valley Experimental Forests are located.
New county records for A. americanum included: Kemper (MS), Madison (FL), Taylor (FL), Gilchrist (FL), ...
28.Rationale: The passage does report predicted 'reduced risk' for I. scapularis in 'southern Florida, eastern Tennessee and Mississippi' and it documents that some active-surveillance sites in the Blue Ridge (Coweeta/Blue Valley/Bent Creek) yielded no ticks. However, the option makes two unsupported claims: 1) it names the 'Mississippi Alluvial Plain' specifically as least suitable while the passage only mentions 'Mississippi' generally (not the Mississippi Alluvial Plain ecoregion by name), and 2) it asserts that observed absence in those ecoregions aligns broadly with model predictions. The passage also documents new county records in Mississippi and Florida (e.g., Kemper (MS), Madison (FL), Taylor (FL), Gilchrist (FL)) which indicates tick presence in parts of those states, so a blanket statement that those entire ecoregions are least suitable and that observed absences align everywhere is not supported. Therefore the specific, generalized claim in the option cannot be derived from the passage.

28.Option: The Southeastern Plains region is unsuitable for tick encounters due to its high elevation and cold climate, which prevent tick survival and activity.
Evidence: We overlaid an U.S. ecoregions boundaries shapefile to evaluate risk based on regional similarities or differences (e.g., geology, soils, climate, vegetation) of distinct geographic areas [52–56].
Values for each variable represent that variables percent contribution to each model, bolded values were those identified as significant contributing variables to the model (greater than 0.1 or 10%).

The model was based on tick encounters collected by Forest Inventory and Analysis Program of the Forest Service, US Department of Agriculture foresters in the southeastern United States (2014–2021). Landcover, elevation, maximum temperature, and vapor pressure were the best predictors of habitat suitability (Table 3).
28.Rationale: The passage does identify environmental predictors (including elevation and temperature) that influence suitability, but it does not state that the 'Southeastern Plains' region is unsuitable, nor does it assert that high elevation and cold climate characterize that region. In fact, the Southeastern Plains are generally not characterized by high elevation and cold climate. The passage does not provide evidence that the Southeastern Plains are unsuitable for ticks or that unsuitability in any region is due specifically to 'high elevation and cold climate.' Therefore this causal assertion about the Southeastern Plains cannot be derived from the passage.

28.Option: The observed absence of ticks in the Mississippi Alluvial Plain and Southern Florida Coastal Plain is primarily due to insufficient sampling by forestry crews, indicating that these regions are not truly unsuitable for tick encounters.
Evidence: Although the true distribution for each tick species may not be fully represented due to differences in collection, the CDC datasets represent the most comprehensive comparison for our models because they include surveillance by ArboNET or literature published each year throughout the Southeast.

The coordinates used for niche modeling are within 1.6 km (1 mile) of the actual encounter sites because (1) many of the plots are located on private lands and these exact locations are kept confidential ... and (2) ticks may have been encountered traveling to or from plots or while working at the plot. We coarsened the spatial resolution of the model to account for differences between plots and uncertainty of collecting locations.

For all three tick species, our potential distribution maps suggested suitable habitats were present in regions scattered throughout Macon County; however, suitable habitats were found scattered throughout Buncombe County for D. variabilis and I. scapularis but not for A. americanum.

Only one model for I. scapularis had an AUC value greater than 0.8 ... The area of potential suitability for I. scapularis included all ecoregions in the southeastern US; however, there was a reduced risk to encounter I. scapularis ticks in southern Florida, eastern Tennessee and Mississippi, and western Arkansas (Fig 4).
28.Rationale: The passage acknowledges sampling and spatial uncertainties (coarsened coordinates, differences in collection) and concedes that true distributions may not be fully represented, which supports caution interpreting absences. However, the option makes a strong causal claim—that observed absences in the Mississippi Alluvial Plain and Southern Florida Coastal Plain are 'primarily due to insufficient sampling' and therefore those regions are 'not truly unsuitable'—which is not supported. The passage also reports model-based 'reduced risk' in southern Florida and parts of Mississippi and presents model predictions of suitable habitats in other areas, indicating that absence could reflect real lower suitability in some places. The authors do not conclude that sampling insufficiency is the primary explanation for observed absences in those named ecoregions. Thus the option overstates what can be inferred.

28.Option: Human-tick encounters in the southeastern U.S. are most likely to occur in areas with high soil moisture and dense forest cover, as these conditions support tick survival and host availability.
Evidence: Soil properties such as percent litter coverage and soil moisture are associated with A. americanum abundance [22,23].
Landscape variables associated with forests (e.g., land cover, primary productivity) are important for host populations and likely regulate infesting tick populations [e.g., 29,30].
For example, normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI), a measure of greenness, has been associated with the abundance of A. americanum [31].

The model was based on tick encounters collected by Forest Inventory and Analysis Program of the Forest Service, US Department of Agriculture foresters in the southeastern United States (2014–2021). Variables soil organic matter, vegetation indices, maximum temperature, gross primary productivity, and evapotranspiration contributed the most to predicting this tick’s geographic suitability.
28.Rationale: The passage explicitly links soil moisture and litter coverage to A. americanum abundance and indicates that forest-associated landscape variables (land cover, primary productivity, NDVI/vegetation indices) are important for host populations and likely regulate tick populations. The niche model results also show strong contributions from vegetation- and soil-related variables. From an epidemiological perspective, these environmental conditions (high soil moisture and dense/productive vegetation/forest cover) are reasonable predictors of higher human-tick encounter likelihood because they favor tick survival and host presence. Therefore this inference is supported by the passage.

28.Option: The removal of landcover as a predictor in the I. scapularis model indicates that land cover has no influence on tick encounters in the southeastern U.S.
Evidence: Due to lower sample size for I. scapularis only the linear, quadratic, and product features were used in Maxent. Additionally, categorical variables (land cover) were removed from the environmental niche model for I. scapularis because the variable contribution was not balanced.

Only one model for I. scapularis had an AUC value greater than 0.8 (Table 2). The high contribution environmental variables were evapotranspiration, gross primary productivity, maximum temperature, vegetation indices, and soil organic matter (Table 3).
28.Rationale: The passage states landcover categories were removed from the I. scapularis model because their contributions were not balanced given the small sample size — a modeling decision driven by data and method constraints — not evidence that land cover has no effect on tick encounters. Moreover, the I. scapularis model retained vegetation-related and productivity variables (evapotranspiration, gross primary productivity, vegetation indices) and soil organic matter, which are ecological correlates of land cover. Thus removal of the categorical landcover variable for modeling reasons does not imply land cover has no influence; the passage provides contrary evidence that vegetation/landscape factors do contribute to suitability. Therefore the option is not supported.

29.Option: Children with Giardia infection who received multi-nutrient supplementation had higher rates of diarrhea compared to uninfected children receiving the same supplementation.
Evidence: Giardia was associated with protection against diarrhea among children receiving zinc or placebo, but not among children receiving multi-nutrients.

Thus analyzed, Giardia infection at baseline was associated with a substantial increase in time to first diarrhea episode with ≥3 watery stools/24 h (p<0.001), but only so among children without multi-nutrients, whereas no association was found between Giardia infection and diarrhea in those receiving multi-nutrients (Figure 3; top panels, and Tables 2 and 3).

When stratified by intervention group, the association between Giardia infection at baseline and diarrhea was similar in children receiving placebo as in those receiving zinc (in both groups the infection was associated with a protection). Likewise, this association was similar in both groups receiving multi-nutrients (no association in either group; Figure 2); we therefore combined children who received multi-nutrients with or without zinc (henceforth referred to as ‘with multi-nutrients’), as well as their peers who received zinc or placebo (‘without multi-nutrients’) as two separate groups.
29.Rationale: The passage reports that among children receiving multi-nutrients there was no association between Giardia infection and diarrhea (i.e., neither increased nor decreased rates), whereas protection was observed only in the non-multi-nutrient groups. The option asserts higher rates in infected children receiving multi-nutrients compared to uninfected children on the same supplementation; this is not supported by the passage, which explicitly states there was no association in the multi-nutrient group.

29.Option: The observed reduction in diarrhea incidence among Giardia-infected children is likely due to improved sanitation and health-care seeking behavior in this population.
Evidence: Children with Giardia infection were on average 3.3 months older than their uninfected peers, resided somewhat closer to the dispensary, had a lower prevalence of inflammation, marginally higher hemoglobin concentrations, as well as marginally lower plasma concentrations of soluble transferrin receptor and folate.

Adjustment for age and distance to the dispensary led to smaller but still substantial associations between baseline Giardia infection and diarrhea (all events), whilst interaction effects between infection and the multi-nutrient intervention remained virtually unchanged (Figure 4).

Further adjustment for baseline factors previously found to be prognostic for diarrhea (height-for-age z-scores, sex, inflammation and use of mosquito nets) led to similar effect estimates (not shown).
29.Rationale: The option posits improved sanitation or health-care seeking as the likely explanation for the reduced diarrhea incidence among Giardia-infected children. The passage does not present evidence of better sanitation or different health-care seeking behavior causing the association. It notes that infected children lived somewhat closer to the dispensary, but the authors adjusted for age and distance to the dispensary (and other baseline factors) and the association persisted. Thus the data do not support attributing the protective association to improved sanitation or care-seeking based on the information provided.

29.Option: The protective association between Giardia infection and reduced diarrhea incidence may be due to the parasite's ability to suppress other enteric pathogens through competitive exclusion or immune modulation.
Evidence: Some reported an association with acute [5] and persistent [6] diarrhea, whereas several studies found no association [6]–[10], or even that Giardia infection was associated with protection against acute diarrhea [9], [11]–[14].
Overall, the incidence of first episodes of diarrhoea was almost 50% lower among children with Giardia at baseline than among those without (Table 2).
Giardia was associated with protection against diarrhea among children receiving zinc or placebo, but not among children receiving multi-nutrients.
29.Rationale: While the passage documents an observed protective association between baseline Giardia infection and reduced diarrhea incidence (and cites prior inconsistent findings), it does not provide evidence about mechanisms such as competitive exclusion or immune modulation. The suggested mechanistic explanation is speculative and not presented or tested in the study. Therefore this causal mechanism cannot be derived from the passage.

29.Option: Giardia infection at baseline was associated with a marked reduction in the rates of subsequent diarrhea, but this protective effect was absent in children receiving multi-nutrient supplementation.
Evidence: Overall, the incidence of first episodes of diarrhoea was almost 50% lower among children with Giardia at baseline than among those without (Table 2).
Giardia was associated with protection against diarrhea among children receiving zinc or placebo, but not among children receiving multi-nutrients.

Thus analyzed, Giardia infection at baseline was associated with a substantial increase in time to first diarrhea episode with ≥3 watery stools/24 h (p<0.001), but only so among children without multi-nutrients, whereas no association was found between Giardia infection and diarrhea in those receiving multi-nutrients (Figure 3; top panels, and Tables 2 and 3).

Adjustment for age and distance to the dispensary led to smaller but still substantial associations between baseline Giardia infection and diarrhea (all events), whilst interaction effects between infection and the multi-nutrient intervention remained virtually unchanged (Figure 4).
29.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that baseline Giardia infection was associated with substantially lower incidence of diarrhea overall (about 50% lower for first episodes) and that this protective association was observed in children receiving zinc or placebo but was absent among children receiving multi-nutrient supplementation. The statement is supported directly by multiple passages, including stratified analyses and adjusted models showing the interaction with the multi-nutrient intervention.

30.Option: The positive associations between helminth infections and anemia observed in previous studies suggest that current control programs are failing to reduce anemia in Zanzibar.
Evidence: There is growing evidence that, besides malaria and nutritional deficiencies, heavy helminth infections as well as multiple helminth species infections of light intensities, are associated with anemia [6]–[8].
The prevalence of anemia was 64.7% in Dole and 50.9% in Bandamaji, with 8.1% (16/198) individuals showing moderate-to-severe anemia, and 2.5% (5/198) individuals being severely anemic.
Participants with an A. lumbricoides infection had a decreased risk of anemia in Bandamaji (OR = 0.55, 95% CI 0.31–0.98).
Large-scale deworming programs in Zanzibar have reduced helminth-related morbidity and are likely to have lowered overall transmission [17], [21].
30.Rationale: The option asserts that positive associations from prior studies imply current control programs are failing to reduce anemia in Zanzibar. The passage does cite prior evidence linking helminth infections and anemia, and it documents high anemia prevalence in the study communities. However, the study's own results do not show consistent positive associations between helminth infections and anemia; in fact, an A. lumbricoides infection was associated with a decreased risk of anemia in Bandamaji. The passage also states that large-scale deworming programs have reduced morbidity and likely lowered transmission. Therefore, the claim that prior positive associations alone indicate that current control programs are failing to reduce anemia in Zanzibar is not supported by the study data presented and cannot be directly derived from the passage.

30.Option: The high prevalence of anemia in both communities, particularly in peri-urban Dole, suggests that anemia is primarily driven by nutritional deficiencies rather than by helminth infections.
Evidence: The prevalence of anemia was 64.7% in Dole and 50.9% in Bandamaji, with 8.1% (16/198) individuals showing moderate-to-severe anemia, and 2.5% (5/198) individuals being severely anemic.
Participants with an A. lumbricoides infection had a decreased risk of anemia in Bandamaji (OR = 0.55, 95% CI 0.31–0.98).
consumption of raw vegetables or salad was a protective factor against anemia (OR = 0.45, 95% CI 0.22–0.93).
30.Rationale: The option claims anemia is primarily driven by nutritional deficiencies rather than helminth infections. The passage provides anemia prevalence and notes that consumption of raw vegetables was protective against anemia (which could suggest a dietary component). However, the study does not provide direct measures of nutritional status (e.g., iron intake, dietary surveys, biomarkers) to establish nutrition as the primary driver. The observation that A. lumbricoides infection was associated with a decreased risk of anemia in one community does not prove that nutritional deficiencies are the dominant cause. Therefore, the assertion that anemia is primarily driven by nutritional deficiencies cannot be conclusively derived from the passage data.

30.Option: The significant differences in seroprevalence between Bandamaji and Dole indicate that the GPELF program was more effective in Dole than in Bandamaji.
Evidence: The seroprevalence of anti-S. haematobium antibodies was significantly higher in Bandamaji than in Dole (46.1% versus 24.8%; χ2 = 14.1, p<0.001). The seroprevalence of S. stercoralis infections was 32.9% in Bandamaji and 12.8% in Dole, which revealed a highly significant difference (χ2 = 15.3, p<0.001).

Both sites received single annual treatments of albendazole and ivermectin in the frame of the GPELF from 2001–2006, and albendazole or mebendazole plus praziquantel via the school-based deworming program till 2006.
30.Rationale: The passage reports higher seroprevalence in Bandamaji than in Dole for certain parasites. However, both communities received the GPELF and school-based deworming during the same program periods, and the passage does not provide data on community-level differences in program implementation, coverage by site, adherence, baseline endemicity, or other confounders. Therefore, one cannot validly conclude from seroprevalence differences alone that the GPELF program was more effective in Dole than in Bandamaji; the passage does not supply the necessary causal evidence to support that attribution.

30.Option: Despite considerable progress made in the control of helminthiases in Zanzibar, the 'worm-problem' and anemia in Zanzibar remains a formidable challenge and cannot be overcome by preventive chemotherapy alone.
Evidence: Large-scale deworming programs in Zanzibar have reduced helminth-related morbidity and are likely to have lowered overall transmission [17], [21].
Of note, in 2006, Zanzibar achieved the minimum target of regular administration of anthelmintic drugs to at least 75% of all school-aged children [16].
The overall prevalence of infection with any helminth species, according to different tests examined under a microscope was 73.7% in Bandamaji and 48.9% in Dole.
The prevalence of anemia was 64.7% in Dole and 50.9% in Bandamaji, with 8.1% (16/198) individuals showing moderate-to-severe anemia, and 2.5% (5/198) individuals being severely anemic.
Combined, this information will provide an important insight into the impact of ongoing interventions and hopefully evidence-based realignment of disease-control priorities.
30.Rationale: The passage states that deworming programs reduced morbidity and met coverage targets, yet the cross-sectional data show substantial ongoing prevalence of helminth infections (notably high in Bandamaji) and high anemia prevalence in both communities. The authors explicitly frame their work as informing a 'realignment of disease-control priorities.' From an epidemiological perspective, persistence of high prevalence and anemia despite high coverage of preventive chemotherapy supports the inference that preventive chemotherapy alone may be insufficient to eliminate the problem and that additional or reoriented interventions are needed. This conclusion can be reasonably derived from the combined statements about progress, remaining high prevalence, anemia, and the need to reassess control priorities.

30.Option: The age-related decline in S. haematobium infection prevalence suggests that the current control strategies have successfully interrupted transmission in older age groups.
Evidence: Regardless of the study setting, S. haematobium infections were most prevalent in the 12–14-year-old age group (17.1% in Bandamaji and 26.3% in Dole). No S. haematobium infections were found in the elderly (≥60 years).

In both communities an incremental increase of age by 1 year was associated with a lower risk of a S. haematobium infection (Bandamaji: OR = 0.93, 95% CI 0.90–0.95; Dole: OR = 0.97, 95% CI 0.95–1.00).
With regard to schistosomiasis, Schistosoma haematobium is the only species endemic in Zanzibar. Its distribution is focal, linked to the distribution of the intermediate host snail [12], [14].

Both sites received single annual treatments of albendazole and ivermectin in the frame of the GPELF from 2001–2006, and albendazole or mebendazole plus praziquantel via the school-based deworming program till 2006.
30.Rationale: While the passage documents an age-related decline in S. haematobium prevalence and no infections in the elderly in the sampled communities, it does not attribute that pattern to interruption of transmission by current control strategies. Age-prevalence declines can result from multiple factors (age-related exposure differences, acquired immunity, focal distribution of the intermediate host, historical exposure patterns). The passage also notes focal snail-linked distribution and does not provide evidence that control measures specifically interrupted transmission in older age groups. Therefore, the causal claim that control strategies have successfully interrupted transmission in older age groups is not supported by the evidence provided.

31.Option: The recombinant yellow-related protein rSP03B and apyrases rSP01B and rSP01, when refolded, show strong immunogenicity in both mice and canine sera and are therefore ideal candidates for monitoring exposure to P. perniciosus in endemic regions.
Evidence: All recombinant proteins except antigen 5 protein rSP07 and D7 protein rSP04 were recognized by the sera of all three repeatedly exposed mice; control sera and the GST tag were negative (Fig. 1A). A similar reactivity of recombinant antigens was found with the sera of the three dogs repeatedly exposed to P. perniciosus (Fig. 1B): all recombinant proteins except antigen 5 protein rSP07 and D7 protein rSP04 were recognized by the sera of repeatedly exposed dogs.

Bitten mice had a highly elevated antibody response to the following seven recombinant proteins: apyrase rSP01B in all three plasmids, both rSP01 apyrases, yellow protein rSP03B and ParSP25-like protein rSP08.

Highly positive correlations with the reaction against SGH were obtained for the two denatured apyrases rSP01B and rSP01 (for both proteins in pET42b with 1 His tag: r = 0.91, p<0.0001; for rSP01B with 2 His tags r = 0.89, p<0.0001; and for rSP01 with 2 His tags r = 0.91, p<0.0001) and yellow-related protein rSP03B (r = 0.89, p<0.0001) (Fig. 3).
All proteins were isolated under denaturing conditions with 8M urea.
Protein refolding was performed using dialysis against PBS using SnakeSkin Dialysis Tubing (10 kDa MWCO, Thermo Scientific Goettiengen, Germany).
31.Rationale: The passage provides direct experimental evidence that rSP03B and the two apyrases rSP01B and rSP01 are strongly recognized by sera from experimentally exposed mice and dogs. ELISA correlations with whole salivary gland homogenate (SGH) are highly positive for these three proteins in canine sera (r ≈ 0.89–0.91, p<0.0001), indicating they reliably reflect exposure. The methods section states proteins were purified under denaturing conditions and then refolded by dialysis, supporting the statement about refolded proteins. Taken together, these statements in the passage support that these recombinant proteins are strong candidates for monitoring exposure to P. perniciosus.

31.Option: The use of recombinant apyrases rSP01B and rSP01 in their refolded form is optimal for monitoring canine exposure to P. perniciosus because they elicit a stronger immune response in dogs than denatured forms and are more stable in field conditions.
Evidence: Highly positive correlations with the reaction against SGH were obtained for the two denatured apyrases rSP01B and rSP01 (for both proteins in pET42b with 1 His tag: r = 0.91, p<0.0001; for rSP01B with 2 His tags r = 0.89, p<0.0001; and for rSP01 with 2 His tags r = 0.91, p<0.0001) and yellow-related protein rSP03B (r = 0.89, p<0.0001) (Fig. 3).
All proteins were isolated under denaturing conditions with 8M urea.
Protein refolding was performed using dialysis against PBS using SnakeSkin Dialysis Tubing (10 kDa MWCO, Thermo Scientific Goettiengen, Germany).
31.Rationale: The passage reports strong immune recognition for the apyrases as tested in the study, but it does not present any comparison demonstrating that refolded apyrases elicit a stronger immune response than denatured forms; in fact, the reported correlations explicitly refer to 'denatured apyrases'. The passage also does not provide any data or statements about stability in field conditions. Therefore, the specific claims that refolded forms are superior to denatured forms and more stable in the field are not supported by the passage.

31.Option: The antigen 5 protein rSP07 and D7 protein rSP04 are the most promising markers for detecting exposure to P. perniciosus in dogs due to their consistent reactivity across multiple experimental methods and their high immunogenicity in both mice and canine sera.
Evidence: All recombinant proteins except antigen 5 protein rSP07 and D7 protein rSP04 were recognized by the sera of all three repeatedly exposed mice; control sera and the GST tag were negative (Fig. 1A).

A similar reactivity of recombinant antigens was found with the sera of the three dogs repeatedly exposed to P. perniciosus (Fig. 1B): all recombinant proteins except antigen 5 protein rSP07 and D7 protein rSP04 were recognized by the sera of repeatedly exposed dogs.
The correlations were not significant for the other four proteins tested (Fig. 3, data not shown for antigen 5 rSP07).
31.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that antigen 5 protein rSP07 and D7 protein rSP04 were not recognized by the sera of repeatedly exposed mice or dogs in immunoblots, and that correlations for several proteins (including rSP07) were not significant in canine ELISA. Therefore, these proteins were not immunogenic in the tested sera and cannot be considered the most promising markers based on the presented data.

31.Option: The ParSP25 protein rSP08 and D7 protein rSP04 are the most reliable markers for monitoring exposure to P. perniciosus in the western Mediterranean because they show strong cross-reactivity with other Larroussius sand fly species and are highly conserved across vector populations.
Evidence: Bitten mice had a highly elevated antibody response to the following seven recombinant proteins: apyrase rSP01B in all three plasmids, both rSP01 apyrases, yellow protein rSP03B and ParSP25-like protein rSP08.

Highly positive correlations with the reaction against SGH were obtained for the two denatured apyrases rSP01B and rSP01 ... and yellow-related protein rSP03B (r = 0.89, p<0.0001) (Fig. 3). The correlations were not significant for the other four proteins tested (Fig. 3, data not shown for antigen 5 rSP07).
CanL occurs frequently around the Mediterranean Basin and in many countries in Latin America, where the prevalence of infection often exceeds 25%.
31.Rationale: While rSP08 elicited a strong response in the small set of mice tested, the passage shows that rSP08 (and especially rSP04) did not give significant correlations in the canine ELISA and that the most reliable canine markers were the apyrases and rSP03B. Crucially, the passage contains no data or statements about cross-reactivity with other Larroussius species or sequence conservation across vector populations, so the claim of broad cross-reactivity and high conservation is unsupported by the presented evidence.

31.Option: Three denatured recombinant proteins from P. perniciosus saliva—apyrases rSP01B and rSP01 and yellow protein rSP03B—are novel recombinant antigens with great promise in screening canine exposure to this important L. infantum vector and for estimating the risk of canine leishmaniases in the western Mediterranean area.
Evidence: Highly positive correlations with the reaction against SGH were obtained for the two denatured apyrases rSP01B and rSP01 (for both proteins in pET42b with 1 His tag: r = 0.91, p<0.0001; for rSP01B with 2 His tags r = 0.89, p<0.0001; and for rSP01 with 2 His tags r = 0.91, p<0.0001) and yellow-related protein rSP03B (r = 0.89, p<0.0001) (Fig. 3).
Measuring the exposure of dogs to sand fly bites is important for estimating the risk of L. infantum transmission.

monitoring canine IgG levels specific for sand fly saliva could indicate their exposure to sand fly bites. Such a monitoring technique would be useful for evaluating the need for, and effectiveness of, anti-vector campaigns [6], [8].
CanL occurs frequently around the Mediterranean Basin
31.Rationale: The passage reports that the denatured apyrases (rSP01B and rSP01) and yellow-related rSP03B show highly positive correlations with total SGH antibody levels in canine sera (strong r values, highly significant p-values), indicating these recombinant antigens reliably reflect exposure. The authors explicitly framed monitoring canine anti-saliva IgG as a means to estimate L. infantum transmission risk and evaluate anti-vector measures, and the study context emphasizes Mediterranean CanL. Therefore, the statement that these three denatured recombinant proteins are promising screening antigens for canine exposure and risk estimation in the Mediterranean is supported by the experimental findings presented.

32.Option: The induction of autophagy by rTsCatL2 is a secondary consequence of cellular damage caused by the physical disruption of tight junctions and cell rounding.
Evidence: When rTsCatL2 was added to Caco-2 cells, the cells gradually shrank and became rounded with increasing incubation time. The tight junctions between the cells disappeared and some of the cells even fell off.
The results indicated that rTsCatL2 induced autophagy in Caco-2 cells.

After rTsCatL2 treatment, Caco-2 cells were stained for MDC fluorescence, and bright green fluorescence was observed upon excitation with UV light (Fig 10A). Transmission electron microscopy revealed the presence of autophagic vesicles and autophagic lysosomes in Caco-2 cells after incubation with rTsCatL2 (Fig 10B).
32.Rationale: The passage documents two observations: (1) rTsCatL2 causes cell rounding and loss of tight junctions, and (2) rTsCatL2 induces autophagy in Caco-2 cells. However, the text does not provide evidence that autophagy is causally secondary to the physical disruption (i.e., it does not show temporal/ mechanistic data demonstrating that junction disruption precedes autophagy or that blocking physical damage prevents autophagy). Therefore the specific causal claim that autophagy is a secondary consequence of physical disruption is not supported by the presented data.

32.Option: The downregulation of claudin 1 expression and the internalization of tight junction proteins by rTsCatL2 are primarily driven by direct proteolytic cleavage of these proteins by the enzyme.
Evidence: When rTsCatL2 was added to the surface of Caco-2 cells, immunofluorescence showed that claudin 1, e-cadherin, occludin and zo-1 were initially located at the tight junctions. After 30 min, the cells became smaller and separated from each other, claudin 1, occludin and zo-1 became smaller in outline and wrapped around each cell, and the cells became less connected to each other. However, e-cadherin aggregated into clusters and was engulfed inside the cells. After 60 min, immunofluorescence showed that claudin 1, e-cadherin, occludin and zo-1 entered the nucleus of the cells, the fluorescence of claudin 1 had disappeared in some cells, the fluorescence of e-cadherin, occludin and zo-1 was still present, and occludin and zo-1 also started to cluster.

It was revealed that the expression of claudin 1 decreased by 43.23% after 30 min incubation and 58.65% after 60 min incubation (F = 81.66, P < 0.05). There was no significant change in the expression of e-cadherin, occludin, laminin and collagen-1 protein in Caco-2 cells (P > 0.05) (Fig 8).
The result showed that rTsCatL2 interacts with laminin and collagen I but not with claudin 1, e-cadherin, occludin or fibronectin.
32.Rationale: While the passage shows that claudin 1 is downregulated and tight junction proteins are internalized after rTsCatL2 treatment, it also reports that rTsCatL2 does not interact with claudin 1, e-cadherin or occludin (Co-IP). The lack of detected interaction undermines the claim that direct proteolytic cleavage of these specific junction proteins by rTsCatL2 is the primary mechanism. The data support protease activity of rTsCatL2 against some substrates (see other sections), but do not provide direct evidence of proteolytic cleavage of the TJ proteins; in fact, Co-IP is negative for direct interaction. Therefore the assertion that direct proteolysis of TJ proteins is the primary driver is not supported by the provided evidence.

32.Option: The interaction of rTsCatL2 with laminin and collagen I suggests that TsCatL plays a primary role in degrading the extracellular matrix to facilitate parasite migration through host tissues.
Evidence: Recombinant TsCatL2 has cysteine protease activity and can hydrolyze host hemoglobin, serum albumin, immunoglobulin and extracellular matrix proteins in vitro with host-specific hydrolytic activity, indicating that TsCatL is a crucial digestive enzyme in T. spiralis [14].
The result showed that rTsCatL2 interacts with laminin and collagen I but not with claudin 1, e-cadherin, occludin or fibronectin.
According to the aforementioned findings, TsCatL facilitated T. spiralis to invade the host epithelial cells.
32.Rationale: The passage documents that recombinant TsCatL2 has cysteine protease activity capable of hydrolyzing extracellular matrix proteins in vitro, and Co-IP demonstrates direct interaction of rTsCatL2 with laminin and collagen I (key extracellular matrix components). The study also links TsCatL to facilitation of larval invasion of epithelial cells. Together, these facts support the epidemiological/biological inference that TsCatL likely contributes to degradation of the extracellular matrix, thereby facilitating parasite migration through host tissues. The language 'primary role' is consistent with the combined enzymatic activity, biochemical binding to ECM components, and functional invasion data presented in the passage.

32.Option: Incubating rTsCatL2 with Caco-2 cells resulted in the loss of intercellular junctions, entry of cell junction proteins into the cytoplasm and cell autophagy, leading to disruption of the intestinal epithelial barrier.
Evidence: When rTsCatL2 was added to Caco-2 cells, the cells gradually shrank and became rounded with increasing incubation time. The tight junctions between the cells disappeared and some of the cells even fell off.

After 60 min, immunofluorescence showed that claudin 1, e-cadherin, occludin and zo-1 entered the nucleus of the cells, the fluorescence of claudin 1 had disappeared in some cells, the fluorescence of e-cadherin, occludin and zo-1 was still present, and occludin and zo-1 also started to cluster (Fig 7A).

When rTsCatL2 was co-cultured with Caco-2 cells, the expression of claudin 1 decreased by 43.23% after 30 min incubation and by 58.65% after 60 min incubation (*P < 0.05), while the protein expression levels of e-cadherin, occludin, laminin and collagen-I did not change significantly (P > 0. 05);
The results indicated that rTsCatL2 induced autophagy in Caco-2 cells.
According to the aforementioned findings, TsCatL facilitated T. spiralis to invade the host epithelial cells.
32.Rationale: The passage provides multiple direct experimental observations: rTsCatL2 causes loss of tight junctions and cell detachment, tight junction proteins relocate into cells and nuclei with a marked decrease in claudin 1 protein levels, and rTsCatL2 induces autophagy in Caco-2 cells. These cellular changes collectively indicate disruption of intercellular junctions and cellular homeostasis consistent with impairment of the intestinal epithelial barrier. The authors also link TsCatL to facilitation of parasite invasion, supporting the inference that these cellular effects lead to barrier disruption.

32.Option: The binding of rTsCatL2 to the cell periphery and its subsequent internalization into the cytoplasm and nucleus indicate that the protein functions as a transcriptional regulator in host cells.
Evidence: rTsCatL2 bound mainly around the cell periphery at the beginning and gradually entered the cytoplasm and nucleus with increasing incubation time;

When rTsCatL2 was added to Caco-2 cells, the cells gradually shrank and became rounded with increasing incubation time. The tight junctions between the cells disappeared and some of the cells even fell off.
The result showed that rTsCatL2 interacts with laminin and collagen I but not with claudin 1, e-cadherin, occludin or fibronectin.
The results indicated that rTsCatL2 induced autophagy in Caco-2 cells.
32.Rationale: Although the passage reports that rTsCatL2 binds to the cell periphery and is internalized into cytoplasm and nucleus, there is no experimental evidence provided that rTsCatL2 acts as a transcriptional regulator (e.g., binding to DNA, altering transcription factor activity, or changing host gene transcription patterns). The data instead document protease activity, effects on junctional proteins, ECM interactions, and induction of autophagy. Thus the specific functional inference that rTsCatL2 serves as a transcriptional regulator is not supported by the presented observations.

33.Option: Patient-reported preferences for oral and topical formulations are primarily driven by aesthetic concerns related to scarring and social stigma, rather than by treatment convenience or burden.
Evidence: In general, patients preferred oral and topical treatments due to the easier administration and being perceived as ‘fitting’ the disease.

This conceptually interesting discussion was mentioned by different patients, who expressed that, since the infection is local, they would prefer the treatment to be also applied locally: a topical treatment for a local infection.
Efficacy was understood as clinical cure in relation to the lesion, the absence of sequelae, scar formation and the prevention of disease relapse/reinfection.
patients discussed the preference for an effective treatment in healing the wound, regardless of scar appearance, potential side effects or treatment duration.
33.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that preferences for oral and topical formulations were driven by easier administration and the perception that a local infection should be treated locally. It does mention scars and stigma as disease consequences and that scar formation is a component of efficacy considerations, but it also reports that patients valued clinical cure even "regardless of scar appearance." Therefore, the claim that preferences are primarily driven by aesthetic concerns is not supported; convenience and perceived fit of formulation to the disease are the stated primary drivers.

33.Option: Incorporating patients' preferences regarding treatment duration, formulation, and cost into Target Product Profiles (TPPs) for cutaneous leishmaniasis can improve the design of new treatments and enhance their real-world effectiveness and accessibility.
Evidence: We present a methodological and analytical approach as to involve patients in the identification of preferred characteristics of a new drug, and how this could inform TPPs.

We present patient preferences along the top-level domains of the DNDi’s TPP, 1 Safety and tolerability, 2 Contraindications, 3 Efficacy, 4 Formulation, 5 Treatment regimen, 6 Target population, 7 Cost, and two emerging categories 8 Perceived barriers and 9 Other development needs.
Themes were then assessed for potential incorporation into a TPP, and for being considered during drug development in general.
Treatment regimen was found to be a TPP category rich in themes and trade-offs.
Costs were frequently mentioned by patients, especially those related to procedures such as administration of injections, consultations, diagnostic tests, and transport.
33.Rationale: The study was explicitly designed to gather patient preferences and assess them for potential incorporation into TPPs. The passage documents that formulation, treatment duration (treatment regimen), and cost are important domains for patients and that themes were evaluated for inclusion in TPPs. From an epidemiological and product-development perspective, integrating these patient-reported preferences into TPPs is a reasonable inference to improve design, uptake, and real-world accessibility of new treatments.

33.Option: The lack of shared decision-making in case management was apparent, with interviewees seeing injections as the only option to get cured, suggesting that patient preferences are often overridden by clinical protocols.
Evidence: one Brazilian patient with contraindications described moments of shared decision making with the treating medical team, which was not observed in other groups.

In contrast, some patients perceived injections as working faster and more effective than alternative modes of administration. While others, such as two Colombian patients, reported injections as the only treatment option.
33.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that shared decision-making occurred in at least one case and that some patients reported injections as the only option or perceived injections as more effective. However, it does not provide systematic evidence that shared decision-making was generally lacking across all sites, nor does it document that patient preferences were 'often overridden by clinical protocols.' The leap from isolated observations (some patients reporting injections as the only option; limited shared decision-making observed in some groups) to a generalized claim about overriding by clinical protocols is not supported by the provided text.

33.Option: The findings suggest that patients in high-income countries are more likely to prioritize treatment efficacy over cost, while those in low-income settings prioritize affordability, indicating a need for region-specific TPPs.
Evidence: Costs were frequently mentioned by patients, especially those related to procedures such as administration of injections, consultations, diagnostic tests, and transport.

In a high-income setting, an Austrian patient describes paying a 140 EUR (154 USD) fee for specialized care to shorten waiting times, while a Brazilian patient described the challenges trying to access specialized care in their town, both due to the costs of the appointment and limited of availability of specialists.
Indirect costs were mainly related to days of work lost and were mentioned as significant.
33.Rationale: While the passage documents that cost concerns and different cost-related experiences exist across settings (including an Austrian patient paying for faster care and patients in LMICs facing transport and indirect costs), it does not present evidence that patients in high-income countries prioritize efficacy over cost or that those in low-income settings prioritize affordability as a consistent, population-level pattern. The data are qualitative and descriptive without comparative quantification to support this specific cross-country prioritization inference; thus the stated conclusion is not directly derivable from the passage.

33.Option: The study demonstrates that discrete-choice experiments (DCEs) are the most effective method for capturing patient preferences in NTD drug development, surpassing qualitative interviews.
Evidence: individual semi-structured interviews of about one-hour duration, related to their disease experiences, preferences and expectations, were conducted with 33 CL patients at four sites in Austria, Brazil and Colombia.
Studies using exploratory approaches (e.g., using qualitative semi-structured interviews or focus group discussions) of patient involvement are scarce [20].

Adepoyibi et al. [18] conducted a survey among laboratory personnel, national tuberculosis control program managers, donors, technical experts, patients and researchers, and asked them to rank the items in a TPP for tuberculosis diagnostic tools by their perceived importance.
33.Rationale: The passage describes the use of qualitative semi-structured interviews as the study method and mentions other methods (surveys) in the literature, but it does not mention discrete-choice experiments (DCEs) or compare methodological effectiveness. Therefore, the claim that DCEs are demonstrated to be the most effective method cannot be supported from the passage.

34.Option: The simulation of parasite development in the SMT suggests that P. vivax parasites spend an average of 25.3 hours as trophozoites and schizonts before termination of the assay, which may explain the variability in EC50 values across different isolates.
Evidence: In the simulations of the growth dynamics under ex vivo assay conditions, the λ parameter for the Poisson distributions represented the duration of each parasite life cycle stage in P. vivax. The mean λ was 19.1 hours (95% CI 11, 29) for rings, 23.1 hours (95% CI 11, 36) for trophozoites, and 2.2 hours (95% CI 0, 7) for the schizonts.

The diversity in parasite life cycle stages in P. vivax infections creates a significant confounding factor, particularly since there is an apparent marked variation of drug susceptibility in different erythrocytic life cycle stages.

In this study we develop a statistical methodology to identify stage specific drug activity in the SMT. We use CQ against P. vivax as a case study and demonstrate that stage specific drug activity has profound consequences for the interpretation of SMT results.
34.Rationale: The passage reports mean durations for trophozoites (23.1 h) and schizonts (2.2 h); their sum is 25.3 h, matching the numeric claim. The authors explicitly state that variation in life-cycle stage composition is a significant confounder because drug susceptibility differs by stage and that stage-specific activity profoundly affects SMT interpretation. Thus it is reasonable, based on the passage, to infer that the simulated duration spent as trophozoites+schizonts (≈25.3 h) can contribute to variability in EC50 measurements across isolates.

34.Option: Because P. falciparum isolates are more synchronous and predominantly contain ring stage parasites, the SMT is more reliable for P. falciparum than for P. vivax, and thus should be prioritized in drug susceptibility testing.
Evidence: The SMT was initially developed for testing drug susceptibility in P. falciparum [13], [14], where almost all parasites in the peripheral circulation are at the immature ring stage.

There was a significantly higher mean proportion of ring stage parasites in samples at the start of the assay (0 hours) for P. falciparum (0.922) compared to P. vivax (0.588) (Wilcoxon rank sum test, p<0.001). 87.5% (189/216) of P. falciparum isolates contained 100% ring stage parasites at 0 hours, compared to only 2.8% (4/141) of P. vivax isolates.

The diversity in parasite life cycle stages in P. vivax infections creates a significant confounding factor, particularly since there is an apparent marked variation of drug susceptibility in different erythrocytic life cycle stages.
34.Rationale: While the passage documents that P. falciparum samples are more synchronous and predominantly ring stages and that the SMT was originally developed for P. falciparum (supporting greater simplicity of interpretation), it does not state that SMT is inherently more reliable for P. falciparum in a way that justifies prioritizing P. falciparum for testing. The policy recommendation to prioritize P. falciparum is not presented or derived in the text. The passage highlights problems specific to P. vivax (stage diversity confounding SMT), but does not make the prescriptive claim that P. falciparum testing should be prioritized; thus the prioritization conclusion is not directly supported by the provided evidence.

34.Option: The emergence of highly chloroquine-resistant P. vivax in Southeast Asia has created an urgent need for improved understanding of drug resistance mechanisms and the development of new antimalarial agents.
Evidence: the emergence of highly chloroquine (CQ) resistant P. vivax in Southeast Asia (CQ remains a front-line treatment for vivax malaria as it is affordable, well tolerated and safe, and its long half-life ensures protection from early relapses [7], [8]) has created an urgent need for an improved understanding of the mechanisms of drug resistance in these parasites, the development of robust tools for defining the spread of resistance, and the discovery of new antimalarial agents.
34.Rationale: This option is a near-verbatim statement from the passage. The authors explicitly link the emergence of highly CQ-resistant P. vivax in Southeast Asia to an urgent need for better understanding of resistance mechanisms and the development of new antimalarials.
