57.Option: The model forecasts indicate that the risk of RVF re-emergence in Mayotte is negligible, even in the presence of new animal imports, due to the island's low vector density and stable climate.
Evidence: the model predicted a very low probability of endemicity (Fig 4B, Forecast 1), with 99.74% of the trajectories indicating extinction in 2016 in a closed ecosystem.

As of October 2016, 97.9% (95%CrI [97.6–98.2]) of the Mayotte livestock is estimated to be susceptible to RVF, such that the import of 40 infectious animals at that date (low NDVI values, Fig 4C, Forecast 6) or in April 2017 (high NDVI values, Fig 4D, Forecast 11) would result in an incidence peak similar to 2008 (Forecast 6: Jul 2017: 1063 cases 95%CrI [242–2385]; Forecast 11: Jul 2018: 936 cases 95%CrI [297–1696]).
We used NDVI as a proxy for climate conditions favouring mosquito habitat commonly used in RVF studies [10,11,44–46], as no data on vector dynamics was available.
A continually high NDVI potentially would allow the sustenance of mosquito breeding and therefore vector transmission even during the dry season [34,35].
57.Rationale: Although the model predicts low endemicity in a closed system, the passage also shows that importation of infectious animals (e.g. 40 animals) would produce large incidence peaks comparable to 2008. The passage does not claim low vector density; instead it notes high NDVI that can sustain mosquito breeding. Thus the claim that re-emergence risk is negligible even with imports and due to low vector density and stable climate is not supported by the passage.

57.Option: The model forecasts indicate a very low probability of RVF virus endemicity and therefore of re-emergence in a closed system, but the livestock population remains vulnerable to the introduction of infectious animals due to a high proportion of naive animals.
Evidence: the model predicted a very low probability of endemicity (Fig 4B, Forecast 1), with 99.74% of the trajectories indicating extinction in 2016 in a closed ecosystem.

As of October 2016, 97.9% (95%CrI [97.6–98.2]) of the Mayotte livestock is estimated to be susceptible to RVF, such that the import of 40 infectious animals at that date (low NDVI values, Fig 4C, Forecast 6) or in April 2017 (high NDVI values, Fig 4D, Forecast 11) would result in an incidence peak similar to 2008 (Forecast 6: Jul 2017: 1063 cases 95%CrI [242–2385]; Forecast 11: Jul 2018: 936 cases 95%CrI [297–1696]).

Models without any animal import (Models 2a and 2b) had the worst fit (Table 2), and failed to capture the IgG prevalence peak (S4 Fig, blue and green solid lines), suggesting that the re-emergence of RVF virus in 2008–10 may have been due to the import of infectious animals.
57.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports the model's forecast of a very low probability of endemicity/extinction in a closed system (99.74% extinction by 2016). It also reports that almost all livestock (≈97.9%) were susceptible in October 2016 and that introducing infectious animals (e.g. 40) would cause large incidence peaks. Together these statements directly support the inference that endemicity is unlikely in a closed system but the herd remains vulnerable to introductions because of high susceptibility.

57.Option: The use of NDVI as a proxy for vector habitat allowed the model to accurately capture the seasonal dynamics of RVF transmission, even in the absence of direct vector data.
Evidence: We used NDVI as a proxy for climate conditions favouring mosquito habitat commonly used in RVF studies [10,11,44–46], as no data on vector dynamics was available.

Both linear and exponential models with seasonal variations of the NDVI including animal imports (Models 1a and 1b) fitted equally well the data (Table 2, DICmodel1a = 522.8 and DICmodel1b = 523.7) and showed a good agreement with the IgG serological data (Fig 2 and Fig 3A–3H).

When transmission was not dependent on NDVI seasonality (Model 3), the IgG prevalence peak was also captured (S4 Fig, black solid line), but the model did not fit the data as well as the NDVI seasonality-dependent models, the DIC being higher (Table 2, DICmodel3 = 543.6).
The seasonal variation of Rst over time reflected the seasonal NDVI values
57.Rationale: The passage states NDVI was used as a proxy because vector data were unavailable, and models incorporating NDVI seasonality fitted the serological data better (lower DIC) than the model without NDVI seasonality. It also notes that seasonal Rst mirrored NDVI. These statements support the inference that NDVI as a proxy enabled the model to capture seasonal transmission dynamics despite lack of direct vector data.

57.Option: The model's prediction of a high proportion of susceptible animals in 2016 suggests that the livestock population has developed herd immunity through natural exposure to sub-clinical infections.
Evidence: As of October 2016, 97.9% (95%CrI [97.6–98.2]) of the Mayotte livestock is estimated to be susceptible to RVF
serological studies carried out in Mayotte in livestock (2004–2016) showed that RVF had been present at least since 2004, and re-emerged in 2008–10, despite no symptoms in animals being detected
57.Rationale: A high proportion susceptible (≈97.9%) indicates little population-level immunity, not herd immunity. While the passage notes RVF was present since 2004 and re-emerged 'despite no symptoms in animals being detected' (i.e. possible sub-clinical infections), the model's estimate of very high susceptibility in 2016 contradicts the claim that herd immunity developed. Therefore the option is not supported by the passage.

58.Option: The persistence of T. infestans populations in the Gran Chaco region is primarily due to low insecticide efficacy in peridomestic structures and unsustained vector control interventions, rather than insecticide resistance.
Evidence: Persistent bug populations that survived the insecticide application at local spatial scales, were related with sources of peridomestic populations, operational failures, reduced residual effect of insecticide or development of resistance to pyrethroid insecticides that decrease the vector control efficacy [22–26].

High levels of insecticide resistance (populations that need 1000 times the amount of active ingredient to kill the same fraction of a susceptible population) leading to control failures were described in the biogeographic region of the Gran Chaco, coinciding with the area of persistent house reinfestation even after insecticide application.
58.Rationale: The passage attributes persistence to multiple factors including reduced residual effect and operational failures but explicitly also identifies development of pyrethroid resistance as a cause of control failures in the Gran Chaco. It does not state that persistence is primarily due to low efficacy/unsustained interventions to the exclusion of resistance; instead both non-resistance operational factors and resistance are implicated. Therefore the claim that persistence is primarily due to non-resistance factors and not resistance is not supported by the passage.

58.Option: The occurrence of pyrethroid resistance in T. infestans is independent of environmental variables and is solely driven by repeated insecticide application across the region.
Evidence: The repeated application of pyrethroid insecticide does not seem to be the only cause of pyrethroid resistance appearance, as resistant populations occurred in areas that received less insecticide pressure than others where resistance did not occur, and because multiple independent resistance mechanisms were detected in several populations.

The diversity of resistance mechanisms and the genetic variability around the putative dispersion center of the T. infestans encouraged the consideration of the influence of environmental variables as another potential cause of pyrethroid resistance occurrence [28].

As far as we know, there is no demonstration of a causal relation between the effect of environmental variables and insecticide resistance, partly because of the difficulty of identifying the individual contribution of the genetic background and the abiotic factors.
58.Rationale: The passage explicitly rejects the idea that repeated insecticide application alone explains resistance, noting resistant populations occurred in areas with less insecticide pressure and that multiple mechanisms exist. It also raises environmental variables and genetic background as potential contributors. Thus the statement that resistance is independent of environmental variables and solely driven by repeated application is contradicted by the passage.

58.Option: The geographic distribution of highly resistant Triatoma infestans populations is associated with a specific combination of environmental variables, particularly temperature and rainfall patterns, and is spatially limited to the region around the putative center of dispersion of the species.
Evidence: The geographic distribution of these groups show a particular concentration of populations with highest LD50 over the region identified as the putative center of dispersion of T. infestans.

The analysis of the geographic distribution of these two T. infestans populations based on the Log (LD50), with 2.6 as the threshold value, showed a significant fit of the GLM model with the environmental variables as predictors.

a model including 5 significant environmental variables was able to explain 55% of the variation in the distribution of the Log (LD50) groups (Table 2). The environmental variables Mean Diurnal Range (Mean of monthly (max temp—min temp)) (Bio2), Mean Temperature of the Driest Quarter (Bio9), Precipitation Seasonality (Coefficient of Variation) (Bio15); Precipitation of the Warmest Quarter (Bio18) are positively correlated, whereas isothermality (Bio2/Bio7) (* 100) (Bio3) is negatively correlated with the occurrence of high LD50 populations.
58.Rationale: The passage reports a geographic concentration of high-LD50 (high-resistance) populations around the species' putative center of dispersion and demonstrates a statistically significant model in which a specific set of temperature- and precipitation-related bioclimatic variables explain a substantial portion (55%) of the variation in high-LD50 occurrence. This supports the inference that highly resistant populations are associated with particular temperature and rainfall patterns and are spatially limited to that region.

58.Option: The bimodal distribution of LD50 values in T. infestans populations reflects differences in laboratory testing methods rather than biological or environmental variation.
Evidence: The descriptive analysis of LD50 values, showed that the frequency distribution of the Log (LD50) is bimodal, suggesting the existence of two statistical subpopulations (groups).
The value Log (LD50) = 2.6 (equivalent to 13.6 ng a.i./insect) is the threshold value that best separates the two subpopulations.

The analysis of the geographic distribution of these two T. infestans populations based on the Log (LD50), with 2.6 as the threshold value, showed a significant fit of the GLM model with the environmental variables as predictors.
a model including 5 significant environmental variables was able to explain 55% of the variation in the distribution of the Log (LD50) groups (Table 2).
58.Rationale: The passage interprets the bimodal LD50 distribution as evidence for two biological subpopulations and demonstrates that environmental variables significantly explain spatial variation in these groups. While the text does note that some reported LD50 values from particular authors lie on different line functions (which could reflect methodological differences), the main analysis treats the bimodality as biologically and environmentally meaningful and finds a strong environmental association. Therefore the claim that bimodality reflects laboratory testing differences rather than biological/environmental variation is not supported by the passage.

58.Option: The intermediate cytogenetic group of T. infestans is the only population with high resistance to pyrethroids, and its presence is solely due to recent genetic mutations caused by insecticide exposure.
Evidence: A map of the distribution of the three T. infestans cytogenetic groups (andean, non-andean and intermediate reported by [21]) and the distribution of Log (LD50) measured on T. infestans populations shows an almost perfect match between the highly resistant T. infestans populations (Log (LD50)> 2.6) and the intermediate cytogenetic group (Fig 3).

The repeated application of pyrethroid insecticide does not seem to be the only cause of pyrethroid resistance appearance, as resistant populations occurred in areas that received less insecticide pressure than others where resistance did not occur, and because multiple independent resistance mechanisms were detected in several populations.

The accumulation of evidence over the last years suggests that the occurrence of insecticide resistance in T. infestans populations is associated with the high genetic variability detected in the historical dispersion site of the species towards the southern cone of South America [21, 42, 43] and the strong spatial structure of the populations derived by low population dispersal rates [24, 44–45].
58.Rationale: While the passage reports an almost perfect geographic match between highly resistant populations and the intermediate cytogenetic group, it does not claim that this cytogenetic group is the only one with high resistance, nor that resistance is solely due to recent mutations caused by insecticide exposure. The text emphasizes multiple independent resistance mechanisms, genetic variability associated with historical dispersion, and that insecticide application alone cannot explain resistance patterns. Thus the exclusivity and sole-causation asserted in the option are not supported by the passage.

59.Option: The small-scale spatial clustering of dengue cases in both cities suggests that reactive spatially targeted interventions such as insecticide spraying are highly effective in reducing transmission.
Evidence: We found that in Ho Chi Minh City cases occurring within a month and 200m of each other were 1.6 times more likely to be homotypic than the probability of any two cases being homotypic at that time point (95% CI: 1.2–2.1).
Significant spatial dependence was observed at distances up to 500m.

The magnitude and extent of spatial dependence observed in Ho Chi Minh City were very similar to previously reported results from Bangkok ... The Bangkok study found cases occurring within the same month were 1.8 times more likely to be homotypic at distances under 200m (95% CI: 1.5–2.2), although significant spatial dependence was observed at distances up to 1km [8].
Understanding how similar dengue epidemics are across settings is also critical for tailoring interventions.
59.Rationale: The passage documents small-scale spatial clustering of serotypes in both cities, but it contains no data or analysis on the effectiveness of specific control measures (for example, insecticide spraying) or on outcomes following reactive spatial interventions. While clustering could make spatial targeting plausible, effectiveness cannot be inferred from clustering alone and requires empirical evaluation of interventions. Therefore the claim that such interventions are highly effective is not supported by the passage.

59.Option: The high similarity in the temporal patterns of dengue transmission between Ho Chi Minh City and Bangkok is likely driven by comparable local environmental factors such as human and vector population density and climate, rather than by significant viral flow between the cities.
Evidence: Bangkok and Ho Chi Minh City are similar in size, population and climate (Table 1 and S1 Fig). These two urban cities in Southeast Asia share similar characteristics with high population density, high rate of immigrants and typical tropical weather with only two seasons: dry and rainy season.
The number of annual cases was highly correlated across the two cities (correlation of 0.77, 95% CI: 0.74–0.79) (Fig 2).
The mean force of infection between 2003 and 2009 was very similar in the two cities with an estimated 2.9% ... in Ho Chi Minh City ... compared to 2.4% in Bangkok (95% CI: 2.3% - 2.5%).
Human movement has been implicated in both local and regional dispersion in epidemics in a number of locations; however, population immunity and climatic factors may also have a major role [6].

Phylogenetic studies have found a rate of viral movement between Ho Chi Minh City and surrounding rural communities [11, 12]. On a broader scale, phylogenetic studies have also suggested that Thailand may act as a source of virus for the region, however, these studies suffer from limited availability of sequences and time periods where sequences are available often do not overlap between countries [6, 11].
59.Rationale: The passage shows strong temporal similarity (high correlation in annual cases and similar forces of infection) and that the two cities are similar in size and climate—facts that make local environmental drivers plausible contributors. However, the passage also explicitly cites human movement and phylogenetic evidence of viral movement as possible drivers and notes limitations in sequence data. The assertion that similarity is 'likely driven' by local environmental factors rather than significant viral flow is a causal attribution the passage does not establish; both sets of factors are presented as plausible and the data do not rule out viral flow between cities. Therefore this stronger, exclusionary claim cannot be derived from the passage.

59.Option: The similarity in the annual force of infection between Ho Chi Minh City and Bangkok is likely due to the shared use of a standardized dengue surveillance system across both countries.
Evidence: To understand the proportion of susceptible people that get infected each year in Ho Chi Minh City, we used hospitalized dengue case data from three referral hospitals in the city: Children’s Hospital Number 1, Children’s Hospital Number 2, and the Hospital for Tropical Diseases.
We used age-specific case data for cases presenting between January 2003 and December 2009 that presented at one of the Bangkok hospitals, made available by the Thailand Ministry of Public Health.

A major strength of this approach is that it is robust to the proportion of cases observed. This is because it does not use on the number of detected cases but instead relies on the age distribution of cases.
59.Rationale: The passage describes different data sources and surveillance structures for the two cities (HCMC data coming from three referral hospitals, Bangkok data from ministry surveillance) and explicitly explains that their force-of-infection method is robust to differences in the proportion of cases observed because it uses age distributions rather than raw counts. There is no evidence that the two countries share a standardized surveillance system, and the passage gives reasons why similar force-of-infection estimates may arise despite different surveillance systems. Thus the specific attribution to a shared standardized surveillance system is not supported by the passage.

59.Option: The absence of synchrony in the prevalence of particular virus serotypes, yet reasonably similar overall epidemiological profiles, suggests that inter-city transmission plays a major role in shaping dengue dynamics in Southeast Asia.
Evidence: We found widely differing levels of correlation by serotype, ranging from high correlation for DENV-1 ... and DENV-3 to no correlation in DENV-2 and negative correlation for DENV-4.
The correlation in annual number of cases was 0.65 (95% CI: 0.54–0.70) for DENV-1, 0.04 (95% CI: -0.19–0.19) for DENV-2, 0.62 (95% CI: 0.35–0.75) for DENV-3 and -0.14 (95% CI: -0.67–0.32) for DENV-4.
The number of annual cases was highly correlated across the two cities (correlation of 0.77, 95% CI: 0.74–0.79) (Fig 2).

Phylogenetic studies have found a rate of viral movement between Ho Chi Minh City and surrounding rural communities [11, 12]. On a broader scale, phylogenetic studies have also suggested that Thailand may act as a source of virus for the region, however, these studies suffer from limited availability of sequences and time periods where sequences are available often do not overlap between countries [6, 11].
59.Rationale: The passage documents that overall case numbers and force of infection are similar between the cities while serotype-specific correlations are variable (some serotypes uncorrelated or negatively correlated). The inference offered—i.e., that this pattern implies inter-city transmission plays a major role—is not supported by the data presented. If inter-city transmission were the dominant driver, one might expect more synchrony in serotype prevalence; the observed lack of serotype synchrony could instead argue against strong coupling or point to complex local dynamics, immunity, or stochasticity. The passage also notes limited phylogenetic evidence and does not conclude that inter-city transmission is the major driver. Therefore the specific causal claim in this option is not derivable from the passage.

59.Option: The high correlation in annual case numbers between the two cities indicates that the dengue epidemics are driven primarily by regional climatic factors that affect both locations simultaneously.
Evidence: The number of annual cases was highly correlated across the two cities (correlation of 0.77, 95% CI: 0.74–0.79) (Fig 2).

Bangkok and Ho Chi Minh City are similar in size, population and climate (Table 1 and S1 Fig). These two urban cities in Southeast Asia share similar characteristics with high population density, high rate of immigrants and typical tropical weather with only two seasons: dry and rainy season.
Human movement has been implicated in both local and regional dispersion in epidemics in a number of locations; however, population immunity and climatic factors may also have a major role [6].
59.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of strong correlation in annual cases and notes that the two cities share similar climates, which makes regional climatic influences a plausible contributor. However, the authors explicitly identify multiple potential drivers (human movement, population immunity, climatic factors) and do not present analysis that isolates climate as the primary driver. The data do not allow attribution of the high correlation primarily to regional climate alone, so the asserted primary causal role of climate is not supported by the passage.

60.Option: The finding that individuals who reported always wearing shoes outside had reduced odds of T. trichiura infection suggests that promoting footwear use should be a core component of STH prevention in this population.
Evidence: Participants who reported always wearing shoes outside were significantly less likely to have T. trichiura infection (AOR 0.19, 95% CI 0.05–0.71, p = 0.013).
60.Rationale: The passage provides a statistically significant protective association between always wearing shoes outside and lower odds of T. trichiura infection (AOR 0.19, p = 0.013). From an epidemiological perspective, an observed strong protective association in an observational study supports consideration of the related behaviour (footwear promotion) as a component of prevention strategies. While causality cannot be proven by this single observational finding alone, the direct association reported in the passage justifies recommending promotion of footwear use as a core, evidence-supported component of STH prevention in this population.

60.Option: The lack of significant differences in A. ceylanicum infection prevalence by sex or age suggests that targeted interventions for this species are unnecessary, and resources should be focused on other STHs.
Evidence: There was no evidence of differences in prevalence by sex or age for A. ceylanicum.

As a zoonotic disease, control of A. ceylanicum requires an intersectoral approach, that incorporates One Health strategies as well as preventive chemotherapy [5] particularly in endemic countries in the Asia-Pacific region where high burdens have been documented [6].
60.Rationale: Although the passage states there were no detectable differences in A. ceylanicum prevalence by sex or age, it also explicitly describes A. ceylanicum as a zoonotic disease that "requires an intersectoral approach" and One Health strategies alongside chemotherapy. Thus the passage does not support the inference that targeted interventions for A. ceylanicum are unnecessary or that resources should be reallocated away from it; rather it indicates that species-specific (including veterinary/One Health) approaches may be required. The option overstates and misinterprets the absence of age/sex differences as justification to ignore species-specific control needs, which is not supported by the passage.

60.Option: The considerable adult reservoir of soil-transmitted helminth infections in Solomon Islands suggests that treatment should ideally be expanded to entire communities, rather than focusing solely on school-aged children.
Evidence: The overall prevalence of infection with any STH was 63.3%, ranging from 27.5 to 91.5% across villages.
Most common were infections with N. americanus (54.5%, range 0–79.2%).

...and there was a pattern of increasing risk of infection with age relative to young children aged 1–5 years (6–11 years [AOR 5.16, 95% CI 3.04–8.77, p<0.001]; 12–17 years [AOR 9.55, 95% CI 4.63–19.68, p<0.001]; 18–34 years [AOR 5.79, 95% CI 3.04–11.01, p<0.001]; 35–49 years [AOR 6.91, 95% CI 3.66–13.02, p<0.001]; ≥50 years [AOR 6.32, 95% CI 3.43–11.64, p<0.001]).

For infection intensity, there was a pattern of increasing N. americanus egg counts with age within in adolescents (IRR 4.04, 95% CI 2.11–7.71, p<0.001) and older adults (≥50 years; IRR 2.55, 95% CI 1.28–5.06, p = 0.007), compared to young children
60.Rationale: The passage documents very high overall STH prevalence and specifically shows increasing prevalence and higher infection intensity of N. americanus (and higher prevalence for some species) in adolescents and adults compared with young children. Epidemiologically, when substantial infection reservoirs exist in adults, control strategies limited to school-aged children may miss a large proportion of the infected population and allow ongoing transmission. Therefore expanding treatment to whole communities (community-wide mass drug administration) is a logically derived implication from the age-related prevalence/intensity patterns presented.

60.Option: The higher annual precipitation in the study area is likely to be a key driver of increased T. trichiura transmission, suggesting that climate adaptation strategies should be prioritized in STH control programmes.
Evidence: Greater annual precipitation was associated with marginally higher odds of infection (AOR 1.14, 95% CI 1.04–1.25, p = 0.008), as was co-infection with N. americanus (AOR 3.39, 95% CI 1.66–6.91, p = 0.001).
60.Rationale: The passage reports a statistical association between greater annual precipitation and modestly higher odds of T. trichiura infection (AOR 1.14). However, from a single observational association it is not possible to conclude that precipitation is the key driver of transmission, nor does the passage provide evidence that climate adaptation strategies should be prioritized over other interventions. The data show an association, but do not establish causality or relative importance compared to other factors (e.g., WASH, behaviour, reservoirs). Thus the stronger policy conclusion advocated by the option is not directly derivable from the passage.

60.Option: The increased risk of Strongyloides spp. infection in adolescents and adults, combined with the zoonotic nature of A. ceylanicum, indicates that control efforts should focus on veterinary interventions in domestic animals.
Evidence: There were no detectable differences in Strongyloides spp. infection prevalence by sex, but again higher levels in adolescents (AOR 10.11, 95% CI 2.58–39.60, p = 0.001) and adults (18–34 years, AOR 5.08, 95% CI 1.17–22.05) than young children.

As a zoonotic disease, control of A. ceylanicum requires an intersectoral approach, that incorporates One Health strategies as well as preventive chemotherapy [5] particularly in endemic countries in the Asia-Pacific region where high burdens have been documented [6].

Similar considerations apply to S. stercoralis also suggested to be a zoonosis [7], and the subject of new targets introduced by WHO to establish a control programme by 2030 [5] which will need to incorporate ivermectin preventive chemotherapy, as albendazole is not effective for this species [8].
60.Rationale: The passage supports two separate points: Strongyloides spp. infections are more common in adolescents and adults, and A. ceylanicum is zoonotic and requires One Health/intersectoral approaches. However, the passage does not state that control efforts should focus solely on veterinary interventions in domestic animals. Instead it advocates combined approaches (One Health plus preventive chemotherapy, including ivermectin). Thus while veterinary interventions may be an important component, the option's exclusive focus on veterinary measures over human treatment and other measures is not supported by the passage. Therefore the option overstates what can be derived from the provided evidence.

61.Option: The attenuated replication of ZIKV-15555 is due to a loss of glycosylation in the envelope protein, which impairs viral entry into host cells.
Evidence: The GUINEA-18 and MR766MC E proteins differ by six amino-acid substitutions with the cluster of mutations I152T/T156I/H158Y that are part of ZIKV glycan loop (GL) region (Table 1).
The GL region of GUINEA-18 but not MR766MC may be N-glycosylated to residue E-N154 [27].
Immunoblot assay using anti-E mAb 4G2 showed that GUINEA-18 E protein migrated slower than MR766MC E protein consistent with a glycan potentially linked to GUINEA-18 E-N154 residue (S1 Fig).
Our data indicated that GUINEA-18 was replicating more slowly, and to lower virus production, in VeroE6 cells compared to MR766MC.
61.Rationale: The option asserts attenuation is due to loss of E glycosylation. The passage states the opposite: GUINEA-18 (ZIKV-15555) likely has an N-glycosylation at E-N154 whereas MR766MC does not. Moreover, the observed attenuated replication occurred in the virus that is potentially glycosylated (GUINEA-18), not in a virus that lost glycosylation. Therefore the evidence in the passage does not support the claim that loss of glycosylation underlies attenuation; it contradicts it.

61.Option: The 5’ and 3’ non-coding regions of the ZIKV-15555 genome are responsible for its attenuated replication in mammalian cells due to structural instability in the RNA secondary structures.
Evidence: At 48 h p.i., there was no significant change in virus production between GUINEA-18/MR766[5’region] and GUINEA-18 (S7A Fig). There were also similar expression levels of E protein (S7B Fig). These results do not argue in favor of major role of the 5’NCR and structural proteins in the replication properties of GUINEA-18.
The swapping of Z-4GUINEA-18 amplicon into MR766MC restrained viral replication by at least 5 log compared with MR766MC.

To determine whether the inability of chimeric MR766/GUINEA-18[3’region] virus to replicate depended on the 3’NCR, site-directed mutagenesis was conducted on Z-4MR766 amplicon to generate a mutant MR766/GUINEA-18[3’NCR] virus bearing all GUINEA-18 3’NCR mutations (Fig 1 and S8A). ... At 48 h p.i., there was comparable virus production between MR766/GUINEA-18[3’NCR] and MR766MC (S8A Fig). ... These results rule out a role for the 3’NCR in the replicative abilities of a chimeric MR766MC virus with the 3’ region of GUINEA-18.
61.Rationale: The passage reports direct experimental tests of the 5’NCR and 3’NCR. Replacement of the 5’ region did not change replication, and specific introduction of GUINEA-18 3’NCR mutations into MR766MC produced virus with comparable replication to MR766MC, leading the authors to state these results "rule out a role for the 3’NCR." Thus the data do not support the inference that the non-coding regions are responsible for attenuation due to RNA structural instability.

61.Option: The NS5 protein of ZIKV-15555 is the primary determinant of its reduced replication capacity due to impaired RNA-dependent RNA polymerase activity.
Evidence: the swapping of Z-4GUINEA-18 amplicon into MR766MC restrained viral replication by at least 5 log compared with MR766MC.

Our data indicated that a chimeric MR766MC virus with GUINEA-18 NS5 protein is unable to sustain infection of mammalian cells, precluding the study of the largest NS protein in attenuation of GUINEA-18.
insertion of GUINEA-18 NS1 to NS4B into MR766MC strongly reduced virus production (Fig 5A). ... Our data thus indicated a key role for NS1 to NS4B proteins in the replication properties of GUINEA-18.
The above results suggest that the replicative properties of GUINEA-18 depend on NS4B protein. ... emphasizing the major role of NS4B in the attenuated phenotype of GUINEA-18 in vitro.
61.Rationale: While swapping the entire Z-4 region (which includes NS5) into MR766MC initially restrained replication, the authors explicitly state that a chimeric MR766MC virus with GUINEA-18 NS5 was unable to sustain infection, preventing direct study of NS5's role. Subsequent, targeted experiments implicate NS1-NS4B (and specifically NS4B) as the key determinants of attenuation. The passage therefore does not support the conclusion that NS5 is the primary determinant of reduced replication; instead, the evidence points away from NS5 as the established primary cause.

61.Option: The attenuated replication phenotype of the contemporary West African ZIKV strain ZIKV-15555 in vitro is primarily driven by its NS1 to NS4B proteins, with a critical role identified for the N-terminal region of the NS4B protein.
Evidence: insertion of GUINEA-18 NS1 to NS4B into MR766MC strongly reduced virus production (Fig 5A). Virus production was similar between MR766/GUINEA-18[NS1-NS4B] and GUINEA-18 (Fig 5A), and the two viruses were comparable for the E protein expression level (Fig 5B) and intracellular vRNA production in VeroE6 cells (Fig 5C).
Our data thus indicated a key role for NS1 to NS4B proteins in the replication properties of GUINEA-18.
The above results suggest that the replicative properties of GUINEA-18 depend on NS4B protein.
Among seven amino-acid substitutions that differentiate NS4B of ZIKV-1555 and MR766, four have been identified at positions 11, 20, 24 and 26 (Table 1).

To better understand the role of NS4B in GUINEA-18 replication, the NS4B gene of MR766MC was mutated to introduce the seven amino-acid substitutions of GUINEA-18 NS4B protein ... infection with mutant MR766MC-NS4B virus resulted in low virus production, comparable to that observed with GUINEA-18 or MR766/GUINEA-18[NS1-NS4B] (Fig 9C). This correlated with weak expression levels of E protein (Fig 9D). Viability of VeroE6 cells infected with MR766MC-NS4B mutant was preserved at 72 h p.i. (Fig 9E).
61.Rationale: The passage provides direct experimental evidence: swapping GUINEA-18 NS1-NS4B into MR766MC recapitulated the attenuated phenotype, indicating these proteins drive the reduced replication. Further, targeted introduction of GUINEA-18 NS4B amino-acid substitutions into MR766MC produced a mutant with similarly low virus production and preserved cell viability, implicating the NS4B N-terminal region substitutions as critical contributors. These statements directly support the option's inference.

61.Option: The reduced replication capacity of ZIKV-15555 in cultured cells is primarily due to its inability to efficiently inhibit stress granule formation, which leads to enhanced antiviral immune responses.
Evidence: As assessed by quantifying eGFP-positive condensates, we observed that GUINEA-18 actively blocked SG formation under environmental stress (Fig 10B).
Thus, GUINEA-18 showed greater efficiency to prevent SG formation in A59 cells stressed with sorbitol, compared to MR766MC.
As evidenced by analysis of fourteen representative ISGs, ISG mRNA levels were significantly lower in A549 cells infected by GUINEA-18 compared to MR766MC.
Moreover, mRNA levels of IFN-β was also much lower in A549 cells infected by GUINEA-18 in comparison with MR766MC.
61.Rationale: This option asserts the attenuated replication is due to inability to inhibit stress granule (SG) formation leading to stronger antiviral responses. The passage reports the opposite: GUINEA-18 more effectively blocked SG formation than MR766MC, and infection with GUINEA-18 induced lower, not higher, IFN-β and ISG mRNA levels compared to MR766MC. Therefore the data do not support the proposed mechanism; indeed, GUINEA-18 inhibits SG formation better and elicits weaker innate responses while being attenuated, so the causal claim is not supported by the presented evidence.

63.Option: In semi-field experiments, a 3:1 ratio of wAlbB-Tw males to wild-type males was sufficient to achieve 70% population suppression of Ae. aegypti, suggesting that this ratio could serve as a suitable starting point for future release trials in Taiwan.
Evidence: To evaluate Wolbachia-transfected mating competitiveness in the semi-field location, three releasing ratios (female WT: male WT: male wAlbB-Tw in 1:1:3, 1:1:5, and 1:1:10) were tested in our study.

During this period, the hatch rate of 1:1:0 group and 1:1:3 group was 72.70 ± 21.66% and 16.57 ± 6.80%, respectively. The hatch rate was significantly decreased in the field containing male wAlbB-Tw (student t-test; p = 0.004, Fig 7A).
According to the above results, the hatch rate was significantly decreased after male wAlbB-Tw competed for mating with male WT in semi-field experiments.
63.Rationale: The passage describes semi-field trials using a 1:1:3 release ratio (which corresponds to a 3:1 ratio of wAlbB-Tw to wild-type males) and reports a drop in hatch rate from 72.70% (control) to 16.57% at that ratio. This corresponds to an approximate relative suppression of (72.70 - 16.57) / 72.70 ≈ 77% reduction in egg hatch, which supports the statement that a 3:1 ratio achieved roughly 70% or greater population suppression and thus could be a reasonable starting point for future release trials in Taiwan.

63.Option: The suppression of wild-type mosquito populations in semi-field trials was not affected by seasonal variations in temperature and humidity, indicating that environmental conditions do not influence the effectiveness of Wolbachia release strategies.
Evidence: We also monitored temperature and humidity of the test site throughout the experiments. During the trial, we did not detect differences in temperature between test sites (site A 17.5 to 29.8°C and site B 18.1 to 30.7°C; median temperature site A = 25.4°C, B = 26.1°C).

During the same period, RH ranged from 77.6% to 99.9% in site A and 79.4% to 100% in site B. Median RH was slightly higher in site B (90.0%) than site A (88.0%). However, in the temperature and RH analysis, this difference was not statistically significant (Mann-Whitney test, p> 0.05) (S4A and S4B Fig).

We also analyzed the results of each mating competitiveness during the test period (S4 Fig and S4 Table), and found that in our test, the temperature and humidity of the test site did not seem to affect the population suppression results of the semi-field. These results suggest that the hatch rate does not fluctuate over time with varying environmental conditions (temperature and humidity) in our test.
63.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that within their semi-field experiments they did not detect statistically significant differences in temperature and humidity between sites and states that, in their test, temperature and humidity 'did not seem to affect the population suppression results' and 'the hatch rate does not fluctuate over time with varying environmental conditions (temperature and humidity) in our test.' However, the option asserts a broader general conclusion — that environmental conditions do not influence effectiveness of Wolbachia release strategies in general. The study only supports the narrower inference that, in these semi-field trials and the specific seasonal/time periods tested, temperature and humidity did not appear to affect suppression. The option overgeneralizes beyond the study's evidence, so it cannot be fully derived from the passage.

63.Option: A 10:1 release ratio of wAlbB-Tw males to wild-type males is optimal for achieving complete population suppression in future trials, as it resulted in 100% suppression in laboratory trials.
Evidence: In the laboratory transparent acrylic cages, WT mosquito populations were suppressed in a ratio-dependent manner with wAlbB-Tw males. ... population suppression reached 100% for males released at the ratio of 1:1:10 (Fig 5A).

From 2020/01/17–2020/06/10, the field environment temperature and humidity were 22.53 ± 3.60°C, 84.65 ± 5.37% and 22.70 ± 3.55°C, 84.75 ± 6.21% in Site A and Site B, respectively. During this period, the hatch rate of 1:1:0 group and 1:1:10 group was 62.73 ± 19.25% and 24.45 ± 11.02%, respectively. The hatch rate was significantly decreased in the field containing male wAlbB-Tw (student t-test; p = 0.02, Fig 7C).
63.Rationale: While the passage reports that a 1:1:10 ratio produced 100% population suppression in controlled laboratory cages, the semi-field (more realistic) experiments at the same ratio did not achieve complete suppression — hatch rates remained about 24.45% versus control ~62.73%. Therefore, the laboratory result alone does not support the stronger claim that a 10:1 release ratio is 'optimal for achieving complete population suppression in future trials.' The evidence shows that 10:1 yielded complete suppression in lab conditions but not in semi-field conditions; hence declaring it optimal for future (field) trials is not supported by the passage.

65.Option: Undernutrition, as defined by low body mass index, is independently associated with an increased risk of developing clinical leprosy, even after adjusting for age, sex, and education.
Evidence: On univariate analysis, both low BMI (OR = 9.25, 95% CI 2.77, 30.81) and low MUAC were significantly associated with leprosy (OR = 6.82, 95% CI 1.78, 26.13).

On multivariate analysis (Table 2), underweight was still significantly associated with leprosy (aOR = 10.32, 95% CI 1.79, 59.67) after controlling for age, sex, and education (as a surrogate marker of socioeconomic status).
Underweight was defined as body mass index (BMI) less than 18.5 kg/m2.
Four (9.8%) controls were characterized as underweight, while 20 (50%) cases were classified as underweight by BMI.
65.Rationale: The passage reports a strong univariate association between low BMI and leprosy and explicitly states that underweight remained significantly associated with leprosy in multivariate analysis after adjustment for age, sex, and education (aOR = 10.32, 95% CI 1.79, 59.67). These statements directly support the inference that low BMI is independently associated with increased odds of clinical leprosy in this study population.

65.Option: The association between underweight status and leprosy in this study population is likely due to the stigma and disability associated with leprosy, which leads to reduced food intake and weight loss.
Evidence: Most individuals affected by leprosy are born and raised in poor environments and continue to be pushed into greater depths of poverty due to the stigma and disabilities associated with the disease [9].
Due to the fact that poverty is such a broad and complex topic to characterize, a causal relationship between poverty and leprosy is difficult to demonstrate.

We hypothesize that undernutrition is a risk factor for leprosy when controlling for S. mansoni co-infection (a highly prevalent helminth that also alters cell-mediated immunity) and socioeconomic status (SES).
65.Rationale: While the passage notes that stigma and disability can push affected individuals into deeper poverty, it also states that demonstrating a causal relationship between poverty and leprosy is difficult. The study was designed to test whether undernutrition is a risk factor for leprosy (i.e., undernutrition potentially precedes disease), and the analyses show an association of underweight with leprosy. The passage does not provide evidence that the observed association is primarily due to reverse causation (leprosy causing weight loss through stigma-related impoverishment or reduced food intake). Therefore, the claim that the association is likely due to stigma-driven weight loss is not supported by the study data as presented.

65.Option: Middle Upper Arm Circumference (MUAC) is a more reliable indicator of nutritional status than BMI in predicting clinical leprosy in this population.
Evidence: On univariate analysis, both low BMI (OR = 9.25, 95% CI 2.77, 30.81) and low MUAC were significantly associated with leprosy (OR = 6.82, 95% CI 1.78, 26.13).

On multivariate analysis (Table 2), underweight was still significantly associated with leprosy (aOR = 10.32, 95% CI 1.79, 59.67) after controlling for age, sex, and education (as a surrogate marker of socioeconomic status). MUAC, also included in the multivariate model, was no longer statistically significantly associated with leprosy (aOR = 1.50, CI 0.22, 10.05).
65.Rationale: Although MUAC showed a univariate association with leprosy, MUAC lost statistical significance in the multivariate model, whereas BMI (underweight) remained strongly and independently associated. This evidence indicates BMI was the more robust predictor in adjusted analyses; therefore the statement that MUAC is more reliable than BMI for predicting clinical leprosy is not supported by the presented data.

65.Option: The high prevalence of multibacillary (MB) leprosy in this study population suggests that undernutrition is a key driver of disease progression from paucibacillary to multibacillary forms.
Evidence: The majority of cases were MB (90%).
An immune response that predisposes an individual to clinically symptomatic leprosy, in particular multibacillary (MB) infection, could be a driver of more infectious cases in the community.

On multivariate analysis (Table 2), underweight was still significantly associated with leprosy (aOR = 10.32, 95% CI 1.79, 59.67) after controlling for age, sex, and education (as a surrogate marker of socioeconomic status).
65.Rationale: While the study reports a high proportion of MB cases and an independent association between underweight and clinical leprosy overall, the data do not analyze or demonstrate that undernutrition specifically drives progression from paucibacillary to multibacillary disease. The presence of many MB cases is descriptive; causation or progression (PB → MB) attributable to undernutrition is not shown in the analyses presented. Therefore the claim that undernutrition is a key driver of progression to MB leprosy is not supported by the passage.

65.Option: Food insecurity, measured by having insufficient funds for meals, is a direct cause of leprosy in this study population, as it leads to malnutrition and subsequent immune suppression.
Evidence: Cutting the size of meals/skipping meals (OR = 2.87, 95% CI 1.0, 8.32) or not having enough money to get more food (OR = 10, 95% CI 3.44, 29.06) was more common in cases of leprosy than controls.
Due to the fact that poverty is such a broad and complex topic to characterize, a causal relationship between poverty and leprosy is difficult to demonstrate.

We hypothesize that undernutrition is a risk factor for leprosy when controlling for S. mansoni co-infection (a highly prevalent helminth that also alters cell-mediated immunity) and socioeconomic status (SES).
65.Rationale: The passage documents strong associations between measures of food insecurity and leprosy in univariate analyses, but also explicitly cautions that causal relationships between poverty (and related factors) and leprosy are difficult to demonstrate. The study design (case-control) and the authors' wording do not establish direct causation—only associations. Thus the statement that food insecurity is a direct cause of leprosy is not supportable from the presented data.

participants in India and Malawi, and participants in HCCs in Benin reported more positive experiences with previous community health programs, like vaccination campaigns. These prior experiences positively influenced their perceptions of cMDA safety and willingness to participate.

MDA treatment data, which indicate that more survey participants (86%) in HCCs were treated via DOT compared to survey participants (69%) in LCCs in Benin. pets were significantly more common (53.5%) in South Indian households (Table 3).

Together these factors (climate, greater cat exposure, and cultural/environmental exposures that increase contact with contaminated soil/water) are supported by the passage as plausible explanations for higher South India seroprevalence. The specific example 'walking barefoot' is not explicitly stated in the passage, but the general notion of cultural practices increasing soil exposure is directly supported by the questionnaire items and living-condition descriptions.

67.Option: The high seroprevalence in South India may be due to the use of untreated water sources, which are more common in rural areas and have been linked to waterborne outbreaks of toxoplasmosis.

68.Option: The fact that only one human case of Lassa fever has been documented in Mali despite high rodent infection rates suggests that human exposure to Lassa virus is rare or ineffective in causing disease.

The claim that human exposure is rare or ineffective cannot be derived: the passage provides no data on the total number of human infections or clinical ascertainment rates in Mali, and substantial rodent infection plus known transmission routes argue against concluding rarity or ineffectiveness solely from the available information.

69.Option: The optimized in vitro assay is robust and capable of detecting subtle differences in morphological changes induced by compounds on juveniles and ex vivo harvested adults, and can be used to identify compounds with activity on advanced liver stages of S. mansoni.
Evidence: The viability of NTS was scored using an Axiovert10 microscope (Zeiss, Germany) according to the scoring system described in [14]. This scoring system of NTS cultures was based on three main criteria: motility, morphology, and granularity.

Hits were determined if the viability score of liver stage (early and late) was ≤ 1.25 at 24 h after exposure to compounds. Thus, the effect of compounds on lung stages was not considered when assigning a viability score.

Out of 340 compounds, 20 compounds were identified as primary hits. In the next step, these hits were then tested on in vitro juvenile worms at three concentrations (3.333 μM, 10 μM, and 30 μM) in duplicates. The same compounds were also tested on adult worms at a single concentration, 30 μM. In both juveniles and adult worms, hits were determined if the average viability score per concentration was ≤ 1.25. From 20 primary hits, 12 compounds were confirmed on juveniles and 2 on adult worms (Fig 4).

From Fig 1, commercial human serum, code S-106B-EU (pooled, mixed gender, off clot) showed comparable performance in the viability of the parasites and development of liver stages (early and late) compared to serum from donors. This allows reproducibility and sustained access to similar batches. Therefore, all following cultivation experiments were performed using commercial human serum S-106B-EU (C_HSe_3).
Z’-factor values of 0.5–1 indicate excellent assay performance.
Z’ ranged from 0.4–0.8 and SSMD between 7.0–19.6, depending on the parasite stage and PZQ concentration.
69.Rationale: The passage describes a defined, morphology-based viability scoring system (motility, morphology, granularity) and documents its application to both juvenile liver stages and ex vivo adult worms. The screening cascade (340 compounds -> 20 primary hits -> confirmed hits on juveniles and adults) and the ability to assign hits based on liver-stage viability (≤ 1.25) demonstrate that the assay can detect compound-induced morphological and viability changes. The discussion of reproducible serum lots and acceptable Z’/SSMD performance metrics supports assay robustness. Therefore the inference that the optimized assay is robust, detects subtle morphological changes in juveniles and adults, and identifies compounds active on advanced liver stages is directly supported by the passage.

69.Option: The platform's ability to detect subtle morphological changes in juveniles and adults suggests it is particularly suited for identifying compounds with irreversible effects on parasite viability.
Evidence: To monitor the effect of compounds on worm cultures, i.e. reversible or irreversible, compounds and controls were removed after a 24-hour exposure from juvenile and adult worm cultures during primary and confirmatory screenings. The supernatant containing compounds was discarded, and worms were washed 3x in HM supplemented with 200 U/mL penicillin and 200 μg/mL streptomycin. After the third wash, the supernatant was removed, and the culture medium was added to each well. Plates were returned to the incubator at 37°C, 5% CO2. The final viability score as a measure of recovery was done on day 7 and day 3 from the initial screening for juvenile and adult worms, respectively.
The damage to juveniles was irreversible after removal of perhexiline with a further decline in viability at day 7 (168 h) (S6 Fig).

As observed in the case of perhexiline, juveniles did not recover on day 7 (168 h). Instead, their viability further declined even after removal of astemizole (S7 Fig); the majority of liver stages were densely granulated, disintegrated and dark brown, and in the ones with shape, motility was present but reduced.
After the compound was washed, male and female adult worms showed a slight improvement in viability (Fig 7).
69.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes a washout protocol designed to distinguish reversible from irreversible compound effects and reports examples where the assay detected irreversible damage in juveniles (perhexiline and astemizole) evidenced by continued decline in viability after washout. The assay also captured partial recovery in adults after washout, showing its capacity to discriminate reversible vs irreversible effects across stages. Thus it is reasonable, based on passage evidence, to infer the platform is well-suited to identify compounds that produce irreversible effects on parasite viability.

69.Option: The use of human serum in the culture medium better mimics the in vivo setting where the drug binds to serum proteins, and consequently, less free drug is therefore available to exert its activity.
Evidence: The pellet was resuspended in culture medium containing HybridoMed Diff 1000 (HM) (Cat. No. F 8055/1, Biochrom GmbH, Germany) supplemented with 200 U/mL penicillin, 200 μg/mL streptomycin (Sigma-Aldrich) and 20% human serum.
During primary screening, the culture medium contained either 20% donor or commercial (S-106B-EU) HSe.

From Fig 1, commercial human serum, code S-106B-EU (pooled, mixed gender, off clot) showed comparable performance in the viability of the parasites and development of liver stages (early and late) compared to serum from donors. This allows reproducibility and sustained access to similar batches. Therefore, all following cultivation experiments were performed using commercial human serum S-106B-EU (C_HSe_3).
PZQ and compounds were tested at 30 μM and DMSO at 0.3% v/v.
69.Rationale: While the passage documents the use of 20% human serum as an essential component of the culture medium and discusses differences between serum lots for parasite development and reproducibility, it does not make any statements about serum-mediated drug binding, reduced free drug concentrations, or mimicking in vivo protein-binding kinetics. The claim that serum use results in less free drug available to exert activity is a pharmacokinetic inference not provided in the passage. Therefore this specific causal inference is not derivable from the provided text.

69.Option: The optimized in vitro assay is capable of identifying compounds with activity on early-stage schistosomula, which are more vulnerable to drug exposure than adult worms.
Evidence: As the majority of schistosomula in each well were in liver stage for NMRI strain, and the aim was to assess the activity of compounds on juveniles, viability score was assigned only to liver stages. Consequently, during primary screening and confirmation, hits were determined if the viability score of liver stage (early and late) was ≤ 1.25 at 24 h after exposure to compounds. Thus, the effect of compounds on lung stages was not considered when assigning a viability score.
Translation from juveniles to adult worms in terms of phenotypic changes was confirmed for two compounds, perhexiline and astemizole (Table 1 and Fig 6).
Although perhexiline and astemizole caused irreversible damage to juvenile worms, the two compounds did not show a detrimental effect on adult worms.
69.Rationale: The assay as described focused on liver-stage juveniles (early and late LiS) for viability scoring and explicitly states the effect on lung stages (earlier stages) was not considered in hit assignment. Thus the passage does not demonstrate that the platform was used to identify compounds acting on early-stage (skin or lung) schistosomula. Moreover, while two compounds caused irreversible damage to juveniles and had lesser effects on adults, this is limited empirical observation and does not establish the general statement that early-stage schistosomula are more vulnerable to drug exposure than adult worms across compounds. Therefore this broad inference is not supported by the provided text.

70.Option: Asymptomatic cysticercosis can be detected in children in endemic areas, and serological screening of children may be highly informative for early detection and treatment.
Evidence: We performed serosurveys using highly specific recombinant antigens for toxocariasis and cysticercosis, and simultaneously analyzed the unpublished data on microscopic observation of soil-transmitted helminthic (STH) infections.
a recombinant chimeric antigen, 100% specific to cysticercosis (0.5 µg/mini gel) was applied for serological confirmation of cysticercosis [16]–[19].
Because the number of cysticercosis seropositivity was rather small (n = 3), we did not analyze the ORs of cysticercosis seropositivity.
70.Rationale: While the passage shows that serology (including a 100% specific recombinant antigen) was used and that some cysticercosis seropositives were detected, it reports only a very small number of seropositive cases (n=3) and provides no data on whether those cases were asymptomatic, nor any evidence that population screening in children would be 'highly informative' for early detection and treatment. The claim about screening being highly informative and actionable for early treatment is not supported by the presented data.

70.Option: The two children with confirmed cysticercosis were symptomatic and exhibited neurological symptoms consistent with epilepsy.
Evidence: A total of 188 people ranged in age from 4 to 78 years (14.9±7.8 years in males and 13.1±6.1 years in females) were examined by clinicians and were asked whether they had experienced symptoms such as cough, haemoptysis, headache, epilepsy, chest pain, and eye disorder and whether they consumed raw and/or undercooked fresh water crabs or pork.
Because the number of cysticercosis seropositivity was rather small (n = 3), we did not analyze the ORs of cysticercosis seropositivity.
70.Rationale: The passage does not report that 'two children' had confirmed cysticercosis, nor does it link the (small number of) cysticercosis seropositive individuals to specific symptoms such as epilepsy. No data are provided on ages or clinical manifestations of the cysticercosis-positive subjects, so the stated specific claim cannot be derived from the passage.

70.Option: The detection of Paragonimus eggs in sputum was more sensitive than serological testing for diagnosing paragonimiasis in this population.
Evidence: Sputum was examined for eggs of P. africanus [11].
Somatic antigens of P. africanus adult worms (5µg/ml), which showed few cross reactivity with other parasitic infections were used for paragonimiasis [11].
Our previous report in this area showed that 8.3% of enrolled people (14/168) suffered from epilepsy but only one of the epileptic patients simultaneously suffered from paragonimiasis [11].
70.Rationale: Although both sputum microscopy for Paragonimus eggs and serology for paragonimiasis were performed (and prior work is referenced), the passage provides no direct comparison of sensitivity or diagnostic performance between sputum egg detection and serology. Therefore the assertion that sputum egg detection was more sensitive is not supported by the presented information.

70.Option: The presence of cysticercosis in children is likely due to consumption of raw or undercooked pork, based on the serological findings.
Evidence: were examined by clinicians and were asked whether they had experienced symptoms such as cough, haemoptysis, headache, epilepsy, chest pain, and eye disorder and whether they consumed raw and/or undercooked fresh water crabs or pork.
Because the number of cysticercosis seropositivity was rather small (n = 3), we did not analyze the ORs of cysticercosis seropositivity.
70.Rationale: The passage records that consumption of raw/undercooked pork was asked in the questionnaire, but it does not provide any data linking seropositive cysticercosis cases to dietary history. Moreover, cysticercosis in humans results from ingestion of T. solium eggs (fecal-oral transmission), whereas eating undercooked pork leads to intestinal taeniasis, not directly to cysticercosis. The passage provides no evidence to support the causal dietary claim.

70.Option: The high prevalence of Toxocara seropositivity in children suggests that toxocariasis is the primary cause of epilepsy in this endemic region.
Evidence: A recombinant antigen of T. canis second-stage larvae (0.5 µg/ml) was used for toxocaraisis [15].

we were lead to obtain more information on the causative agents of epilepsy in developing countries, since many helminthic diseases including toxocariasis, paragonimiasis, onchocerciasis etc., and also protozoan diseases including malaria, toxoplasmosis and others may cause epilepsy [4]–[6].
70.Rationale: The passage describes that toxocariasis was assayed serologically, and mentions that various parasitic infections may cause epilepsy, but it does not present any results demonstrating a 'high prevalence' of Toxocara seropositivity in children, nor does it provide evidence that toxocariasis is the primary cause of epilepsy in the region. Thus this causal inference is not supported by the provided data.

71.Option: Despite high geographical and therapeutic coverage of ivermectin mass drug administration, the continuous transmission of Onchocerca volvulus in the Mbam drainage basin is maintained by abundant blackflies with high vector capacity and efficiency, which can acquire microfilariae from infected individuals who do not comply with treatment or harbor parasite strains with sub-optimal response to ivermectin.
Evidence: Ivermectin must be repeatedly given once a year for more than 15 years (with at least 60% geographical and chemotherapeutic coverage of MDA) to successfully achieve the elimination of onchocerciasis by the year 2030 as desired by the World Health Organization [12].

Following the adoption of IVM-MDA as a control/elimination strategy in Cameroon, it has been demonstrated that the Community Directed Treatment with Ivermectin (CDTI) project of Central Region at the level of Bafia had limited impact on onchocerciasis in the human population despite more than 15 years of repeated annual treatment with IVM [8].
In some of the communities, about 60% of individuals screened for onchocerciasis were still harbouring Mf in their skin [8].
The presence of infection in humans even after numerous rounds of IVM treatment means that there is continuous transmission by the vectors.
The biting rate of Simulium vector for O. volvulus in the Mbam river basin fluctuates between 729 and 1,539 flies/man/day (Fig 2).

The analysis of the Mbam river drainage revealed a very high rate of water discharge during both rainy and dry seasons in Bafia (study site). ... The high speed and volume of water plus the presence of rocks and vegetation in the river serves as excellent breeding site for blackflies in both the rainy and dry seasons of the year.
As of the year 2007, WHO [24] reported a 99.5% geographical coverage of all 15 CDTI projects in Cameroon including the Centre 1 CDTI project where the Mbam drainage basin is situated.

The therapeutic coverage in the Centre 1 CDTI project increased from as low as 42% in 2000 to above 75% in 2009, ranged between 75–80% from 2010 to 2014 and remained above 80% during the 2015, 2016 and 2017 treatment periods [23].
However, Kamga and colleagues observed a lower therapeutic coverage than that reported during the 2014 MDA campaign in the study site [23].
71.Rationale: The passage supports that despite high geographical and improved therapeutic coverage in the program area, onchocerciasis persists and there is continuous transmission by abundant blackflies in the Mbam drainage basin (quotes show persistent Mf in humans, high biting rates, and excellent perennial breeding sites created by river dynamics/dams). It also documents variability and instances of lower therapeutic coverage in the area, which plausibly allows acquisition of microfilariae from untreated/non-compliant individuals. However, the option adds an assertion that parasite strains with sub-optimal response to ivermectin are responsible; the Passage Body provides no direct evidence of ivermectin resistance or sub-optimal drug response. Because the option combines supported (vector abundance, continued transmission, potential non-compliance) and unsupported (drug resistance) claims into a single causal statement, the entire statement cannot be fully derived from the passage and is therefore rated Incorrect.

71.Option: The construction of the Mape and Bamendjing dams has led to a permanent increase in blackfly breeding sites, making onchocerciasis elimination impossible in the Mbam drainage basin without large-scale dam removal.
Evidence: The Bamendjing dam is located between longitude 10 ° 30 ’03 " East and latitude 5 ° 41’ 55" north, and is built on the Noun river. This reservoir dam was put into service in May 1974. It created an artificial lake whose surface varies from 25,500 ha to 33,300 ha depending on the season.

The Mape reservoir-dam, located at 11 km Northeast of Magba, on the Mape, was built in 1988 ... It has an altitude of 724m with a retention capacity of 3.2 billion m3 of water on an area of 50,000 ha.

The analysis of the Mbam river drainage revealed a very high rate of water discharge during both rainy and dry seasons in Bafia (study site) due to the presence of two dams on the Mbam tributaries (Noun and Mape) at Bamendjing and Mape, respectively.
The high speed and volume of water plus the presence of rocks and vegetation in the river serves as excellent breeding site for blackflies in both the rainy and dry seasons of the year.

Using marked positions on tree trunks and branches as reference points, we noticed very minimal changes in water level (height) between the rainy and dry seasons within the nine months of fly collection.
71.Rationale: The Passage Body documents the presence of two dams, year-round high water discharge, and states that the river conditions provide excellent breeding sites for blackflies in both seasons. These facts support that dams and regulated flow contribute to persistent blackfly breeding. However, the specific causal claim that elimination is impossible without large-scale dam removal is not supported by the passage. The authors do not state that elimination cannot be achieved without removing dams nor present evidence that dam removal is necessary; therefore the strong prescriptive conclusion in the option is not derivable from the passage and is Incorrect.

71.Option: The high monthly transmission potential (MTP) observed at 6 and 9 months post-MDA indicates that ivermectin treatment is ineffective in reducing microfilarial load in the human population, leading to sustained transmission by vectors.
Evidence: The monthly transmission potential of O. volvulus infective larvae from flies to humans dropped from 340 L3H/man/month before IVM-MDA to 229 L3H/man/month after 30 days of treatment. The MTP further decreased to 95 L3H/man/month at D90 but drastically increased to 205 and 471 L3H/man/month at six and nine months post treatment, respectively (Fig 4). Within the first three months post MDA, MTP dropped from 340 to 95 (72% reduction).

If IVM is administered in large scale, there will be a potential reduction of microfilaria (Mf) in the human population as it is known that a single standard dose (150 μg/kg body weight) of IVM can induce 98–99% reduction in skin microfilarial (Mf) load of O. volvulus within one month of treatment [16].
71.Rationale: The passage shows a substantial initial reduction in MTP (72% within three months) and cites evidence that a single IVM dose induces a 98–99% reduction in skin microfilarial load within one month, demonstrating short-term efficacy. The later rebound in MTP at 6 and 9 months is documented, but this pattern does not logically imply that ivermectin is entirely ineffective at reducing microfilarial load; rather the data show temporary reduction followed by recrudescence of transmission potential. The passage does not provide evidence that ivermectin fails to reduce Mf overall or that the observed rebound is due solely to drug ineffectiveness; other explanations (e.g., vector abundance, incomplete coverage, reinfection) are possible. Therefore the option's categorical conclusion that ivermectin treatment is ineffective is not supported and is Incorrect.

71.Option: The detection of O. ochengi in two body pools at 9 months post-MDA suggests that animal Onchocerca species are a significant source of transmission in the Mbam drainage basin, contributing to the persistence of onchocerciasis despite ivermectin treatment.
Evidence: Using the triplex real-time PCR for the detection of O. ochengi parasites (larvae) in the Simulium damnosum, none of the 129 head pools were positive (0%) while two of the 129 body pools (1.6%) had positive signals.
This gave us a zero infective rate and an infection rate of 1.4 (n = 129 pools of 100 flies with 127 negative pools) per 1,000 parous flies.
The two pools that were positive for O. ochengi were collected at 9 months post MDA in April 2017.
71.Rationale: The passage reports only two positive body pools for O. ochengi (1.6% of body pools), with zero positive head pools and a calculated zero infective rate. These data indicate a very low prevalence of animal Onchocerca DNA in vectors and show no evidence of infective-stage larvae (L3) of O. ochengi in heads. The authors do not present data that animal Onchocerca species are a significant source of human O. volvulus transmission or that they materially contribute to persistence of human onchocerciasis in the area. Therefore the option's claim that O. ochengi is a significant contributor is not supported by the passage and is Incorrect.

72.Option: Healthcare workers who perform skin biopsies on patients with cutaneous leishmaniasis are at risk of occupational infection through accidental needlestick injuries, and clinicians should consider this route of transmission when evaluating unexplained skin lesions in such individuals.
Evidence: We present a case of an accidental direct inoculation of Leishmania panamensis.

In November 2021, a 29-year-old otherwise healthy dermatology resident treated a Haitian immigrant patient with a presumptive diagnosis of an infectious dermatosis. She performed a skin biopsy on the patient and suffered an accidental needlestick injury on the left index finger.
In January 2022, 2 months after the puncture, the resident developed an erythematous, painless papule at the point of inoculation
A skin biopsy was compatible with leishmaniasis.
72.Rationale: The passage documents a clear instance of occupational transmission via needlestick during a skin biopsy: an accidental direct inoculation occurred, followed by development of a lesion at the puncture site that was biopsy-confirmed as leishmaniasis. Therefore it is epidemiologically justified to infer that healthcare workers performing such procedures are at risk from needlestick transmission and that clinicians should consider this route when evaluating unexplained skin lesions in such workers.

72.Option: The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention has guidelines for preventing the transmission of infectious diseases in the healthcare workplace, including needlestick and other sharps-related injury prevention programs, which should be strictly followed to prevent occupational leishmaniasis.
Evidence: The protocol for the management of needlestick and sharps-related injuries was implemented; blood tests, including a panel for HIV, hepatitis B, and hepatitis C virus, had no significant findings.
72.Rationale: The passage notes that a protocol for management of needlestick/sharps injuries was implemented after the incident, but it does not mention the CDC, specific CDC guidelines, or any evidence that such guidelines will prevent occupational leishmaniasis. Inferring the existence or applicability of CDC guidelines, or that strict adherence to them would prevent occupational leishmaniasis, requires information not provided in the passage and therefore cannot be derived solely from the presented text.

72.Option: The successful treatment of the resident with meglumine antimoniate and intralesional injections indicates that cutaneous leishmaniasis following occupational exposure is inherently less severe than the mucocutaneous form seen in the patient.
Evidence: The Haitian patient was diagnosed with mucocutaneous leishmaniasis caused by Leishmania panamensis, and treatment was established with liposomal amphotericin B at 3 mg/kg per day intravenously (IV) for 10 days, followed by meglumine antimoniate at 20 mg/kg per day IV for 28 days, with complete remission.

A 20-day treatment with meglumine antimoniate, at 20 mg/kg per day IV, was established. After completing treatment, a small defect remained, so three additional doses of 1 ml intralesional meglumine antimoniate were administered. A week after the last injection, the ulcer had healed completely.
72.Rationale: While the passage describes successful treatments for both the mucocutaneous patient and the resident with cutaneous disease, it provides only a single occupational exposure case and no comparative data on severity by exposure route. Concluding that occupationally acquired cutaneous leishmaniasis is inherently less severe than mucocutaneous disease is an overgeneralization not supported by the case report evidence.

72.Option: The delayed onset of symptoms two months after the needlestick injury suggests that the resident’s immune system was unable to control the Leishmania infection initially, leading to a prolonged incubation period and eventual clinical manifestation.
Evidence: In January 2022, 2 months after the puncture, the resident developed an erythematous, painless papule at the point of inoculation
Clinical manifestations vary from self-limited cutaneous disease to a life-threatening visceral infection, depending on the parasite species, vector, biology, and host’s immune response [1].
72.Rationale: The passage states the timing of symptom onset (2 months after puncture) and that clinical manifestations depend in part on the host's immune response, but it does not provide evidence that the delayed onset was due to an inability of the resident's immune system to control the infection. Inferring a specific immunologic failure as the cause of the incubation period is speculative and not directly supported by the presented case details.

73.Option: The observed clinical signs and onset of disease are alike with what has been described for HPS in humans and the ANDV model in Syrian hamsters, suggesting that the immune system plays a central role in disease development through overactivation.
Evidence: HPS onset is characterized by typical viral symptoms: headache, fever, myalgia, nausea, diarrhea and even abdominal pain are frequently present.

This model, immunocompetent golden Syrian hamsters were infected with Andes virus (ANDV). Starting 11 days post infection, hamsters died or showed signs of respiratory distress and a human-like disease pattern [13].
Surprisingly, only ANDV was potentially lethal for hamsters whereas other New World hantaviruses showed no such outcomes.
Building further on this model, the use of immunosuppressive agents was tested by Brocato et al. with the goal of adapting the model for Sin Nombre virus (SNV) [14].
Infection of immunosuppressed hamsters with New World hantaviruses results in high mortality
The onset of disease was abrupt and mortality occurred within 24–36 hours after the first signs.
73.Rationale: The passage does support that the clinical signs and abrupt onset in the experimental model resemble HPS (quotes 1,2,6). However, the key causal claim in the option — that the immune system plays a central role through overactivation (i.e., immunopathology/overactive immune response) — is not supported by the passage. In fact, the passage emphasizes that applying immunosuppressive agents enabled otherwise non-lethal New World hantaviruses to cause lethal disease in hamsters (quotes 4 and 5), which argues that reducing immunity increased susceptibility. That is inconsistent with concluding that disease is driven by immune overactivation based on the provided data. Therefore the specific claim about overactivation cannot be derived from the passage.

73.Option: The passive immunization experiment confirms that the immune system is responsible for the observed disease pathology, as neutralizing antibodies prevented mortality in infected hamsters.
Evidence: Passive immunization was carried out to confirm that the virus was responsible for the observed disease and mortality.
Two test groups received 10,000 neutralizing antibody units of BCCV or BAYV on day -1. On day 0 all groups were infected with 2,000 PFU of BCCV.

In the control groups animals died between 11 and 17 days post infection. In the test groups, receiving the BAYV and BCCV antibodies, all 8 hamsters in each group were still alive at the end of the experiment on day 35 post infection (Fig 3).
RT-PCR performed on kidney, liver and lungs from the infected groups showed the presence of viral RNA in all animals.
73.Rationale: The passage clearly shows that passive transfer of neutralizing antibodies protected immunosuppressed hamsters from mortality (quotes 2–3), and that virus was present in infected animals (quote 4).
