This demonstrates that neutralizing antibodies can prevent death and confirms that the virus causes the observed lethal disease. However, the option's wording that "the immune system is responsible for the observed disease pathology" is ambiguous and can be read to claim that the immune response causes the pathology (immune-mediated disease). The experimental data instead show that antibody-mediated immunity is protective, not that the immune system is the cause of the pathology. Thus, while the experiment confirms a protective role for antibodies and confirms viral causation, it does not support the stronger claim that the immune system is responsible for producing the pathology; therefore the option overstates what can be inferred from the passage.

73.Option: The fact that immunosuppressive agents are able to make Syrian hamsters vulnerable for hantaviruses other than Andes virus indicates that an interesting and probably complicated interplay between the virus and the immune system is responsible for the occurrence of hantavirus disease and its associated mortality.
Evidence: Surprisingly, only ANDV was potentially lethal for hamsters whereas other New World hantaviruses showed no such outcomes.
Building further on this model, the use of immunosuppressive agents was tested by Brocato et al. with the goal of adapting the model for Sin Nombre virus (SNV) [14].
The scope of the current study is to further explore the use of immunosuppressive treatment to achieve an animal disease model for New World hantaviruses.
Infection of immunosuppressed hamsters with New World hantaviruses results in high mortality

For ANDV, 100% mortality was observed between 12 and 15 days post infection (dpi), for Bayou virus (BAYV) between 14 and 18 dpi, for Black Creek Canal virus (BCCV) between 12 and 15 dpi, for Caño Delgadito virus (CDV) between 13 and 20 dpi and for SNV between 11 and 14 dpi.
73.Rationale: The passage documents that in immunocompetent hamsters only ANDV caused lethal disease, whereas after immunosuppressive treatment multiple New World hantaviruses produced high mortality (quotes 1–5). This experimental pattern supports the inference that interactions between the viruses and the host immune system determine whether infection leads to lethal disease; because changing host immune status (immunosuppression) altered outcomes across viruses, it is reasonable to infer a complex interplay between virus and immune response underlies disease occurrence and mortality. The option is cautiously worded ('interesting and probably complicated interplay') and therefore is supported by the presented data.

73.Option: The lack of mortality in immunocompetent Syrian hamsters infected with New World hantaviruses other than Andes virus suggests that viral pathogenicity is primarily determined by host-specific immune responses rather than viral genetic factors.
Evidence: Surprisingly, only ANDV was potentially lethal for hamsters whereas other New World hantaviruses showed no such outcomes.
SNV was previously tested in immunocompetent hamsters but did not cause any disease[13].
Building further on this model, the use of immunosuppressive agents was tested by Brocato et al. with the goal of adapting the model for Sin Nombre virus (SNV) [14].
Infection of immunosuppressed hamsters with New World hantaviruses results in high mortality
For ANDV, 100% mortality was observed between 12 and 15 days post infection (dpi), ... and for SNV between 11 and 14 dpi.
73.Rationale: The data show that host immune status clearly influences outcome: immunocompetent hamsters were resistant to many New World hantaviruses while immunosuppressed hamsters experienced high mortality (quotes 1,2,4). However, the statement that viral pathogenicity is 'primarily determined' by host-specific immune responses (rather than viral genetic factors) is too strong and not supported exclusively by the passage. The passage also indicates that ANDV alone was lethal in immunocompetent hamsters (quote 1), implying viral genetic or intrinsic viral factors can determine pathogenicity in at least some contexts. The experiments demonstrate an important role for host immunity but do not rule out significant contributions of viral genetic factors; therefore the assertion that pathogenicity is primarily determined by host immune responses cannot be derived solely from the provided evidence.

73.Option: The use of immunosuppressive agents enables the induction of lethal disease in Syrian hamsters infected with multiple New World hantaviruses, indicating that the immune system plays a critical role in modulating disease severity and that viral pathogenicity is not solely determined by the virus itself.
Evidence: Surprisingly, only ANDV was potentially lethal for hamsters whereas other New World hantaviruses showed no such outcomes.
Building further on this model, the use of immunosuppressive agents was tested by Brocato et al. with the goal of adapting the model for Sin Nombre virus (SNV) [14].
The scope of the current study is to further explore the use of immunosuppressive treatment to achieve an animal disease model for New World hantaviruses.

In order to develop a disease model for New World hantaviruses, 10 groups of 8 Syrian gold hamsters were treated with a dexamethasone/cyclophosphamide combination (DEX/CYP) according to the treatment scheme in Table 1.
Infection of immunosuppressed hamsters with New World hantaviruses results in high mortality

For ANDV, 100% mortality was observed between 12 and 15 days post infection (dpi), for Bayou virus (BAYV) between 14 and 18 dpi, for Black Creek Canal virus (BCCV) between 12 and 15 dpi, for Caño Delgadito virus (CDV) between 13 and 20 dpi and for SNV between 11 and 14 dpi.
73.Rationale: The passage documents that while only ANDV caused lethal disease in immunocompetent hamsters, treating hamsters with immunosuppressive agents (DEX/CYP) prior to infection allowed multiple New World hantaviruses to cause high mortality (quotes 1–6). This experimental result directly supports the inference that the host immune system critically modulates disease severity and that viral pathogenicity cannot be understood solely by viral properties in isolation. Thus the option correctly summarizes what can be inferred from the experimental results.

74.Option: The variation in infection prevalence between villages over time is best explained by differences in treatment coverage and local socioeconomic factors.
Evidence: The 16 villages varied widely in their pre-treatment and post-treatment prevalence, and in how rapidly infection returned after treatments had been discontinued (Figure 2).
The mean coverage of antibiotic at a village visit was 94.1% relative to the census, ranging from 73.9% to 100% of those eligible for treatment (Table 1).
The program has had remarkable success in areas with moderate levels of infection and in areas where socioeconomic improvements are occurring in concert with the trachoma program.
74.Rationale: The passage documents wide variation between villages and provides two plausible contributing factors (variation in coverage and socioeconomic improvements). However, it does not present analyses that demonstrate these factors are the primary or 'best' explanation for the between-village variation. The statement in the option is a strong causal claim ('best explained by') that is not directly tested or established in the passage, so it cannot be derived solely from the provided evidence.

74.Option: The return of infection after treatment discontinuation suggests that the antibiotic resistance is developing in the chlamydial population.
Evidence: Infection increased in the 24 months after the last treatment in 14 of 16 villages, but remained lower than it had been before the first treatment in all 16 villages.
The first treatment reduced infection in all 16 villages, and the next three treatments further reduced the prevalence in 14 of 16 villages.
The next three biannual distributions continued to reduce the prevalence to 2.6% (range 0.0% to 7.0%), a significant decrease from after a single treatment (P = 0.0004).
74.Rationale: While the passage reports that infection returned in most villages after treatments were stopped, it provides no data or discussion indicating the emergence of antibiotic resistance. The documented continued efficacy of repeated distributions (further reductions to 2.6%) argues against concluding resistance from the observed resurgence. The return of infection after stopping treatment can be explained by re-emergence or ongoing transmission rather than development of resistance, but the passage does not provide evidence to support the specific claim of resistance.

74.Option: Infection may never return if it is brought to a low enough level, analogous to the Allee effect found in population biology.
Evidence: Even a single distribution can dramatically reduce the prevalence of chlamydia[3],[4],[5],[6]. In modestly infected communities, infection may then remain low[5].
In fact, in some areas, infectious trachoma is decreasing even in the absence of treatment[7],[8],[9].
From 6 to 24 months after the last treatment (the 24 and 42 month visits), infection had returned to an average of 25.2% (range 2.0% to 70.0%).
74.Rationale: The passage notes that in some modestly infected communities infection may remain low and that infectious trachoma has been decreasing in some areas without treatment, which suggests that sustained low levels are possible in some contexts. However, the study's observed pattern shows that infection returned in most villages after treatment cessation (average return to 25.2%), so there is no general support for the strong claim that infection 'may never return' if reduced enough. The specific ecological concept of an Allee effect is not discussed or evidenced in the passage, making this option an unsupported extrapolation.

74.Option: Even after four biannual distributions of azithromycin, infection predictably returns in trachoma-endemic communities, indicating that mass antibiotic distributions cannot be discontinued without risk of resurgence.
Evidence: The first treatment reduced infection in all 16 villages, and the next three treatments further reduced the prevalence in 14 of 16 villages.
The next three biannual distributions continued to reduce the prevalence to 2.6% (range 0.0% to 7.0%), a significant decrease from after a single treatment (P = 0.0004).
Infection increased in the 24 months after the last treatment in 14 of 16 villages, but remained lower than it had been before the first treatment in all 16 villages.
From 6 to 24 months after the last treatment (the 24 and 42 month visits), infection had returned to an average of 25.2% (range 2.0% to 70.0%).
At 24 months after the last treatment, the mean prevalence of infection was far higher than at 6 months after the last treatment (P = 0.008), but was still only 40% of that at baseline (P<0.0001).
74.Rationale: The passage provides direct empirical evidence from the study that although repeated (four biannual) azithromycin distributions substantially reduced infection prevalence, infection increased again in the majority of villages after treatments were discontinued. This pattern indicates a substantial risk of resurgence following cessation of mass antibiotic distributions in endemic communities. Therefore, it is epidemiologically justified to infer that stopping distributions carries a risk of infection returning.

76.Option: The higher total costs associated with P. vivax infections compared to other Plasmodium species may be explained by longer treatment duration, more frequent clinic visits, and higher caregiver involvement due to the younger age profile of affected patients.
Evidence: P. vivax patients presented with the highest total costs at $199.05 (SD=173.87; Table 4).
most of the P. vivax costs were direct costs (63%) where hospital and clinic out-of-pocket costs were double the direct costs of malaria due to other Plasmodium species (Table E in S1 Text).
P. vivax (11.0 days) and P. falciparum (10.3 days) had a higher number of days affected by illness compared to P. knowlesi (8.7 days) and P. malariae (8.5 days).
P. knowlesi is most prevalent in males aged between 20 and 40 years in Malaysia, who are the most commonly reported demographic in formal employment [15,18,19].
76.Rationale: The passage documents that P. vivax had the highest mean total cost and that most of these costs were direct (hospital and clinic costs). It also shows P. vivax had a higher number of days affected by illness. However, the proposed explanatory factors in the option — longer treatment duration, more frequent clinic visits, higher caregiver involvement, and a younger age profile of affected patients — are not explicitly supported by the passage. The passage does not state that P. vivax cases have a younger age profile or increased caregiver involvement; instead it highlights that P. knowlesi is most prevalent in working-age males. Therefore the specific causal explanations offered go beyond what can be derived from the provided data and are not supported by direct evidence in the passage.

76.Option: The use of standardized wage estimates from national surveys to value productivity losses resulted in a 78% increase in total cost per episode, indicating that the base case underestimates the true economic burden of malaria on households.
Evidence: The mean total household cost per malaria episode was US$130.53 (SD=102.24) with a median of $96.11 (IQR=$70.73, $153.01) (Table 3).

Compared to the base case estimate of US$130.53 per person, the cost per episode decreased to US$114.30 when wage loss estimates were only applied to adults who reported their wages for Scenario 1 (Table 6).

Finally, when the household inequities survey for Sabah was utilized (US$389.21 per month) [31], the total cost increased by 78% (US$236.03) when applied to all adults in Scenario 4 and 104% (US$270.41) when applied to all patients in Scenario 5.
76.Rationale: The passage shows a base-case mean total cost per episode of US$130.53 and documents that applying higher, standardized wage estimates from survey sources (household inequities survey) increased the total cost by 78% (to US$236.03) in one scenario. From these data it is reasonable to infer that the base-case (which used self-reported wages with imputations) may underestimate the economic burden compared to estimates using standardized survey wages. The quoted scenario results directly support the numerical increase stated in the option, and the inference about underestimation follows from those comparisons.

76.Option: Households in Sabah face a disproportionate financial burden from malaria due to the high prevalence of zoonotic P. knowlesi transmission among working-age males in rural agricultural areas, which increases both direct and indirect costs.
Evidence: P. knowlesi is most prevalent in males aged between 20 and 40 years in Malaysia, who are the most commonly reported demographic in formal employment [15,18,19].

More than half of P. knowlesi cases nationally have been attributed to agriculture and plantation-based work activities, with many of the remaining cases linked to timber logging and forestry work [22].
In 2017 Sabah accounted for 45% of all reported malaria cases in Malaysia, with an almost five times greater annual case-incidence than the national average [26].

The total cost for working-age males was higher than the remainder of the participants, with a mean of US$146.34 (SD=106.90) versus US$112.01 (SD=93.88), respectively (p=0.006; Table D in S1 Text). While direct costs were similar between working-age males and the rest of the population, working-age males reported higher indirect costs at US$95.66, compared to US$52.62 for others (p<0.001).
P. falciparum and P. knowlesi had similar mean costs of US$119.69 (SD=82.68) and US$119.94 (SD=81.39), respectively.
76.Rationale: The passage supports parts of the option: P. knowlesi is prevalent among working-age males in agricultural settings, Sabah has high incidence, and working-age males incur higher total and notably higher indirect costs. However, the statement that this pattern "increases both direct and indirect costs" is not supported: the passage explicitly states that direct costs were similar between working-age males and others, while only indirect costs were higher for working-age males. Additionally, mean costs for P. knowlesi were similar to P. falciparum, so it is not shown that P. knowlesi prevalence per se increases direct costs. Therefore the causal claim that high P. knowlesi prevalence among working-age males increases both direct and indirect household costs cannot be fully derived from the passage.

76.Option: Severe malaria cases, all due to P. knowlesi infection, were associated with substantially higher indirect costs, primarily due to longer hospital stays and greater productivity losses among working-age males.
Evidence: The seven severe malaria cases (all P. knowlesi) had 91% higher total costs than non-severe cases (US$239.64 versus US$125.27 respectively, p=0.006), predominantly due to substantially higher indirect costs (US$89.25 mean difference) for severe malaria.

The average number of days lost to illness increased to 13.9 (SD = 15.6) for severe P. knowlesi cases, comparatively higher than non-severe P. knowlesi cases at 8.3 days (SD = 6.5, p = 0.141) and all non-zoonotic malaria (P. falciparum, P. vivax and P. malariae) cases at 10.4 days (SD = 5.80, p = 0.477).

Patients reported an average length of stay in hospital of 4.1 days (SD=1.4; range 1-10). The length of hospitalization for severe P. knowlesi cases (mean = 5.6 days, SD = 1.4) was higher than for non-severe malaria cases due to any species (mean = 3.9, SD = 1.4, p=0.004).

The total cost for working-age males was higher than the remainder of the participants ... While direct costs were similar between working-age males and the rest of the population, working-age males reported higher indirect costs at US$95.66, compared to US$52.62 for others (p<0.001).
76.Rationale: The passage clearly shows that the seven severe cases (all P. knowlesi) had much higher total costs driven predominantly by greater indirect costs, and it documents that severe P. knowlesi cases had longer hospital stays and more days lost to illness — both plausible drivers of indirect cost. However, the option asserts that these higher indirect costs are "primarily due to ... greater productivity losses among working-age males." The passage does show that working-age males have higher indirect costs overall, but it does not provide direct evidence that the severe cases (all P. knowlesi) were concentrated among working-age males or that the excess indirect costs for severe cases are primarily attributable to working-age males. Thus the latter causal attribution is not directly supported by the data in the passage.

76.Option: Despite the provision of free health-facility-related costs of malaria care by the Ministry of Health, Malaysia, patients in Sabah faced substantial out-of-pocket costs for a single malaria episode, with a mean of $131, and a large proportion of households experienced catastrophic health expenditure, particularly when using lower income thresholds.
Evidence: Malaysia has free universal access to malaria screening, testing, and treatment through public health facilities [6,7].
The mean total household cost per malaria episode was US$130.53 (SD=102.24) with a median of $96.11 (IQR=$70.73, $153.01) (Table 3).

Direct household costs equated to US$53.89 (SD=66.20) per person (41% of the total cost). ... Indirect household costs were the most significant contributor to the total cost burden (58%), with a mean total of US$75.84 (SD=69.67) for each household.

Direct health expenditure for a single malaria episode accounted for 27% of monthly income. Eighty percent (122/152) of patients reported direct health costs above 10% of monthly income, indicating catastrophic health expenditure. When using a higher defined threshold for catastrophic health expenditure of 25% of monthly income, the direct costs of a malaria episode reported by 38% (57/152) of patients in this survey remained at levels consistent with catastrophic health expenditure.
76.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that Malaysia provides free universal malaria services through public facilities, yet reports a mean out-of-pocket household cost per malaria episode of approximately US$130.53. It further quantifies catastrophic expenditure: 80% of patients had direct costs above 10% of monthly income and 38% exceeded 25% (using the study's self-reported income), showing a large proportion of households experienced catastrophic health expenditure, especially at the lower (10%) threshold. These statements directly support the option's inference.

77.Option: Negative perceptions of tsetse traps are prone to change over time, particularly after exposure to the technology and through community education, leading to sustained acceptance and active participation in control efforts.
Evidence: Yes, we were afraid; when we saw the traps for the first time (female, FGD15, HAT+).
If you don't know [an object] you can get scared. So we feared, of course (female, FGD 5, HAT-)!

Before we heard of them [traps] we didn't like them; but now we have got to know them [the traps], we like them (…). We like them because since that time they deployed them this has reduced the number of tsetse flies in the area (female, FGD11, HAT+).

Some participants from the HAT- group, for instance reported that, on some occasion their first contact with traps was accompanied by the explanation of the trap purpose or general explanation that traps are beneficial. This communication mostly happened through personnel carrying out trap deployment or another member of community, who had been informed about it previously. According to participants this information helped them to put aside associations with supernatural powers.

[When I saw a trap] a lot of insects were gathered there including tsetse flies; but now that thing [idea of witchcraft] went out of my head; I just knew that it is for tsetse flies and it was inserted by the right people [not those of witchcraft] (female, FGD 5, HAT-).
Regardless of the group, participants expressed willingness and appeared motivated to be involved in tsetse control programs; although the HAT+ group expressed this willingness more frequently.
77.Rationale: The passage documents clear initial fear and suspicion of traps among communities, followed by examples where exposure to traps, observing trapped insects, and receiving explanations from deploying personnel led to attitudes changing to positive. HAT+ villages report liking the traps now and engaging in maintenance, and participants across groups expressed willingness to be involved. The passage explicitly links information/exposure to reduced supernatural associations and sustained acceptance, supporting the inference about a trajectory from initial negative perceptions to sustained acceptance and active participation after education/exposure.

77.Option: The geographical proximity of HAT+ and HAT− villages suggests that information about tsetse control programs spreads easily between communities, leading to similar levels of acceptance over time.
Evidence: Villages from HAT+ and HAT− areas lie within 10 kilometres of each other. Villagers from different villages often mix in the local markets and during social events such as celebrations of country independence, women's day, Christmas; hence the differences in views described below were surprising.
By contrast, villagers in the HAT- area mostly regarded HAT as disease of the “old days” and reported never having witnessed it.
Despite many negative first reactions and attitudes towards traps, participants from both groups expressed that these attitudes are prone to change.
77.Rationale: While the passage notes geographic proximity and social mixing, it explicitly states that the observed differences in views between HAT+ and HAT- villages were surprising. The data show that proximity did not automatically produce similar knowledge or acceptance: HAT+ villages had prior exposure, knowledge of tsetse transmission, and positive attitudes, whereas HAT- villages lacked knowledge and initially feared traps. Thus the claimed inference that proximity implies easy spread of information leading to similar acceptance is not supported by the passage.

77.Option: The effectiveness of tsetse traps in reducing disease incidence is the main reason for community acceptance, regardless of prior exposure or cultural beliefs.
Evidence: We like them because since that time they deployed them this has reduced the number of tsetse flies in the area (female, FGD11, HAT+).

In both groups, the majority of participants reported or assumed that traps work effectively... reduction in tsetse numbers, reduction of tsetse bites, protection of community against tsetse, and reduction of HAT cases in the area.

Some participants from the HAT- group, for instance reported that, on some occasion their first contact with traps was accompanied by the explanation of the trap purpose or general explanation that traps are beneficial. ... this information helped them to put aside associations with supernatural powers.
If you don't know [an object] you can get scared. So we feared, of course (female, FGD 5, HAT-)!

The suspicions and fear were not reported to be in any way related to the appearance of traps, but rather its position next to the river, uncertainty about the person who carried out the deployment and the unknown purpose of this action.
77.Rationale: Although participants cited observed effectiveness (fewer flies, fewer bites, fewer HAT cases) as a reason for acceptance, the passage also makes clear that prior exposure, explanation from deploying personnel, and overcoming cultural/supernatural associations were important drivers of attitude change. Acceptance did not arise solely from effectiveness 'regardless' of prior exposure or beliefs; rather effectiveness often worked together with information and experience to change perceptions. Therefore the absolute claim that effectiveness is the main reason regardless of exposure or beliefs is not supported.

77.Option: Community willingness to participate in tsetse control is primarily driven by financial incentives, as evidenced by men's expressed interest in government involvement and financial motivation.
Evidence: Regardless of the group, participants expressed willingness and appeared motivated to be involved in tsetse control programs; although the HAT+ group expressed this willingness more frequently.

More women than men reported being involved in these activities. In both groups women expressed more need for empowerment (see a quote below) and requested access to traps more often, while men expressed more need for village decision making, financial motivation and government involvement in tsetse control programs.

Yes… We can participate doing the tsetse control work (here). For example here (in our village), we have a village health team (volunteers)… So if enough traps were given to us, the village health team would look for more, let's say six people to add to the team of two (already recruited as village health team), so that we become eight, and we would unite ourselves to do the work with the team; to do tsetse control intervention (female, FGD 9, HAT+).
77.Rationale: While men did express interest in government involvement and financial motivation, the passage shows multiple motivations for participation — including perceived health benefits, empowerment (especially among women), and voluntary involvement in maintenance. The overall willingness described is not characterized as 'primarily' driven by financial incentives; financial motivation is one of several factors, so the statement that it is the primary driver is not supported by the evidence.

77.Option: The initial fear and suspicion toward tsetse traps in HAT− villages are primarily due to the traps' appearance and color, rather than cultural or supernatural associations.
Evidence: The suspicions and fear were not reported to be in any way related to the appearance of traps, but rather its position next to the river, uncertainty about the person who carried out the deployment and the unknown purpose of this action, as one of the participants explained:
Since it's just a cloth, there would not be much fear… The fear would be: who has placed it there! For what [purpose] (male, FGD 4, HAT-)?

Associations of traps with witchcraft and ghosts. ... According to participants of both groups, these objects, activities and people could be associated with two forms of supernatural powers: i) witchcraft (Lugbara: “ojo azi ngazu”) and ii) ghosts from the river (Lugbara: “orindi onzi”; orindi-ghost/spirit, onzi-evil/bad).

The issue of ghost comes in those [parts] of the days when it gets dark, or like it's late in the evening; and you are passing the river, and you could find light either inside the river, or along the river side... This is what scares the people who meet the traps for the first time before being told what it is (female, FGD 9, HAT+).
77.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that fear was not related to the traps' appearance, and instead attributes initial fear to the traps' placement, unknown purpose, and strong cultural/supernatural associations (witchcraft and ghosts). Therefore the claim that appearance/color was the primary cause is contradicted by the text.

78.Option: The persistence of clinical signs of trachoma after the clearance of C. trachomatis infection indicates that the current WHO guidelines for trachoma elimination are ineffective in preventing long-term ocular damage.
Evidence: Typically, control programmes rely on the clinical signs of active trachoma to determine whether treatment should be given or stopped. Unfortunately, there is often a mismatch between disease and infection: infection may detected without clinical disease and disease may be found without infection.
The correlation between disease and infection is not particularly strong and tends to weaken as the community prevalence drops [5], [6].

For the “AFE” components the UIG is to bring TF%1–9 below 5%. At this level it is expected that C. trachomatis infection will be too infrequent to drive scarring, although, there are no published data demonstrating this.

After the two-month follow-up TF%1–9 was found to exceed the 10% treatment threshold in several villages on one or more occasions (Table 4). However, in only 4/12 (33%) of these occasions were cases of chlamydial infection detected in the same village (Table 5).
78.Rationale: The passage documents a mismatch between clinical signs (TF) and infection and explicitly notes that there are no published data demonstrating that the WHO UIG (TF%1–9 below 5%) prevents scarring. However, it does not present direct evidence that WHO guidelines are ineffective at preventing long-term ocular damage. The available text highlights uncertainty and lack of data rather than demonstrating ineffectiveness. Therefore the categorical conclusion that the WHO guidelines are ineffective is not supported by the Passage Body.

78.Option: The lack of a control group in the study design means that the observed decline in C. trachomatis infection cannot be attributed to mass drug administration, and therefore antibiotic treatment should not be used in low prevalence trachoma settings.
Evidence: Mass antibiotic treatment of the entire population of the study area was conducted by the study team immediately after the baseline clinical assessment in April 2001.
During the follow-up period no further treatment was given for active trachoma by the study team or the National Eye Care Programme (NECP).
These communities had not received any mass antibiotic treatment for trachoma control prior to this study.

Trachoma was endemic in many parts of Europe in the 19th century; however, it gradually declined in the absence of any trachoma-specific interventions such as antibiotic treatment. This was attributed to general improvements in living conditions and hygiene, and underlines their importance in controlling trachoma.
78.Rationale: While the study did treat the entire defined population (no untreated internal control arm) and concurrent non-antibiotic factors and secular trends can influence trachoma prevalence, the Passage Body does not state that the decline cannot be attributed to MDA, nor does it provide evidence to support the prescriptive recommendation to discontinue antibiotic treatment in low-prevalence settings. The statement 'therefore antibiotic treatment should not be used' is a policy prescription that cannot be derived solely from the presented data and reasoning in the Passage Body.

78.Option: The observed re-infection episode in two villages shortly after mass antibiotic treatment was associated with travel to a religious festival in Senegal, suggesting that large-scale community gatherings may act as critical hubs for the reintroduction of C. trachomatis in low prevalence settings.
Evidence: In contrast, in villages 1 and 3 the prevalence of infection increased at two months (Table 5). This increase was strongly associated with the travel of most village residents to a religious festival in Senegal one month after receiving treatment, which has been previously reported (OR 12.2, p<0.0001, 95% CI 4.0–44.1) [9].
78.Rationale: The passage explicitly links the transient rise in infection in two villages to travel to a religious festival, with a strong measured association (OR 12.2, p<0.0001). From an epidemiological perspective, it is reasonable to infer that large-scale community gatherings can serve as points for reintroduction of infection into low-prevalence communities; this inference follows directly from the provided evidence.

78.Option: The marked reduction in fly-eye contacts and unclean faces observed during the study suggests that health education on facial cleanliness was the primary driver of the decline in trachoma prevalence, independent of antibiotic treatment.
Evidence: The project team provided health education about trachoma to the communities involved throughout the course of the study.
After the 17-month follow-up new concrete pit latrines were installed in each household.

Antibiotic treatment coverage at baseline was 83.3% (95% CI: 81.4–85.1) of the study area population and 91.3% (95% CI: 89.0–93.7) of 1 to 9 year olds: oral azithromycin 1079 (81%), oral erythromycin 226 (17%), topical tetracycline 23 (2%).

There was a marked reduction in observed fly-eye contacts at the time of examination in 1 to 9 year olds from 18.0% (95% CI 14.7 to 21.5) at baseline to 1.5% (95% CI 0.5–2.5) at 5 years. Similarly there was a large reduction in the proportion of 1 to 9 year olds with unclean faces (nasal or ocular discharge present): 38.4% (95% CI 34.1 to 42.7) at baseline and 5.0% (95% CI 3.1 to 6.8) at 5 years.
78.Rationale: Although the passage documents large reductions in fly-eye contacts and unclean faces and reports that health education and sanitation improvements were implemented, these interventions occurred alongside high-coverage mass antibiotic treatment. The study design and presented data do not isolate the effect of health education from antibiotics and latrine installation, so concluding that health education alone was the primary driver (independent of antibiotic treatment) is not supported by the evidence in the Passage Body.

78.Option: Applying the current WHO intervention algorithms in low prevalence trachoma settings may lead to unnecessary rounds of mass drug administration in uninfected communities.
Evidence: Typically, control programmes rely on the clinical signs of active trachoma to determine whether treatment should be given or stopped. Unfortunately, there is often a mismatch between disease and infection: infection may detected without clinical disease and disease may be found without infection.
The correlation between disease and infection is not particularly strong and tends to weaken as the community prevalence drops [5], [6].

Where TF%1–9 exceeds 10%, three annual MDAs followed by re-assessment, to determine whether to continue treating, is recommended [8]. This strategy increases the chance that infected persons are treated. However, in low prevalence settings where infection and disease are poorly correlated it may not represent the most efficient way to eliminate trachoma.

After the two-month follow-up TF%1–9 was found to exceed the 10% treatment threshold in several villages on one or more occasions (Table 4). However, in only 4/12 (33%) of these occasions were cases of chlamydial infection detected in the same village (Table 5).
78.Rationale: The Passage Body explicitly describes a weakening correlation between clinical TF and C. trachomatis infection in low-prevalence settings, states the WHO threshold that triggers rounds of MDA, and notes that this approach 'may not represent the most efficient way' to eliminate trachoma in such settings. The empirical finding that many occasions with TF%1–9>10% did not have detectable infection supports the inference that applying the TF-based algorithm could result in unnecessary MDAs in communities without current infection.

79.Option: The preference for decentralized care stems from the belief that outpatient treatment is more effective in healing BU lesions than hospital-based care.
Evidence: Our research revealed a strong preference for decentralized BU treatment. Six reasons were identified from interviews that asked women about the advantages of decentralized care.
First and foremost, decentralized care does not disrupt a household’s daily routine by removing a mother from her household.

Decentralized treatment, for all but very severe cases of BU, still enables a woman to do chores and watch her children as well as engage in entrepreneurial activities essential for household survival.
Second, decentralized treatment avoids the many indirect and opportunity costs associated with hospitalization.
Third, it allows children to stay in school while being treated, and it reduces the need to remove children from school and apprenticeships to serve as patient caretakers.
Fifth, she also does not have to worry about pernicious rumors undermining her own or a daughter’s reputation.

Studies of BU in West Africa have found that biomedical treatment for BU is often delayed for reasons linked to perceptions of causality, fear of surgery and amputation, and the logistics and costs of seeking “free care,”[27].
79.Rationale: The passage lists specific practical, social, and economic reasons for the preference for decentralized care (disruption of household routines, indirect and opportunity costs, schooling, rumors, stress/abandonment). It does not state that households believe outpatient treatment is more effective than hospital care. The evidence shows preference is driven by household production, social risk, and cost considerations rather than a stated belief in superior clinical efficacy of outpatient care. Therefore the claimed causal belief (greater perceived effectiveness) is not supported by the passage.

79.Option: The preference for decentralized BU treatment is primarily due to the greater availability of trained health staff at local health stations compared to centralized hospitals.
Evidence: Treatment for BU is provided free in most West African countries either at hospitals (centralized in-patient care) or at local health stations (decentralized outpatient treatment care).

Decentralized management of BU patients is well established in the Atlantic, Ouémé and Couffo regions of Benin. In these regions, most cases of mild to moderate BU (Category I and II) are treated at health stations staffed by nurses. More serious (category II and III) cases are referred to reference hospitals.
Our research revealed a strong preference for decentralized BU treatment. Six reasons were identified from interviews that asked women about the advantages of decentralized care.
Second, decentralized treatment avoids the many indirect and opportunity costs associated with hospitalization.
79.Rationale: While the passage notes that health stations are staffed by nurses and treat mild to moderate cases, it does not state that greater availability of trained health staff at local stations (relative to hospitals) is the primary reason households prefer decentralized care. Instead, the passage explicitly lists household-level practical, economic, and social reasons for the preference. There is no evidence in the passage that staff availability at local stations (vs hospitals) is the principal driver.

79.Option: Households prefer decentralized care because hospital-based treatment for BU is often delayed due to long waiting lists and limited bed availability.
Evidence: Studies of BU in West Africa have found that biomedical treatment for BU is often delayed for reasons linked to perceptions of causality, fear of surgery and amputation, and the logistics and costs of seeking “free care,”[27].
Our research revealed a strong preference for decentralized BU treatment. Six reasons were identified from interviews that asked women about the advantages of decentralized care.
Second, decentralized treatment avoids the many indirect and opportunity costs associated with hospitalization.
Sixth, remaining at home during treatment is less stressful. A woman worries less about abandonment.
79.Rationale: The passage documents causes of delayed biomedical treatment (perceptions of causality, fear of surgery, logistics and costs) but does not mention long waiting lists or limited bed availability as reasons for preferring decentralized care. The stated reasons for preference focus on indirect/opportunity costs, household disruption, schooling, social risk, and stress. There is no evidence provided about hospital capacity constraints driving the preference.

79.Option: The preference for decentralized BU treatment over hospital care is driven not only by geographical accessibility but also by the need to avoid indirect and opportunity costs, preserve social capital, and mitigate social risks such as rumors about moral impropriety.
Evidence: Our research revealed a strong preference for decentralized BU treatment. Six reasons were identified from interviews that asked women about the advantages of decentralized care.
First and foremost, decentralized care does not disrupt a household’s daily routine by removing a mother from her household.

Decentralized treatment, for all but very severe cases of BU, still enables a woman to do chores and watch her children as well as engage in entrepreneurial activities essential for household survival.
Second, decentralized treatment avoids the many indirect and opportunity costs associated with hospitalization.
Third, it allows children to stay in school while being treated, and it reduces the need to remove children from school and apprenticeships to serve as patient caretakers.
Fifth, she also does not have to worry about pernicious rumors undermining her own or a daughter’s reputation.
When a woman leaves the confines of her village either to visit a health post some distance away or to reside in a hospital, she risks becoming the subject of rumors about sexual indiscretion.

The enabling factors most commonly referenced in interviews about hospital-based BU care were the indirect costs of “free treatment” such as transportation costs, food and incidental costs (soap, mobile phone credit, etc.), and the opportunity cost of lost labor.
79.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies multiple non-geographic drivers of preference for decentralized care: avoidance of indirect and opportunity costs, ability to remain economically productive and care for children, preservation of schooling and apprenticeships, concerns about childcare quality, fear of rumors and damage to reputation, and reduced stress/abandonment risk. These statements together directly support the inference in the option.

79.Option: Households prefer decentralized care because it allows for greater involvement of traditional healers in the treatment process, which strengthens cultural continuity and trust.
Evidence: Members may include kin, friends, community health workers, health staff, and traditional healers. In short, the TMG is composed of everyone who weighs in or contributes to health care in some way.
He initially planned to send the boy to his grandmother’s house in a nearby village during school holidays to be treated by a traditional healer.
Our research revealed a strong preference for decentralized BU treatment. Six reasons were identified from interviews that asked women about the advantages of decentralized care.
Second, decentralized treatment avoids the many indirect and opportunity costs associated with hospitalization.
Third, it allows children to stay in school while being treated, and it reduces the need to remove children from school and apprenticeships to serve as patient caretakers.
79.Rationale: Although the passage acknowledges traditional healers as potential members of therapy management groups and gives an example of a father who at first considered a traditional healer, it does not state that households prefer decentralized biomedical care because it permits greater involvement of traditional healers. The documented reasons for preferring decentralized care are practical, economic, and social (opportunity costs, childcare, schooling, rumors), not to enhance traditional-healer involvement. Thus the option is unsupported by the passage.

80.Option: The model's conclusion that full access to antivenom is cost-saving is contingent on all snakebite victims in ASEAN countries switching from traditional to conventional treatment, which may not be feasible in remote rural areas.
Evidence: In the full access scenario, all snakebite victims who firstly sought traditional healers when access is now full are assumed to switch to conventional treatment.

However, not all victims with snakebite envenoming in ASEAN countries could access to geographically appropriate antivenoms for many reasons including inadequate supplies of antivenom, inefficient supply chain system, and inappropriate treatment seeking behavior [9].
Especially in Indonesia, Philippines, Vietnam, Lao PDR, and Myanmar, where 4–64% of victims with snakebite envenoming were treated with antivenoms [5].
80.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the full access scenario assumes that victims who initially sought traditional healers would switch to conventional treatment, so the model does incorporate that assumption. However, the additional claim that this switching "may not be feasible in remote rural areas" is a conjecture about real-world feasibility that is not directly established in the Passage Body. While the passage notes barriers to access (inadequate supplies, inefficient supply chains, inappropriate treatment-seeking), it does not provide specific evidence about feasibility in remote rural areas or quantify the extent to which the cost-saving conclusion is contingent on this single assumption. Therefore the conditional feasibility statement goes beyond what can be strictly derived from the passage.

80.Option: Improving access to antivenom in ASEAN countries would be cost-effective only if antivenom effectiveness exceeds a relative risk of death of 1.19 in Myanmar and 0.35 in Lao PDR.
Evidence: Threshold analyses of antivenom effectiveness and costs of antivenom treatment resulted in an ICER of 0 are shown in S4 Table. The lowest level of antivenom effectiveness, presented as a risk ratio of death in indicated victims who were not treated with antivenom compared to those treated with antivenom, was ranging from 0.35 in Lao PDR to 1.19 in Myanmar.

Threshold sensitivity analyses were performed to estimate the lowest level of antivenom effectiveness and the highest level of costs of antivenom treatment that would result in the “not cost-saving” situation where ICER is equal to zero but still considered cost-effective.
80.Rationale: The passage reports threshold analyses and gives the range of risk-ratio values (0.35 to 1.19) associated with an ICER of zero. However, the option's wording is imprecise and potentially misleading: the passage does not state the categorical rule "only if effectiveness exceeds" those exact numbers for cost-effectiveness across all contexts. The threshold analyses identify parameter values at which the model yields an ICER of zero, but cost-effectiveness is assessed against willingness-to-pay thresholds and via multiple sensitivity analyses (including probabilistic analyses showing high probability of cost-savings). Thus the absolute "only if" statement cannot be strictly derived from the Passage Body as presented.

80.Option: The high burden of snakebite in ASEAN countries, including 243,000 victims and 16,000 deaths annually, underscores the urgent need for increased antivenom production to meet demand.
Evidence: Our previous study estimated that there were approximately 243,000 snakebite victims with 16,000 deaths and 950 amputations from snakebite envenoming in seven ASEAN countries, namely, Malaysia, Thailand, Indonesia, Philippines, Vietnam, Lao PDR, and Myanmar.

However, not all victims with snakebite envenoming in ASEAN countries could access to geographically appropriate antivenoms for many reasons including inadequate supplies of antivenom, inefficient supply chain system, and inappropriate treatment seeking behavior [9].

improving access to antivenoms could not be solely done by increasing the production of antivenoms. It requires a multifaceted approach involving strengthening the whole system surrounding the management of snakebite victims, such as accurate informatics, rigorous regulations of antivenoms, efficient supply chain, rational use of antivenoms, appropriate treatment seeking behaviors, and good governance to support a strong healthcare system.
80.Rationale: The passage does provide the cited burden estimates (243,000 victims and 16,000 deaths). However, the conclusion that this "underscores the urgent need for increased antivenom production" is not directly supported as a singular inference. The authors explicitly state that improving access cannot be achieved solely by increasing production and emphasize a multifaceted approach (informatics, regulations, supply chain, treatment-seeking behavior, governance). Therefore, while increased production may be part of the response, the passage does not support the unqualified claim that increased production alone is the urgent or sole inference.

80.Option: The inclusion of post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) in the model significantly increased the incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) per DALY averted, making the intervention less cost-effective.
Evidence: Incorporating PTSD in the model, assuming that antivenom could reduce the risk of amputation, and increasing the logistic costs of antivenom resulted in similar ICERs per death averted and ICERs per DALY averted in all countries indicating that improving access will result in cost-saving.
80.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that incorporating PTSD (among other scenario changes) "resulted in similar ICERs ... indicating that improving access will result in cost-saving." Therefore the assertion that including PTSD significantly increased the ICER per DALY and made the intervention less cost-effective contradicts the provided results and is not supported by the Passage Body.

80.Option: Improving access to snake antivenom from the current level to full access in ASEAN countries would result in cost-saving despite higher direct costs, due to substantial reductions in indirect costs from avoided productivity losses.
Evidence: Full access to antivenom had higher direct costs (2 million USD in Philippines to 50 million USD in Indonesia) but less indirect costs (-1,091 million USD in Indonesia to -28 million USD in Philippines) when compared to current access which resulted in total cost savings of 27 million USD in Philippines to 1,040 million USD in Indonesia.

In total, when compared to current access, full access to snake antivenom in ASEAN countries resulted in 9,362 deaths averted (-59%), 230,075 DALYs averted (-59%), and cost savings of 1.3 billion USD (-53%).

Base-case analyses of ICERs per death averted and ICERs per DALY averted of full access compared to current access found higher outcomes ... but lower costs ... in all five ASEAN countries. Thus, improving access to snake antivenom will result in cost-saving (Table 2).
80.Rationale: The Passage Body directly reports that although full access increases direct costs, it substantially reduces indirect costs (primarily productivity losses), producing net total cost savings in each studied country and region-wide. The authors conclude full access averted deaths and DALYs and was cost-saving overall. This option accurately reflects those reported model outcomes and the stated economic mechanism (reductions in indirect costs outweigh increases in direct costs).

81.Option: The FP assay's ability to detect compound binding to Brugia Hsp90 is compromised by high background fluorescence from nematode extracts, which necessitates the use of complex normalization procedures in drug screening.
Evidence: cy3B-GA bound to the Brugia Hsp90 species (Figure 1A) with a high affinity, similar to the tumor Hsp90 species (Figure 1B).
The signal amplified with time, reached equilibrium at 8 h and remained stable thereafter, resulting in an excellent assay window of approximately 200 mP (Figure 1A).
To evaluate the applicability of the 96-well FP assay for HTS, the assay performance parameters S:N and Z' were determined to be 32 and 0.87, respectively (Figure 2D).

In contrast, cy3B-GA bound the C. elegans extracts with very low affinity (Figure 1C). The signal at each time point was virtually indistinguishable from time 0, indicating that the low increase in mP, observed especially at high extract concentrations, was caused by non-specific binding of the tracer or by autofluorescence from the worm extract.
81.Rationale: The passage shows that for Brugia extracts the FP assay produced a high-affinity binding signal, a large assay window (~200 mP) and excellent assay performance metrics (S:N 32, Z' 0.87), indicating background fluorescence did not compromise detection for Brugia. The passage does note autofluorescence/non-specific signal for C. elegans extracts, but this is specific to C. elegans and not to Brugia. The text does not state that complex normalization procedures are required for Brugia screening; on the contrary, assay metrics support robust detection. Therefore the broad claim that the assay's ability to detect binding to Brugia Hsp90 is compromised by high background fluorescence (necessitating complex normalization) is not supported by the passage.

81.Option: The FP assay is not suitable for identifying selective inhibitors of Brugia Hsp90 because the structural similarity between Brugia and human Hsp90 prevents meaningful discrimination between the two species.
Evidence: While GA interacted with identical affinity with both human and Brugia species, a good selectivity was noted in the PU-series, with a selectivity ratio of 2, 3.5 and 9 for PU-24FCl, PU-H71 and PU-WS10, respectively (Figure 4C).

The 3-fold change in selectivity ratio between PU-H71 and PU-WS10, two agents of almost identical chemical structure, further confirms the sensitivity of the Hsp90 pocket to the cellular environment and suggests that it may be possible to identify molecules that specifically target the parasite Hsp90.
81.Rationale: The passage explicitly demonstrates that despite structural similarity, the FP assay distinguished differences in binding between human and Brugia Hsp90 for PU-series compounds, yielding selectivity ratios (up to 9-fold). The authors conclude this sensitivity 'suggests that it may be possible to identify molecules that specifically target the parasite Hsp90.' Thus the assertion that the assay is not suitable for identifying selective inhibitors because of structural similarity is contradicted by the presented data.

81.Option: The FP assay developed for tumor cell Hsp90 is applicable to the parasitic nematode Brugia, as demonstrated by specific and dose-dependent inhibition of cy3B-GA binding by known Hsp90 inhibitors and the ability to distinguish between Brugia and human Hsp90 species.
Evidence: Here we investigated whether the FP assay could be applied to a more complex, multicellular species such as a nematode worm.
cy3B-GA bound to the Brugia Hsp90 species (Figure 1A) with a high affinity, similar to the tumor Hsp90 species (Figure 1B).
Rewardingly, cy3B-GA bound to the Brugia Hsp90 species ... resulting in an excellent assay window of approximately 200 mP (Figure 1A).
As expected, unlabelled GA displaced cy3B-GA binding to Brugia extracts in a dose-dependent manner, resulting in near 100% displacement at 250–500 nM (Figure 3A).
Only competitive binders of the N-terminal binding site of Hsp90, such as GA, radicicol and the endogenous ligand ADP, resulted in dose-dependent reduction of the FP signal (Figure 3B).
We show that the assay is specific and sensitive and able to detect compounds that bind in the Brugia Hsp90 ATP pocket.

While GA interacted with identical affinity with both human and Brugia species, a good selectivity was noted in the PU-series ... suggests that it may be possible to identify molecules that specifically target the parasite Hsp90.
The assay is suitable for high-throughput screening, does not require the production of purified recombinant protein and could be adapted to screen Hsp90 inhibitors against other parasites...
81.Rationale: Multiple passages demonstrate that the tumor-cell FP assay format translates to Brugia extracts: cy3B-GA binds Brugia Hsp90 with high affinity and a large assay window; known N-terminal Hsp90 binders displace cy3B-GA in a dose-dependent manner; the assay is specific, sensitive, and suitable for HTS; and it can discriminate relative affinities between human and Brugia Hsp90, supporting its applicability for identifying and prioritizing selective inhibitors for parasitic nematode drug discovery.

81.Option: The high-throughput screening capability of the FP assay is limited by the need for purified recombinant Hsp90 protein, which increases cost and complexity in drug discovery efforts for parasitic nematodes.
Evidence: The assay is suitable for high-throughput screening, does not require the production of purified recombinant protein and could be adapted to screen Hsp90 inhibitors against other parasites, where Hsp90 is known to be a valid target [24], [25].
To evaluate the applicability of the 96-well FP assay for HTS, the assay performance parameters S:N and Z' were determined to be 32 and 0.87, respectively (Figure 2D).
Assays with a Z' factor between 0.5 and 1.0 are considered to be reliable, robust, and suitable for HTS [32].
81.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the assay 'does not require the production of purified recombinant protein' and reports excellent HTS performance metrics (S:N 32, Z' 0.87), indicating the assay is amenable to high-throughput screening without needing purified recombinant Hsp90. Therefore the claim that HTS capability is limited by need for purified recombinant protein is not supported.

82.Option: The presence of TcTASV-C in extracellular vesicles from multiple T. cruzi strains suggests that this secretion pattern is a conserved virulence strategy across all parasite lineages.
Evidence: The TcTASV family is conserved among all the T. cruzi lineages analyzed so far and has no orthologues in other species, including the closely-related trypanosomatids T. brucei, T. rangeli and Leishmania sp. [9].
Overall, TcTASV-C was secreted in EVs in all the strains analyzed (Fig 4).
these findings also outpoint towards TcTASVs as potential virulence factors and as interesting targets for study and rational intervention.
Delivery of molecules in vesicles is a well known immune evasion mechanism exploited by parasites [27].
82.Rationale: The passage states that the TcTASV family is conserved across the T. cruzi lineages examined and explicitly reports that TcTASV-C was secreted in EVs in all strains analyzed. The authors also describe TcTASVs as potential virulence factors and note that delivery of molecules in vesicles is a known parasite immune evasion mechanism. From these facts it is epidemiologically reasonable to infer that secretion of TcTASV-C in EVs is a conserved (lineage-spanning) secretion pattern and that it likely functions as part of a virulence/immune-evasion strategy across the parasite lineages examined.

82.Option: The differential expression of TcTASV-C between bloodstream and culture-derived trypomastigotes indicates that in vitro culture conditions fail to replicate the host immune pressure necessary for proper antigen expression.
Evidence: TcTASV-C was detected in 1x10^6 (or even less) bloodstream parasites (Fig 5A, upper panel, BT lanes) while it was necessary to load more than tenfold of culture-derived trypomastigotes to be weakly detected (Fig 5A, Cult lanes, upper panel).
This finding was observed in all T. cruzi strains assayed, belonging to different lineages, and proved that TcTASV-C is upregulated in bloodstream forms.
82.Rationale: The passage documents a clear differential expression—TcTASV-C is upregulated in bloodstream trypomastigotes relative to culture-derived forms. However, the specific assertion that this difference is caused by in vitro culture failing to replicate 'host immune pressure' is not supported by the text. The authors do not provide evidence that immune pressure is the proximate cause of the differential expression; other host-derived signals or environmental conditions could explain upregulation. Thus the causal claim about immune pressure cannot be derived from the passage.

82.Option: The secretion of TcTASV-C into extracellular vesicles is a parasite-driven immune evasion mechanism that helps conceal the protein from host immune surveillance.
Evidence: TcTASV-C was secreted in both EVs populations in the CL Brener strain (Fig 3B); in the small EV fraction (V16, Fig 3B, upper panel) TcTASV-C appeared as a highly abundant component, while it was almost undetectable on parasite pellets at these conditions.
Delivery of molecules in vesicles is a well known immune evasion mechanism exploited by parasites [27].

Pooled sera from humans, mice or rabbits chronically infected with T. cruzi failed to efficiently detect EVs antigens, although they strongly reacted with trypomastigote antigens and with “naked” secreted proteins, both from RA and CL Brener strains (Fig 7 and S6 Fig).
82.Rationale: The passage shows TcTASV-C is abundantly secreted in EVs and cites that vesicle-mediated delivery is a known immune evasion mechanism. It further demonstrates that sera from chronically infected hosts poorly recognize EV antigens while reacting with non-vesicle ('naked') secreted proteins. Together these observations support the inference that TcTASV-C secretion in EVs functions to conceal the protein from host immune detection, i.e., is an immune evasion strategy.

82.Option: The upregulation of TcTASV-C in bloodstream trypomastigotes compared to culture-derived forms suggests that the protein plays a critical role in parasite survival within the host environment.
Evidence: TcTASV-C was detected in 1x10^6 (or even less) bloodstream parasites (Fig 5A, upper panel, BT lanes) while it was necessary to load more than tenfold of culture-derived trypomastigotes to be weakly detected (Fig 5A, Cult lanes, upper panel).
This finding was observed in all T. cruzi strains assayed, belonging to different lineages, and proved that TcTASV-C is upregulated in bloodstream forms.
TcTASV-C (but not the control protein GST) exhibited a dose-dependent adhesive capacity, suggesting a ligand-receptor interaction.
these findings also outpoint towards TcTASVs as potential virulence factors and as interesting targets for study and rational intervention.

TcTASV-C vaccinated mice exhibited a delayed appearance of circulating trypomastigotes and lower parasitemia peaks... Immunized mice also showed higher survival rates than controls... All animals that survived at the end of experiments belonged to TcTASV-C vaccinated groups.
82.Rationale: The consistent upregulation of TcTASV-C in bloodstream (host) forms, its adhesive interaction with mammalian cells, the authors' characterization of TcTASVs as potential virulence factors, and the functional vaccine data showing that immune responses to TcTASV-C delay parasitemia and improve survival together support the inference that TcTASV-C contributes to parasite success in the host. From an epidemiological/biological perspective it is reasonable to infer it plays an important role in parasite survival within the host environment.

82.Option: The failure of chronically infected hosts to recognize TcTASV-C in extracellular vesicles suggests that the protein is degraded before reaching the immune system.
Evidence: Pooled sera from humans, mice or rabbits chronically infected with T. cruzi failed to efficiently detect EVs antigens, although they strongly reacted with trypomastigote antigens and with “naked” secreted proteins, both from RA and CL Brener strains (Fig 7 and S6 Fig).
Delivery of molecules in vesicles is a well known immune evasion mechanism exploited by parasites [27].
TcTASV-C was secreted in both EVs populations in the CL Brener strain (Fig 3B); in the small EV fraction (V16, Fig 3B, upper panel) TcTASV-C appeared as a highly abundant component...
82.Rationale: While the passage documents that chronically infected hosts' sera poorly recognize EV-associated antigens, it provides an alternative and explicitly mentioned explanation: vesicle-mediated delivery as an immune evasion mechanism. There is no evidence presented that TcTASV-C is degraded before reaching the immune system. The assertion of degradation is speculative and not supported by the data, so this conclusion cannot be derived from the passage.

83.Option: Exposure to sand fly saliva in individuals with filarial infection leads to a shift toward a Th1-dominated immune response, enhancing protection against Leishmania.
Evidence: monocytes of SF+/MF+ individuals showed a significantly higher expression of CCL22, a type-2 chemokine (Fig 4), suggesting that exposure to sand fly saliva does not change the MF-induced type-2 phenotype.

stimulation with SG Pd did not induce any of the proinflammatory, type-2 or regulatory cytokines in monocytes of both groups despite having originated from individuals naturally exposed to sand fly bites (Fig 3).

a significant increase was observed in the frequency of CCL22+ monocytes and IL-10+ mDCs in SF+/MF+ as compared to SF+/MF- individuals (Fig 5). These data suggest that exposure to sand fly saliva through bites doesn’t change the regulatory phenotype that has already been established by helminth infection.
83.Rationale: The passage reports increased expression of the type-2 chemokine CCL22 and increased frequencies of CCL22+ monocytes and IL-10+ mDCs in SF+/MF+ individuals, and explicitly states that exposure to sand fly saliva does not change the helminth-induced type-2/regulatory phenotype. It also shows that stimulation did not induce proinflammatory cytokines in SF+ individuals. These findings contradict a conclusion that exposure shifts responses toward Th1 and enhances protection against Leishmania; instead they indicate a type-2/regulatory bias in MF+ individuals.

83.Option: Repeated exposure to sand fly saliva renders human monocytes unresponsive to subsequent antigenic stimulation, including with filarial and leishmanial antigens or LPS.
Evidence: stimulation with SG Pd did not induce any of the proinflammatory, type-2 or regulatory cytokines in monocytes of both groups despite having originated from individuals naturally exposed to sand fly bites (Fig 3).
while IL-6 and CCL4 were upregulated in monocytes from sand fly bite-naive individuals (Fig 2), this response was lost in SF+ individuals regardless of their MF status (Fig 3).
More importantly, exposure to sand fly bites also dampened the monocyte response to MF antigen and even to LPS, observed for some cytokines (Fig 3).
83.Rationale: The passage directly reports that monocytes from individuals naturally exposed to sand fly bites (SF+) failed to upregulate cytokines in response to SG Pd and that the previously observed upregulation seen in bite-naive donors was lost in SF+ individuals. It further states that exposure to sand fly bites dampened monocyte responses to MF antigen and to LPS for some cytokines. These statements support the inference that repeated exposure renders monocytes relatively unresponsive to subsequent antigenic stimulation.

83.Option: The heightened baseline cytokine production in monocytes from Malian individuals may be due to chronic exposure to sand fly bites, leading to a state of persistent immune activation.
Evidence: Collectively, these data suggest that monocytes from individuals repeatedly exposed to sand fly bites exhibit a dampened response to stimulation with any antigen. This could be due to a much higher basal cytokine expression in monocytes of individuals naturally exposed to sand fly bite (Fig 3; closed circles) than unexposed individuals from North America (Fig 1; closed circles).
In fact, while IL-6 and CCL4 were upregulated in monocytes from sand fly bite-naive individuals (Fig 2), this response was lost in SF+ individuals regardless of their MF status (Fig 3).
83.Rationale: The passage explicitly raises the possibility that the observed dampened responsiveness in monocytes from exposed individuals may be attributable to higher basal cytokine expression in those monocytes compared with unexposed donors. The option is cautious ('may be due to') and aligns with the authors' suggested explanation that chronic exposure to sand fly bites could underlie persistent baseline activation, thereby supporting this inference from the passage.

83.Option: The dampened monocyte response to LPS in SF+ individuals is primarily due to the presence of microfilariae, rather than prior exposure to sand fly saliva.
Evidence: this response was lost in SF+ individuals regardless of their MF status (Fig 3).
monocytes of MF+ individuals were less responsive than monocytes of MF- individuals since LPS significantly induced the production of CCL4 and IL-10 only in MF- individuals (Fig 3).
83.Rationale: While the passage notes that monocytes from MF+ individuals are less responsive to LPS than those from MF- individuals, it also states that the loss of response (e.g., to SG Pd, and diminished responses to stimuli) occurred in SF+ individuals regardless of MF status. Thus the data indicate that prior exposure to sand fly bites (SF+) is associated with dampening independent of microfilarial status. Therefore it is not supported to claim the dampening is primarily due to microfilariae rather than prior sand fly exposure.

83.Option: The upregulation of CCL22 and increased frequency of CCL22+ monocytes in SF+/MF+ individuals is a direct result of repeated exposure to sand fly saliva.
Evidence: monocytes of SF+/MF+ individuals showed a significantly higher expression of CCL22, a type-2 chemokine (Fig 4), suggesting that exposure to sand fly saliva does not change the MF-induced type-2 phenotype.
These data suggest that exposure to sand fly saliva through bites doesn’t change the regulatory phenotype that has already been established by helminth infection.
83.Rationale: The passage attributes the increased CCL22 expression and higher frequency of CCL22+ monocytes in SF+/MF+ individuals to the presence of filarial infection (MF) and explicitly states that exposure to sand fly saliva does not alter the helminth-induced type-2/regulatory phenotype. Therefore, asserting that the upregulation is a direct result of repeated sand fly exposure is not supported by the presented data; the evidence points toward the helminth infection as the established driver.

84.Option: The lower seroconversion rates in Bolivia may be attributed to the use of rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) in the Sucre program, which have lower sensitivity and may have led to misclassification of true positive cases.
Evidence: In Sucre, screening was conducting using Chagas Stat-Pak on whole blood.
As in Entre Ríos, positive results were confirmed using conventional ELISA and HAI, with tiebreakers assessed via recombinant ELISA.
To date, of 276 patients followed up between 9 and 27 months post-treatment, no patient has been found to have seroconverted in Sucre.
84.Rationale: The passage confirms that Sucre used an RDT for screening, but it also states that positive RDT results were systematically confirmed with conventional ELISA and HAI (with recombinant ELISA as tiebreaker). Because positives were confirmed by established ELISA/HAI methods, misclassification of true positives due to RDT screening alone is unlikely to explain the low seroconversion rates. The passage provides data showing very low seroconversion in Sucre, but does not state that RDT sensitivity caused misclassification; rather, it describes confirmatory testing. Therefore the claim that RDT use (and lower sensitivity) explains the lower seroconversion is not supported by the information given.

84.Option: The lack of a reliable test for parasitological cure is the main reason for the observed differences in seroconversion rates between Central and South American programs.
Evidence: The first post-treatment serologic evaluation was generally conducted at 18 or 36 months post-treatment.
Based on WHO protocol, cure was defined as two non-reactive ELISA tests (one conventional, one recombinant) performed on the same sample on the same date.
In Yoro, Honduras, seroconversion rate for T. cruzi was 87.1% (202/232) at 18 months post-treatment ... In Olopa, Guatemala ... seroconversion at 18 months post-treatment was 58.1% (18/31).

Preliminary results of overall seroconversion post-treatment was 5.4% (59/1,101) in Entre Ríos by up to 60 months post-treatment ... To date, of 276 patients followed up between 9 and 27 months post-treatment, no patient has been found to have seroconverted in Sucre.
84.Rationale: The passage describes the serologic method and WHO-based definition used to assess cure (two non-reactive ELISA tests) and specifies long post-treatment follow-up times. It does not state that there is no reliable test for parasitological cure or claim that differences in test reliability are the main explanation for regional differences. Instead, it reports differing seroconversion rates and provides age-stratified data. The information therefore does not support the strong claim that lack of a reliable parasitological cure test is the main reason for the observed differences.

84.Option: The higher seroconversion rates in Central America compared to Bolivia may be due to more effective vector control measures implemented in the earlier programs, which reduced the time between infection and treatment initiation.
Evidence: As a precondition for treatment in national programs for Chagas disease, MSF involvement in vector control activities was adapted to each country's situation and capacity.
In Yoro, Honduras, MSF was directly involved in vector control activities; in the programs in Guatemala and Bolivia, vector control was directed by the national programs.
Before starting diagnosis and treatment of patients, a prerequisite was that infestation rates of the given community had to be <3% following national protocols.
All the programs focused on pediatric and adolescent patients, but with an increase in age group treated over time: Yoro, <12 years old; Olopa and Entre Ríos, <15 years; and Sucre, <18 years.
84.Rationale: The passage documents that vector control was a program component and notes differing modes of involvement (MSF directly in Yoro; national programs elsewhere) and the infestation <3% prerequisite. However, it does not provide evidence that vector control was more effective in Central America than in Bolivia, nor does it present data on time from infection to treatment initiation. The passage emphasizes differences in age groups treated and age-related seroconversion patterns, but does not support the specific causal chain that more effective vector control (reducing time-to-treatment) explains higher central American seroconversion. Therefore this option is not justified by the provided information.

84.Option: The observed differences in seroconversion rates between the Central American and Bolivian programs may be explained by geographic variation in Trypanosoma cruzi parasite lineages, with T. cruzi type I predominating in Central America and type II in South America.
Evidence: Chagas disease (American trypanosomiasis) is an endemic disease of the Americas, caused by infection with the protozoan parasite Trypanosoma cruzi.
In Yoro, Honduras, seroconversion rate for T. cruzi was 87.1% (202/232) at 18 months post-treatment ... In Olopa, Guatemala ... seroconversion at 18 months post-treatment was 58.1% (18/31).

Preliminary results of overall seroconversion post-treatment was 5.4% (59/1,101) in Entre Ríos by up to 60 months post-treatment ... To date, of 276 patients followed up between 9 and 27 months post-treatment, no patient has been found to have seroconverted in Sucre.
84.Rationale: The passage identifies the etiologic agent as Trypanosoma cruzi and reports regional differences in seroconversion rates, but it contains no information about parasite genetic lineages, their geographic distribution, or differential treatment response by lineage. The specific assertion about T. cruzi types I and II is not mentioned in the text and therefore cannot be derived from the passage. While geographic lineage variation is a biologically plausible explanation externally, it is not supported by the provided document.

84.Option: The differences in seroconversion rates between regions may be explained by variations in the age distribution of treated patients, with younger children in Central America having higher cure rates and older children in Bolivia being less responsive to treatment.
Evidence: All the programs focused on pediatric and adolescent patients, but with an increase in age group treated over time: Yoro, <12 years old; Olopa and Entre Ríos, <15 years; and Sucre, <18 years.
The increase in treatment age groups over time and projects reflected MSF's strategy to first diagnose and treat young children and then expand these services to older children and adolescents.

In Yoro, Honduras, seroconversion rate for T. cruzi was 87.1% (202/232) at 18 months post-treatment, showing a high seroconversion rate achieved in a relatively short period of time (Table 1). At 36 months, seroconversion rate was 92.7% (215/232).
In Olopa, Guatemala, from available patient data (25.5% of the treatment cohort), seroconversion at 18 months post-treatment was 58.1% (18/31).

Preliminary results of overall seroconversion post-treatment was 5.4% (59/1,101) in Entre Ríos by up to 60 months post-treatment ... To date, of 276 patients followed up between 9 and 27 months post-treatment, no patient has been found to have seroconverted in Sucre.
