Therefore concluding males are more likely to transmit is not supported by the passage.

160.Option: The significantly higher number of female P. perniciosus trapped in the cage baited with the infected dog compared to the uninfected dog indicates that female sand flies are more likely to feed on infected dogs due to increased blood meal size or nutritional quality.
Evidence: The numbers of female P. perniciosus attracted towards infected dogs and uninfected dogs were 205.30 ± 16.94 and 80.57 ± 7.82 respectively and 18.80 ± 6.25 did not respond (NR) (Fig 3).

The numbers of female P. perniciosus that fed on the infected and uninfected dogs were (186.16 ± 16.98), and (66.83 ± 7.58), respectively. The proportion of P. perniciosus females that fed on infected and uninfected dogs were 0.90 ± 0.02 and 0.81 ± 0.03 respectively ... Feeding success was significantly affected by the infection status of dogs (ANOVA: F = 7.5714, DF = 1, P = 0.0079).

The number of eggs laid by each female P. perniciosus fed on infected dogs and uninfected dogs were 36.46 ± 0.16, and 43.35 ± 0.15, respectively (Fig 5). Fecundity was highly affected by the infection status of dogs (ANOVA: F = 13.35, DF = 1, P<0.001). Females that had fed on infected dogs laid significantly fewer eggs than females that had fed on uninfected dogs (EMMEANS: t = -3.654, P<0.001).
160.Rationale: The data show significantly more female sand flies were attracted to and fed on infected dogs. However, the option asserts a causal mechanism—that this is due to increased blood meal size or better nutritional quality from infected hosts. The study did not measure blood meal size or host nutritional content. Moreover, fecundity (eggs laid) was significantly lower in females that fed on infected dogs, which contradicts the idea that infected-host blood provided higher nutritional quality. Thus the specific causal explanation (increased blood meal size or nutritional quality) is not supported by the passage.

160.Option: The enhanced attractiveness of infected dogs to both female and male Phlebotomus perniciosus and Phlebotomus perfiliewi under natural conditions suggests a zoopotentiation effect that increases the transmission success of Leishmania infantum.
Evidence: Infected dog attracted more P. perniciosus than uninfected dog (MCP: z = 3.666, P<0.001).
Infected dog attracted more P. perfiliewi than uninfected dog (EMMEANS: z = 7.931, p<0.001).

The number of females P. perniciosus trapped in the cage baited with the infected dog (59.44 ± 11.82) was significantly greater than the number of males trapped (34.11 ± 8.18) (EMMEANS: z = 2.933, P = 0.0393).

A minimum of 36% to 100% of the lab-colonised P. perniciosus that were fed on infected dogs developed L. infantum infections when dissected and examined ... whereas none of the sand flies fed on uninfected dogs were found to be infected with parasites.
Zoonotic visceral leishmaniasis (ZVL) ... is transmitted by the bite of female phlebotomine sand flies.

We hypothesized that physiological changes in dogs parasitized by L. infantum change the dog’s odour making them more attractive to P. perniciosus and therefore enhancing the parasite’ s transmission success.
160.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence consistent with a zoopotentiation effect (infected reservoir hosts increasing vector attraction and thereby amplifying transmission): infected dogs attracted significantly more P. perniciosus and P. perfiliewi in the field; more females (the transmitting sex) were attracted to infected dogs; and xenodiagnosis showed sand flies feeding on infected dogs became infected while those fed on uninfected dogs did not. The authors also explicitly hypothesized parasite-induced changes in host odour could enhance transmission success. Together these observations support the inference that infected dogs enhance vector contact and thus likely increase transmission success of L. infantum.

161.Option: The use of reagent strips alone for diagnosing urinary schistosomiasis could reduce survey costs by 2%, but this approach would result in considerable misclassification of IUs for treatment in Southern Sudan due to low specificity, making it unsuitable for implementation.
Evidence: Testing for urinary schistosomiasis infection could, for example, be conducted only with Hemastix reagent sticks (Bayer Diagnostics, Basingstoke, UK), instead of the current procedure whereby all samples with blood in urine and a sub-sample of negatives were cross checked using urine filtration. By cutting out the cost of these and other supplies for urine filtration, the cost per implementation unit surveyed would have decreased by 2% (Table 3).

The rationale for cutting out urine filtration was that the use of reagent strips alone is a reliable diagnostic procedure in some African countries [35] and may, after validation, be applicable in high schistosomiasis transmission settings in Southern Sudan.
161.Rationale: The passage explicitly supports the first clause: switching to reagent strips alone would have decreased cost per IU by about 2%. However, the passage does not state that reagent strips have low specificity in Southern Sudan or that their use would cause ‘considerable misclassification’ of IUs. Instead the authors note reagent strips "are a reliable diagnostic procedure in some African countries" and that they "may, after validation, be applicable" in Southern Sudan. The claim that this approach is unsuitable due to low specificity is not supported by any quoted text and therefore cannot be derived from the Passage Body.

161.Option: Survey costs in Southern Sudan are likely to be higher than in other countries due to the need for international staff and post-conflict reconstruction challenges, which increase both personnel and operational expenses.
Evidence: To capture the indirect costs of project management and administration we applied an overhead of 25% to the financial cost estimate for all budget lines. This rate is higher than those applicable to operations in more stable settings of eastern Africa [e.g. [33], but in our experience provides an accurate reflection of the cost involved in implementing programmes in a land-locked country undergoing post-conflict reconstruction.

Due to severe human resource constraints affecting Southern Sudan, only two national laboratory technicians per team could be recruited, with additional technicians recruited as short-term consultants from the Vector Control Division, MoH, Uganda.
Costs of MoH staff were based on the GoSS pay scale for 2009 while the time of international volunteers was valued using equivalent Malaria Consortium salaries for this setting.
Owing to the poor infrastructure in Northern Bahr-el-Ghazal State and fluctuating security, teams established camps in locations that were centrally located between study communities.
161.Rationale: The passage documents factors that elevate costs in Southern Sudan relative to more stable settings: a deliberately higher overhead (25%) justified by post-conflict reconstruction, reliance on international/outsourced technicians due to local human resource constraints, valuation of international volunteer time, and operational challenges from poor infrastructure and security requiring camping and extra logistics. From these statements it is epidemiologically and economically reasonable to infer that survey costs in Southern Sudan are likely higher than in other, more stable countries because of the need for international staff and post-conflict operational challenges.

161.Option: Capital costs accounted for only 4.3% of total survey costs, indicating that infrastructure investment is not a major concern in integrated NTD surveys, and therefore should be deprioritized in future planning.
Evidence: Because all assets above USD 100 in value and with a lifespan >1 year were annualised and only their time in use during the survey was included, capital cost amounted to only 4.3% of the total survey costs and were largely comprised of vehicle costs.
Owing to the poor infrastructure in Northern Bahr-el-Ghazal State and fluctuating security, teams established camps in locations that were centrally located between study communities.
Three Toyota Land Cruisers were used during the surveys.
161.Rationale: While the passage states capital costs composed only 4.3% of this survey's total (largely vehicles), it also describes serious infrastructure challenges (poor infrastructure, need to camp, use of multiple vehicles) that necessitated those capital inputs. The observation that capital comprised a small share of costs in this specific survey does not support the broad prescription to deprioritize infrastructure investment in future planning. The Passage Body does not conclude that infrastructure is unimportant; on the contrary, the described operational context implies infrastructure is a practical concern. Therefore the policy recommendation to deprioritize infrastructure cannot be derived from the passage.

161.Option: The major cost drivers of an integrated NTD survey in Southern Sudan were personnel and consumables, and this observation is likely applicable to most other settings, warranting detailed budgeting for these inputs during planning.
Evidence: The total financial costs for the survey amounted to USD 182,067, the majority of which was spent on recurrent items. The principal cost drivers were personnel (34.4%) followed by survey consumables (30.4%) (Table 2).
As for the financial costs, most economic costs were taken up by survey consumables and personnel, accounting for 27% and 38% of the total, respectively (Table 2).

Such cost analyses should be undertaken using an approach that is ‘generalisable’, hence allowing comparison between settings and use of results to plan and budget for similar undertakings elsewhere [20], [21].
In addition, to help compare our results to those from other settings and estimate the costs of integrated surveys elsewhere, we present a standardised approach to costing integrated NTD surveys.
161.Rationale: The Passage Body explicitly identifies personnel and consumables as the principal cost drivers in the Southern Sudan integrated survey. The authors also emphasize using a generalisable, standardised costing approach to allow comparison and planning in other settings. From these statements it is a reasonable epidemiological and programmatic inference that detailed budgeting for personnel and consumables should be prioritized when planning similar integrated NTD surveys in comparable settings.

161.Option: The cost of survey consumables is likely to be similar in other African countries, as most of them are not produced in the region, while personnel costs might differ considerably between settings, particularly when comparing Southern Sudan to countries that have not been affected by decades of conflict.
Evidence: The principal cost drivers were personnel (34.4%) followed by survey consumables (30.4%) (Table 2).
As for the financial costs, most economic costs were taken up by survey consumables and personnel, accounting for 27% and 38% of the total, respectively (Table 2).
ICTs that cost >USD 4.0 per test.

Due to severe human resource constraints affecting Southern Sudan, only two national laboratory technicians per team could be recruited, with additional technicians recruited as short-term consultants from the Vector Control Division, MoH, Uganda.

To capture the indirect costs of project management and administration we applied an overhead of 25% to the financial cost estimate for all budget lines. This rate is higher than those applicable to operations in more stable settings of eastern Africa...
161.Rationale: The passage documents that consumables and personnel were major cost drivers and that ICTs are relatively costly (>USD 4/test). It also documents that personnel costs and overheads are influenced by Southern Sudan's post-conflict context. However, the specific claim that consumable costs are 'likely to be similar in other African countries because most of them are not produced in the region' is not stated or supported in the Passage Body—there is no information about the production origin of consumables or their price uniformity across countries. While the passage supports the idea that personnel costs may vary across settings (and provides reasons for higher personnel/overhead costs in Southern Sudan), the assertion about consumables' similarity across Africa cannot be derived from the provided text.

162.Option: The seasonal variation in HFRS incidence is primarily driven by changes in air temperature, with outbreaks occurring during periods of high temperature and low wind conditions from late July to late September.
Evidence: Fifteen studies indicated the negative correlation between air temperature and HFRS activity, while seven studies found the positive correlation between air temperature and HFRS activity.

There were also two studies that defined a certain temperature as the dividing point between HFRS activity and air temperature, the correlation curve was inverted ‘U-shaped’ [23,29]. Also, three studies did not find statistically significant associations between HFRS activity and air temperature.

The study from Changchun City, Jilin Province [18] and Changsha City [33] found that HFRS was prone to outbreaks and epidemics during relatively dry, high temperature and low wind conditions from late July to late September.
162.Rationale: The passage does report outbreaks occurring in late July to late September under dry, high-temperature, low-wind conditions (supporting the specific seasonal window). However, it also documents widely inconsistent associations between air temperature and HFRS (negative, positive, inverted U-shaped, and null findings). Therefore the stronger causal claim that seasonal variation is "primarily driven" by air temperature is not supported by the presented, heterogeneous evidence.

162.Option: The inconsistent findings across studies regarding the correlation between air temperature and HFRS incidence are primarily due to differences in statistical methods used, rather than variations in geographical scale or population characteristics.
Evidence: the choice of appropriate statistical methods according to the type of HFRS epidemic area, the availability, fineness and data distribution characteristics of historical data such as epidemic situation or meteorological data in various regions is being another challenge.

the researchers adopted various statistical methods to explore the epidemiological links between HFRS incidence and air temperature, at different spatial and temporal scale and among those studies, the length of lag period also varied.
The associations observed at one scale were not present at another one.
162.Rationale: The passage notes that a variety of statistical methods were used (which could contribute to inconsistent findings), but it also explicitly states that associations observed at one geographical scale were not present at another, indicating that scale and population characteristics contribute to heterogeneity. The text does not support the claim that methodological differences are the primary cause to the exclusion of geographic or population variation; rather, it presents multiple plausible sources of inconsistency.

162.Option: The correlation between air temperature and HFRS incidence is strongest in populations with high occupational exposure to rodents, such as agricultural workers, indicating that behavioral factors are more influential than meteorological factors in disease transmission.
Evidence: Effect of air temperature on the HFRS activity varied among different populations

The study in Huludao City, Liaoning province found that HFRS activity in the population aged 35–59 years was significantly affected by air temperature, but this phenomenon could not be found in the population of other age groups [25]; the authors of this Huludao study attributed the phenomenon to the increased virus exposure in agricultural labor work in the middle-aged population.
Fifteen studies indicated the negative correlation between air temperature and HFRS activity, while seven studies found the positive correlation between air temperature and HFRS activity.
162.Rationale: The Huludao study provides an example where an age group (middle-aged agricultural workers) showed a significant temperature–HFRS association and the authors attributed this to increased occupational exposure, which supports interaction between behavior and meteorology. However, the passage does not provide general evidence that the correlation is strongest in occupationally exposed groups across studies, nor that behavioral factors are more influential than meteorological factors overall. The heterogeneous study findings prevent concluding the stated generalization.

162.Option: The effect of air temperature on HFRS activity varies by population group, suggesting that demographic characteristics should be considered when estimating or predicting the relationship between meteorological factors and HFRS incidence.
Evidence: Effect of air temperature on the HFRS activity varied among different populations

The study in Huludao City, Liaoning province found that HFRS activity in the population aged 35–59 years was significantly affected by air temperature, but this phenomenon could not be found in the population of other age groups [25]; the authors of this Huludao study attributed the phenomenon to the increased virus exposure in agricultural labor work in the middle-aged population.

This study in Huludao also indicated that the correlations between HFRS activity and air temperature were statistically significant in both males and females, while with different length of period, two months in the male and no lag in the female population [25].
162.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the effect of air temperature varies among different populations and provides direct study evidence (Huludao) showing differences by age, occupation-related exposure, sex, and lag structure. From an epidemiological perspective, this supports the inference that demographic and population characteristics should be included when estimating or predicting temperature–HFRS relationships.

163.Option: The low incidence of visceral leishmaniasis in this cohort of HIV-infected individuals on long-term antiretroviral therapy with relatively high CD4 counts suggests that the risk of progression to overt disease is low in this subgroup, supporting the prioritization of interventions for patients diagnosed with both HIV and VL simultaneously.
Evidence: The majority of patients (95.5%) were on ART, with a median of 52.2 months on treatment (IQR 27.3–79.5).
At study enrolment, the median CD4 count was 377 cells/mm3 (IQR 250–518) and the median BMI was 19.7 kg/m2 (IQR 18.2–21.5).
In January 2017, the accumulating evidence of the pilot study suggested the incidence of VL to be very low in the study population. Consequently, the study site was closed.
Amongst all individuals enrolled in the study, one was clinically diagnosed with VL at month nine of follow-up.
163.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence that most participants were on long-term ART with a median CD4 count of 377 and that only a single clinical VL case occurred during follow-up, and explicitly states the investigators regarded incidence of VL as very low. These facts support the first clause that the risk of progression to overt VL was low in this cohort. However, the latter clause recommending prioritization of interventions for patients diagnosed with both HIV and VL simultaneously is a policy recommendation not derived from the presented data. The passage does not provide evidence comparing different prioritization strategies or explicitly recommend shifting priority to coinfected individuals over other groups; it instead describes the low observed incidence and the rationale for exploring a potential screen-and-treat strategy. Because the policy assertion is not supported by evidence in the passage, the overall option cannot be accepted as fully derivable from the Passage Body.

163.Option: The relatively high incidence of asymptomatic Leishmania infection in this cohort indicates that current screening strategies for HIV patients in VL-endemic areas are insufficient and should be expanded to include routine testing for Leishmania markers.
Evidence: Of the 462 individuals testing negative on all Leishmania markers at enrolment (Leishmania-negative individuals), 426 had follow-up samples available, with a total follow-up time of 353 person-years. Out of these, 36 developed asymptomatic infection, yielding an incidence rate of 10.2/100 person-years.
The estimated cumulative incidence was 5.0% by six months, and 9.5% by 12 months (Table 5).

As a first step to construct the evidence-base, we conducted a longitudinal study in a large HIV patient cohort in a VL-endemic region in North-West Ethiopia to determine the prevalence, incidence and determinants of asymptomatic Leishmania infection in HIV-coinfected patients, and the associated risk of VL. The study was set-up as a pilot study to assess the relevance and feasibility of a potential screen and treat strategy in the study area.
163.Rationale: The passage documents measurable incidence of asymptomatic infection (10.2/100 person-years; cumulative 9.5% at 12 months) and states the study was designed to investigate the feasibility of screening. However, it does not present data on existing ‘‘current screening strategies’’ in routine HIV care or provide evidence that those strategies are insufficient in practice. While the incidence figures show that asymptomatic infection occurs, the leap to conclude that routine testing should be expanded is a policy recommendation that goes beyond the study results presented. The study was exploratory/pilot in nature and aimed to assess relevance and feasibility, but it does not provide direct evidence that existing screening is inadequate or that routine testing should be implemented.

163.Option: The observed reversion of Leishmania markers in nearly half of the patients with prevalent infection suggests that the immune system in HIV-infected individuals on ART can effectively clear Leishmania parasites without clinical intervention.
Evidence: Of the 49 cases with prevalent infection at baseline, 46 had follow-up samples available, with a total patient follow-up time of 33 person-years. Of these, 16 reverted to negative Leishmania markers during follow-up, with a reversion rate of 48.5/100 person years.
The estimated probability of reversion was 26.3% by six months, and 40.1% by 12 months (Table 5).
The majority of patients (95.5%) were on ART, with a median of 52.2 months on treatment (IQR 27.3–79.5).
Self-reported ART adherence during follow-up was generally good with > 90% of individuals reporting good ART adherence (missing <3 tablets/month since the previous visit).
163.Rationale: The passage documents that a substantial proportion of participants who had prevalent Leishmania markers at baseline reverted to negative markers during follow-up (16/46; substantial reversion rates and probabilities at 6 and 12 months). These reversions occurred in a cohort where almost all participants were on long-term ART with generally good adherence and moderate median CD4 counts, and there is no indication that participants received treatment for Leishmania during follow-up. From an epidemiological perspective, spontaneous marker reversion in this treated HIV cohort is consistent with the inference that immune recovery on ART enabled clearance or suppression of detectable infection without specific anti-Leishmania clinical intervention. While marker reversion is not definitive proof of complete parasitological clearance (tests measure markers rather than direct viability), the observed pattern supports the inference that many asymptomatic infections resolved over time in this ART-treated cohort.

163.Option: The association between concurrent malaria infection and asymptomatic Leishmania infection suggests that malaria may act as a cofactor that enhances immune suppression, thereby increasing the risk of Leishmania progression to clinical disease.
Evidence: Risk factors identified in univariate analysis were being male, sleeping outside and having malaria (Table 3).

Multivariate analysis showed the risk of asymptomatic Leishmania infection to be increased for male patients (OR 3.2, 95% CI 1.4–7.0, p = 0.002), and for patients with malaria infection (OR 6.1, 95% CI 1.9–18.9, p = 0.004) (Table 4).
Amongst all individuals enrolled in the study, one was clinically diagnosed with VL at month nine of follow-up. The patient was on ART at enrolment but with low CD4 cell counts (41 cells/mm3).
163.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence of an association between concurrent malaria infection and asymptomatic Leishmania infection (multivariate OR 6.1). However, this is an association with asymptomatic infection status, not evidence that malaria acts as a causal cofactor that enhances immune suppression or that it increases the risk of progression from asymptomatic infection to clinical VL. The study presents no longitudinal analysis demonstrating that malaria coinfection leads to higher rates of progression to overt disease, nor does it provide mechanistic data on malaria-induced immune suppression leading to VL progression. Therefore, the specific causal inference implied by the option (malaria enhancing immune suppression and increasing progression risk) is not supported by the data in the passage.

163.Option: The high prevalence of asymptomatic Leishmania infection in this cohort, combined with the detection of parasite DNA in blood and urine, suggests that asymptomatic individuals are likely to be infectious and could contribute to ongoing transmission of the disease in the community.
Evidence: At the time of recruitment, 7.4% of patients tested positive on the rK39 RDT, 4.3% on DAT, 0.2% on PCR and 0.2% on KAtex.
Of the 36 incident cases, five tested positive on rK39 only (rK39 seroconversion), 10 on DAT only, one on both DAT and rK39 and 20 on PCR only (Table 6).
There have also been concerns that some HIV patients infected with Leishmania might be more likely to transmit, also at the asymptomatic stage [3–5]. This would hamper VL control efforts.
163.Rationale: The passage documents that asymptomatic infections were detectable by serology, antigen (KAtex), and PCR (parasite DNA) — including incident cases with PCR positivity — which shows that some asymptomatic individuals had measurable parasite markers in blood and urine. However, the study does not measure infectiousness (for example sandfly infection rates from human hosts) or transmission from asymptomatic individuals to vectors or other humans. While the passage notes existing concerns in the literature that some HIV patients might be more likely to transmit at the asymptomatic stage, this is presented as concern rather than demonstrated by the study data. Therefore, concluding that asymptomatic individuals are likely to be infectious and contribute to transmission goes beyond the evidence presented and is not directly derivable from the Passage Body.

164.Option: Because plasma troponin T is elevated in infants with clinical beriberi and correlates with low basal ETK, it can be used as a definitive diagnostic test for infantile beriberi in resource-limited settings.
Evidence: Using continuous measures or previously published cut-offs, infant basal ETK, activated ETK and plasma troponin T were the only infant biochemical markers that predicted infantile beriberi consistently (Table 1).

the odds of beriberi in infants with detectable plasma troponin T were 3.4 times higher when compared with infants without detectable troponin T (OR 3.4, 95%CI 1.22 to 9.73, P = 0.019, n = 28 matched sets providing 69 observations).
Detectable troponin T had a sensitivity (95%CI) of 78.6 (59.0 to 91.7) % and specificity (95%CI) of 56.1 (39.7 to 71.5) % for predicting infantile beriberi.
Infants with low basal ETK tended to have higher troponin T (Spearman's rho = −0.39, p = 0.02, Fig. 1).

In addition to the uncertainty of appropriate ETK assessment of infantile beriberi, there is a need for more straightforward and inexpensive tests to evaluate thiamin deficiency. Classical infantile beriberi may be easy to recognize but it is likely that in chronically thiamin deficient societies, infants may present with other thiamin deficient syndromes and that the clinical features of beriberi may be obscured by other precipitating diseases [19].

recently described circulating markers of cardiac dysfunction, such as brain natriuretic peptide (BNP), N-terminal pro-brain natriuretic peptide (ntBNP) and troponin T [33], are likely to be elevated in infantile beriberi and could, in the absence of confounding diseases, suggest a diagnosis of beriberi.
164.Rationale: The passage shows troponin T is associated with clinical beriberi, correlates with low basal ETK, and has moderate sensitivity (78.6%) but low specificity (56.1%). These test characteristics and the authors' caution that cardiac markers could suggest a diagnosis only "in the absence of confounding diseases" contradict the claim that troponin T can serve as a definitive diagnostic test. The study does not claim troponin T is definitive, nor does it provide evidence that it is practical or validated as a stand-alone diagnostic in resource-limited settings. Therefore the strong assertion in the option is not supported by the passage.

164.Option: The lack of a significant association between plasma troponin T and maternal thiamin status suggests that troponin T is not influenced by maternal nutritional factors and is therefore a reliable indicator of infant-specific thiamin deficiency.
Evidence: Maternal troponin T could not be compared as only one mother had detectable troponin T.

A low maternal basal ETK (<lower limit reference range for age, Table 2) was significantly associated with infantile beriberi (OR 5.4, 95%CI 1.11 to 26.0, n = 17 matched sets giving 45 observations for analysis).
Maternal basal ETK was significantly linearly correlated with infant basal ETK (Pearson's r = 0.66, p<0.001) (Fig. 2).
164.Rationale: The passage explicitly states maternal troponin T could not be meaningfully compared because only one mother had detectable troponin T, so no inference about association (or lack thereof) with maternal thiamin status can be made. Moreover, maternal basal ETK is associated with infant beriberi and correlates with infant basal ETK, indicating maternal nutritional status is relevant. Thus the claim that troponin T is unaffected by maternal factors and is therefore a reliable infant-specific indicator is not supported by the data presented.

164.Option: The study's findings indicate that plasma troponin T is a highly specific and sensitive biomarker for diagnosing infantile beriberi, with a specificity of 94.1% and sensitivity of 78.6% in this population.
Evidence: Detectable troponin T had a sensitivity (95%CI) of 78.6 (59.0 to 91.7) % and specificity (95%CI) of 56.1 (39.7 to 71.5) % for predicting infantile beriberi.

Using the Soldin et al. [41] cutoffs for ntBNP, eleven (73.3%) infants with beriberi, but only one of the controls (4.8%), had raised ntBNP. Prediction using these ntBNP cut-offs gave a sensitivity (95%CI) of 63.6 (30.8 to 89.1) % and specificity (95%CI) of 94.1 (71.3 to 99.9) %.
164.Rationale: The option misstates the reported specificity. The passage reports troponin T specificity as 56.1%, not 94.1%. The 94.1% specificity refers to ntBNP using Soldin cutoffs, not troponin T. While troponin T sensitivity was reported at 78.6%, its specificity is modest, so describing it as "highly specific and sensitive" with the stated specificity is incorrect based on the passage.

164.Option: Plasma troponin T is the most reliable biochemical marker for infantile beriberi because it shows the strongest association with clinical diagnosis and is unaffected by other cardiac conditions.
Evidence: Using continuous measures or previously published cut-offs, infant basal ETK, activated ETK and plasma troponin T were the only infant biochemical markers that predicted infantile beriberi consistently (Table 1).
Mean (95%CI) infant basal ETK was lower in cases (0.53 (0.47–0.60) micromoles/min/gHb, n = 17), than in those without beriberi (0.77 (0.68–0.86), n = 29, p = 0.02)...

Basal ETK<0.59 micromoles/min/gHb gave a sensitivity (95%CI) of 75.0 (47.6 to 92.7)% and specificity (95%CI) of 85.2 (66.3 to 95.8)% for predicting beriberi. The odds of having beriberi were 15.9 times higher (95%CI 2.03 to 124.2; p = 0.008, area under ROC curve = 0.80) for infants with basal ETK<0.59 micromoles/min/gHb when compared with patients with basal value ≥0.59.
the odds of beriberi in infants with detectable plasma troponin T were 3.4 times higher when compared with infants without detectable troponin T (OR 3.4, 95%CI 1.22 to 9.73...).

recently described circulating markers of cardiac dysfunction, such as brain natriuretic peptide (BNP), N-terminal pro-brain natriuretic peptide (ntBNP) and troponin T [33], are likely to be elevated in infantile beriberi and could, in the absence of confounding diseases, suggest a diagnosis of beriberi.
164.Rationale: The passage does not support the claim that troponin T is the "most reliable" marker. Basal ETK showed a stronger association with clinical beriberi (OR ~15.9 for basal ETK<0.59) and better specificity (85.2%) than troponin T (OR 3.4; specificity 56.1%). Furthermore, the authors explicitly note that cardiac markers "could, in the absence of confounding diseases, suggest a diagnosis," which implies troponin T can be affected by other cardiac conditions. Thus the statements that troponin T is the strongest marker and unaffected by other cardiac conditions are not supported.

164.Option: The data suggest that markers of cardiac dysfunction, such as plasma troponin T, could be useful surrogate markers in the diagnosis of infantile beriberi.
Evidence: In addition to the uncertainty of appropriate ETK assessment of infantile beriberi, there is a need for more straightforward and inexpensive tests to evaluate thiamin deficiency. ... recently described circulating markers of cardiac dysfunction, such as brain natriuretic peptide (BNP), N-terminal pro-brain natriuretic peptide (ntBNP) and troponin T [33], are likely to be elevated in infantile beriberi and could, in the absence of confounding diseases, suggest a diagnosis of beriberi.

Using continuous measures or previously published cut-offs, infant basal ETK, activated ETK and plasma troponin T were the only infant biochemical markers that predicted infantile beriberi consistently (Table 1).

the odds of beriberi in infants with detectable plasma troponin T were 3.4 times higher when compared with infants without detectable troponin T (OR 3.4, 95%CI 1.22 to 9.73, P = 0.019, n = 28 matched sets providing 69 observations).
Detectable troponin T had a sensitivity (95%CI) of 78.6 (59.0 to 91.7) % and specificity (95%CI) of 56.1 (39.7 to 71.5) % for predicting infantile beriberi.

Detectable plasma troponin T and infant basal ETK<59 micromoles/min/gHb remained significant independent predictors for infantile beriberi (OR 4.3, 95%CI 1.25 to 15.0, adjusted for mother observed food taboos, and OR 15.9, 95%CI 2.03 to 124.2, for detectable troponin T and ETK<59 micromoles/min/gHb, respectively).
164.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that troponin T is elevated in infants with clinical beriberi, that detectable troponin T is associated with higher odds of beriberi, that it has reasonable sensitivity, and that it remained an independent predictor in multivariate analysis. The authors explicitly proposed that cardiac markers could suggest the diagnosis ("could, in the absence of confounding diseases, suggest a diagnosis of beriberi"). Thus it is epidemiologically justifiable to infer that cardiac markers including troponin T could serve as useful surrogate markers for diagnosing infantile beriberi, while acknowledging limitations (imperfect specificity and potential confounders) that prevent calling them definitive diagnostic tests.

165.Option: The projected FOI estimates from LF case data and reporting probabilities appear to be more accurate than the FOI estimates from our ensemble model that used spatial covariates to improve model predictions in areas lacking data, particularly in areas that are projected to have the highest incidence.
Evidence: Due to the over-smoothing effect observed in the ensemble model, we focus on LF incidence rates derived from the projected FOI estimates in the rest of our results.

Our ensemble model consisted of a weighted combination of the FOI predictions of each individual statistical regression model, along with a noise term. Due to the poor cross-validation performance of all of the statistical models, the null model received the largest weighting within the ensemble models at both the 1st and 2nd administrative level. As a result, there was limited spatial heterogeneity in the FOI predictions from the ensemble model (S7 Fig).
There was a wider range of annual LF incidence rates with the projected FOI estimates than the ensemble model FOI estimates, due to the smoothing effects of the ensemble model.

The performance of the model predictions on data held out of the regression for model validation was much lower for the 1st administrative level, with random forest providing the best fit to the testing data (r2 = 0.11), suggesting that the models are overfitting to the training dataset (S5 Fig). The cross-validation performance of all of the statistical models at the 2nd administrative unit was also poor, with an r2 < 0.10 for every model (S6 Fig).
165.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the ensemble (covariate-based) models suffered from poor cross-validation performance, received high weight for the null model, and produced over-smoothed FOI predictions with limited spatial heterogeneity. The authors therefore state they focused subsequent incidence results on the projected FOI estimates derived from case/death data and reporting probabilities. These statements support the inference that the projected FOI estimates were preferred (and effectively more useful/accurate for high-incidence areas) than the ensemble covariate-based FOI estimates.

165.Option: The spatial heterogeneity in LASV spillover rates across West Africa, particularly within Nigeria, Sierra Leone, and Guinea, was the primary determinant of the variation in projected symptomatic case rates.
Evidence: FOI projections from LF case data and estimated reporting probabilities varied considerably across the study region, with evidence of spatial heterogeneity within and between countries (Figs 4, S1 and S2). In particular, there was substantial heterogeneity in FOI estimates at the admin1 and admin2 levels within Nigeria.
Due to the spatial heterogeneity in our FOI estimates, the highest LF incidence rates were found at the admin2 as opposed to the admin1 level.

The substantial spatial and temporal variation in LASV prevalence is an important consideration for vaccine trial site selection, as potential trial locations might have different LF incidence rates despite their proximity and similar environmental conditions.
At the admin2 level, LF incidence rates calculated from projected FOI estimates were >10 per 1,000 for several administrative units with seroreversion rates of 3% or 6%, but not 0%.
165.Rationale: The passage repeatedly attributes differences in projected LF incidence to spatial heterogeneity in FOI/spillover rates across administrative units and within countries (notably Nigeria, and also Sierra Leone and Guinea where serology-informed FOI estimates exist). It explicitly links spatial heterogeneity in FOI to variation in incidence rates and notes that highest incidence appears at finer admin2 scales because of this heterogeneity. Therefore, spatial heterogeneity in spillover rates is a key driver of variation in projected symptomatic case rates.

165.Option: The level of protection against reinfection and disease among both seropositive and seroreverted individuals influenced expected LF incidence rates in our model, with higher levels of protection against disease resulting in lower expected incidence rates.
Evidence: For a given seroreversion rate, LF case incidence rates were lowest when seropositive individuals were assumed to be protected from infection, intermediate when they had a relative risk of 0.36 for developing LF compared to seronegative individuals, and highest when they had a relative risk of 0.53 for reinfection and disease.
When seroreverted individuals were assumed to have partial protection against LF, incidence rates were lower than when seroreverted individuals were assumed to be fully susceptible.

Therefore, estimated LF incidence rates were highest when we assumed that seroreversion was frequent (6%), and that both seroreverted and seropositive individuals remained susceptible to infection and disease.

165.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes multiple model scenarios altering the susceptibility of seropositive and seroreverted individuals and shows how these assumptions change LF incidence: greater immunity (protection) in these groups lowers predicted incidence, while greater susceptibility raises it. Thus the level of protection among seropositive and seroreverted individuals is a key factor influencing projected symptomatic case counts.

166.Option: The stability of high-risk hotspots across different data cleaning methods and threshold settings suggests that sub-county level prioritization is robust and reliable for long-term cholera control planning.
Evidence: The sensitivity analysis showed the same high-risk hotspots in both arms of the analysis, identifying over 3 million people at high-risk regardless of which data set is used for the analysis (Fig D and Table C in S1 Text).

Based on discussions with the MoH regarding the feasibility, cost, and logistics for targeting interventions across the country and the relative importance of each metric to country priorities, we selected the 90th percentile value as the MAI threshold (33 per 100,000) and the median value as the persistence threshold (2.88%) at the sub-county scale.
166.Rationale: The passage reports a sensitivity analysis in which alternative methods of handling records that could not be geolocated produced the same high-risk hotspots and identified ~3 million people at high risk, demonstrating robustness to data-cleaning assumptions. The authors also explored alternate MAI thresholds and report the resulting changes in the population prioritized, indicating that while threshold choice affects the number prioritized, the analysis still produces identifiable priority areas. The selection of thresholds was guided by feasibility considerations with the Ministry of Health, implying operational relevance. Together, these statements in the Passage Body support the inference that sub-county level prioritization is robust to reasonable data-cleaning choices and threshold selections and therefore is reliable for informing ongoing cholera control planning.

166.Option: Targeting cholera interventions at the sub-county level is more efficient and feasible than at the county level due to smaller population sizes and improved precision in identifying high-risk areas.
Evidence: In Kenya, there are 47 counties, with an average population size of 1.1 million in 2019, and 301 sub-counties, with an average population size of 176,000 in 2019.
When performing the same prioritization analysis at the county scale instead of the sub-county scale, classification becomes much less efficient due to reduced resolution (Fig 6).

While the county-level analysis identified three high priority counties in Northeast Kenya (Garissa, Tana River and Wajir), the sub-county analysis identified thirteen high priority sub-counties from eight counties, including those in Nairobi County and five additional counties that border neighboring countries.

For example, roughly 660,000 individuals living in sub-counties with no reported cases were classified as living in a high priority county, and 1.6 million living in high priority sub-counties were classified as living in a medium priority county.
166.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that sub-counties are much smaller population units than counties and that analyses at the county level suffer loss of resolution and produce misclassification (over- and under-prioritization) relative to sub-county analysis. The fact that sub-county analysis identifies many more and geographically distinct high-priority areas (13 sub-counties vs 3 counties) while county-level analysis leads to large numbers of people being misclassified supports the epidemiological inference that targeting interventions at sub-county scale is more precise and therefore operationally more efficient. The passage also notes feasibility considerations were used when selecting thresholds, supporting the feasibility claim in practice.

166.Option: The misclassification of populations in high-priority counties due to coarse administrative resolution suggests that county-level planning leads to more equitable distribution of resources across regions.
Evidence: When performing the same prioritization analysis at the county scale instead of the sub-county scale, classification becomes much less efficient due to reduced resolution (Fig 6).

While the county-level analysis identified three high priority counties in Northeast Kenya (Garissa, Tana River and Wajir), the sub-county analysis identified thirteen high priority sub-counties from eight counties, including those in Nairobi County and five additional counties that border neighboring countries.

For example, roughly 660,000 individuals living in sub-counties with no reported cases were classified as living in a high priority county, and 1.6 million living in high priority sub-counties were classified as living in a medium priority county.

166.Rationale: The Passage Body documents that county-level analysis produces misclassification and 'over-prioritizations'—for example, hundreds of thousands of people in sub-counties with no cases would be classified as living in high priority counties, and many in high-priority sub-counties would be down-classified to medium priority counties. These findings indicate inequities and inaccuracies in resource targeting when using coarse administrative units. The option's claim that county-level planning leads to more equitable distribution contradicts the passage evidence; the passage instead implies the opposite—that county-level resolution reduces precision and can lead to inappropriate allocation. Therefore this inference is not supported by the Passage Body.

167.Option: The great diversity of circulating Leishmania species in Colombia plays a fundamental role in the permanence and propagation of the disease, given that neither epidemiological surveillance, nor treatment, take this variable into account.
Evidence: Colombia is the country with the greatest number of circulating species in the world, 10 in total (L. panamensis, L. braziliensis, L. guyanensis, L. infantum chagasi, L. mexicana, L. lainsoni, L. amazonensis, L. colombiensis, L. equatoriensis, L. naiffi), and that the most prevalent species exhibit high genetic diversity [9–11].

Considering the particular characteristics found in the country, it is important that robust epidemiological surveillance models be implemented, which can detect and categorize cases not only by geographical location, but also by species.

Currently, public health authorities in Colombia collect data on various diseases by means of a system called SIVIGILA, which gathers data on a variety of illnesses that are of public health concern [12]. In spite of the existence of this system, it is presumed that inadequate reporting is the norm in rural areas with no direct access to information systems.
167.Rationale: The passage explicitly documents a high diversity of Leishmania species in Colombia and states a need for surveillance models that can detect and categorize cases by species, which implies existing surveillance may not fully capture species-level data. However, the option asserts that neither epidemiological surveillance nor treatment take species diversity into account. While the passage supports the claim about surveillance limitations, it does not provide any evidence about treatment practices or that treatments uniformly ignore species differences. Therefore, the full statement (surveillance and treatment both not taking species into account and species being a fundamental driver of permanence/propagation) cannot be fully derived from the Passage Body.

167.Option: The Andean, Amazon, and Orinoco regions account for over 75% of the cases of TL in Colombia, so that public health efforts should be extended there, particularly away from urban areas.

In spite of the existence of this system, it is presumed that inadequate reporting is the norm in rural areas with no direct access to information systems.
167.Rationale: The passage indicates that the Andean, Amazon, and Orinoco regions have high numbers or rates of TL and highlights rural risk factors (absence of preventive measures in rural areas, internal displacement, inadequate reporting in rural areas). However, the numeric claim that these three regions account for over 75% of cases is not presented or calculable from the provided data. Moreover, while rural-focused public health efforts are suggested by the passage's emphasis on rural risk factors and inadequate reporting, the specific quantitative threshold (>75%) and the prescriptive phrasing "particularly away from urban areas" are not directly supported by the Passage Body. Thus the full inference cannot be derived solely from the text.

Evidence: This clustering of the disease has been associated with various circumstances, including the presence of armed conflict, which leads to internal displacement and increases the probability of contact between vectors, reservoirs and hosts. This occurs in both the civilian and military populations.

Currently, public health authorities in Colombia collect data on various diseases by means of a system called SIVIGILA, which gathers data on a variety of illnesses that are of public health concern [12]. However, it does not mention the FARC by name, nor does it discuss the effects of conflict resolution, transitional zones, or the specific likelihood that former combatants will be newly detected by surveillance. 168.Option: The downregulation of Tim-3 on HTLV-1-specific CD8+ T cells is a direct consequence of high proviral load and viral replication.
Evidence: We observed a significant decrease in the frequency of Tim-3+ PD-1− expressing CD8+ and CD4+ T cells among HTLV-1 seropositive subjects (CD8+: median 8.01%, IQR 5.42–10.50; CD4+: median 4.3%, IQR 3.50–5.99) compared to HTLV-1 seronegative controls (CD8+ median 15.10%, IQR 10.50–17.60; CD4+: median 6.84%, IQR 5.74–7.85) (Figure 1A and B).

168.Option: The elevated expression of PD-1 on HTLV-1-specific CD8+ T cells in asymptomatic carriers indicates a protective immune response that prevents disease progression.
Evidence: the frequency of Tim-3− PD1+ T cells trended to an increase in subjects with HTLV-1 infection (CD8+: median 18.80%, IQR 10.42–24.90; CD4+: median 20.70%, IQR 13.6–25.35) compared to healthy uninfected controls (CD8+: median 9.22%, IQR 8.97–15.50; CD4+: median 13.60%, IQR 12.7–18.6) (Figure 1A and B).

Evidence: Programmed death receptor-1 (PD-1) is also known as another immune exhaustion biomarker expressed in chronic viral infections [28], [37], [38], [39], [40], [41], [42], [43].

Using linear regression analysis we observed no association between the frequency of Tim-3 or PD-1 expression on CD8+ T cells in HTLV-1 infected subjects and proviral load. (p = 0.68; r = 0.1043; or p = 0.89; r = −0.03202, respectively).
168.Rationale: While PD-1 is an exhaustion marker in general, the passage's data do not demonstrate that PD-1 expression on HTLV-1-specific CD8+ T cells is linked to impaired control of viral replication: PD-1 levels did not correlate with IFN-γ secretion for antigen-specific cells and there was no association between PD-1 (or Tim-3) expression and proviral load. Evidence: We observed a significant decrease in the frequency of Tim-3+ PD-1− expressing CD8+ and CD4+ T cells among HTLV-1 seropositive subjects (CD8+: median 8.01%, IQR 5.42–10.50; CD4+: median 4.3%, IQR 3.50–5.99) compared to HTLV-1 seronegative controls (CD8+ median 15.10%, IQR 10.50–17.60; CD4+: median 6.84%, IQR 5.74–7.85) (Figure 1A and B).

we observed a statistically significant inverse correlation between the frequency of Tim-3 on both Tax-specific as well as CMV-specific CD8+ T cells and the amount of IFN-γ secreted (r = −0.8982; P = 0.0046; r = 0.9710; P = 0.0028; Figure 3A).

that are thought to play a immunopathogenic role, either by release of neurotoxic cytokines, such as TNF-α and IFN-γ [23], [24], or by direct cytotoxicity.

168.Rationale: The passage reports an inverse correlation between Tim-3 frequency and IFN-γ secretion (a functional cytokine readout), but provides no direct measurements of T cell proliferation in these experiments. Evidence: No pathogenetic injury was found in the germarium, oocytes, trophocytes and follicular epithelium in the ZIKV-positive ovaries compared with the control ovaries.

which suggested that ZIKV may negatively influence fecundity but not oviposition of the infected mosquitoes.

the effective population transmission rate (minimum of 1.87%) was calculated in F1 adult tissues ... this result indicated that ZIKV can not only vertically transmit to F1 eggs but also persistently exist in F1 adults, even though the ratio was not high.
169.Rationale: The passage does report no histopathologic injury in infected ovaries, but it also reports a significant reduction in egg numbers at 33 dpi and states that ZIKV may negatively influence fecundity. Moreover, vertical transmission to F1 adults was demonstrated (with an effective population transmission rate), and F1 females could transmit to mice. Therefore one cannot conclude that vertical transmission has no impact on fitness or is negligible as a transmission route solely based on the absence of overt ovarian pathology.

169.Option: Even though the vertical transmission rates were low, the female mosquitoes infected via the congenital route horizontally transmitted ZIKV to immunocompetent mice through feeding.

the effective population transmission rate (minimum of 1.87%) was calculated in F1 adult tissues ... even though the ratio was not high.
thirteen C57BL/6 suckling mice were fed on by ZIKV-positive F1 females (Table 2).

oviposition rates were calculated, and no significant difference was detected, which suggested that ZIKV may negatively influence fecundity but not oviposition of the infected mosquitoes.
These results suggested that ZIKV infection did not prevent the growth and development of the offspring.

Evidence: the minimal filial infection rates in eggs from two gonotrophic cycles were 2.06% and 0.69%, without statistical significance (p>0.05).
the effective population transmission rate (minimum of 1.87%) was calculated in F1 adult tissues ... even though the ratio was not high.

170.Option: The low availability of diagnostic tests for rheumatic fever indicates that most patients with RHD in Uganda are being diagnosed at tertiary hospitals, which is the most efficient and cost-effective approach to case identification.
Evidence: Almost no facilities were able to perform the blood tests that are used to diagnose rheumatic fever; the exception was the erythrocyte sedimentation rate test, which was available at some health centers IV and hospitals (predominately private).
Only 63% of hospitals and 28% of health centers IV had a functional ultrasound machine, and only one hospital (7%) had an electrocardiography machine.

The proportions of facilities that had all the requisite inputs for primary prevention, secondary prevention, and RHD medical management were 1%, 2%, and 2%, respectively (Table D in S1 Text).
170.Rationale: The passage documents that diagnostic tests are scarce across facility levels (limited ultrasound, almost no blood tests, only one ECG), and that very few Ugandans with RHD have been identified by the health system. Although the Uganda Heart Institute is the sole public specialized cardiovascular center, the passage does not state that most patients are being diagnosed at tertiary hospitals, nor does it claim that such centralization is efficient or cost-effective. On the contrary, the evidence of scarce diagnostics and very low proportions of facilities with requisite inputs suggests under-detection rather than an efficient tertiary-centered case-identification system. Therefore the option's conclusions are not supported by the passage.

170.Option: The Ugandan health system’s readiness to prevent and treat RHD is currently low, and the country’s national RHD strategy should emphasize decentralization of diagnostic capability to the district level, integration of RHD education in health professional training, and strengthening of supply chains for essential medicines.
Evidence: The proportions of facilities that had all the requisite inputs for primary prevention, secondary prevention, and RHD medical management were 1%, 2%, and 2%, respectively (Table D in S1 Text).

Almost no facilities were able to perform the blood tests that are used to diagnose rheumatic fever; the exception was the erythrocyte sedimentation rate test, which was available at some health centers IV and hospitals (predominately private).

If the patient comes here with [RHD] and I personally don’t know the signs and symptoms, I just continue giving antibiotics… it takes [a] long [time] to be diagnosed… [with] heart disease." (HW 005 Wakiso)
I feel I should be trained about [rheumatic fever and RHD]… so that I can take care of [these patients]… I [am] confident [that I can] take care of them, but I need the knowledge" (HW 009 Lira)
170.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence that RHD-specific readiness is low (very small proportions of facilities with all necessary inputs, limited diagnostic capacity, limited availability of key medications/tests, and low provider training). The authors explicitly note that low availability can be remediated by reforms including decentralization of preventive care. Health worker quotations document a desire for training and note diagnostic/logistical gaps. Strengthening supply chains is implied by inconsistent availability of essential cardiac medicines and anticoagulation testing. Thus the recommendation to emphasize decentralization of diagnostics, integration of RHD education into health professional training, and strengthening medicine supply chains follows logically from the study findings and stated remediation strategies in the passage.

170.Option: The fact that health centers III have better RHD service readiness than health centers II suggests that the Ugandan health system is already capable of scaling up RHD prevention programs without additional reforms.
Evidence: Health centers III had higher RHD service readiness than health centers II (adjusted rate ratio 1.17, 95% CI: 1.02–1.35, p-value: 0.026), including inputs for both primary and secondary prevention.
The proportions of facilities that had all the requisite inputs for primary prevention, secondary prevention, and RHD medical management were 1%, 2%, and 2%, respectively (Table D in S1 Text).
Most respondents reported that their facilities had not undergone any training on management of pharyngitis in the past two years.

Low availability of RHD preventive interventions and treatments can be remediated by health system reforms including the decentralization of preventive care, formation of national control programs, and focused expansion of tertiary services.
170.Rationale: While the data show that health centers III perform statistically better than health centers II on RHD readiness, the absolute readiness across the system remains very low (only 1–2% of facilities had all requisite inputs for the different service domains) and training is largely absent. The passage also highlights the need for health system reforms (decentralization, national programs) to remediate low availability. Therefore, the observed difference between HCIII and HCII does not support the strong claim that the system is already capable of scaling up prevention programs without additional reforms; that inference is not supported by the overall evidence.

170.Option: The availability of benzathine penicillin G and amoxicillin across all health facility levels suggests that the Ugandan health system is well-prepared to deliver primary prevention for RHD.
