The proportions of facilities that had all the requisite inputs for primary prevention, secondary prevention, and RHD medical management were 1%, 2%, and 2%, respectively (Table D in S1 Text).

"If the patient comes here with [RHD] and I personally don’t know the signs and symptoms, I just continue giving antibiotics… it takes [a] long [time] to be diagnosed… [with] heart disease." (HW 005 Wakiso)
170.Rationale: Although the passage states that amoxicillin and benzathine penicillin G are widely available, primary prevention readiness requires more than drug availability: trained staff, diagnostic/clinical recognition of pharyngitis/rheumatic fever, and other requisite inputs. The passage documents very low levels of training on pharyngitis management, and only 1% of facilities had all requisite inputs for primary prevention. Health worker quotes indicate knowledge gaps leading to missed or delayed diagnosis. Therefore, the mere availability of antibiotics does not justify concluding the system is well-prepared to deliver primary prevention.

171.Option: The high viral titers detected in the lungs of intracranially inoculated hamsters suggest that the virus disseminates efficiently from the CNS to the lungs via hematogenous spread, mirroring the systemic spread seen in natural human infection.
Evidence: Despite direct inoculation into the CNS, high viral loads were detected in the lungs of animals that reached euthanasia criteria (Fig 2C and 2D).
However, virus titers in the lungs were lower than in the brains.
These regions of positivity did not extend beyond one or two alveolar sacs.
Together, these data suggest that NiV can disseminate out of the CNS after intracranial inoculation.

Since lesions in Syrian hamsters inoculated via the intranasal or intraperitoneal route are similar to those described in humans [10] and African green monkeys [19–21], the lesions observed in intracranially inoculated hamsters do not represent NiV lesions observed during natural infection.
171.Rationale: The passage does show that virus can disseminate from the CNS to the lungs after intracranial inoculation and that lung viral loads were detected, but it also notes lung titers were lower than brain and lung positivity was limited ("did not extend beyond one or two alveolar sacs"). Crucially, the authors state that lesions in the intracranial model do not represent those seen in natural infection. The passage does not provide evidence for the route of dissemination (e.g., hematogenous spread) nor that this pattern mirrors systemic spread in natural human infection. Therefore the specific inference about efficient hematogenous spread mirroring human systemic infection is not supported by the passage.

171.Option: Although the intracranial inoculation model induces consistent, lethal neurological disease, the lesions observed are not consistent with those seen in NiV-infected patients or in animals infected via natural routes, suggesting limited utility for studying NiV neuropathogenesis despite its potential for testing antiviral efficacy in the CNS.
Evidence: We found that intracranial inoculation in the cerebellomedullary cistern induces a rapidly lethal neurological disease with 100% of the animals showing neurological signs such as seizures, head tilt, paralysis, and ataxia.

The histopathological changes observed upon intracranial inoculation (Fig 6A and S1 Table) are in stark contrast with those observed in hamsters inoculated via the intraperitoneal or intranasal routes as previously reported [12,13,18].

Since lesions in Syrian hamsters inoculated via the intranasal or intraperitoneal route are similar to those described in humans [10] and African green monkeys [19–21], the lesions observed in intracranially inoculated hamsters do not represent NiV lesions observed during natural infection.
Such a model would also allow the efficacy testing of potential antivirals when administered once virus may have already reached the CNS.
171.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that intracranial inoculation produces uniform, lethal neurological disease, but also states the resulting histopathology is markedly different from that seen after natural routes of infection and from human cases. The authors themselves note the model does not represent NiV lesions observed during natural infection while suggesting it could be useful to test antivirals when virus is already in the CNS. Thus the inference that the model has limited utility for studying natural neuropathogenesis but may be useful for CNS antiviral testing is directly supported by the passage.

171.Option: The presence of neutrophils in the brains of intracranially inoculated hamsters indicates a robust innate immune response that is characteristic of Nipah virus encephalitis in humans.
Evidence: One of the unusual features of neuropathological lesions observed upon intracranial inoculation is the presence of large number of neutrophils.
The presence of neutrophils is often caused by bacterial infection. However, no bacteria were visible in tissue slides and neutrophils were not observed in intracranially inoculated mock animals.
Thus, the neutrophilic infiltrate observed in intracranially inoculated hamsters was due to NiV infection.

Encephalitis in central nervous system (CNS) specimens from patients who succumbed to NiV infection in Malaysia was characterized by vasculitis accompanied by thrombosis, perivascular cuffing, and necrosis [10].

Since lesions in Syrian hamsters inoculated via the intranasal or intraperitoneal route are similar to those described in humans [10] and African green monkeys [19–21], the lesions observed in intracranially inoculated hamsters do not represent NiV lesions observed during natural infection.
171.Rationale: The passage documents a notable neutrophilic infiltrate in intracranially inoculated hamsters and rules out bacterial contamination, showing the neutrophils are virus-associated. However, it also describes human NiV encephalitis pathology (vasculitis, thrombosis, perivascular cuffing, necrosis) without mentioning neutrophil predominance, and explicitly states the intracranial lesions do not represent natural infection. Therefore one cannot infer from the passage that neutrophil presence in this model is characteristic of human Nipah encephalitis; that claim is unsupported by the provided text.

171.Option: The intracranial inoculation model is ideal for studying the early stages of Nipah virus encephalitis because it replicates the initial neurological signs and immune response seen in human patients.
Evidence: Animals inoculated with 1–3125 TCID50 NiV-M uniformly displayed severe neurological signs and reached endpoint criteria within 2–6 days post inoculation (dpi).
Animals requiring euthanasia primarily displayed neurological signs such as seizures, torticollis, paralysis, and ataxia.
Only 6 out of 9 assayed cytokines and chemokines could be detected in brain samples... none of these differences were statistically significant (Fig 4).

The histopathological changes observed upon intracranial inoculation (Fig 6A and S1 Table) are in stark contrast with those observed in hamsters inoculated via the intraperitoneal or intranasal routes as previously reported [12,13,18].

Since lesions in Syrian hamsters inoculated via the intranasal or intraperitoneal route are similar to those described in humans [10] and African green monkeys [19–21], the lesions observed in intracranially inoculated hamsters do not represent NiV lesions observed during natural infection.
171.Rationale: While the model reproducibly produces rapid neurological signs, the passage reports a limited and not statistically significant cytokine response in brain tissue and emphasizes that the histopathology differs substantially from that seen after natural infection and in human cases. Given these discrepancies in pathology and immune profile, the passage does not support the stronger claim that the model 'replicates the initial neurological signs and immune response' seen in humans, so calling it 'ideal' for studying early human disease is not supported.

171.Option: The rapid progression of neurological disease in intracranially inoculated hamsters is due to the direct breach of the blood-brain barrier by the virus, which mimics the mechanism of CNS entry observed in human Nipah virus infection.
Evidence: We directly targeted the CNS of hamsters through intracranial inoculation with NiV in the cerebellomedullary cistern.

The procedure was as follows: ... A 30G needle was inserted into the skin to a depth of 4 mm at the back of the foramen magnum and the inoculum was slowly injected into the cerebellomedullary cistern.

We found that intracranial inoculation in the cerebellomedullary cistern induces a rapidly lethal neurological disease with 100% of the animals showing neurological signs such as seizures, head tilt, paralysis, and ataxia.

Since lesions in Syrian hamsters inoculated via the intranasal or intraperitoneal route are similar to those described in humans [10] and African green monkeys [19–21], the lesions observed in intracranially inoculated hamsters do not represent NiV lesions observed during natural infection.
171.Rationale: The passage makes clear that investigators directly injected virus into the CNS (i.e., bypassing barriers experimentally), which explains rapid disease in this model. However, the authors do not claim that this mimics the mechanism of CNS entry in humans; indeed they emphasize the intracranial lesions do not reflect natural infection. The passage contains no evidence that rapid progression is due to a virus-mediated breach of the blood-brain barrier during natural infection or that the intracranial route models human CNS entry, so the causal claim and presumed mimicry of human mechanism are not supported.

172.Option: The ability of R. felis to infect both ticks and mosquitoes via cofeeding suggests that the pathogen has evolved to exploit multiple arthropod feeding strategies, including capillary feeding and dermal feeding, to ensure transmission across diverse hosts.
Evidence: cofeeding bioassays were employed to examine rickettsial transmission from infected cat fleas (C. felis) to ticks (D. variabilis) and mosquitoes (Anopheles quadrimaculatus) on a vertebrate host.
More recently, in sub-Saharan African countries where R. felis has been reported as an emerging cause of unknown fever, R. felis has been detected in mosquitoes [3,15,16].
laboratory studies have demonstrated R. felis was vertically transmitted by the American dog tick, Dermacentor variabilis, when adult ticks were exposed via capillary feeding [14].
172.Rationale: The passage documents that R. felis can be transmitted via cofeeding to both ticks and mosquitoes and that capillary feeding has been used in laboratory exposure of ticks. However, the option makes an evolutionary claim — that the pathogen "has evolved to exploit multiple arthropod feeding strategies... to ensure transmission" — which goes beyond the experimental observations. The data demonstrate capability of transmission across vector types under experimental conditions but do not provide evidence of evolutionary adaptation or of deliberate exploitation of distinct feeding modes in nature. Therefore this stronger evolutionary inference cannot be derived solely from the Passage Body.

172.Option: The detection of R. felis in field-collected male and unfed female mosquitoes suggests that vertical transmission of R. felis occurs naturally in mosquito populations, contributing to the persistence of the pathogen in the environment.
Evidence: Detection of R. felis in male mosquitoes in China and unfed adult mosquitoes in the United States suggests vertical transmission in nature [17,18].
Nucleic acid from R. felis and related R. felis-like organisms (RFLOs) has been detected in other hematophagous arthropods, including more than 40 species of fleas, ticks, and mosquitoes [4,10].
172.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that detection of R. felis in male mosquitoes and unfed adult mosquitoes "suggests vertical transmission in nature." This statement supports the inference that vertical transmission likely occurs naturally in mosquito populations, which epidemiologically would contribute to environmental persistence of the agent. The option restates what the passage itself suggests, so it is derivable from the provided text.

172.Option: The absence of R. felis in control ticks cofed with uninfected fleas confirms that the pathogen is not capable of spontaneous or environmental contamination in laboratory settings.
Evidence: None of the ticks that cofed with uninfected fleas were positive for R. felis (Table 1).
A negative environmental control, a 1.7 ml microcentrifuge tube containing buffer(s) but no sample, was included in each DNA extraction procedure as a negative environmental control for qPCR.
172.Rationale: The Passage Body reports that control ticks cofed with uninfected fleas were negative and that negative extraction controls were included. These statements demonstrate effective negative controls within the experiments, but they do not allow the broader conclusion that the pathogen is incapable of any spontaneous or environmental contamination in laboratory settings. Absence of contamination in these controlled procedures supports the validity of these experiments but does not 'confirm' impossibility of contamination under all laboratory conditions. Thus the stronger claim in the option is not supported by the passage.

172.Option: The high rickettsial load in cat fleas compared to mosquitoes during cofeeding indicates that mosquitoes are less competent vectors for R. felis than cat fleas, limiting their role in disease transmission.
Evidence: donor cat fleas exposed to R. felis prior to cofeeding consistently had significantly higher rickettsial loads than the mosquitoes with which they were cofed (Fig 2).
Of the cofeeding An. quadrimaculatus, 31.6% (6/19), 40% (8/20), and 60% (12/20) were R. felis-positive on days 1, 2, and 3 post-exposure, respectively.
At days 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, and 12 dpe. At each time point, 100% of blood fed mosquitoes had detectable R. felis in whole body samples, compared to 0% of the control (mock-exposed) individuals.
Combined, the data indicate that An. quadrimaculatus are susceptible to rickettsial infection via cofeeding with infected cat fleas.
172.Rationale: While the Passage Body reports that donor cat fleas had significantly higher rickettsial loads than cofed mosquitoes, it also shows that mosquitoes can and do acquire R. felis (with substantial proportions infected and maintenance of infection over 12 days after oral exposure). Vector 'competence' encompasses multiple factors (infection rate, ability to maintain and transmit infection to vertebrate hosts, etc.) and cannot be inferred solely from lower rickettsial load in mosquitoes in these cofeeding assays. The passage does not provide data on mosquitoes transmitting R. felis onward or on comparative transmission efficiency to hosts. Therefore the conclusion that mosquitoes are less competent and thus limited in role is not supported by the provided evidence.

172.Option: R. felis-infected cat fleas can transmit the agent to naïve vectors via cofeeding on a vertebrate host, suggesting a mechanism for inter-class and inter-order transmission that may contribute to the widespread distribution of R. felis in hematophagous arthropods.
Evidence: cofeeding bioassays were employed to examine rickettsial transmission from infected cat fleas (C. felis) to ticks (D. variabilis) and mosquitoes (Anopheles quadrimaculatus) on a vertebrate host.
The data suggest R. felis-infected cat fleas transmit rickettsiae to other hematophagous vectors via cofeeding on a vertebrate host.
R. felis was detected in 75% (15/20) D. variabilis nymphs that were forcibly removed from the host 24 hrs after cofeeding.
Naïve An. quadrimaculatus were able to acquire infection through cofeeding with R. felis-infected cat fleas.
172.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct experimental evidence that R. felis-infected cat fleas can transmit rickettsiae to naïve ticks and mosquitoes via cofeeding on a vertebrate host, with substantial proportions of recipient arthropods testing positive. The authors explicitly conclude that their data suggest transmission to other hematophagous vectors via cofeeding. This supports the epidemiological inference that cofeeding can serve as a mechanism for inter-order (fleas to ticks and fleas to mosquitoes) transmission, which plausibly contributes to the observed widespread detection of R. felis across diverse hematophagous arthropods.

173.Option: Dengue fever is a major cause of severe pregnancy outcomes in pregnant women admitted to hospital in Vientiane, Laos, and its impact may be exacerbated by fluid balance changes during pregnancy.
Evidence: Among those (132, 53%) who had a single disease, dengue was the most commonly found (76 (30%)),

Of 76 (30%) patients with dengue as a single disease, 71/76 (93%) had positive NS1 and/or PCR assays and, 40/58 (68%) patients had seroconversion; ... Among 76 patients with dengue, 21 (28%) had severe outcome, including 1 maternal death, 6 miscarriages, 2 stillbirths, 9 preterm births, 1 congenital abnormality (ventricular septal defect (VSD)) and 1 vertical transmission (Table 4).
173.Rationale: The passage supports the first clause: dengue was the most common single laboratory-confirmed disease among febrile pregnant women admitted (76/250) and a substantial fraction (21/76; 28%) experienced severe pregnancy outcomes. However, the passage contains no data or discussion about pregnancy-related fluid balance changes exacerbating dengue severity. The assertion that impact "may be exacerbated by fluid balance changes during pregnancy" is not supported by any quoted text and therefore cannot be derived from the passage.

173.Option: Dengue fever in pregnancy is primarily responsible for the high maternal mortality ratio in Laos, as evidenced by the 1 maternal death among 76 dengue cases.
Evidence: Although, Laos achieved 75% reductions in maternal mortality ratio (MMR) from 1990–2015, it still has the highest estimated MMR in mainland Southeast Asia (197/100,000 live births) [1,2]. Direct causes such as haemorrhage and eclampsia are the most common reasons [37].

Among 76 patients with dengue, 21 (28%) had severe outcome, including 1 maternal death, 6 miscarriages, 2 stillbirths, 9 preterm births, 1 congenital abnormality (ventricular septal defect (VSD)) and 1 vertical transmission (Table 4).
173.Rationale: While the study reports one maternal death among 76 dengue cases, the passage also states that direct obstetric causes (haemorrhage and eclampsia) are the most common reasons for maternal mortality in Laos. From a single maternal death in this hospital-based series one cannot infer that dengue is primarily responsible for the high national MMR. The passage does not provide population-level attribution or comparative data to support the strong causal claim about national MMR.

173.Option: Congenital abnormalities in infants born to mothers with dengue are primarily due to the virus itself rather than maternal fever, based on the evidence from this study.
Evidence: Among 76 patients with dengue, 21 (28%) had severe outcome, including 1 maternal death, 6 miscarriages, 2 stillbirths, 9 preterm births, 1 congenital abnormality (ventricular septal defect (VSD)) and 1 vertical transmission (Table 4).
173.Rationale: The passage documents a single congenital abnormality (VSD) among 76 dengue cases but provides no evidence attributing causation to dengue virus versus maternal fever or other factors. There are no analyses or data distinguishing causes of congenital anomalies. Therefore the specific causal inference that congenital abnormalities are primarily due to the virus rather than maternal fever is not supported by the study data.

173.Option: The study provides definitive evidence that dengue fever increases the risk of severe pregnancy outcomes in all pregnant women, regardless of gestational age or prior immunity.
Evidence: We recruited all pregnant women admitted with tympanic temperature ≥37.5°C who provided written informed consent.

Of 76 (30%) patients with dengue as a single disease, 71/76 (93%) had positive NS1 and/or PCR assays and, 40/58 (68%) patients had seroconversion; 16/57 (28%) had primary dengue and 41/57 (72%) had secondary dengue.

Among 76 patients with dengue, 21 (28%) had severe outcome, including 1 maternal death, 6 miscarriages, 2 stillbirths, 9 preterm births, 1 congenital abnormality (ventricular septal defect (VSD)) and 1 vertical transmission (Table 4).
173.Rationale: The study documents that hospitalized pregnant women with laboratory-confirmed dengue experienced severe pregnancy outcomes at appreciable frequency (21/76). However, the study is hospital-based and restricted to febrile, admitted pregnant women and does not provide population-based risk estimates or stratified analyses by gestational age or by immunity status sufficient to support a definitive, blanket statement that dengue increases risk in all pregnant women regardless of gestational age or prior immunity. Thus the universal and definitive claim is not derivable from the presented data.

173.Option: The high frequency of dengue in pregnant women admitted with fever suggests that dengue is the most common cause of fever in the general population of Vientiane, Laos.
Evidence: Among those (132, 53%) who had a single disease, dengue was the most commonly found (76 (30%)),
We recruited all pregnant women admitted with tympanic temperature ≥37.5°C who provided written informed consent.
Most (218, 87%) were from Vientiane City,
173.Rationale: The passage shows dengue was the most common laboratory-confirmed cause among this cohort of febrile pregnant women admitted to two hospitals in Vientiane. However, these data are restricted to hospitalized pregnant women with fever and cannot be extrapolated directly to the general (non-pregnant) population of Vientiane. The study design and sampling frame do not provide evidence about the cause distribution of fever in the general population, so the general-population inference is not supported by the passage.

174.Option: The 2011–2012 cluster of closely related genotype 1.1 isolates likely resulted from a single introduction of a new S. Typhi strain from a neighboring country during the period of hyperendemic transmission.
Evidence: A cluster of closely related genotype 1.1 S. Typhi isolates was detected in the years 2011 (1 isolate) and 2012 (19 isolates). These isolates also exhibit lower genetic variation (median SNP distance of 8) than those observed among the two 1980s 1.1 isolates (119 SNPs).
The intermingling of historical and recent sequences suggests that the 2010s isolates originated from progenitors within the same pool of S. Typhi circulating in the 1980s.
The S. Typhi isolates from Santiago of genotypes 3.5, 2, 1.1 and 1.2.1 clustered together and were genetically distinct from those sequenced previously from other countries.
174.Rationale: The option claims a single introduction from a neighboring country during the hyperendemic period. The passage, however, indicates the 2010s isolates are phylogenetically linked to the local 1980s pool ("originated from progenitors within the same pool of S. Typhi circulating in the 1980s") and that Santiago isolates are genetically distinct from isolates sequenced from other countries. Thus the evidence favors a local origin (e.g., persistence/carrier-related source) rather than a recent single importation from a neighboring country. Therefore this option cannot be derived from the passage.

174.Option: The persistence of ancient S. Typhi clades in Santiago, Chile, despite the interruption of hyperendemic transmission, suggests that asymptomatic typhoid carriers are likely responsible for ongoing sporadic cases.
Evidence: we describe a potential carrier-related cluster of the “ancient” 1.1 clade, and the identification of a new variant of plasmid pHCM2 carried by 2 isolates of another ancient genotype (1.2.1).

The current rare sporadic cases of typhoid in Santiago include illnesses among: travellers who visit countries with high incidence rates of typhoid; immigrants from typhoid-endemic countries; and transmission from Chilean chronic carriers who were originally infected decades earlier (pre-1991) during the era of hyperendemic transmission.
The intermingling of historical and recent sequences suggests that the 2010s isolates originated from progenitors within the same pool of S. Typhi circulating in the 1980s.

The twenty isolates from 2011–2012 showed an interesting cluster with a median SNP distance of 8 SNPs which is unlikely to have evolved among acute cases, where the mutation rate is calculated to be < 1 SNP/genome/year, in one year.
174.Rationale: The passage explicitly notes a "potential carrier-related cluster" of the ancient 1.1 clade and states that current sporadic cases include transmission from Chilean chronic carriers infected decades earlier. The phylogenetic intermingling of historical and recent isolates and the low SNP diversity in the 2011–2012 cluster—too low to arise rapidly among acute cases—support the epidemiologic inference that prolonged asymptomatic carriage (chronic carriers) is a plausible source of ongoing sporadic cases. This conclusion is directly supported by multiple statements in the passage.

174.Option: The detection of a novel variant of plasmid pHCM2 in two genotype 1.2.1 isolates from Santiago suggests that this plasmid may play a role in the persistence of S. Typhi in the human host.
Evidence: Two pHCM2 plasmids were found among our isolates from genotype 1.2.1; one in a 1984 isolate (ERR2710544) and the other from 2013 (ERR4289185). These plasmids were highly similar to each other, but significantly different from the pHCM2 found on S. Typhi reference CT18 (accession no: AL513384), and from another novel pHCM2 found in a 1973 isolate from Thailand [37]. The pHCM2 sequences described here differed by >2000 SNPs (98.4% nucleotide identity, 89% coverage) from the reference (Fig 4).
pHCM2 plasmids are found in ~15% of S. Typhi and are generally highly conserved (Fig 4A).
174.Rationale: While the passage documents the presence of novel pHCM2 variants in two isolates and notes their divergence from the reference, it does not provide any data linking these plasmids to persistence in the human host (no functional, epidemiologic, or mechanistic evidence is presented). Detection of a plasmid variant alone does not allow the inference that the plasmid plays a role in persistence. Therefore the causal claim in this option is not supported by the passage.

174.Option: The global dominance of the H58 S. Typhi clade in other industrialized nations suggests that imported cases are the primary source of sporadic typhoid in Santiago, Chile.
Evidence: The majority of publicly available sequenced isolates come from hyperendemic regions in South Asia and East Africa where H58 (4.3.1) genotypes dominate [17].
These include three previously described importations of isolates belonging to H58 genotypes 4.3.1.1 and 4.3.1.2 [35].
The S. Typhi isolates from Santiago of genotypes 3.5, 2, 1.1 and 1.2.1 clustered together and were genetically distinct from those sequenced previously from other countries.

The current rare sporadic cases of typhoid in Santiago include illnesses among: travellers who visit countries with high incidence rates of typhoid; immigrants from typhoid-endemic countries; and transmission from Chilean chronic carriers who were originally infected decades earlier (pre-1991) during the era of hyperendemic transmission.
174.Rationale: Although H58 dominates many global collections, the passage shows that Santiago isolates are largely of other genotypes and cluster together distinct from international isolates. Only three H58 importations are described, and the passage explicitly lists multiple sources for sporadic cases including local chronic carriers, travellers, and immigrants. The evidence does not support the stronger claim that imported cases are the primary source of sporadic typhoid in Santiago. Thus this option is not supported by the passage.

174.Option: The low genetic diversity observed in the 2011–2012 genotype 1.1 cluster indicates that the S. Typhi population in Santiago has undergone a recent bottleneck event, leading to the loss of most ancestral genotypes.
Evidence: These isolates also exhibit lower genetic variation (median SNP distance of 8) than those observed among the two 1980s 1.1 isolates (119 SNPs).

The twenty isolates from 2011–2012 showed an interesting cluster with a median SNP distance of 8 SNPs which is unlikely to have evolved among acute cases, where the mutation rate is calculated to be < 1 SNP/genome/year, in one year.

The genomic population structure of S. Typhi in Santiago appeared highly consistent between the hyperendemic and post-hyperendemic eras (Fig 1). Both rare “ancient” genotypes 1.1 and 1.2.1, as well as more commonly found genotypes 2 and 3.5, were frequently present across both study periods.
174.Rationale: While the cluster shows low diversity consistent with a recent common ancestor or clonal expansion, the passage provides no evidence of a population-wide bottleneck causing loss of most ancestral genotypes. In fact, the passage emphasizes that the overall genomic population structure remained "highly consistent" across eras and that multiple genotypes persisted. The low diversity in one genotype cluster is more plausibly explained by a recent clonal expansion (for example from a carrier) rather than a broad bottleneck eliminating ancestral diversity across the population. Therefore the specific bottleneck conclusion cannot be derived from the provided text.

175.Option: Ergot alkaloids are efficacious modulators of planarian regeneration and motility, and their structural-activity relationships suggest potential for developing novel chemotherapeutics targeting parasite muscle function.
Evidence: Intriguingly, serotonergic screens highlight ergot alkaloids as a class of compounds that potently and penetrantly miscued planarian regeneration and schistosomule muscle function, with structure activity insight from active compounds highlighting modifications of the ergot scaffold predictive for flatworm efficacy.

This broad efficacy of ergot alkaloids as a chemical class permitted structural-activity insight into features associated with specific polarity effects: for example, all ergots that caused bipolarity were either alkylated on the indole nitrogen or halogenated at the adjacent 2-position (Fig 3A).

In schistosome contractility assays, the compounds that caused planarian bipolarity all inhibited schistosomule motility (just like PZQ), whereas the compounds that inhibited planarian head regeneration caused the opposite effect, stimulating contractile activity (Fig 3B).
Therefore, we contend that the ergot alkaloid scaffold merits further exploration to yield novel chemotherapeutics with selective efficacy against parasite musculature.
175.Rationale: The passage directly states that ergot alkaloids potently affect planarian regenerative polarity and schistosomule muscle function and that structural-activity relationships (e.g., indole N-alkylation or 2-halogenation) correlate with specific effects. The authors explicitly conclude the ergot scaffold merits further exploration to yield novel chemotherapeutics targeting parasite musculature. Thus, this inference is directly supported by multiple statements in the passage.

175.Option: The structural-activity relationships identified in planarian regenerative screens are sufficient to guarantee the development of parasite-specific drugs with minimal off-target effects in humans.
Evidence: This broad efficacy of ergot alkaloids as a chemical class permitted structural-activity insight into features associated with specific polarity effects: for example, all ergots that caused bipolarity were either alkylated on the indole nitrogen or halogenated at the adjacent 2-position (Fig 3A).
Therefore, we contend that the ergot alkaloid scaffold merits further exploration to yield novel chemotherapeutics with selective efficacy against parasite musculature.
Similarly, alkylation of ergot derivatives at the N1 position also can cause decreased receptor activation yielding compounds that act as 5-HT receptor antagonists [30].
In contrast, all ergots that inhibited head regeneration lacked such modifications (Fig 3A).
175.Rationale: While the passage documents structural-activity relationships and suggests the ergot scaffold is worthy of further exploration, it does not claim these SAR findings are sufficient to guarantee development of parasite-specific drugs or minimal off-target effects in humans. Indeed, the passage notes ergots can act as 5-HT agonists or antagonists in other systems, implying possible activity on host bioaminergic receptors. The authors call for further exploration rather than asserting any guarantee of parasite specificity or safety, so the strong claim in the option is not supported by the passage.

175.Option: Since PZQ’s mechanism of action is unresolved, the discovery that ergot alkaloids mimic its effects in planarians suggests that PZQ’s primary target is the serotonergic system in flatworms.
Evidence: Development of alternative therapies to PZQ has been hampered by the fact that the mechanism of action of PZQ remains unresolved and rationally designed derivatives of PZQ typically prove less efficacious [7,14,15].

In planarian regenerative screens, the bipolarizing efficacy of PZQ depends on a coupling of voltage-operated Ca2+ channels to bioaminergic signals [20], which likely regulate polarity signaling from flatworm muscle cells to coordinate regenerative outcomes [21].
These data show that PZQ action mimics the bipolarizing ability of serotonergic blockers, and is opposed by 5-HT agonists.
175.Rationale: The passage states PZQ's mechanism is unresolved and shows that PZQ mimics effects of serotonergic blockers and is functionally opposed by 5-HT agonists, indicating an interaction with bioaminergic signaling. However, these observations do not demonstrate that PZQ's primary molecular target is the serotonergic system; the passage also implicates coupling between voltage-operated Ca2+ channels and bioaminergic signals. The data are consistent with involvement of serotonergic pathways but are insufficient to conclude PZQ's primary target is serotonergic receptors.

175.Option: Because ergot alkaloids are already used clinically for migraine, obstetrics, and Parkinson’s disease, they are likely to be safe and effective for treating schistosomiasis without requiring further development.
Evidence: Ergot alkaloids are a historically important class of compounds that realize their effects because of the close structural similarity of the ergoline scaffold to bioaminergic transmitters.
This broad efficacy of ergot alkaloids as a chemical class permitted structural-activity insight into features associated with specific polarity effects...
Therefore, we contend that the ergot alkaloid scaffold merits further exploration to yield novel chemotherapeutics with selective efficacy against parasite musculature.
Similarly, alkylation of ergot derivatives at the N1 position also can cause decreased receptor activation yielding compounds that act as 5-HT receptor antagonists [30].
175.Rationale: The passage emphasizes that ergot alkaloids affect bioaminergic receptors and that the scaffold merits further exploration for antiparasitic chemotherapeutics. It does not claim ergot compounds are already safe and effective against schistosomiasis without additional development. Moreover, because ergots interact with host bioaminergic receptors (as agonists or antagonists in other systems), their clinical repurposing would require further development and safety assessment. The option's assertion of likely safety and no further development is not supported by the passage.

175.Option: The functional antagonism between serotonergic signaling and PZQ action suggests that PZQ may directly target flatworm bioaminergic receptors as an ergomimetic, which could be confirmed through heterologous expression of platyhelminth GpCRs.
Evidence: This effect is shown to be unique to serotonin (5-HT), and highlights the importance of characterizing serotonergic receptors to identify 5-HT blockers that could potentiate, or phenocopy, PZQ action.
These data show that PZQ action mimics the bipolarizing ability of serotonergic blockers, and is opposed by 5-HT agonists.

Notably, bioaminergic receptors that respond to different ligands ... human 5-HT sequences present no more than one polar residue ([27], Table 1). The planarian sequences also conform to this principle ...

We do note that both the planarian receptor (S7.4, DjSER-7 [22]) and a schistosome S7-like receptor have been successfully deorphanized following heterologous expression and shown to respond to 5-HT [25,26].
175.Rationale: The passage documents functional antagonism between serotonergic signaling and PZQ action and notes that heterologous expression has been used to deorphanize planarian and schistosome 5-HT receptors. However, it does not provide direct evidence that PZQ itself directly targets bioaminergic G protein-coupled receptors as an ergomimetic. The observed antagonism is compatible with multiple mechanisms (e.g., modulation of coupling between Ca2+ channels and bioaminergic signaling) and does not demonstrate direct binding of PZQ to GpCRs. While heterologous expression of platyhelminth GpCRs could be an appropriate experimental test, the claim that the data suggest PZQ may directly target these receptors is not a conclusion that can be definitively derived from the passage.

176.Option: The decision tree algorithm for prognostication achieved high positive predictive value in low-prevalence settings, making it ideal for identifying severe dengue cases in non-outbreak periods.
Evidence: The overall error rate using k-fold crossover validation analysis was 20.5%, with a sensitivity of 78.2% and specificity of 80.2% (Figure 4B).

All 3 DHF cases were correctly classified using this algorithm, one into the group with a platelet count of 108,000 mm3 or less, the other two into the group with pre-existing anti-dengue IgG antibodies.
176.Rationale: The passage provides sensitivity, specificity and overall error rate for the prognostication algorithm, and notes correct classification of three DHF cases, but it does not provide data on positive predictive value (PPV) or performance stratified by disease prevalence. PPV depends on prevalence and no analyses or statements about low-prevalence settings or non-outbreak periods are given. Therefore the claim that the algorithm "achieved high positive predictive value in low-prevalence settings" and is "ideal for identifying severe dengue cases in non-outbreak periods" cannot be derived from the passage.

176.Option: The decision tree model for dengue diagnosis is more effective than traditional laboratory tests such as RT-PCR and ELISA in early detection of dengue infection.
Evidence: Definitive early dengue diagnosis thus requires laboratory tests and those suitable for use at this stage of illness are either costly, such as RT-PCR for dengue; not sufficiently rapid, such as virus isolation; or undergoing field trials, such as ELISA for NS1 protein of dengue virus [8],[9].

we show here that a decision tree approach can be useful to develop an intuitive diagnostic algorithm, using clinical and haematological parameters, that is able to distinguish dengue from non-dengue disease in the first 72 hours of illness.
The overall error rate estimated after k-fold cross validation was 15.7%, with a sensitivity and specificity of 71.2% and 90.1%, respectively (Figure 2B).
176.Rationale: The passage describes limitations of laboratory tests (cost, speed, availability) and reports performance metrics for the decision tree diagnostic algorithm. However, it does not present any head-to-head comparison showing that the decision tree is more effective than RT-PCR or ELISA in early detection. No direct comparative effectiveness data are provided, so superiority of the decision tree over laboratory tests cannot be concluded from the passage.

176.Option: The use of a decision tree approach can differentiate dengue from non-dengue febrile illness and predict disease outcome using simple haematological and virological parameters obtained within the first 72 hours of illness.
Evidence: we show here that a decision tree approach can be useful to develop an intuitive diagnostic algorithm, using clinical and haematological parameters, that is able to distinguish dengue from non-dengue disease in the first 72 hours of illness.

The resulting diagnostic algorithm is shown in Figure 1. The first splitting parameter is a platelet count of 196,000/mm3 blood or less followed by the total white cell or lymphocyte counts, body temperature, haematocrit or neutrophil count and another platelet count at presentation.
For the EDEN cohort, we used a platelet count of less than 50,000/mm3 on days 5 to 7 of illness as a marker of severe disease.

The best performing decision algorithm made used of platelet count, the crossover value (Ct) of the real-time RT-PCR for dengue viral RNA (a marker for viremia levels) and the presence of anti-dengue IgG antibodies, as the first, second and third splitting parameters, respectively (Figure 3). All 3 parameters were obtained from the first visit.
The overall error rate estimated after k-fold cross validation was 15.7%, with a sensitivity and specificity of 71.2% and 90.1%, respectively (Figure 2B).
The overall error rate using k-fold crossover validation analysis was 20.5%, with a sensitivity of 78.2% and specificity of 80.2% (Figure 4B).
176.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that a decision tree can distinguish dengue from non-dengue within the first 72 hours using routinely obtainable clinical and haematological parameters (platelets, WBC, lymphocytes, temperature, haematocrit, neutrophils). It also describes a prognostic tree using platelet count, RT-PCR Ct (viremia marker) and anti-dengue IgG obtained at the first visit to predict severity, and reports validation performance metrics for both diagnostic and prognostic models. These statements directly support the inference in the option.

176.Option: The use of a decision tree approach allows for the integration of dynamic laboratory parameters into a single predictive model, enabling real-time clinical decision-making during dengue outbreaks.
Evidence: we show here that a decision tree approach can be useful to develop an intuitive diagnostic algorithm, using clinical and haematological parameters, that is able to distinguish dengue from non-dengue disease in the first 72 hours of illness.

The best performing decision algorithm made used of platelet count, the crossover value (Ct) of the real-time RT-PCR for dengue viral RNA (a marker for viremia levels) and the presence of anti-dengue IgG antibodies, as the first, second and third splitting parameters, respectively (Figure 3). All 3 parameters were obtained from the first visit.

Evidence-based triage strategies that identify individuals likely to have dengue infection in the early stages of illness are needed to direct patient stratification in clinical investigations, management and healthcare resource planning.
176.Rationale: The passage supports that the decision tree integrates laboratory parameters (platelet count, RT-PCR Ct, IgG) into a predictive model and that such strategies are useful for early recognition, triage, management and resource planning. However, the specific claims that this enables "real-time clinical decision-making" and that it is applicable "during dengue outbreaks" are not explicitly stated or demonstrated. The passage does not present evidence about real-time deployment, operational implementation during outbreaks, or time-to-decision in outbreak settings. Therefore the full claim in the option is not directly derivable from the passage.

176.Option: The lack of evidence-based diagnostic algorithm for early dengue diagnosis as well as prognostic triage strategies limits effective patient management, use of healthcare resources and disease surveillance efforts.
Evidence: Dengue illness is often confused with other viral febrile states, confounding both clinical management [4]–[6] and disease surveillance for viral transmission prevention [7].

Definitive early dengue diagnosis thus requires laboratory tests and those suitable for use at this stage of illness are either costly, such as RT-PCR for dengue; not sufficiently rapid, such as virus isolation; or undergoing field trials, such as ELISA for NS1 protein of dengue virus [8],[9].

Furthermore, many dengue endemic places lack the necessary laboratory infrastructure or support [7] and thus a scheme for reliable clinical diagnosis, using data that can be obtained routinely, would be useful for early recognition of dengue fever, not only for case management but also for dengue surveillance.

Evidence-based triage strategies that identify individuals likely to have dengue infection in the early stages of illness are needed to direct patient stratification in clinical investigations, management and healthcare resource planning.
176.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes that early dengue is difficult to distinguish clinically, that definitive early laboratory diagnosis is limited by cost, speed and availability, and that many endemic areas lack laboratory infrastructure. It states a need for reliable clinical diagnosis and evidence-based triage strategies to guide case management, resource planning and surveillance. From these statements it is reasonable and directly supported to infer that the lack of such evidence-based algorithms limits effective patient management, healthcare resource use and disease surveillance.

177.Option: Surveillance methods with greater sensitivity and more targeted sampling strategies to better detect residual hotspots than the current TAS methodology will be required.
Evidence: It is acknowledged that the TAS methodology is resource-intensive and may also lack sensitivity in low-prevalence settings[5, 7].
Table 6 shows that alternative surveillance methods can identify evidence to support ongoing transmission in areas which passed TAS.

Where stated (n = 42), the most common approach to selecting a sampling location involved non-random methods, such as purposive or convenience sampling (n = 30, 71.4%). In most cases surveillance was conducted in response to identification of a hotspot of infection.
177.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that TAS can lack sensitivity in low-prevalence settings and that alternative surveillance methods have identified ongoing transmission in areas that passed TAS. It also notes that many studies used purposive or targeted (non-random) sampling in response to suspected hotspots. From these points, an epidemiological inference that future monitoring should use more sensitive methods and targeted sampling to detect residual hotspots is directly supported by the evidence presented.

177.Option: Because mosquito surveillance provides a snapshot of current infection and is less affected by the persistence of markers in the host, it should replace human-based surveillance in all post-validation settings.
Evidence: Overall, there was great variability in survey methods and results which limited comparisons.

Table 5 summarises studies which performed both human testing and xenomonitoring in the same geographical area. Overall, there was great variability in survey methods and results which limited comparisons.

There were also examples where human testing did not detect significant transmission but xenomonitoring did. For example, the study by Ramaiah et al. reported a mosquito infection rate of 4.7% of mosquitoes when a community survey performed concurrently found no evidence of human infection on ICT testing.
Table 5 ... Interpretation is also limited by the fact that there are currently no recommended species-specific Mosquito Infectivity Rate (MIR) thresholds for LF[8, 53].
Xenomonitoring surveys also appear to have utility in identifying hotspots, as in the case of Rao et al. (2018) who detected a MIR of 5.2% in an area which had recently passed TAS[38].
177.Rationale: While the passage documents that mosquito (xenomonitoring) surveys can detect transmission missed by human surveys and that xenomonitoring can be useful for hotspot detection, it also emphasizes great variability in results, limited comparability, and the absence of recommended MIR thresholds. The passage does not state that mosquito surveillance is universally superior or that it should replace human surveillance in all settings. Therefore the strong recommendation to replace human-based surveillance everywhere is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

177.Option: The use of antibody tests such as Wb123 Ab may be particularly useful in post-validation settings due to their early positivity and faster decay after infection clearance, making them more suitable for detecting current transmission than antigen tests.
Evidence: Compared to Binax Now or Alere ICT (the most commonly used tests at the time of most of these surveys) as the index test, Table 3 shows that antibody tests produce a higher proportion of positive results. Bm14Ab and Wb123Ab values are, on average, 5.1 and 6.7 times higher respectively than the corresponding BinaxNOW values, based on the median value of this ratio across the selected studies.
Og4C3Ag values are similar to BinaxNOW values in studies where both are used (median ratio = 0.95, range 0.2–1.6).
177.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that antibody tests (including Wb123 Ab) yield higher proportions of positive results than certain antigen tests in the surveyed studies. However, it does not provide any data or statements about the timing of antibody positivity relative to infection onset, nor about the decay kinetics of antibody markers after infection clearance. The specific claims that antibody tests are 'early positive' and 'faster decay after infection clearance'—and thus more suitable for detecting current transmission—are not supported by the text. Therefore this inference cannot be derived from the Passage Body.

177.Option: The increased risk of disease recrudescence in post-validation settings means that countries should immediately resume mass drug administration (MDA) upon detection of any positive case in surveillance.
Evidence: Following validation of elimination of LF as a public health problem, the WHO recommend that countries continue surveillance for LF to detect any possible recrudescence of infection but there are no clear recommendations on specific surveillance methods and thresholds to be used[4, 6].
Table 6 shows that alternative surveillance methods can identify evidence to support ongoing transmission in areas which passed TAS.
177.Rationale: The passage states WHO recommends continued surveillance for possible recrudescence but explicitly notes there are no clear recommendations on surveillance methods or thresholds. While alternative methods sometimes detect transmission after a passed TAS, the passage does not provide guidance that any single positive result should trigger immediate resumption of MDA. Without defined thresholds or programmatic recommendations in the text, the policy-level statement that countries should immediately resume MDA upon any positive surveillance finding cannot be derived from the passage.

178.Option: The current WHO monitoring protocol for LF elimination is sufficient to support the detection of system-level changes in parasite dynamics, as it aligns with the time points at which significant reductions in model uncertainty occur.
Evidence: This protocol proposes that infection data be collected in sentinel sites before the first round of MDA to establish baseline conditions, no sooner than 6 months following the third round of MDA, and no sooner than 6 months following the fifth MDA to assess whether transmission has been interrupted (defined as reduction of mf prevalence to below 1% in a population) [44, 51].

To be managerially relevant to current WHO-specified LF intervention surveillance efforts, we evaluated the usefulness of infection data collected in these sites at the time points proposed by the WHO monitoring framework in carrying out the present assessment [44].

data streams including post-MDA 5 mf survey data (scenarios 3 and 4) reduced model uncertainty (based on both the variance and entropy measures) most compared to data streams containing only baseline and/or post-MDA 3 mf survey data (scenarios 1 and 2) (Table 2).
scenario 3, which includes baseline, post-MDA 3, and post-MDA 5 data, was shown to most often reduce model uncertainty the greatest.
178.Rationale: The passage states the WHO monitoring time points (baseline, post-MDA3, post-MDA5) and explicitly reports that data streams including the post-MDA5 survey (i.e., the WHO-recommended later monitoring point) reduced model uncertainty the most, with scenario 3 (which follows WHO timing) most often producing the greatest reduction. This supports the inference that the WHO protocol aligns with time points at which significant reductions in model uncertainty occur and is therefore sufficient for detecting system-level changes for the modelling purposes assessed.

178.Option: The most accurate predictions of LF elimination timelines are achieved when models are calibrated using data collected at the time of the first post-MDA survey, as this early data point provides the strongest constraint on model parameters.
Evidence: data streams including post-MDA 5 mf survey data (scenarios 3 and 4) reduced model uncertainty (based on both the variance and entropy measures) most compared to data streams containing only baseline and/or post-MDA 3 mf survey data (scenarios 1 and 2) (Table 2).
scenario 3, which includes baseline, post-MDA 3, and post-MDA 5 data, was shown to most often reduce model uncertainty the greatest.

Thus, for all models and sites, model-only predictions made in the absence of data (scenario 0) showed the highest prediction uncertainty, highlighting the need for data to improve the predictive performance of the present models.

Therefore, the first post-MDA survey alone did not provide the strongest constraint; later monitoring (post-MDA5) was more informative. The statement in the option is contradicted by the passage.

178.Option: Model predictions of LF elimination timelines are most sensitive to the initial baseline infection data, and additional longitudinal data have minimal impact on reducing prediction uncertainty.
Evidence: Thus, for all models and sites, model-only predictions made in the absence of data (scenario 0) showed the highest prediction uncertainty, highlighting the need for data to improve the predictive performance of the present models.
These results indicate unequivocally that models constrained by data provided significantly more precise intervention predictions compared to model-only predictions (Table 2).
as much as 92% to 96% reductions in prediction variance were achieved by these scenarios in comparison to model-only predictions between the three models (Table 2).

data streams including post-MDA 5 mf survey data (scenarios 3 and 4) reduced model uncertainty (based on both the variance and entropy measures) most compared to data streams containing only baseline and/or post-MDA 3 mf survey data (scenarios 1 and 2) (Table 2).
178.Rationale: The passage shows that baseline data alone (or model-only) leave the highest uncertainty and that additional longitudinal data substantially reduce prediction uncertainty (up to 92–96% variance reduction). It further emphasizes that later post-MDA surveys (post-MDA5) are particularly informative. Thus the claim that predictions are driven mainly by baseline and that additional longitudinal data have minimal impact is contradicted by the evidence.

178.Option: The use of longitudinal data from at least five years post-MDA is sufficient to constrain model predictions of the impact of LF interventions and detect qualitative changes in system behavior, with additional data beyond this point not leading to significant further improvements in predictive performance.
Evidence: data streams including post-MDA 5 mf survey data (scenarios 3 and 4) reduced model uncertainty (based on both the variance and entropy measures) most compared to data streams containing only baseline and/or post-MDA 3 mf survey data (scenarios 1 and 2) (Table 2).
scenario 3, which includes baseline, post-MDA 3, and post-MDA 5 data, was shown to most often reduce model uncertainty the greatest.

EPIFIL was also used to expand the number of calibration scenarios using a dataset with longer term post-MDA data from Villupuram district, India. ... Scenario 6 thus contained the most post-MDA data and was demonstrated to be the most effective for reducing model uncertainty, but this effect was not statistically significantly different from the reductions produced by assimilating data contained in scenarios 3 and 5 (Table 4).
The inclusion of more data than are considered in scenario 3 therefore did not result in any significant additional reduction in model uncertainty.
178.Rationale: The passage identifies post-MDA5 data (i.e., data collected after five rounds/years of MDA) as the most informative for reducing model uncertainty, with scenario 3 (which includes post-MDA5) commonly producing the largest improvements. Although the most-extensive data scenario (including post-MDA7 and post-MDA9) was numerically most effective, the passage explicitly states this was not statistically significantly better than scenario 3, and that adding more data beyond the post-MDA5 point did not yield significant additional reductions. This supports the inference that longitudinal data up to the five-year post-MDA period are sufficient for constraining model predictions, and that further follow-up offers diminishing returns for predictive performance.

178.Option: The optimal duration of longitudinal surveillance for parasite elimination models is determined by the length of the MDA program itself, with data collection required for the full duration of interventions to ensure accurate forecasts.
Evidence: data streams including post-MDA 5 mf survey data (scenarios 3 and 4) reduced model uncertainty (based on both the variance and entropy measures) most compared to data streams containing only baseline and/or post-MDA 3 mf survey data (scenarios 1 and 2) (Table 2).
scenario 3, which includes baseline, post-MDA 3, and post-MDA 5 data, was shown to most often reduce model uncertainty the greatest.

Scenario 6 thus contained the most post-MDA data and was demonstrated to be the most effective for reducing model uncertainty, but this effect was not statistically significantly different from the reductions produced by assimilating data contained in scenarios 3 and 5 (Table 4).
The inclusion of more data than are considered in scenario 3 therefore did not result in any significant additional reduction in model uncertainty.
178.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that data collected up to post-MDA5 are generally sufficient to achieve the largest reductions in model uncertainty, and that adding more longitudinal data for later rounds (beyond MDA5) does not produce statistically significant further improvements. Therefore, optimal surveillance duration is not necessarily dictated by collecting data for the full length of the MDA program; rather, a focused set of observations up to about five rounds is sufficient for accurate model constraining in the contexts examined. The option's claim that data collection must cover the full duration of interventions is not supported by the presented results.

179.Option: The under-diagnosis of zoonoses is mainly due to a lack of integration between animal and human healthcare sectors, which prevents the sharing of case information and limits cross-sectoral awareness.
Evidence: The recognition and successful clinical management of zoonoses depend on several factors, including individual healthcare seeking behavior; differential consideration of a zoonosis by patients and clinicians; availability, accuracy, and uptake of diagnostic testing; and treatment accessibility.

It is widely recognized that limited testing capacity (considering both test availability and performance) in sub-Saharan Africa severely limits the options for accurate laboratory diagnostic testing for zoonoses [12].
The few studies conducted to date reveal limited awareness of zoonoses among health workers [15–17].

Forty-six respondents (78%) pointed to the lack of available tests for zoonoses in the quantitative survey and diagnostic constraints were described explicitly in interviews: "People come to the hospital with fever, but when you test they don’t have malaria, then what do they have? You start to guess, but if we had facilities to test diseases then you could test and confirm" (BZQ-037, Human healthcare sector respondent).
179.Rationale: The passage identifies multiple factors driving under-diagnosis—especially limited testing capacity and low clinician awareness—and explicitly highlights nonspecific clinical presentations. It does not present lack of integration between animal and human health sectors as the main cause. While sector differences are described (e.g., mapping and differences in awareness of some diseases), the text emphasizes limited testing and clinician knowledge as primary contributors rather than cross-sectoral data-sharing failures. Therefore the option claiming 'mainly due to lack of integration' is not supported by the passage.

179.Option: The high visibility and dramatic clinical presentations of diseases like anthrax, rabies, and Ebola virus disease lead healthcare providers to prioritize these conditions over less conspicuous zoonoses such as brucellosis, leptospirosis, and Q fever.
Evidence: All (100%) respondents named rabies as a disease that could be transmitted by dogs.
Knowledge of one or more diseases transmitted by rodents was reported by 52 (84%) respondents, of whom 48 (92%) named plague.
In total, 38 (61%) respondents named one or more diseases that could cause abortions in livestock, including 28 (74%) brucellosis, 7 (18%) anthrax, and 4 (11%) Rift Valley fever.

Clinical recognition of many zoonoses is made difficult by their nonspecific clinical presentations that overlap with other febrile illnesses. This may partly explain the frequent misattribution of febrile zoonoses to pathogens for which there is greater awareness, such as malaria and typhoid [9].
The few studies conducted to date reveal limited awareness of zoonoses among health workers [15–17].
179.Rationale: Although the passage documents high awareness of some dramatic zoonoses (e.g., rabies, plague, anthrax mentioned by some respondents), it does not state that providers prioritize dramatic diseases over less conspicuous ones, nor does it mention Ebola in this context. Instead, the text attributes under-diagnosis of conditions like brucellosis, leptospirosis, and Q fever to nonspecific presentations, limited testing capacity, and low awareness. Thus the specific causal claim about prioritization driven by visibility of dramatic diseases is not supported by the passage.

179.Option: Persistent over-diagnosis of malaria is primarily driven by a lack of confidence in the accuracy of malaria rapid diagnostic tests (mRDTs), which leads healthcare providers to default to clinical diagnosis and overlook other causes of fever.
Evidence: Previous studies in northern Tanzania have indicated that clinicians’ perceptions of patient pressure play a role in malaria over-diagnosis and over-treatment, suggesting a need to raise awareness about alternative causes of febrile illness, including zoonoses, among both the healthcare provider and patient populations [13,14].
Seventeen (29%) respondents mentioned a common assumption among healthcare providers that a fever indicates malaria.

Twelve (20%) respondents indicated a lack of confidence in malaria rapid diagnostic tests (mRDTs), and 11 (19%) insisted that sometimes mRDTs produced false negative malaria results when other tests had shown ‘true’ positives.

"The doctors have developed a habit where they assume people with fever symptoms are suffering from malaria. That is why many clinicians do not remember to check zoonotic diseases" (BZQ-062, Human healthcare sector respondent).
179.Rationale: The passage reports lack of confidence in mRDTs among some respondents, but it also identifies other contributors to malaria over-diagnosis—clinician assumptions that fever equals malaria and perceived patient pressure. The text does not single out mRDT distrust as the primary driver. Therefore claiming it is 'primarily driven' by mRDT lack of confidence is not supported by the passage evidence.

179.Option: The non-specific clinical presentation of many zoonoses, combined with limited diagnostic testing capacity, contributes to a failure to recognize and diagnose these diseases in clinical practice.
Evidence: Clinical recognition of many zoonoses is made difficult by their nonspecific clinical presentations that overlap with other febrile illnesses. This may partly explain the frequent misattribution of febrile zoonoses to pathogens for which there is greater awareness, such as malaria and typhoid [9].

The recognition and successful clinical management of zoonoses depend on several factors, including individual healthcare seeking behavior; differential consideration of a zoonosis by patients and clinicians; availability, accuracy, and uptake of diagnostic testing; and treatment accessibility.

It is widely recognized that limited testing capacity (considering both test availability and performance) in sub-Saharan Africa severely limits the options for accurate laboratory diagnostic testing for zoonoses [12].

Forty-six respondents (78%) pointed to the lack of available tests for zoonoses in the quantitative survey and diagnostic constraints were described explicitly in interviews: "People come to the hospital with fever, but when you test they don’t have malaria, then what do they have? You start to guess, but if we had facilities to test diseases then you could test and confirm" (BZQ-037, Human healthcare sector respondent).
179.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that nonspecific clinical presentations that overlap with other febrile illnesses and limited testing capacity are key reasons zoonoses are misattributed or not diagnosed. Multiple quantitative and qualitative data points (including the high proportion of respondents citing lack of tests) support the conclusion that these two factors together contribute to failure to recognize and diagnose zoonoses in clinical practice.

179.Option: The low frequency of reported zoonoses cases in the past year is due to the fact that most patients with zoonotic infections do not seek healthcare, resulting in under-ascertainment of disease burden.
Evidence: The recognition and successful clinical management of zoonoses depend on several factors, including individual healthcare seeking behavior; differential consideration of a zoonosis by patients and clinicians; availability, accuracy, and uptake of diagnostic testing; and treatment accessibility.

Forty-six respondents (78%) pointed to the lack of available tests for zoonoses in the quantitative survey and diagnostic constraints were described explicitly in interviews: "People come to the hospital with fever, but when you test they don’t have malaria, then what do they have? You start to guess, but if we had facilities to test diseases then you could test and confirm" (BZQ-037, Human healthcare sector respondent).
When asked about possible explanations for the apparent under-reporting of zoonoses, 44 (75%) respondents indicated that healthcare providers lacked knowledge or awareness of zoonoses.
179.Rationale: Although the passage lists individual healthcare-seeking behavior as one factor affecting recognition, it provides no evidence that most patients with zoonotic infections do not seek care and therefore that non-presentation is the principal cause of low reported cases. Instead, the passage emphasizes low clinician awareness and limited diagnostic testing as explanations for under-reporting. Thus the specific causal claim about patient non-presentation being the main reason is not supported by the provided text.

180.Option: Cx43 plays a key role in cell proliferation, potentially leading to cholangiocarcinoma development upon stimulation by excretory-secretory products of Clonorchis sinensis and the carcinogen NDMA.
Evidence: We found that the silencing of Cx43 reduced ESP- and NDMA-induced cell proliferation and the expression of Cox-2.
The expression levels of Cx26 and Cx43 were high in NDMA + ESP-treated cells, as confirmed by immunoblotting (Fig 3).

Proliferation was higher in H69 cells treated with NDMA and ESP of C. sinensis than in control cells. Compared to control cells, the average increase for cells treated with NDMA was 112%, for cells treated with ESP was 120% (P < 0.05), and for cells treated with NDMA + ESP was 146% (P < 0.001). NDMA and ESP had synergistic effects on cell proliferation (Fig 1A).

The expression levels of several cell proliferation- including E2F1, Ki-67 and Cy-19, and Cox-2 (an essential regulator of the G2/M transition) as cancer-related makers, were upregulated, especially in NDMA + ESP-treated cells (Fig 2).
Clonorchis sinensis is a human liver fluke that induces cholangiocarcinoma (CHCA) in humans [1].
180.Rationale: The passage shows that Cx43 expression is increased by NDMA + ESP treatment and that silencing Cx43 reduces the NDMA/ESP-induced increases in cell proliferation and Cox-2 expression. The treatments (NDMA + ESP) also induce proliferation and upregulate cancer-related markers and are linked to CHCA. These facts support the inference that Cx43 plays a key role in the observed pro-proliferative, cancer-associated cellular changes and therefore may contribute to cholangiocarcinoma development in this experimental context.

180.Option: The upregulation of Cx43 expression in H69 cells treated with NDMA and ESP of C. sinensis is likely an adaptive protective response of the liver to enable better cell-to-cell communication.
Evidence: The expression levels of Cx26 and Cx43 were high in NDMA + ESP-treated cells, as confirmed by immunoblotting (Fig 3).
The substantial role of Cx43 in carcinogenesis is highlighted by the fact that high levels of Cx-43 expression increase the invasion of breast tumor cells and promote tumors in melanoma [22].
We found that the silencing of Cx43 reduced ESP- and NDMA-induced cell proliferation and the expression of Cox-2.
180.Rationale: While Cx43 is upregulated after NDMA + ESP treatment, the passage provides evidence linking high Cx43 levels to tumor-promoting behavior in other cancers and shows that silencing Cx43 reduces proliferation and Cox-2 expression in this system. Those findings are inconsistent with the claim that Cx43 upregulation is an adaptive protective response; instead, the data indicate a pro-proliferative, potentially pro-tumor role for Cx43 in this model.

180.Option: The inhibition of Cox-2 expression following Cx43 knockdown suggests that Cx43 functions as a direct suppressor of inflammatory signaling in cholangiocytes exposed to NDMA and ESP.
Evidence: Real-time PCR showed that Cx43 silencing resulted in the downregulation of Cx26 and Cox-2 (Fig 5C and 5D, respectively).

The expression levels of several cell proliferation- including E2F1, Ki-67 and Cy-19, and Cox-2 (an essential regulator of the G2/M transition) as cancer-related makers, were upregulated, especially in NDMA + ESP-treated cells (Fig 2).
180.Rationale: The data show that knocking down Cx43 reduces Cox-2 expression. If Cx43 silencing lowers Cox-2, this indicates that Cx43 promotes (rather than suppresses) Cox-2 expression and associated inflammatory/cancer-related signaling in this setting. Therefore the statement that Cx43 functions as a direct suppressor of inflammatory signaling contradicts the observed direction of effect and is not supported by the passage.

180.Option: The downregulation of Cx32 expression in H69 cells treated with NDMA and ESP indicates that Cx32 acts as a tumor suppressor in the context of Clonorchis sinensis-induced carcinogenesis.
Evidence: The expression of Cx32 (blue) was markedly lower in cells treated with NDMA + ESP than in other cells.

In hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), for instance, reduced Cx32 expression is accompanied by increased expression of Cx43, which promotes HCC via cell-to-cell communication [16, 20, 23]. Fujimoto et al. [23] have shown that Cx32 has a suppressive effect in metastatic renal cell carcinoma.
Proliferation was higher in H69 cells treated with NDMA and ESP of C. sinensis than in control cells. ... NDMA and ESP had synergistic effects on cell proliferation (Fig 1A).
180.Rationale: The passage documents that treatments which drive proliferation and cancer-related marker upregulation (NDMA + ESP) are associated with a marked reduction in Cx32 expression. The authors also cite literature showing reduced Cx32 in HCC and a suppressive effect of Cx32 in another cancer type. Taken together, the observed loss of Cx32 concurrent with pro-proliferative, pro-cancer changes supports the inference that Cx32 functions as a tumor suppressor in this context.

180.Option: The synergistic increase in cell proliferation and G2/M phase progression observed in H69 cells treated with NDMA and ESP is primarily driven by the upregulation of Cx26 rather than Cx43.
Evidence: The expression levels of Cx26 and Cx43 were high in NDMA + ESP-treated cells, as confirmed by immunoblotting (Fig 3).
Reduced cell proliferation upon NDMA and ESP treatment in H69 cells by connexin 43 silencing.
Real-time PCR showed that Cx43 silencing resulted in the downregulation of Cx26 and Cox-2 (Fig 5C and 5D, respectively).
180.Rationale: Both Cx26 and Cx43 are upregulated by NDMA + ESP, but the experimental manipulation (Cx43 siRNA) reduced the proliferation response and also caused downregulation of Cx26 and Cox-2. This indicates Cx43 is functionally upstream and required for the full proliferative response and for Cx26 expression in this model. There is no experimental evidence in the passage showing that Cx26 alone primarily drives proliferation; instead, the data implicate Cx43 as a key driver. Therefore the claim that Cx26 is the primary driver is not supported.

181.Option: The high compliance with repeated PZQ treatments and the low frequency of adverse events suggest that multiple doses of praziquantel are both safe and highly effective in eliminating Schistosoma mansoni infections in school-aged children.
Evidence: In the intense treatment group, compliance to each following treatment was unexpectedly high, from 100% in week 2 (second treatment) to 98% in week 6 (fourth and final treatment).
Most of the adverse events were mild and all of them resolved 24 hours after treatment. Adverse events decreased with subsequent treatments in the intense treatment group.
In the intense treatment group, who received a total of four doses of PZQ, the prevalence decreased to 14% (95% CI 8–23%), measured 10 weeks after the first treatment.
POC-CCA-based CRs were much lower compared to CRs based on the KK technique; only 18% (95% CI 11–27%) in the standard treatment group and 36% (95% CI 26–46%) in the intense treatment group (P<0.01).
In both groups, most children remained POC-CCA positive at the final time point.
181.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of excellent compliance and that adverse events were mostly mild and transient, supporting that repeated PZQ dosing was well tolerated (safety). However, the claim that multiple doses are "highly effective in eliminating" infections is not supported. Although KK-based measures showed large reductions (e.g., prevalence to 14% and KK CR 86% in the intense group), the POC-CCA test—described in the passage as detecting active worm infections—showed much lower cure rates and most children remained POC-CCA positive at the final time point. Thus elimination is not demonstrated by the study data, and the option overstates effectiveness beyond what the diagnostics jointly indicate.

181.Option: Since the POC-CCA test detects active infections and the KK technique detects eggs, the higher cure rate observed with KK must be due to the test's greater specificity, indicating that PZQ is highly effective in killing adult worms.
Evidence: The field-applicable and commercially available point-of-care circulating cathodic antigen (POC-CCA) urine test, which identifies active worm infections by detection of schistosome CCA in urine, has shown a higher sensitivity for detecting S. mansoni infections than the KK technique [20–22].

Most studies reporting on the efficacy of PZQ have used microscopy-based methods, such as ... the stool-based Kato-Katz (KK) technique ... However, these methods lack sensitivity, especially for detection of low-intensity infections [17, 18]. Hence, reported CRs based on these parasitological methods are likely an overestimation [19, 20].
In the standard treatment group, CR based on triplicate KK thick smears was 42% (95% CI 31–53%). A significantly higher CR (86%, 95% CI 75–92%) was observed in the intense treatment group.
POC-CCA-based CRs were much lower compared to CRs based on the KK technique; only 18% (95% CI 11–27%) in the standard treatment group and 36% (95% CI 26–46%) in the intense treatment group (P<0.01).
181.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that POC-CCA detects active worm infections and that POC-CCA has higher sensitivity than KK; it also states that KK (microscopy) lacks sensitivity and likely overestimates cure rates. The option asserts the opposite (that KK's higher observed cure rate is due to greater specificity and proves PZQ highly effective at killing adult worms). That inference is not supported by the passage. The data and methodological statements instead indicate that higher KK-based CRs are likely an overestimation due to lower sensitivity of KK, not evidence of superior specificity or definitive proof that PZQ has eliminated adult worms.

181.Option: The use of the POC-CCA test revealed a significantly lower cure rate compared to the KK technique, indicating that PZQ may be less efficacious than previously reported due to persistent active infections despite treatment.
Evidence: The field-applicable and commercially available point-of-care circulating cathodic antigen (POC-CCA) urine test, which identifies active worm infections by detection of schistosome CCA in urine, has shown a higher sensitivity for detecting S. mansoni infections than the KK technique [20–22].
POC-CCA-based CRs were much lower compared to CRs based on the KK technique; only 18% (95% CI 11–27%) in the standard treatment group and 36% (95% CI 26–46%) in the intense treatment group (P<0.01).
