236.Rationale: While the passage documents that CHVs carried out monitoring (using the app) and indicates the app improved follow-up, it does not provide evidence that the intervention "significantly reduced the burden on health facilities." There are no measures or comparisons of facility workload, resource use, or explicit evidence that responsibility was shifted in a way that reduced facility burden. The passage even states that lack of information in control groups "precluded evaluation of the influence of active follow-up monitoring on management of adverse events or the outcome of treatment," indicating the study did not assess system-level effects like facility burden or access improvements directly. Therefore the causal claim that burden was significantly reduced is not derivable from the passage.

236.Option: Despite the challenges of poor connectivity and mobility, the mHealth intervention led to a complete resolution of treatment non-adherence in the intervention arm, enabling full therapeutic monitoring for all patients.
Evidence: There was no statistically significant difference in the compliance with the prescribed treatment between the randomized arms (Table 2).
The average number of ampoules administered, as a percentage of the number prescribed, was 88% or higher in each of the three groups.

Of the 75 participants in the two randomized arms, 74 had information on whether or not they were followed up at target study week 26 (the primary endpoint of the trial). Among these, 26/49 (53.1%) were evaluated by active follow-up at this time in the intervention arm, and none (0/25, 0%) of those in the randomized control arm
Due to a technical error, the exact dates of visits were not retained in the app dataset, but rather only as the target study week.
236.Rationale: The passage provides no evidence that the mHealth intervention produced a "complete resolution of treatment non-adherence" or "full therapeutic monitoring for all patients." It explicitly states there was no statistically significant difference in treatment compliance between randomized arms, and that the average proportion of prescribed ampoules actually administered was about 88% or higher (i.e., not 100%). Additionally, only 26 of 49 in the intervention arm were evaluated at week 26 (about half), so monitoring was not complete for all patients. A technical error is also reported. Thus the strong claim of complete resolution of non-adherence and universal monitoring is not supported by the passage.

237.Option: The spatial clustering of high household density and lower SES in coastal Kenya is primarily driven by proximity to main roads and aquatic habitats favorable for Anopheles mosquitoes and snails.
Evidence: Clusters of high household density were often located near the main road (Figure 5, Figure S1 in Text S1).
In contrast, people with lower SES were usually clustered away from the main roads (Figure 5, Figure S1 in Text S1).
Low-income houses were clustered closer to sites that were suitable for snail hosts of S. haematobium (Figure 5 and Figure S1 in Text S1).
Clusters of the various helminthic infections overlapped in all villages, and were located near the main roads in all communities, with the exception of Kinango.
237.Rationale: The passage reports specific spatial associations (household density clusters often near main roads; low-SES households usually clustered away from main roads; low-income houses clustered closer to sites suitable for snail hosts). These statements do not support the stronger claim that clustering of both high household density AND lower SES is primarily driven by proximity to main roads. In fact, the passage explicitly states lower SES clusters are usually away from main roads (contradicting the "primarily driven by proximity to main roads" assertion). While proximity to snail-suitable sites is documented for low-income houses, the statement about Anopheles aquatic habitats as a primary driver is not supported by explicit text. Therefore the option's claim of a single primary driver (main roads and Anopheles/snail habitats) overstates and misrepresents the study's findings.

237.Option: The challenge of polyparasitism is increasingly recognized through i) our increasing appreciation of the health and social burden of chronic/recurrent infections, ii) more sensitive diagnostics, which indicate that concurrent polyparasitism is much more prevalent than previously thought, and iii) new inexpensive approaches to treatment and transmission control that are increasingly more accessible.
Evidence: Because of their combined long-term effects, these infections appear to play a significant but, as yet, incompletely defined synergistic role in the causation of chronic clinical and sub-clinical human disease and poverty [6], [7], [8].

new advances in diagnostic technology have increased test sensitivity and specificity for these parasites, revealing that in endemic areas, chronic parasitic infection with Schistosoma spp. [24], Plasmodia spp. [25], and/or filaria [26] are much more common than previously thought.
malaria prevalence, as detected by PCR is 60–75%, more than double the previous estimates of 20–33% by blood smear microscopy, with ≥10% carrying two or more malaria species [25], [27].

advances in filaria antigen detection techniques indicate that past community surveys have underestimated prevalence of filariasis by 40% [29], while standard screening techniques for S. haematobium have probably missed 50–60% of low level infections with this parasite [30].
now in the era of integrated parasite control programs, it is important to define those factors that enhance risk of co-infection.
237.Rationale: The passage clearly supports points (i) and (ii): it emphasizes the combined long-term health/social burden of infections and documents that more sensitive diagnostics have revealed polyparasitism to be more common than previously thought (with concrete PCR and antigen-detection examples). However, the passage does not provide evidence for point (iii) as stated — it mentions 'integrated parasite control programs' and the need to inform control strategies but does not state that there are 'new inexpensive approaches to treatment and transmission control that are increasingly more accessible.' That specific claim about inexpensive and increasingly accessible approaches is not supported by quoted text, so the full three-part statement cannot be derived solely from the passage.

237.Option: The high prevalence of co-infections in children and young adults is primarily due to the prior mass drug administration (MDA) campaign in 2003, which reduced hookworm and Trichuris infections in the 10–19 age group.
Evidence: Overall, children and young adults were more likely to be infected with malaria parasites, S. haematobium, and Trichuris, while adults were more likely to be infected with filariasis and hookworms. Polyparasitism (two or more infections) was highest in the 10–19 age group.
In Milalani and Nganja, hookworm infections declined in the 10–19 age group before rising again in the older age groups.
Infected subjects received standard anti-parasite treatments at the time of the survey according to the Ministry of Health guidelines.
237.Rationale: The passage documents age-specific prevalence patterns (polyparasitism highest in 10–19) and notes a local decline in hookworm in that age group in certain villages. However, the passage contains no information about a prior 2003 mass drug administration (MDA) campaign or any historical MDA being the cause of the observed age patterns. The only treatment-related sentence refers to treatments given at the time of the survey. Therefore attributing the high prevalence pattern primarily to a 2003 MDA is not supported by the passage.

237.Option: The integration of socio-ecological data and spatial analysis techniques in this study demonstrates that environmental factors are the dominant drivers of polyparasitism in coastal Kenya.
Evidence: Village and environmental factors were major independent correlates of infection.

demographic factors (age, gender, and education), SES, and household characteristics (construction, use of bednets, water source, sanitation, and number of inhabitants) were significantly associated with parasite infections and co-infections.
After controlling for demographic factors and SES, living in Kinango was protective with regard to several single infections and for co-infections.
237.Rationale: The passage shows that environmental and village factors are important independent correlates of infection, and that the study used socio-ecological and spatial analyses to examine polyparasitism. However, it also documents that demographic, socioeconomic, and household-level variables were significantly associated with single infections and co-infections. The evidence supports that environmental factors are major contributors but does not establish they are the dominant drivers to the exclusion of these other significant influences. Thus the stronger claim that environmental factors are demonstrably the dominant drivers is not fully supported by the text.

237.Option: The lack of significant correlation between mosquito abundance and malaria or filariasis cases suggests that vector control measures are ineffective in reducing transmission in coastal Kenya.
Evidence: Results from the GAM applied to the entomological data (Table 4) showed no significant correlation between presence or number of collected mosquitoes and malaria or filariasis cases.
However, not surprisingly, bednet use was protective against malaria.

Mosquito collections from January 2011 to March 2011 were inconsistent, with only 7 mosquito collection efforts conducted out of the possible 24 for both PSC and Prokopack aspirator. No mosquito collections were performed in January and most of February 2011 due to logistical difficulties.
Mosquito collection by all methods always started at 06:00 h and ended no later than 10:00 h.
237.Rationale: While the study found no significant correlation between the number of collected mosquitoes and malaria or filariasis cases in the entomological analysis, this empirical finding alone does not justify the causal conclusion that vector control measures are ineffective. The passage also reports that bednet use was protective against malaria, indicating some effectiveness of interventions. Moreover, the mosquito sampling had temporal and methodological limitations (inconsistent collections during part of the study and restricted collection times), which can affect the entomological-infection correlation. The option asserts a causal policy conclusion (vector control ineffective) that is not supported by the study data or text.

238.Option: Despite significant creatinine elevation in one patient, significant differences were not recorded in any of mean laboratory values at the end of the therapy, suggesting that routine laboratory assessment is not mandatory during oral miltefosine treatment.
Evidence: Creatinine elevation was the only laboratory data complication which detected in one patient (3.70%) which raised from 0.80 mg/dl in the beginning to 2.0 mg/dl after 14 days.
Patient with disturbed laboratory data were excluded from the study and referred to the nephrologist.
There were no significant differences in mean values of hematological, hepatic, and renal parameters in treatment groups.
238.Rationale: The passage does report a single case of marked creatinine elevation and also states that mean laboratory values did not change significantly. However, concluding that 'routine laboratory assessment is not mandatory' is a clinical recommendation that cannot be derived from these data alone. The study excluded and withdrew a patient with abnormal labs and referred them to a nephrologist, which indicates laboratory monitoring had clinical consequences. The absence of significant mean changes does not justify asserting that monitoring is unnecessary for all patients; this causal or policy claim is not supported by the passage.

238.Option: The high rate of clinical improvement and low recurrence over 12 months of follow-up in this cohort suggests that oral miltefosine treatment leads to permanent sterilization of L. tropica infection in most patients.
Evidence: In the first follow up visit (three months after treatment) 23 patients (85.18%) gained complete or partial cure, including 14 definitely cured patients and 9 partially cured patients.

In both second and third follow up sessions (sixth and twelfth month post treatment) 22 patients had complete or partial cure (81.48%) including 17 definitely cured patients, corresponding to a cure rate of 68% on a per protocol analysis, and 62.96% according to intention to treat analysis and 5 partially cured patients.
Complete response (CR) for each lesion was defined as complete resolve of lesion induration and inflammation. All CR lesions were confirmed according to negative skin smear test.
Recurrence of lesions was only occurred in one patient (3.70%) during 6 month follow up.
238.Rationale: The passage documents substantial clinical improvement (CR and partial cures) and low observed recurrence up to 12 months, and indicates CR lesions had negative skin smears. However, claiming 'permanent sterilization' is stronger than the data allow: the study follow-up is up to 12 months and does not demonstrate lifelong parasite clearance, nor does it provide comprehensive parasitological testing beyond smear negativity of CR lesions. Therefore the inference of permanent sterilization in most patients cannot be derived from the presented data.

238.Option: The program to investigate miltefosine for new world cutaneous leishmaniasis (NWCL) was initiated in 2001 with an uncontrolled, open-label, dose-ranging study in Colombia, in which the per-protocol cure rate of 94% was reported by 2.5 mg/kg oral miltefosine per day after four weeks.
Evidence: Oral miltefosine has previously demonstrated a 95% cure rate in Indian visceral leishmaniasis [6–7].

In Iran, as endemic place for OWCL, therapeutic effects of oral miltefosine on ZCL have been studied previously. Mohebali and colleagues (2007) declared miltefosine at least as good as MA for the treatment of ZCL [8].
238.Rationale: The passage mentions prior miltefosine results in Indian visceral leishmaniasis and that studies in Iran evaluated miltefosine for ZCL, but it does not describe a 2001 Colombia NWCL program, nor provide the specific 94% per-protocol cure rate or study design details asserted in the option. Therefore the specific historical and outcome claim about a Colombia study cannot be derived from the provided passage.

238.Option: The therapeutic effect of oral miltefosine on MA-resistant anthropontic cutaneous leishmaniasis caused by L. tropica in Iran is effective and nearly safe, as evidenced by a high rate of clinical improvement and low recurrence over 12 months of follow-up.
Evidence: It was an uncontrolled before-after study, conducted during 2018–2019 in two major leishmaniasis centers in eastern of Iran ... on 27 patients with MA resistant ACL.
In the first follow up visit (three months after treatment) 23 patients (85.18%) gained complete or partial cure, including 14 definitely cured patients and 9 partially cured patients.

In both second and third follow up sessions (sixth and twelfth month post treatment) 22 patients had complete or partial cure (81.48%) including 17 definitely cured patients, corresponding to a cure rate of 68% on a per protocol analysis, and 62.96% according to intention to treat analysis and 5 partially cured patients.
Recurrence of lesions was only occurred in one patient (3.70%) during 6 month follow up.
Nausea, but not vomiting, was the most subjective complication during the study which detected in 9 (33.33%) patients.
Creatinine elevation was the only laboratory data complication which detected in one patient (3.70%) which raised from 0.80 mg/dl in the beginning to 2.0 mg/dl after 14 days.
There were no significant differences in mean values of hematological, hepatic, and renal parameters in treatment groups.
238.Rationale: The passage provides direct clinical outcome data in MA-resistant L. tropica ACL patients treated with oral miltefosine: high proportions of complete or partial cures at 3, 6 and 12 months (85.18% early, 81.48% at later follow-ups; 68% per-protocol definite cure), very low documented recurrence (one patient), and adverse events consisting mainly of nausea and a single case of creatinine elevation. The balance of substantial clinical benefit and relatively limited serious laboratory toxicity in this cohort justifies the epidemiological inference that miltefosine in this setting appears effective and 'nearly safe' as described.

238.Option: The higher cure rates observed in previous studies of miltefosine for zoonotic CL in Iran may be attributed to the self-limiting nature of L. major infections, which could explain the relatively lower cure rate in this cohort of patients with chronic L. tropica infections.
Evidence: ACL and zoonotic cutaneous leishmaniasis (ZCL) caused by L. tropica and L. major respectively, are the most common types of CL in Iran [3].

In Iran, as endemic place for OWCL, therapeutic effects of oral miltefosine on ZCL have been studied previously. Mohebali and colleagues (2007) declared miltefosine at least as good as MA for the treatment of ZCL [8].
Mean duration of lesions was 3.45±0.76 years (ranges between 8 months and 20 years).
238.Rationale: The passage notes that prior miltefosine work has been done in ZCL (L. major) and that this study cohort had long-lasting lesions due to L. tropica. However, it does not present data demonstrating that L. major infections are self-limiting nor does it provide comparative cure rates that would permit attributing differences to self-limitation. The causal attribution offered in the option (that higher prior cure rates are due to self-limiting L. major) is not supported by the passage and requires external evidence, so it cannot be derived from the provided text.

239.Option: The majority of V. cholerae isolates from household contacts and water sources within the same household were closely related by MLVA, indicating that transmission occurred either through a shared environmental source or person-to-person contact.
Evidence: When the relatedness of water and clinical isolates from the same household was compared, all households had at least one clinical and water isolate with an identical MLVA genotype or a single locus variant of the same MLVA genotype.
We identified 31 MLVA genotypes: 7 MLVA genotypes from both clinical and water isolates, 9 genotypes from only water isolates, and 15 genotypes from only clinical isolates.

Isolates collected from the same household had significantly fewer pairwise differences in MLVA loci than those from different households (mean: 0.98 pairwise differences in MLVA loci (same household) vs. 1.79 (different household), p<0.0001).
239.Rationale: The passage provides direct genetic evidence that clinical and water isolates within households were frequently identical or single-locus variants and that isolates from the same household had significantly fewer genetic differences than isolates from different households. These findings support the epidemiological inference that transmission occurred within households via either a shared environmental source (e.g., contaminated household water) or person-to-person spread; the evidence does not force a single exclusive route but indicates close relatedness consistent with these transmission pathways.

239.Option: The presence of V. cholerae in both source and stored water within households indicates that household water contamination is primarily due to poor sanitation infrastructure rather than person-to-person transmission.
Evidence: All households with detectable V. cholerae in their water source had unimproved latrines.
All households with V. cholerae in their source water also had V. cholerae in stored drinking water.
The findings showed a combination of person-to-person and water-to-person cholera transmission with the proportions of the two modes varying within and between outbreaks [17].
239.Rationale: While the passage links detectable V. cholerae in source water with unimproved latrines (supporting an association with poor sanitation), it explicitly reports a combination of person-to-person and water-to-person transmission. The data do not support the stronger claim that contamination is 'primarily due to poor sanitation infrastructure rather than person-to-person transmission.' That assignment of primary causality is not established by the presented evidence.

239.Option: The fact that stored water samples had a higher proportion of V. cholerae than source water samples indicates that household-level contamination is the primary driver of V. cholerae transmission, independent of the index case.
Evidence: Thirteen percent of households (4) had V. cholerae in their source water during the surveillance period (all groundwater sources), and 27% (8) had V. cholerae in stored household drinking water.
All households with V. cholerae in their source water also had V. cholerae in stored drinking water.
The findings showed a combination of person-to-person and water-to-person cholera transmission with the proportions of the two modes varying within and between outbreaks [17].
239.Rationale: Although stored water was positive more often than source water (27% vs. 13%), the passage does not support the strong inference that household-level contamination is the primary driver independent of the index case. The observed co-occurrence of source and stored water positivity and MLVA evidence of close relatedness between clinical and water isolates indicate linked transmission dynamics; the data do not isolate household-level contamination as the primary, index-case–independent driver.

239.Option: The high diversity of MLVA genotypes observed in clinical and water samples suggests that V. cholerae infections in household contacts are primarily due to multiple independent environmental exposures rather than person-to-person transmission.
Evidence: We identified 31 MLVA genotypes: 7 MLVA genotypes from both clinical and water isolates, 9 genotypes from only water isolates, and 15 genotypes from only clinical isolates.
Eighty one percent of clinical samples (25/31) had at least two isolates with different MLVA genotypes compared to 83% (15/18) of water samples.

Among the ten households with multiple infected household members, nine out of ten had isolates from different household members with identical MLVA genotypes or single locus variants of the same MLVA genotype.
239.Rationale: While there is clear genotype diversity across samples and multiple genotypes per sample, the within-household data show frequent identical or single-locus-variant genotypes among household members. Additionally, the study explicitly reports a mix of person-to-person and water-to-person transmission. Therefore, diversity alone does not establish that infections are primarily due to multiple independent environmental exposures rather than person-to-person transmission.

239.Option: The significantly higher odds of symptomatic V. cholerae infection among household contacts with V. cholerae in their water source suggests that waterborne transmission is the dominant mode of infection, with person-to-person transmission playing a minor role.
Evidence: Contacts with V. cholerae in their source water had a significantly higher odds of a symptomatic V. cholerae infection (OR: 5.49, 95% CI: 1.07, 28.08) (25% (V. cholerae in water source) vs. 6% (no V. cholerae in water source)) (Table 4).

When the relatedness of water and clinical isolates from the same household was compared, all households had at least one clinical and water isolate with an identical MLVA genotype or a single locus variant of the same MLVA genotype.

Among the ten households with multiple infected household members, nine out of ten had isolates from different household members with identical MLVA genotypes or single locus variants of the same MLVA genotype.
The findings showed a combination of person-to-person and water-to-person cholera transmission with the proportions of the two modes varying within and between outbreaks [17].
239.Rationale: The elevated odds of symptomatic infection associated with contaminated source water indicate that waterborne transmission is an important pathway. However, genetic and household-level data demonstrating identical genotypes among household members and the authors' statement that both person-to-person and water-to-person transmission occur prevent concluding that waterborne transmission is dominant and person-to-person transmission is minor. The evidence supports an important role for waterborne transmission but not exclusive or dominant status to the exclusion of meaningful person-to-person spread.

240.Option: The immunosuppressive effect of M. corti tetrathyridia excretory-secretory products is due to the presence of multiple low-molecular-weight proteins that are selectively secreted during active infection.
Evidence: Active fractions were principally found within the MW group (i) suggesting that the active principle within McES might be of a MW >20kDa since fractions from group ii and iii showed minimal to no activity (Fig 9C and 9D). Taken together, our bio-activity based fractionation analyses suggest that (a) protein(s) of MW higher than 20 kDa might mediate the immunosuppressive activity of McES.

as little as 5 μg/ml of McES reduced LPS-driven IL-12p70 production by 50% in BMDC cultures whereas a similar amount of McH failed to show any suppressive effect on LPS-driven IL-12p70 release by BMDCs (Fig 5). Our data therefore suggested that the DC-suppressing glycoprotein(s) from M. corti tetrathyridia is/are specifically secreted by the larva and not that somatic products were leaking from the larval soma.

We found that viable tetrathyridia significantly recruited cells within the peritoneum at day 7 p.i. when compared to dead tetrathyridia or to the mock control (3-fold more than the dead larvae and 15-fold more than the mock injections) (Fig 1C).
240.Rationale: The option asserts the active components are multiple low-molecular-weight proteins secreted during active infection. The passage provides direct experimental evidence that the immunosuppressive activity maps to fractions with proteins >20 kDa, and that low-MW groups (group ii and iii) showed minimal to no activity. While the study does support that the active suppressors are secreted by metabolically active larvae (live vs dead larvae and McES vs McH), it contradicts the claim that they are low-molecular-weight proteins. Therefore the statement is not derivable from the presented data.

240.Option: The immunosuppressive effect of M. corti tetrathyridia excretory-secretory products on dendritic cells is mediated by glycoproteins with a molecular weight greater than 20 kDa.
Evidence: we found that all tested McES concentrations significantly inhibited LPS-driven release of IL-12p70 by BMDCs in a dose-dependent manner.
hiMcES completely failed to show such an ability (Fig 4B) indicating that parasite proteins are important for McES-driven BMDC suppression.

Predictably, Mock McESΔCHO impaired LPS-driven IL-12p70 release by BMDCs whereas McESΔCHO failed to show such an effect (Fig 4B). These results indicated that parasite carbohydrates are important for the suppressive effect of McES.
Together, these findings demonstrated that both parasite proteins and carbohydrates are necessary for the immunosuppressive effect of McES.

the mixture of protein-depleted McES (hiMcES) and carbohydrate-depleted McES (McESΔCHO) failed to inhibit LPS-driven IL-12p70 production by BMDCs whereas the mixture of mock controls (mock hiMcES and mock McESΔCHO) mediated this suppressive effect. These results indicated that one or several intact parasite glycoproteins, but not separate protein and carbohydrate entities, mediate(s) DC suppression by McES.

Active fractions were principally found within the MW group (i) suggesting that the active principle within McES might be of a MW >20kDa since fractions from group ii and iii showed minimal to no activity (Fig 9C and 9D). Taken together, our bio-activity based fractionation analyses suggest that (a) protein(s) of MW higher than 20 kDa might mediate the immunosuppressive activity of McES.
240.Rationale: The passage shows (1) McES suppress IL-12p70 release by BMDCs; (2) heat-inactivation (protein denaturation) abolishes activity, indicating proteins are required; (3) periodate treatment removing carbohydrates abolishes activity, indicating carbohydrates are required; (4) combination experiments indicate intact glycoproteins (protein+carbohydrate together) mediate suppression; and (5) bioactivity-guided fractionation localized activity to fractions with molecular weight >20 kDa. Together these statements directly support the inference that glycoproteins >20 kDa mediate the DC-suppressive effect.

240.Option: The ability of M. corti tetrathyridia to suppress dendritic cell activation is dependent on the metabolic activity of the parasite, as heat-killed larvae failed to recruit CD11c+ cells in vivo.
Evidence: we injected either live, heat-killed M. corti tetrathyridia or the PBS carrier solution (mock, negative control) into the peritoneum of BALB/c mice (Fig 1A) and analyzed the frequency of host CD11c+ cells within the total peritoneal exudate cells (Fig 1B).

We found that viable tetrathyridia significantly recruited cells within the peritoneum at day 7 p.i. when compared to dead tetrathyridia or to the mock control (3-fold more than the dead larvae and 15-fold more than the mock injections) (Fig 1C).

live M. corti tetrathyridia recruited growing proportion of CD11c+ (12% of the peritoneal exudate cells at day 3 p.i. and 27% at day 7 p.i.) (Fig 1D and 1E). In contrast, cells recruited less efficiently by heat-killed M. corti tetrathyridia harbored fewer CD11c+ host cells over time (10% of the peritoneal exudate cells at day 3 p.i. and 4% at day 7 p.i.) (Fig 1D and 1E).

Given the ability of live helminths to excrete-secrete molecules which directly interact with host immune cells [11,23] and considering the reported predominance of dendritic cells within murine peritoneal CD11c+ cells, our data suggest a central role for ES products of M. corti (McES) in the modulation of host DC responses in vivo.
240.Rationale: The study shows that live (metabolically active) tetrathyridia recruit substantially more CD11c+ cells than heat-killed larvae, and the authors explicitly link this to the capacity of live helminths to excrete-secrete molecules that interact with host immune cells. Because McES (products of live parasites) mediate DC suppression in vitro and heat-killed larvae do not produce sustained recruitment (and thus would not produce ES products), it is reasonable from the presented data to infer that the suppression depends on parasite metabolic activity (i.e., secretion by live parasites). This inference is directly supported by the live vs. dead comparison and the authors' stated interpretation.

240.Option: The immunosuppressive effect of M. corti tetrathyridia excretory-secretory products on dendritic cells is primarily due to the presence of cysteine-rich secretory proteins and annexins, which are known to inhibit proteases and modulate membrane dynamics.
Evidence: Specifically and by descending order of magnitude, the 5 most represented McES protein entities identified were: a novel hypothetical protein (MCOS_959401, exclusively detected in McES), an annexin homologue (Number 1, MCOS_561301, exclusively detected in McES), a hypothetical protein conserved among parasitic cestodes (Number 2, MCOS_155201, ~3-fold enrichment in McES), a cystatin (MCOS_775601, exclusively detected in McES) and an endophilin (MCOS_968201, 2.4-fold enrichment in McES) (S4 Table).

Close to 77% (27/37) of all identified proteins were exclusive to the active fractions (Fig 11) of which 40% (11/27), not detectable in the parasite somatic extracts, were associable to McES immunomodulatory potential ... those are by descending order of representation ... an annexin (MCOS_561201) ...
240.Rationale: The proteomic analysis did identify annexins and a cystatin among abundant/exclusive proteins in McES and active fractions, but the study did not functionally demonstrate that these specific molecules are the primary mediators of DC suppression. The option additionally claims 'cysteine-rich secretory proteins' (a distinct protein family) rather than the identified cystatin (a cysteine protease inhibitor), which is not explicitly reported in the passage. The authors present a list of candidate proteins and state that these will need functional testing; they do not conclude that annexins or cystatin are primarily responsible. Therefore the strong causal claim in the option is not supported by the presented data.

240.Option: The immunosuppressive activity of M. corti tetrathyridia excretory-secretory products is mediated by a combination of proteins and lipids that act synergistically to inhibit IL-12p70 production.
Evidence: lipid-free ES significantly diminished the production of LPS-driven IL-12p70 by BMDCs indicating that parasite released lipids are not required for McES-mediated BMDC suppression.
hiMcES completely failed to show such an ability (Fig 4B) indicating that parasite proteins are important for McES-driven BMDC suppression.

Predictably, Mock McESΔCHO impaired LPS-driven IL-12p70 release by BMDCs whereas McESΔCHO failed to show such an effect (Fig 4B). These results indicated that parasite carbohydrates are important for the suppressive effect of McES.
240.Rationale: The passage includes an experiment showing that lipid-depleted (lipid-free) McES retain suppressive activity, explicitly indicating that parasite lipids are not required. In contrast, protein denaturation and carbohydrate removal both abolished activity, supporting a protein-carbohydrate (glycoprotein) mediator. Thus the specific claim that proteins and lipids act synergistically is contradicted by the data and cannot be derived from the study.

241.Option: Because the PLC primer set is more sensitive than TgsGP and SRA primers, it can detect infections even when the target subspecies DNA is present at extremely low levels.
Evidence: To differentiate between these two scenarios, primers have been designed to screen for other single-copy genes [21], namely the single-copy phospholipase-C (PLC) gene, present in all members of T. brucei s. l. [22–25]. Samples found to have sufficient DNA can then be screened using primers specific for T. brucei s. l. subspecies.

When the performance of the HRM was compared to TgsGP and SRA PCR on the same DNA dilution series, the HRM was as sensitive at detecting T. b. gambiense DNA and tenfold more sensitive for T. b. rhodesiense DNA (S2 and S3 Figs).
PLC PCR identified 19 samples (19.8%) with sufficient DNA quantity (Table 2). All samples which were positive by PLC PCR were also positive by HRM.
241.Rationale: The passage describes the role of the PLC primers as a control to indicate whether sufficient DNA is present for subspecies-specific single-copy assays (TgsGP, SRA) to work, but it does not state that the PLC primers are more sensitive than TgsGP or SRA. The only explicit sensitivity comparison reported is between the HRM assay overall and the TgsGP/SRA PCRs, not between PLC primers and the subspecies primers. Thus the claim that PLC is more sensitive and can detect extremely low-level infections is not supported by the passage.

241.Option: The PLC primer set improves the overall sensitivity of the assay by amplifying a highly conserved region of the trypanosome genome, enabling detection of all T. brucei s. l. subspecies.
Evidence: To differentiate between these two scenarios, primers have been designed to screen for other single-copy genes [21], namely the single-copy phospholipase-C (PLC) gene, present in all members of T. brucei s. l. [22–25]. Samples found to have sufficient DNA can then be screened using primers specific for T. brucei s. l. subspecies.
To improve the reliability of the assay primers were included to determine if sufficient DNA is present for the sub-species primers to work.
241.Rationale: While the passage states that the PLC gene is present in all members of T. brucei s. l. and that PLC primers are used to determine whether sufficient DNA is present, it does not claim that the PLC primer set improves the assay's overall analytical sensitivity or that its purpose is to detect all subspecies directly. Its described purpose is to act as a DNA-quantity/integrity control so that negative subspecies results can be interpreted (i.e. absence vs insufficient DNA), not to increase sensitivity for subspecies detection.

241.Option: The inclusion of the PLC primer set enables the assay to detect both T. b. gambiense and T. b. rhodesiense in addition to confirming the presence of sufficient DNA, making it a comprehensive diagnostic tool.
Evidence: We aim to develop a qPCR HRM assay designed to distinguish between the two human infective sub-species of T. brucei s. l. To improve the reliability of the assay primers were included to determine if sufficient DNA is present for the sub-species primers to work.
these primers amplify a single-copy phospholipase-C (PLC) gene found in all members of the Trypanozoon group [22–25].

When the performance of the HRM was compared to TgsGP and SRA PCR on the same DNA dilution series, the HRM was as sensitive at detecting T. b. gambiense DNA and tenfold more sensitive for T. b. rhodesiense DNA (S2 and S3 Figs).
PLC positive control peaks were produced by 43 samples (44.8%), indicating sufficient target DNA for identification of either T. b. gambiense or T. b. rhodesiense.
241.Rationale: The multiplex HRM assay as a whole was designed to detect the two subspecies (via TgsGP and SRA targets) and the PLC primer set is included to confirm sufficient DNA quantity. However, the PLC primer set itself is not the subspecies-specific detector — it is a control for DNA sufficiency. The statement that PLC 'enables the assay to detect both T. b. gambiense and T. b. rhodesiense' misattributes the subspecies detection function to the PLC primers rather than to the subspecies-specific primers; therefore the option overstates the role of PLC and is not fully supported.

241.Option: The HRM assay's ability to detect sufficient DNA through the PLC primer set allows for more reliable identification of samples that are truly negative for T. b. gambiense or T. b. rhodesiense, reducing the risk of false-negative results due to low DNA quantity.
Evidence: For detection of these low copy genes, the presence of sufficient genetic material is crucial. A negative result may indicate the absence of the target species or simply that insufficient DNA is present.

To differentiate between these two scenarios, primers have been designed to screen for other single-copy genes [21], namely the single-copy phospholipase-C (PLC) gene, present in all members of T. brucei s. l. [22–25]. Samples found to have sufficient DNA can then be screened using primers specific for T. brucei s. l. subspecies.
PLC positive control peaks were produced by 43 samples (44.8%), indicating sufficient target DNA for identification of either T. b. gambiense or T. b. rhodesiense.
PLC PCR identified 19 samples (19.8%) with sufficient DNA quantity (Table 2). All samples which were positive by PLC PCR were also positive by HRM.
241.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that single-copy subspecies-specific targets require sufficient genetic material and that a negative subspecies result could reflect absence of the target or inadequate DNA. The PLC primers were included to distinguish these possibilities by indicating whether sufficient DNA is present. Thus including PLC improves confidence that a negative subspecies result is a true negative rather than due to low DNA, directly supporting this inference.

241.Option: The PLC primer set allows for the differentiation between active and latent infections in tsetse flies by detecting the presence of viable parasite DNA.
Evidence: PCR is widely used for the detection of trypanosome DNA, with highly sensitive assays developed for T. brucei s. l. [17] which includes T. b. gambiense and T. b. rhodesiense along with the animal trypanosome T. b. brucei. Successful amplification indicates the presence of one of the members of T. brucei s. l. but does not identify which member of T. brucei s. l. is present.

For detection of these low copy genes, the presence of sufficient genetic material is crucial. A negative result may indicate the absence of the target species or simply that insufficient DNA is present.

To differentiate between these two scenarios, primers have been designed to screen for other single-copy genes [21], namely the single-copy phospholipase-C (PLC) gene, present in all members of T. brucei s. l. [22–25].
241.Rationale: The assay (including PLC) detects parasite DNA and is used to assess whether sufficient DNA is present to interpret subspecies PCR results. The passage contains no information indicating that PCR or the PLC control distinguishes viable (active) infections from non-viable or latent infections. The quoted text indicates that successful amplification 'indicates the presence' of DNA but does not address viability; therefore the claim that PLC differentiates active vs latent infection is unsupported by the passage.

242.Option: The F1-ATP synthase γ subunit mutations that enable kDNA independence in T. evansi are directly responsible for reduced drug sensitivity in type A strains compared to type B.
Evidence: Bloodstream forms of T. evansi, T. equiperdum and laboratory-generated T. brucei strains that have lost all or critical parts of their kDNA, can survive without kDNA due to specific single amino acid mutations in the gamma (γ) subunit of the mitochondrial F1-ATP synthase [28].
Once the genetically modified T. brucei are independent from kDNA maintenance and expression, they become multidrug resistant to the diamidine and phenanthridine class of drugs [30].

Anaplasma is transmitted by different species of hard ticks [6] as well as mechanically via contaminated mouthparts of Stomoxys calcitrans and Tabanidae, among other biting flies, albeit with reduced efficiency [7,8]. From an epidemiological perspective, higher infection rates in a host population that experiences continuous and multiple-vector exposure can be inferred to reflect greater exposure-driven risk (i.e., greater effective susceptibility at the population level due to exposure). The available passage evidence supports this exposure-based inference; although it does not prove innate biological susceptibility, it does support that prolonged/multiple-vector exposure of camels is a plausible explanation for their higher infection rates.

Evidence: The present study was designed to investigate by means of survival curves the risk factors associated with reactional episodes by testing Brazilian leprosy patients and the effects of 15 SNPs at 8 genes.
From the 15 candidate SNPs (total counts and frequencies detailed at S3 Table), 2 showed different Kaplan–Meier curves between genotype groups (log-rank p-value < 0.05).

Here, we confirmed the association between IL6 and NOD2 SNPs and leprosy reactions among Brazilians, and demonstrated that the NOD2 genotype associated with a decreased risk for developing a reaction is also related to lower levels of IL-6 in the sera of non-reactional patients.

The passage therefore does not support the broad conclusion that genetic markers are unreliable overall or that research should cease; rather it indicates that results can vary by marker and population and that some markers warrant further study. 247.Option: The presence of risk alleles for leprosy reactions does not have additive or synergistic effects, suggesting that combining multiple genetic markers may not be feasible for developing a predictive genetic test.
Evidence: The present study was designed to investigate by means of survival curves the risk factors associated with reactional episodes by testing Brazilian leprosy patients and the effects of 15 SNPs at 8 genes.
From the 15 candidate SNPs (total counts and frequencies detailed at S3 Table), 2 showed different Kaplan–Meier curves between genotype groups (log-rank p-value < 0.05).

247.Rationale: The study reports single-marker survival and Cox analyses and identifies two SNPs with significant associations, but it does not report analyses of additive or synergistic effects among multiple risk alleles (no interaction or combined-marker model is presented). Because the passage contains no direct evidence testing additivity or synergy, one cannot conclude from these data that combining markers is infeasible. Absence of reported combined analyses is not evidence that additive/synergistic effects do not exist.

individuals carrying NOD2 rs751271-GT showed a borderline difference (p = 0.05), suggesting lower IL-6 production (GT median: 0.008) as compared to GG individuals (GG median: 0.53) (Fig 3B).

Thus some genetic markers do relate to measurable biological outcomes while others do not, and the existence of one null genotype–cytokine correlation does not justify the sweeping conclusion that genetic markers are not useful for prediction. The option overgeneralizes beyond the data.

The passage contains no data on human immunogenicity, safety, or duration of protection in humans, nor does it test IgY as a human vaccine. The observation of high titers in geese alone is insufficient to infer suitability as a prophylactic human vaccine; therefore the conclusion goes beyond what can be derived from the passage.

neither the anti-DENV IgY nor control IgY sera were enhancing at any dilution tested.

The option posits a specific mechanism (non-specific immune activation enhancing innate responses and reducing viral load independently of neutralizing antibodies), which is speculative and not supported by data in the passage. The observed partial protection could have multiple explanations (e.g., low-level cross-reactivity, experimental variability), none of which are established in the text. Therefore the causal mechanistic inference is not derivable from the passage.

248.Option: Because IgY does not bind human complement, it is unlikely to contribute to immune enhancement during secondary dengue infections, thereby reducing the risk of vascular leakage and severe disease.

However, the authors explicitly caution that the inability of IgY to induce complement activation "could prove to be detrimental or beneficial depending on the individual disease state." The option asserts a definitive beneficial effect (reduced risk of vascular leakage and severe disease) based solely on lack of complement activation. The passage does not provide evidence that absence of complement activation by IgY will reduce immune enhancement or vascular leakage in humans; indeed it states the effect could be either detrimental or beneficial. Thus the option overstates and is not supported by the passage.

249.Option: Despite repeated rounds of school-based deworming, hookworm remains prevalent in adult men in Kwale County, suggesting that school-based deworming alone is insufficient to control community-wide hookworm transmission.

Prevalence of hookworm infection was 19.1% (95% confidence interval [CI]: 16.4–21.7%); 12.5% in under 5s, 17.4% in 5–14 years and 20.7% in adults; with over 90% (3,394/3,750) of infected individuals exhibiting light infections (<= 1,999 epg).

249.Option: The persistence of moderate-to-heavy hookworm infections in adults indicates that the current deworming strategy has failed to reduce transmission, and therefore, the treatment regimen should be changed to target only the most vulnerable age groups.
Evidence: Prevalence of hookworm infection was 19.1% (95% confidence interval [CI]: 16.4–21.7%); 12.5% in under 5s, 17.4% in 5–14 years and 20.7% in adults; with over 90% (3,394/3,750) of infected individuals exhibiting light infections (<= 1,999 epg).

A higher proportion of the MHI infections were found in adults (ρ = 0.77, p<0.001) (Fig 4b), consistent with patterns shown in Fig 3a.

However, the recommendation to change the regimen to target only the most vulnerable age groups (which would typically mean children) is inconsistent with these findings: adults, not children, bear a substantial portion of infection and MHI infections and likely serve as a reservoir for transmission. Thus the proposed policy change (target only the most vulnerable age groups) is not supported by the evidence in the passage and would be counter to the implication that adults contribute importantly to ongoing transmission.

250.Option: The combination of CPA, CPB and CPC resulted in enhanced protection in liver (>93%) and spleen (>98%) which was even higher than all other CPs based vaccines tested against Leishmania so far.
Evidence: Of interest, the mean hepatic and splenic parasite burden (LDU) were lowered by ∼93% and ∼98% respectively, in animals immunized with cocktail antigens as compared to the adjuvant controls at 3 months post infection.

However, these research efforts have been largely restricted to cutaneous and zoonotic VL with variable success rate but have never been investigated against L. donovani, the causative agent of kala-azar.

However, the second clause—claiming the cocktail protection was higher than "all other CPs based vaccines tested against Leishmania so far"—is not supported by the passage. The text states prior CP vaccine work was largely in cutaneous/zoonotic models and had variable success and had not been investigated against L. donovani, so a blanket comparison to "all other CPs based vaccines" cannot be derived from the presented data.

Protein and DNA vaccine attempts using CPA, CPB and CPC in different adjuvants have been reported against L. major [15], [16] and L. infantum [17] in mice and canine models [18]. However, these research efforts have been largely restricted to cutaneous and zoonotic VL with variable success rate but have never been investigated against L. donovani, the causative agent of kala-azar.

However, the specific comparison to other named vaccine candidates (gp63, ORFF, LelF-2) tested in murine VL is not made anywhere in the passage. There is no data or quoted comparison presented to support the claim that rCPC is superior to those other candidates; thus this broader comparative assertion cannot be derived from the passage.

250.Option: The cocktail vaccine induced a synergistic enhancement of protection due to the cumulative increase in T-cell epitopes in a non-antagonistic manner.
Evidence: Taken together, these finding suggest that all three CPs present in the cocktail vaccine act synergistically to counteract the impaired T cell response after challenge for improved protection.

Highest level of protection against L. donovani was however achieved in hamsters immunized with the liposomal triple antigen cocktail which showed 13 log-fold and 16 log-fold reductions in the parasite burden in liver and spleen, respectively, compared to adjuvant controls (p<0.0001) after 3 months of infectious challenge.

Highest percent lymphoproliferation was observed in hamsters vaccinated with triple antigen cocktail which was significantly higher than rCPA (p<0.0001), rCPB (p<0.0001) and rCPC (p<0.0001) as single antigen.

MΦ from liposomal cocktail antigen immunized animals released the highest amount (7 µM) of NO which was ∼4.3 fold more than that of infected controls and ∼2-fold more than the single antigen (rCPA, rCPB, rCPC) immunized groups after 72 h post culture.
250.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the three CPs in the cocktail "act synergistically" to improve T-cell responses and shows multiple measures (greatest reductions in organ parasite burden, highest lymphoproliferation, highest NO production, and highest survival) consistent with synergistic enhancement of protection. While the precise mechanistic phrase "cumulative increase in T-cell epitopes" is a mechanistic interpretation, the authors' explicit use of the term "synergistically" together with the immunologic and efficacy data supports the inference that the cocktail produced enhanced, non-antagonistic T-cell–mediated protection.

250.Option: The use of TLR agonists with cationic liposomes allows concurrent antigen presentation along with targeting pattern recognition receptor (PRR) pathways, for effective expansion of effector T cells.
Evidence: Monophosphoryl lipid A (MPLA) is a Toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4) agonist with more than 100,000 human doses safely administered as a part of licensed hepatitis B and Human papillomavirus vaccines [6].

Mycobacterial glycolipid trehalose-6,6′-dimycolate (TDM; cord factor) is a potent immunostimulant known for its macrophage activation properties and induction of proinflammatory cytokines, and anti-tumor activity [7]. ... Recently, TDM has been shown to act via macrophage receptor with collagenous structure (MARCO), TLR2, CD14 and also macrophage-inducible C-type lectin (Mincle) receptors to exert its immunomodifying effects [8], [9].
When used together, both the adjuvants i.e. MPL and TDM non-specifically activate the immune system, allowing a better response to the associated immunogen [10].

Cationic liposomes protect the labile antigens from lysosomal degradation and take the advantage of electrostatic interactions with the cells' negative charge which makes them a natural target for antigen presenting cells (APCs), crucial for immune stimulation [4], [5].

Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) of MΦ cells incubated for 2 hrs with both Rh123-labeled liposomes (green), LysoTracker Red (red) and DAPI(blue) simultaneously showed successful co-localization (yellow) of labeled liposomes in acidic compartments such as lysosomes (Fig. S3, B) which confirms antigen presentation by MΦ.

MPL-TDM alone activates MΦs from naïve hamsters resulting in increased NO and ROS production, which are regarded as the potent microbicidal molecules responsible for parasite clearance from infected MΦs [45].
These data indicated that combining MPL-TDM with cationic liposomes result in increased the adjuvant activity of the formulation on the APCs in vitro, serving our rationale for its utilization.

All immunized groups receiving either of liposomal rCPA, rCPB and rCPC singly or as cocktail produced significant upregulation of IL-2 and IFN-γ mRNAs in comparison to controls, which progressively increased at 2 and 3 months post infection (Fig. 7A).
250.Rationale: The passage documents that MPL is a TLR4 agonist and TDM signals via PRR-type receptors (TLR2, Mincle, MARCO), and that both together non-specifically activate the immune system. It also shows that cationic liposomes target APCs and deliver antigen into lysosomal compartments (confirming antigen presentation). The combination (liposomes + MPL-TDM) increased APC activation (NO/ROS) and led to upregulation of Th1 cytokines and enhanced lymphoproliferation/DTH, consistent with effective expansion of effector T cell responses. Thus the inference that TLR agonists with cationic liposomes enable concurrent antigen presentation while engaging PRR pathways to expand effector T cells is supported by the presented experimental data.

250.Option: The liposomal formulation of rCPC alone provided the highest level of protection among all tested antigen formulations, surpassing even the cocktail vaccine.
Evidence: Of interest, rCPC induced greater protection than both rCPA and rCPB, when injected alone.

Highest level of protection against L. donovani was however achieved in hamsters immunized with the liposomal triple antigen cocktail which showed 13 log-fold and 16 log-fold reductions in the parasite burden in liver and spleen, respectively, compared to adjuvant controls (p<0.0001) after 3 months of infectious challenge.

Highest percent lymphoproliferation was observed in hamsters vaccinated with triple antigen cocktail which was significantly higher than rCPA (p<0.0001), rCPB (p<0.0001) and rCPC (p<0.0001) as single antigen.
250.Rationale: While the passage indicates that rCPC alone induced greater protection than rCPA and rCPB when administered singly, it also clearly states that the triple-antigen cocktail produced the highest level of protection overall (largest reductions in parasite burdens and highest lymphoproliferation). Therefore the claim that rCPC alone provided the highest protection and surpassed the cocktail is contradicted by the reported data.

251.Option: The absence of differences in life cycle duration between infected and non-infected ticks indicates that R. conorii conorii infection does not affect the overall fitness of Rh. sanguineus in laboratory conditions.
Evidence: No difference was observed between the duration of life cycle of R. conorii conorii-infected and non-infected ticks held at 25°C, but the average weight of engorged females and eggs was found to be significantly lower in infected ticks.

In conclusion, under laboratory conditions we did not find any difference in the duration of developmental stages of the life cycle of R. conorii conorii-infected Rh. sanguineus when compared to non-infected ticks [27].
The average weight of 31 infected engorged females was 0.38111 g (range, 0.2518–0.5389 g) compared to 0.4749 g (range, 0.2333–0.6203 g) for six non-infected engorged females (p = 0.0136).
The average weight of eggs from one female infected tick was 0.2099 g (range, 0.0998–0.2976 g, 31 samples) compared to 0.2919 g (range, 0.1321–0.4271 g, 6 samples) for non-infected eggs (p = 0.0021).
251.Rationale: The passage does report no difference in life cycle duration at 25°C, but it also documents statistically significant reductions in engorged female weight and egg weight in infected ticks. "Overall fitness" encompasses more than duration of developmental stages (including fecundity and body condition). Because the passage shows negative effects on weight and egg weight, it does not support the stronger claim that infection does not affect overall fitness in laboratory conditions.

251.Option: The lower fecundity of R. conorii conorii-infected female ticks, indicated by reduced engorged female and egg weights, suggests that the prevalence of infection in a tick population would gradually decline and disappear without periodic re-infection.
Evidence: No difference was observed between the duration of life cycle of R. conorii conorii-infected and non-infected ticks held at 25°C, but the average weight of engorged females and eggs was found to be significantly lower in infected ticks.
The average weight of 31 infected engorged females was 0.38111 g ... compared to 0.4749 g ... (p = 0.0136).
The average weight of eggs from one female infected tick was 0.2099 g ... compared to 0.2919 g ... (p = 0.0021).
Twelve successive generations were obtained, and the infected colony is still growing in our laboratory as of this writing (Figure 2).
These data suggest 100% transstadial and transovarial transmission of R. conorii conorii in Rh. sanguineus ticks.
251.Rationale: While the passage documents reduced engorged female and egg weights (evidence of reduced fecundity), it simultaneously shows strong evidence that infection is efficiently transmitted vertically (100% transstadial and transovarial transmission, high filial infection rates) and that an infected colony persisted and grew for at least twelve generations in the laboratory. Those data argue against a simple conclusion that prevalence would necessarily 'gradually decline and disappear' without re-infection. The passage provides contradictory evidence (reduced fecundity versus robust vertical maintenance), so the claim that prevalence would disappear cannot be derived solely from the passage.

251.Option: The 100% transovarial transmission of R. conorii conorii in Rh. sanguineus over twelve generations implies that the infection can be maintained indefinitely in tick populations without the need for vertebrate reservoirs.
Evidence: Twelve successive generations were obtained, and the infected colony is still growing in our laboratory as of this writing (Figure 2).

For the 10th generation, all engorged nymphs (20/20), all adults randomly chosen (16/16), 4/4 females after laying eggs and the pools of eggs from these females tested positive by RT-PCR for rickettsial DNA. These data suggest 100% transstadial and transovarial transmission of R. conorii conorii in Rh. sanguineus ticks.

The filial transmission rate (FIR, proportion of infected eggs or larvae obtained from an infected female) of R. conorii conorii was 99.07% (107/108), 94.3% (66/70) and 95.5% (21/22) in larvae from several infected females of the 3rd, 4th, and 11th generations, respectively.

These data suggested that transstadial transmission ... but also transovarial transmission (TOT ... ) of R. conorii conorii occurs in ticks and consequently that Rh. sanguineus ... could act not only as a vector but also as a reservoir of R. conorii conorii [1].
251.Rationale: The passage provides direct experimental evidence of essentially complete vertical (transovarial) and transstadial transmission across many laboratory generations, plus very high filial infection rates and an infected colony "still growing." These findings support the epidemiological inference that R. conorii conorii infection can be self-sustaining in tick populations without requiring vertebrate reservoirs to maintain transmission, i.e., ticks can act as a reservoir. While 'indefinitely' is a strong word, the laboratory data demonstrating maintenance through a dozen generations logically imply potential long-term persistence in tick populations via vertical transmission.

251.Option: The high mortality rate of R. conorii conorii-infected engorged nymphs and adults at 37°C compared to non-infected ticks suggests that temperature extremes may prevent the establishment of new MSF foci in warmer regions.
Evidence: Significantly more engorged, infected nymphs maintained at 37°C died than non-infected nymphs maintained at the same temperature (17–67%, 101/300 and 4–7%, 15/300, respectively, p = 0.0001).
In the third batch, which was maintained at 37°C for one month, 37.5–85% of infected ticks and 15–40% of non-infected ticks died (75/120 vs. 29/120, p = 0.0001).
In conclusion, infected ticks (nymphs and adults) had a comparatively higher rate of mortality at 37°C than at 25°C...

Those ecological and epidemiologic observations contradict the sweeping inference that temperature extremes would prevent establishment of foci in warmer regions. The laboratory mortality data alone are insufficient to conclude prevention of establishment in real-world warm regions where ticks experience variable microclimates and other ecological factors; therefore the option overreaches beyond the passage evidence.

251.Option: The reactivation phenomenon observed in R. conorii conorii-infected ticks at elevated temperatures explains why infected ticks are more likely to survive winter conditions in temperate climates.

On the contrary, the experimental results show increased mortality of infected ticks after exposure to low (4°C) or high (37°C) temperatures and after cooling, indicating infected ticks fared worse under these temperature stresses. There is no evidence in the passage that infected ticks are more likely to survive winter conditions; the data suggest the opposite for some stages. Therefore the proposed explanation invoking reactivation is unsupported by the passage.

252.Option: The addition of Quil A significantly (P<0.05) increased IgG2c but not significantly IgG1 titers by ∼5–10 fold (Fig. 5B–C). In summary, a single 1 µg dose of insect-cell derived CHIKV VLPs induced potent CHIKV-specific neutralizing antibody and IgG titers.
Evidence: The addition of Quil A significantly (P<0.05) increased IgG2c but not significantly IgG1 titers by ∼5–10 fold (Fig. 5B–C).
In summary, a single 1 µg dose of insect-cell derived CHIKV VLPs induced potent CHIKV-specific neutralizing antibody and IgG titers.

All groups receiving CHIKV antigens generated neutralizing antibody titers that are significantly different (P<0.01) from both control groups (PBS and Ac-GFP) but not significantly different from one another (Fig. 5A).
252.Rationale: The statements in the option are direct assertions made in the Passage Body. The passage explicitly reports that Quil A significantly increased IgG2c (but not IgG1) by ~5–10 fold and that a single 1 µg VLP dose induced potent CHIKV-specific neutralizing antibody and IgG titers. Therefore this option is derivable from the passage text.

252.Option: The complete CHIKV structural cassette was cloned downstream the strong polyhedrin promoter of the AcMNPV baculovirus (Ac-S27). Western analysis on cell and medium fractions of Sf21-cells that were infected with Ac-S27 indicated that glycoprotein processing (glycosylation, furin cleavage and surface localization) is efficient and that the functionality of E1 as fusion protein is retained.
Evidence: The coding sequence of the structural polyprotein was cloned downstream the polyhedrin promoter in an AcMNPV backbone, after which Sf21 cells were infected with a MOI = 10 TCID50/ml.

Western analysis of the cell fraction yielded protein bands of ∼50 kDa for α-E1 (Fig. 1B, lane 2) and two bands of ∼50 kDa and ∼57 kDa for α-E2 (Fig. 1C, lane 2). These sizes correspond to predicted molecular masses of E1 (47.5 kDa), E2 (47.3 kDa) and its precursor E3E2 (54.6 kDa), respectively.

PNGase F treatment resulted in an expected reduction in molecular mass of CHIKV-E1 in both the cell fraction and purified VLPs (Fig. 1C, lane 2–5), indicating that E1 was efficiently N-glycosylated by the insect cells.
Expression analysis has made clear that E1 and E2 are both glycosylated and that a fraction of PE2 is processed by furin.
Treated cells displayed ring-like structures (Fig. 2, middle and bottom), indicating that the glycoproteins are indeed exposed at the surface of the infected cells.
Therefore, it can be concluded that E1 retains its fusogenic properties when expressed in Sf-cells.
252.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes cloning the structural cassette downstream of the polyhedrin promoter in AcMNPV (Ac-S27). Western blots showed bands matching mature and precursor glycoproteins, PNGase F experiments demonstrated N-glycosylation, the text notes PE2 processing by furin, immunofluorescence showed surface exposure, and syncytia assays demonstrated retained E1 fusogenicity. All elements of the option are directly supported by those quoted passages.

252.Option: The 1 µg non-adjuvanted VLP vaccine elicited a similar neutralizing antibody response as compared to 10 µg of inactivated virus (Fig. 5A), yet induced lower IgG2c titers (Fig. 5C). This might suggest that chemical inactivation results in generation of antibodies with reduced neutralizing activity, although further experimentation would be required to confirm this.
Evidence: The 1 µg VLP dose induced neutralizing antibody titers comparable with those observed after vaccination with 10 µg of inactivated CHIKV (Fig. 5A).
At the 1 µg dose, VLPs produced ∼10–20 fold, significantly lower antibody titers (IgG1 P<0.05, IgG2c P<0.01) than 10 µg inactivated CHIKV.
252.Rationale: The first two clauses of the option accurately restate results reported in the passage: 1 µg VLPs produced neutralizing titers comparable to 10 µg inactivated virus, and the VLPs had lower IgG1/IgG2c ELISA titers than the inactivated preparation. However, the speculative causal inference that "chemical inactivation results in generation of antibodies with reduced neutralizing activity" is not supported by the passage. In fact, neutralizing activity was similar between 1 µg VLP and 10 µg inactivated CHIKV, so the data do not indicate that inactivation produced antibodies with reduced neutralizing activity. That causal suggestion requires additional evidence not provided in the Passage Body; thus the option as written is not derivable from the passage.

252.Option: A single low-dose immunization with 1 µg of non-adjuvanted CHIKV VLPs provided complete protection against viraemia and foot swelling caused by CHIKV infection.
Evidence: All animals vaccinated with 1 µg VLPs were completely protected against viraemia (Fig. 6A).
The 1 µg dose of VLPs (with or without Quil A) provided complete protection against foot swelling, while the 0.1 µg dose reduced the peak foot swelling from 60–70% to 20–30% (Fig. 6B).
252.Rationale: The passage reports challenge results showing that all mice given a single 1 µg VLP dose had no detectable viraemia and were completely protected from foot swelling. Therefore the statement that a single low-dose (1 µg) non-adjuvanted VLP immunization provided complete protection against both outcomes is directly supported by the Passage Body.

252.Option: The VLPs induced a balanced IgG1/IgG2c response, in contrast with CHIKV infection where IgG2c responses dominate [12]. Whether this would have an important effect on protection is unclear.
Evidence: CHIKV-specific IgG1 and IgG2c titers were determined by ELISA, and broadly similar titers were seen for IgG1 and IgG2c (Fig. 5B, C), which contrasts with natural CHIKV infection where IgG2c dominates [12].
252.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that vaccinated mice had broadly similar IgG1 and IgG2c titers (a balanced response) and notes this contrasts with natural infection where IgG2c dominates. The second sentence in the option noting uncertainty about whether this difference affects protection is a cautious statement not contradicted by the passage; the Passage Body does not provide evidence that the isotype balance materially changes protective efficacy, so concluding that the effect on protection is unclear is a reasonable and derivable statement from the information provided.

253.Option: The persistence of H. ducreyi after MDA is due to the high rate of reinfection following clinical cure, which is more common in children than in adults.
Evidence: The infectivity of H. ducreyi is high [7], and reinfections following clinical and microbiological cure of H. ducreyi genital ulcers are common [8].
Overall, 16 (21%, 95%CI 12–31) of 78 asymptomatic participants had H. ducreyi bacterial DNA on the skin, and 1 (1%) had T. p. pertenue DNA.
253.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that reinfections following cure of H. ducreyi genital ulcers are common, so reinfection is a plausible contributor to persistence. However, the passage provides no evidence that reinfection rates differ by age or that reinfections are "more common in children than in adults." The data presented about asymptomatic carriage are from children, but nothing in the text supports the specific claim that reinfection is more common in children. Therefore the age-specific portion of the statement is not derivable from the passage.

253.Option: The presence of H. ducreyi DNA in flies and bed linens indicates that these vectors are the primary source of new infections in the community.
Evidence: Flies (n = 10) were caught outside 10 houses of patients with ulcers; 9 (90%) of 10 flies had H. ducreyi DNA and 5 (50%) had amplifiable T. p. pertenue DNA (Table 3).

The buffer used to transport the flies yielded amplifiable H. ducreyi DNA in 6 (60%) of 10 specimens, but none yielded amplifiable T. p. pertenue DNA, perhaps suggesting different location of the two bacteria in flies (i.e. T. p. pertenue as a regurgitating pathogen in the gut), or dilution effect related to differential bacterial load of H. ducreyi and T. p. pertenue carried by affected flies.
Environmental contamination was observed in 3 (50%) of 6 bed sheets sampled from 6 households with index cases; 2 sheets had detectable H. ducreyi DNA and 1 had detectable T. p. pertenue DNA.
Nevertheless, there is no clear evidence of H. ducreyi or yaws transmission by insects or fomites.
253.Rationale: While the study detected H. ducreyi DNA on flies and bed linens, the passage explicitly states there is "no clear evidence of H. ducreyi or yaws transmission by insects or fomites." Detection of DNA alone does not prove that these are the primary sources of new infections. The data support that flies and linens may be environmental reservoirs or vectors worth investigating, but do not establish them as the primary source of community transmission; that stronger causal inference is not provided in the passage.

253.Option: The persistence of H. ducreyi after mass azithromycin treatment may be explained by asymptomatic skin colonization, environmental reservoirs such as flies and bed linens, and incomplete penetration of azithromycin to the skin surface.
Evidence: However, in rural villages of Lihir Island, MDA drastically decreased the proportion of ulcers containing T. p. pertenue DNA, but had limited impact on H. ducreyi [12].

If H. ducreyi adheres and survives on the healthy skin of asymptomatic carriers, this could enhance its persistence in the community and its transmission, because skin-colonizing bacteria might escape systemic azithromycin treatment and could infect the skin after a minor abrasion.
Overall, 16 (21%, 95%CI 12–31) of 78 asymptomatic participants had H. ducreyi bacterial DNA on the skin, and 1 (1%) had T. p. pertenue DNA.

Of 17 cultures obtained from asymptomatic contacts, 8 (47%) yielded no growth and 9 (53%) yielded small gram negative rods or coccobacilli. Six of the gram negative organisms were tested by PCR and 2 were confirmed to be H. ducreyi.
Flies (n = 10) were caught outside 10 houses of patients with ulcers; 9 (90%) of 10 flies had H. ducreyi DNA ...
Environmental contamination was observed in 3 (50%) of 6 bed sheets sampled from 6 households with index cases; 2 sheets had detectable H. ducreyi DNA and 1 had detectable T. p. pertenue DNA.
253.Rationale: The passage documents limited impact of MDA on H. ducreyi despite effectiveness against T. p. pertenue, reports detection of H. ducreyi DNA (and culture-confirmed organisms) on intact skin of asymptomatic children, and shows environmental contamination (flies, bed linens). The authors explicitly hypothesize that skin-colonizing bacteria might "escape systemic azithromycin treatment," providing a mechanistic explanation for persistence. Together, these statements support the multi-factor explanation in the option (asymptomatic colonization, environmental reservoirs, and incomplete clearance by systemic azithromycin) as derivable from the passage.

253.Option: The high prevalence of H. ducreyi in asymptomatic children suggests that the organism is more transmissible through sexual contact than through non-intact skin contact.
Evidence: Both infections are thought to be exclusively transmitted through non-intact skin contact with infectious lesions.
Overall, 16 (21%, 95%CI 12–31) of 78 asymptomatic participants had H. ducreyi bacterial DNA on the skin, and 1 (1%) had T. p. pertenue DNA.

Asymptomatic carriage of H. ducreyi was demonstrated by PCR in the genital mucosa of about 2% of sex workers in The Gambia [14], but no studies address whether either H. ducreyi or T. p. pertenue potentially colonize non-genital skin.
253.Rationale: The passage states that both infections are thought to be transmitted via non-intact skin contact with infectious lesions and documents asymptomatic carriage on non-genital skin of children. There is no evidence in the passage to support the claim that H. ducreyi is "more transmissible through sexual contact." On the contrary, the data on asymptomatic skin carriage in children and the stated transmission route through non-intact skin point away from a sexual-contact predominance in this setting. Thus the option is not supported by the passage.

253.Option: The limited impact of azithromycin on H. ducreyi is likely due to widespread resistance to macrolides in the H. ducreyi population.
Evidence: A single oral dose of 30 mg/kg azithromycin is highly effective for treatment of yaws [9], while 15 mg/kg generally provides effective treatment and prophylaxis against H. ducreyi genital ulcer strains [10].
Cutaneous strains of H. ducreyi are also susceptible to macrolides in vitro [11].
However, in rural villages of Lihir Island, MDA drastically decreased the proportion of ulcers containing T. p. pertenue DNA, but had limited impact on H. ducreyi [12].
253.Rationale: The passage indicates that H. ducreyi (genital and cutaneous strains) is susceptible to macrolides and that the azithromycin regimens are generally effective against H. ducreyi strains, arguing against the conclusion that widespread macrolide resistance explains the limited impact. The authors instead explore alternative explanations (asymptomatic skin colonization, environmental reservoirs, escape from systemic treatment). The passage provides no data demonstrating macrolide resistance in H. ducreyi in this setting, so the resistance explanation cannot be derived from the passage.

254.Option: The frequent and prolonged interactions between humans and bats in buildings, combined with common attempts to remove bats, create repeated opportunities for pathogen exposure through both direct and indirect contact, as well as through domestic animals that may act as intermediate hosts.
Evidence: We surveyed 102 indigenous Taita people who lived or worked in buildings used by bats (S1 Table). Over 70% of people reported bat use of their buildings for >5 years (n = 72), with bat presence for 5–10 years most commonly reported (χ2 = 36.52, P < 0.01, Fig 1). Most properties (88%) had bat presence year-round (n = 90).

Survey participants described frequent exposure to bats that would support pathogen transmission through two main routes: direct and indirect (fecal/oral) contact, with indirect contact between bats and humans reported more frequently than direct contacts (χ2 = 24.77, P < 0.01, Fig 2A).
Close to half of participants (39%) reported direct contact with bats, including people touching bats (n = 40) and one report of being bitten.

Over 90% of respondents reported indirect contact with bats, mostly through interactions with their feces and urine (n = 98). Daily occurrences of indirect contact were reported by most participants (78%, χ2 = 285.06, P < 0.01, Fig 2B) and children, spouses, house guests, and custodians were also involved in these interactions.
Attempts to remove bats from buildings were reported by almost 80% of participants (n = 81). Of those reporting removal efforts, almost half reported direct contact with bats (n = 34).
Domestic animals can also be exposed to these pathogens after contact with bats and their excreta or fluids [25].

Some also reported burning (n = 6), feeding domestic cats (n = 2), and burying bat carcasses (n = 1. Interestingly, 13 respondents reported seeing domestic animals (dogs, cats, and chickens) consume dead bats on their property, most often their own animals.
254.Rationale: The passage documents long-term and year-round bat presence in buildings and explicitly describes frequent exposures by residents via both direct (touching, occasional bites) and indirect (feces, urine) routes, including daily indirect contact. It also reports that attempts to remove bats are common and often involve direct contact, and that domestic animals consume dead bats or are fed carcasses.
