The specific assertion that low sensitivity biased the observed association toward the null is speculative and requires external methodological assumptions not provided in the text. Therefore this inference cannot be directly derived from the Passage Body.

295.Option: The detection of Bunyamwera virus in Anopheles gambiae and Culex univittatus in the Lake Victoria region indicates that these species are the primary vectors responsible for human outbreaks of Bunyamwera fever in East Africa.
Evidence: Among mosquito pools from 32 species sampled in Homa Bay County, Bunyamwera virus (Orthobunyavirus) was the only vertically transmitted arbovirus (pathogenic to vertebrates) detected.

It was identified by HRM analysis (Fig 2A), culture, and DNA sequencing (143 nt; 100% identity to GenBank accession KM507344, S1 Fig) from female Anopheles gambiae from Luanda Nyamasare (1/77 pools) and Cx. univittatus from Rusinga (2/31 pools) (Table 2) that were reared from larvae sampled in November 2012.
295.Rationale: The passage documents detection of Bunyamwera virus in adult An. gambiae and Cx. univittatus reared from larvae in the Lake Victoria region, but provides no data linking these species to human outbreak transmission or demonstrating they are the primary vectors for human disease. Presence in field-collected immatures indicates infection and possible vertical maintenance, but does not establish primary vector status for human outbreaks; that would require epidemiologic linkage to human cases, vector competence/transmission efficiency in nature, or comparative vector prevalence data not provided in the passage.

295.Option: The vertical transmission of Bunyamwera virus in Anopheles gambiae and Culex univittatus indicates that these mosquito species are more likely to transmit the virus to humans than other known vectors in the region.
Evidence: Among mosquito pools from 32 species sampled in Homa Bay County, Bunyamwera virus (Orthobunyavirus) was the only vertically transmitted arbovirus (pathogenic to vertebrates) detected.

It was identified by HRM analysis (Fig 2A), culture, and DNA sequencing (143 nt; 100% identity to GenBank accession KM507344, S1 Fig) from female Anopheles gambiae from Luanda Nyamasare (1/77 pools) and Cx. univittatus from Rusinga (2/31 pools) (Table 2) that were reared from larvae sampled in November 2012.
295.Rationale: While the study shows vertical transmission/detection of Bunyamwera virus in these two species, the passage contains no comparative data on transmission rates to humans, biting behavior, host preferences, or comparative vector competence among regional species. Therefore one cannot infer from these results alone that An. gambiae and Cx. univittatus are more likely than other vectors to transmit the virus to humans.

295.Option: The presence of Bunyamwera virus in adult mosquitoes reared from field-collected larvae suggests that the virus is primarily maintained in the environment through contamination of aquatic breeding sites rather than through vertical transmission.
Evidence: We collected eggs, larvae, and pupae with 350-ml standard dippers (Bioquip Products, USA) from their breeding sites and transported them to the Martin Lüscher Emerging Infectious Disease (ML-EID) Laboratory ... In the laboratory, we reared them to adults in their field-collected breeding water at 28°C temperature, 80% relative humidity, and 12-hour day and night cycles [20,21].
Among mosquito pools from 32 species sampled in Homa Bay County, Bunyamwera virus (Orthobunyavirus) was the only vertically transmitted arbovirus (pathogenic to vertebrates) detected.
It was identified by HRM analysis (Fig 2A), culture, and DNA sequencing ... from female Anopheles gambiae ... and Cx. univittatus ... that were reared from larvae sampled in November 2012.
295.Rationale: The study interprets detection in adults reared from field-collected immatures as evidence of vertical transmission (the passage explicitly labels this a 'vertically transmitted arbovirus'). Although rearing in field-collected water introduces a theoretical possibility of environmental contamination, the authors present the finding as vertical transmission and do not provide data showing that aquatic-breeding-site contamination is the primary maintenance mechanism. The passage does not support the strong claim that environmental contamination, rather than vertical transmission, is the primary means of maintenance.

295.Option: The absence of Bunyamwera virus in Baringo County samples suggests that the virus is not capable of persisting in mosquito populations outside the Lake Victoria region due to climatic or ecological differences.
Evidence: However, no vertically transmitted pathogenic arbovirus was detected in Baringo County samples.
Among mosquito pools from 32 species sampled in Homa Bay County, Bunyamwera virus (Orthobunyavirus) was the only vertically transmitted arbovirus (pathogenic to vertebrates) detected.
295.Rationale: The passage reports non-detection of vertically transmitted pathogenic arboviruses in Baringo County samples, but absence of detection in a given sampling effort does not prove inability of the virus to persist there or that climatic/ecological differences prevent persistence. Many alternative explanations (sampling timing, sample size, stochasticity, local prevalence below detection limit) could account for non-detection. The data do not permit the causal inference asserted in this option.

295.Option: The detection of Bunyamwera virus in adult mosquitoes reared from field-collected larvae indicates that this pathogenic arbovirus can persist through transstadial development and may be maintained in mosquito populations via vertical transmission, suggesting a potential reservoir role for Anopheles gambiae and Culex univittatus in the Lake Victoria region.
Evidence: vertical transmission of arboviruses from adult female mosquitoes to their offspring can also maintain viruses in circulation for generations within mosquito populations [6–10].
Among mosquito pools from 32 species sampled in Homa Bay County, Bunyamwera virus (Orthobunyavirus) was the only vertically transmitted arbovirus (pathogenic to vertebrates) detected.

It was identified by HRM analysis (Fig 2A), culture, and DNA sequencing (143 nt; 100% identity to GenBank accession KM507344, S1 Fig) from female Anopheles gambiae from Luanda Nyamasare (1/77 pools) and Cx. univittatus from Rusinga (2/31 pools) (Table 2) that were reared from larvae sampled in November 2012.
295.Rationale: The passage explicitly frames vertical transmission as a mechanism that can maintain arboviruses across mosquito generations and documents Bunyamwera virus detection (confirmed by HRM, culture, and sequencing) in adults reared from field-collected larvae of An. gambiae and Cx. univittatus. These facts support the epidemiologic inference that Bunyamwera virus can persist through mosquito development stages and may be maintained in mosquito populations via vertical transmission, which in turn supports the plausible interpretation that these species could act as a reservoir in the Lake Victoria area. The wording in the option is appropriately cautious ('may be maintained', 'potential reservoir role') and is directly supported by the study findings.

296.Option: The inter-population redundancy of amino acid sequences in V1 and V4 indicates that these regions are functionally constrained and therefore less likely to contribute to immune evasion in syphilis patients.
Evidence: the genetic variation in tprK is localized to seven variable regions (V1-V7) flanked by highly conserved domains [18–20].
The 14 strains are specified by coloured solid circles, and the predominant sequence and minor variants within each V region of one strain are represented by a bold arc and thin arcs, respectively.
V1 and V4 presented a strong shared sequence capacity.
The sequence IASDGGAIKH in V1 was observed in five strains (5/14) and DVGHKKENAANVNGTVGA in V4 was shared across seven strains (7/14).
For the V3 and V6 regions, which were rarely consistent with sequences, the shared sequence in V3 occurred only between two strains, and there was no consistent sequence found in V6.
296.Rationale: The passage documents that V1 and V4 show inter-population redundancy (identical amino-acid sequences shared across multiple strains), whereas other regions (e.g., V3, V6) are far less conserved. In evolutionary genetics, recurrent conservation of sequence across independent strains is evidence consistent with functional constraint (selection against change). Because immune evasion by antigenic variation requires variability, regions that are conserved across populations are less likely to be the primary contributors to antigenic escape. Thus, given the provided data showing V1 and V4 sequence sharing across strains, it is reasonable to infer these regions are functionally constrained and less likely to be central to immune evasion in the sampled patients.

296.Option: The low frequency of minor variants in tprK (mostly 1–5%) indicates that these variants are non-functional and do not contribute to the antigenic diversity of T. pallidum during primary infection.
Evidence: Apart from the detected predominant sequence within seven V regions, there was still a mixture of minor variants in each V region.
Altogether, the frequency of all detected minor variants was almost below 20% (231/237) (Fig 2A).
The major proportion of the variants in primary syphilis samples was in the 1–5% (181/237) range, and the lowest was in the 10–60% (22/237) range.
Theoretically, through gene conversion, variations in the V regions would generate millions of chimeric tprK variants, resulting in a constant alteration in the T. pallidum antigenic profile [21].
Therefore, the tprK gene is acknowledged to have a pivotal role in immune evasion and pathogen persistence [22, 23].
296.Rationale: While the passage documents that most minor variants are present at low frequencies (1–5%), it does not state that these variants are non-functional or irrelevant to antigenic diversity. In fact, the text describes a mechanism (gene conversion) capable of generating vast tprK diversity and explicitly links tprK variation to immune evasion. Low observed frequency in a cross-sectional sample does not prove non-functionality; such variants could represent a reservoir for selection under immune pressure. Therefore the categorical conclusion that they do not contribute to antigenic diversity is not supported by the passage.

296.Option: The high frequency of predominant sequences in V1 and V6 regions suggests that these regions are under stronger purifying selection compared to other variable regions.
Evidence: The number of distinct sequences in the seven regions of the tprK gene ranged from 21–76, with the highest number in V6 and the lowest in V1 across all samples (Fig 1 and S2 Table).

The proportion distribution of distinct sequences in seven regions of the tprK gene... there was a predominant sequence in each V region of ten samples directly from primary syphilis patients, and the proportion of this sequence was almost above 80%.
Then the frequency was found to be decreased in the V2, V5, V6 and V7 regions, and the frequency in V6 of X-6 was even lower at 20.8%.
V1 and V4 presented a strong shared sequence capacity.
For the V3 and V6 regions, which were rarely consistent with sequences, the shared sequence in V3 occurred only between two strains, and there was no consistent sequence found in V6.
296.Rationale: The passage indicates that V1 has the lowest number of distinct sequences and shows shared predominant sequences across strains, which could be consistent with stronger purifying selection. However, the data for V6 contradicts the premise: V6 has the highest number of distinct sequences, shows less inter-population consistency, and in some samples has a decreased proportion of predominant sequences (e.g., V6 frequency as low as 20.8%). Therefore one cannot conclude that both V1 and V6 are under stronger purifying selection; the passage supports purifying constraint for V1 (and V4) but not for V6. The option's grouping of V1 and V6 as similarly constrained is not supported by the evidence.

296.Option: The length variation in tprK sequences, particularly in V3, V6, and V7, is likely due to random insertions and deletions during PCR amplification, which introduces artifacts into the sequencing data.
Evidence: The length of the captured sequences within each V region was also found to be variable, particularly in V3, V6 and V7, with 11 or 12 forms.
When the length of all sequences within each sample was calculated, the length of all differed by 3 bp or multiples of 3 bp.
Interestingly, although the lengths of V3, V6 and V7 were particularly variable across all populations, these lengths continued to change by 3 bp.
No sequence yielded a tprK frame shift or premature termination.
The amplicons were gel purified and stored at -20°C for further processing as the template for segmented amplification described below.
296.Rationale: The passage documents systematic length variation that preserves the reading frame (changes by 3 bp or multiples thereof) and reports that no frameshifts or premature terminations were observed. These findings argue against random PCR-induced indel artifacts (which would be expected to produce random frame shifts and stochastic errors) and instead support biologically regulated, frame-preserving length variation. Additionally, the methods description shows careful amplification and purification steps, and high sequencing depth and coverage were reported, reducing the likelihood that the observed length polymorphisms are merely PCR artifacts. Therefore the specific causal claim attributing length variation to PCR artifacts is not supported by the provided text.

296.Option: The presence of a high proportion of a predominant sequence and numerous low-frequency minor variants in the tprK gene of primary syphilis patients suggests a fitness-based evolutionary strategy where dominant sequences are optimized to evade immune recognition, while minor variants may represent transient diversity that could be selected for under immune pressure.
Evidence: the tprK gene possesses high genetic diversity at both the intra- and inter-strain levels, and the genetic variation in tprK is localized to seven variable regions (V1-V7) flanked by highly conserved domains [18–20].
Theoretically, through gene conversion, variations in the V regions would generate millions of chimeric tprK variants, resulting in a constant alteration in the T. pallidum antigenic profile [21].
Therefore, the tprK gene is acknowledged to have a pivotal role in immune evasion and pathogen persistence [22, 23].
As Fig 2A shows, there was a predominant sequence in each V region of ten samples directly from primary syphilis patients, and the proportion of this sequence was almost above 80%.
Apart from the detected predominant sequence within seven V regions, there was still a mixture of minor variants in each V region.
The major proportion of the variants in primary syphilis samples was in the 1–5% (181/237) range...
296.Rationale: The passage documents a pattern of one dominant (predominant) sequence per V region in most samples, together with numerous low-frequency minor variants, and explicitly links tprK diversity and gene-conversion-driven generation of many variants to immune evasion and persistence. From these facts it is epidemiologically and evolutionarily reasonable to infer a fitness-based strategy: dominant sequences prevailing within hosts (consistent with being currently advantageous under prevailing immune pressures), while low-frequency variants provide a reservoir of diversity that could be selected if immune pressure changes. This interpretation directly follows from the documented distribution of predominant versus minor variants and the described mechanism and role of tprK variation.

297.Option: The fact that leprosy prevalence remains high in this region is likely due to the underreporting of PKDL cases, as both diseases share similar clinical presentations and diagnostic challenges.
Evidence: Considerable underreporting of PKDL is suspected.
An important condition in the differential diagnosis of PKDL is leprosy.
Among those screened, 2,466 (9.6%) had skin lesions. ... There were four PKDL suspects and 116 leprosy suspects.
Out of the four PKDL suspects, three were confirmed, equivalent to a prevalence rate of 1.2 per 10,000.
bringing the total number of prevalent active leprosy cases to 44 ... i.e. a prevalence rate of 17.1 per 10,000.
297.Rationale: The passage states (1) that PKDL underreporting is suspected and (2) that leprosy is an important differential diagnosis for PKDL, and it provides prevalence estimates for both PKDL and leprosy. However, the option asserts a causal explanation—that the high measured leprosy prevalence is "likely due to" underreporting of PKDL. The passage does not provide evidence that underreporting of PKDL has caused inflation of leprosy counts (misclassification), nor that PKDL cases are being systematically recorded as leprosy. It only notes diagnostic overlap and suspected underreporting of PKDL; that is insufficient to conclude that underreporting of PKDL explains the high leprosy prevalence. Therefore the claim is not supported by the passage.

297.Option: The observed prevalence of leprosy in the study population (17.1 per 10,000) is consistent with national elimination targets, suggesting that the disease has been effectively controlled at the local level.
Evidence: bringing the total number of prevalent active leprosy cases to 44 ... i.e. a prevalence rate of 17.1 per 10,000.
The global target of a prevalence of less than 1 case per 10,000 population was achieved by 2000.
India was among the countries that by 2005 had achieved an overall leprosy prevalence of less than 1 per 10,000, an enormous reduction from the 57.8 per 10,000 observed in 1983.
297.Rationale: The passage explicitly gives the study area's leprosy prevalence as 17.1 per 10,000 and states the global (and national) elimination target is a prevalence of less than 1 per 10,000. A prevalence of 17.1 per 10,000 is far above the elimination threshold, so the data are not consistent with the elimination targets and do not support the conclusion that leprosy has been effectively controlled locally.

297.Option: The low prevalence of PKDL (1.1 per 10,000) in the studied region indicates that the elimination of visceral leishmaniasis has been successfully achieved and sustained in this area.
Evidence: Out of the four PKDL suspects, three were confirmed, equivalent to a prevalence rate of 1.2 per 10,000.

In this area, exhaustive household surveys are organized on an annual basis to record demographic data. ... From this area, we selected the 27 villages that had reported the highest numbers of VL cases (276 out of 339) in the 10 years preceding the survey.

The VL elimination initiative on the ISC which aims to bring down the annual incidence of VL at sub-district level ... to below 1 per 10,000 population by the end of 2015. This target date has been extended to 2017 and more recently to 2020.
297.Rationale: While the passage reports a low measured PKDL prevalence (1.2 per 10,000), it does not provide evidence that this implies successful and sustained elimination of visceral leishmaniasis (VL). The area surveyed had reported substantial numbers of VL cases in the preceding decade (276 of 339 cases were in the selected villages), and the passage does not present VL incidence data demonstrating achievement of the VL elimination target. Moreover, PKDL prevalence alone is not sufficient to infer VL elimination status. Therefore the conclusion that VL elimination has been achieved and sustained cannot be derived from the presented data.

297.Option: The high spatial clustering of leprosy cases within 25 meters of an affected household suggests that leprosy transmission is primarily driven by close household contact, making household-based screening sufficient for effective case detection.
Evidence: Four out of 44 cases (9.1%) were found among 402 persons living in the same household as a leprosy patient, who make up 1.2% of the population.
Among the neighbors at less than 25 meters of an active leprosy case, who make up 3.5% of the total population, 15.9% of the leprosy patients were found.
At a distance of 50 meters or more from a leprosy case, the clear excess of leprosy patients no longer seemed to be present.

With those living at more than 100 meters as reference category we found rate ratios of 6.3 (95% CI 1.9–21.0) and 3.6 (95% CI 1.3–10.2) respectively for those living in the same household and neighbors at less than 25 meters of a leprosy patient.
297.Rationale: The passage demonstrates strong spatial clustering of leprosy at household and very-near-neighbor distances (elevated rate ratios for same household and <25 m). This supports that risk is higher among close contacts. However, inferring that transmission is "primarily driven by close household contact" is a causal statement not directly proven by spatial association alone, and the data also show elevated prevalence among neighbors <25 m (not only household), indicating that restricting screening to household members would miss nearby non-household cases. The passage does not provide evidence that household-based screening alone would be sufficient for effective case detection. Thus the stronger claim of sufficiency and exclusive primary driving by household contact is not supported.

297.Option: Leprosy is far from eliminated and requires intensified case finding approaches, as evidenced by a prevalence rate of 17.1 per 10,000 in a rural area of Bihar, India, which is 17 times the elimination target.
Evidence: bringing the total number of prevalent active leprosy cases to 44 ... i.e. a prevalence rate of 17.1 per 10,000.
The global target of a prevalence of less than 1 case per 10,000 population was achieved by 2000.

The fact that the leprosy is now considered eliminated from India as the target has been met, in all probability means that less resources are made available, less case finding activities are conducted, and therefore the incidence does not change. To examine this hypothesis, more data on leprosy at population level are needed.
Based on mathematical models, Blok et al. predict that leprosy is likely to remain a problem in highly endemic regions.
297.Rationale: The passage provides the local prevalence estimate of 17.1 per 10,000, and it states the elimination benchmark is <1 per 10,000. A prevalence of 17.1 per 10,000 is therefore far above the elimination threshold (about 17 times higher). The authors also discuss that reduced resources and reduced case-finding after declaring elimination may mean ongoing transmission and call for more data, and cite model-based predictions that leprosy will remain a problem in highly endemic regions. From these statements it is reasonable to infer that leprosy is not eliminated in this rural area and that intensified case finding is warranted. This inference is directly supported by the passage.

298.Option: Monoclonal antibodies that bind to the N- and C-terminus of GP1 and GP2 are sufficient to provide complete protection in guinea pigs when administered at any time after infection.
Evidence: they bound to a variety of GP segments, and that all 8 MAbs were able to pull down ZEBOV GP1,2 in an immunoprecipitation assay.

In those groups receiving treatment, with the exception of 2G4 and 4G7, none of the guinea pigs survived, but the mean time to death was significantly extended (range of 9.4–11.7 days, p<0.050). For 2G4 or 4G7, the survival rate was 60%, demonstrating that the MAbs can provide levels of protective efficacy individually in the more stringent guinea pig model.

all animals receiving the neutralizing MAb combination at 2 dpi. survived. When the treatment was given on 3 dpi the percent survival dropped to 66.7% with a mean time to death of 11.17±3.09 days. Receiving the combination either one day before or after resulted in a survival rate of 50%, with the meantime to death of 11.17±3.09 and 7.92±0.42, respectively.
298.Rationale: The passage does not state that MAbs specifically binding the N- and C-termini of GP1 and GP2 are sufficient; it only reports that MAbs bound to a variety of GP segments and pulled down GP1,2. Moreover, protection in guinea pigs depended on which MAb(s) and the timing: individual MAbs generally did not produce complete protection (except 2G4 and 4G7 at 60%), and the neutralizing combination provided 100% survival only when given at 2 dpi, with lower survival at other times. Thus the claim that such MAbs are sufficient to provide complete protection when administered at any time after infection is not supported by the passage.

298.Option: The failure of KZ52 to protect non-human primates despite success in guinea pigs suggests that monoclonal antibodies are unlikely to be effective in higher animal models.
Evidence: One neutralizing MAb KZ52 was 100% efficacious in guinea pigs, but did not protect NHPs.
Overall, the MAbs tested so far are not 100% efficacious in all animal models therefore further research is needed for more effective antibodies.
298.Rationale: The passage documents one example (KZ52) where efficacy in guinea pigs did not translate to NHPs, and explicitly notes that MAbs tested so far are not universally efficacious across models. However, the single example does not justify the broad inference that monoclonal antibodies are unlikely to be effective in higher animal models generally. The passage does not provide systematic evidence that rules out efficacy in higher models; it only shows variability and the need for further research. Therefore the broad negative conclusion about all monoclonal antibodies in higher models cannot be derived from the passage.

298.Option: The ability of monoclonal antibodies to improve survival in guinea pigs is primarily due to their neutralizing capacity, as demonstrated by the complete protection seen with the combination of neutralizing MAbs 1H3, 2G4, and 4G7.
Evidence: Overall, there are a variety of mechanisms employed by MAbs to improve survival, and the ability of the MAb to neutralize the virus is not essential.
The PRNT50 demonstrated that MAbs 1H3, 2G4, and 4G7 were neutralizing with a PRNT50 at a 1/200, 1/800, and 1/6,400 dilution, respectively (Figure 1).
all animals receiving the neutralizing MAb combination at 2 dpi. survived.
In those groups receiving treatment, with the exception of 2G4 and 4G7, none of the guinea pigs survived, but the mean time to death was significantly extended (range of 9.4–11.7 days, p<0.050).
298.Rationale: While the neutralizing combination (1H3, 2G4, 4G7) did produce complete protection at 2 dpi, the passage explicitly states that neutralization is not essential and that multiple mechanisms can mediate protection. Additionally, some non-neutralizing MAbs contributed to extended survival or, when combined with neutralizing MAbs, to complete protection. Therefore it is not justified from the passage to conclude that improved survival in guinea pigs is primarily due to neutralizing capacity; the evidence shows neutralization can be effective, but is not shown to be the sole or primary mechanism.

298.Option: A combination of neutralizing monoclonal antibodies targeting different regions of the Ebola virus glycoprotein provides complete protection in guinea pigs when administered up to 2 days after infection, representing a clinically feasible treatment window.
Evidence: they bound to a variety of GP segments, and that all 8 MAbs were able to pull down ZEBOV GP1,2 in an immunoprecipitation assay.
The PRNT50 demonstrated that MAbs 1H3, 2G4, and 4G7 were neutralizing with a PRNT50 at a 1/200, 1/800, and 1/6,400 dilution, respectively (Figure 1).
The combination of neutralizing MAbs (1.5 mg 1H3+1.5 mg 2G4+2 mg 4G7) was given to guinea pigs either 1 day before, or 1, 2 or 3 days after a 1000 LD50 GA-ZEBOV infection, and survival followed.
In contrast all animals receiving the neutralizing MAb combination at 2 dpi. survived.
When the treatment was given on 3 dpi the percent survival dropped to 66.7%... Receiving the combination either one day before or after resulted in a survival rate of 50%...
298.Rationale: The passage shows that the three named MAbs are neutralizing and that a combination of these neutralizing MAbs given to guinea pigs produced complete protection when administered at 2 days post-infection, with reduced protection at 3 dpi or when given earlier/later. The passage also states MAbs bound to a variety of GP segments, supporting the idea that combinations can target different regions of GP. From these data one can infer that a neutralizing MAb combination can provide complete protection in guinea pigs when administered up to 2 days after infection, which implies a post-exposure treatment window that could be considered clinically relevant as an actionable timeframe.

298.Option: The most effective monoclonal antibody treatment protocol involves administering a single dose of neutralizing MAbs 1 day before infection, as this timing maximizes survival in both mice and guinea pigs.
Evidence: In the majority of cases, treatment 1 day before infection resulted in lower survival rates than 4 days before infection.

treating the mice on 1 or 2 dpi was the most effective treatment time, with some MAbs (5D2; 100%, 5E6; 93%, 7G4; 100%, 10C8; 87%) being more protective when given at 1 dpi, and the others (1H3; 50%, 2G4; 70%, 4G7; 100%, 7C9; 90%) when given at 2 dpi.
all animals receiving the neutralizing MAb combination at 2 dpi. survived. ... Receiving the combination either one day before or after resulted in a survival rate of 50%...
298.Rationale: The passage indicates that 1 day before infection is not generally the most effective timing. In mice, post-exposure treatment (1–2 dpi) was typically most effective and in some cases pre-treatment 4 days before gave better results than 1 day before. In guinea pigs the neutralizing combination produced 100% survival at 2 dpi, whereas administration 1 day before produced only 50% survival. Therefore the statement that a single dose 1 day before infection maximizes survival in both models is not supported by the data.

299.Option: The urine-based ELISA and dipstick tests show significant clearance of anti-Leishmania antibodies in 90% of treated VL patients within six months, suggesting their potential as a test of cure.
Evidence: To determine the urine antibody levels in response to treatment, we selected paired urine samples from 18 VL patients at day 0, day 30 and day >180. There is no significant decline in the titre just after completing the treatment (day 30). Out of 18 urine samples, 16 showed a significant decrease (p<0.0001) in the urine IgG titre only after six months (day > 180) since the treatment started (Fig 5).

Out of 18 follow-up samples, 16 were entirely negative on and after six months of the treatment initiation (day >180). Two samples remained positive after 6 months of treatment till the data reported (S1 Table).
Our results confirmed the potential of urine samples in ELISA and dipstick test for the noninvasive diagnosis of VL and PKDL and as a test of clinical cure for human VL.
299.Rationale: The passage reports longitudinal data on 18 treated patients showing that 16/18 (≈89%) had a significant decrease in urine IgG titres by >180 days and that 16/18 were entirely negative on dipstick at six months. This corresponds to roughly 90% clearance within six months and the authors explicitly state the tests have potential as a test of clinical cure. Therefore the inference that urine-based ELISA and dipstick show significant clearance in ~90% of treated patients and suggest potential as a test of cure is directly supported by the presented data.

299.Option: Urine-based ELISA and dipstick tests are superior to serum-based rK39-RDTs for diagnosing VL in all endemic regions, including East Africa, due to their higher sensitivity and specificity.
Evidence: In the present study, we aimed to develop and evaluate urine-based ELISA and dipstick test as noninvasive diagnostic tools and compared with serum-based commercially available rK39-RDT using parasitologically confirmed VL cases from geographically important endemic areas of India.

rK39-RDTs are the most commonly used rapid test for sero-diagnosis of VL, especially in the Indian subcontinent where it gave sensitivity and specificity estimates of 97% and 90.2%, respectively [7]. However, their diagnostic performance varies from moderate in Latin America to even low in East African region [8].

Results of the dipstick test showed positivity in all VL urine samples, acquiring a sensitivity of 100% (97/97). None of the urine samples taken from negative controls showed reactivity with the dipstick assay, resulting in 100% specificity (75/75) of the test.
In comparison to urine dipstick test, serum-based rK39-RDT showed 95.87% sensitivity (93/97) and 97.33% specificity (73/75).
299.Rationale: While the study shows superior sensitivity and specificity of urine tests compared to rK39-RDT within this Indian study population, the authors explicitly note that rK39 performance varies by region (including low performance in East Africa) and the urine assays were evaluated using Indian samples. The passage does not provide data from other endemic regions (e.g., East Africa) to support the blanket claim of superiority 'in all endemic regions'. Therefore this broader geographic generalization is not supported by the presented evidence.

299.Option: The dipstick test's high specificity and sensitivity in detecting VL and PKDL make it a reliable tool for diagnosing relapse in patients who remain positive after six months of treatment.
Evidence: Results of the dipstick test showed positivity in all VL urine samples, acquiring a sensitivity of 100% (97/97). None of the urine samples taken from negative controls showed reactivity with the dipstick assay, resulting in 100% specificity (75/75) of the test.
All 14 PKDL urine samples tested had positive results in dipstick assay thus showing 100% sensitivity of the test.

Out of 18 follow-up samples, 16 were entirely negative on and after six months of the treatment initiation (day >180). Two samples remained positive after 6 months of treatment till the data reported (S1 Table).
299.Rationale: The passage supports that the dipstick has excellent diagnostic sensitivity and specificity in this study and that two patients remained dipstick-positive at six months. However, there is no evidence presented linking persistent dipstick positivity at six months to true relapse (e.g., clinical recurrence, parasitological confirmation, or outcome). The authors do not claim the positive six-month results represent relapse, so concluding that the dipstick is a reliable tool to 'diagnose relapse' is not supported by the data in the passage.

299.Option: The delayed decline in urine antibody titres after treatment suggests that the immune response to Leishmania is slower to resolve in urine than in serum, indicating a longer duration of immune activity in the urinary system.
Evidence: Leishmania-specific antibodies remain within the infected blood for years after treatment. Thus serum-based antibody detection tests cannot serve as a test of cure [12].

There is no significant decline in the titre just after completing the treatment (day 30). Out of 18 urine samples, 16 showed a significant decrease (p<0.0001) in the urine IgG titre only after six months (day > 180) since the treatment started (Fig 5).
299.Rationale: The passage states that serum antibodies can persist for years after treatment, whereas urine antibody titres showed a significant decline by six months in most patients. Thus the evidence points the other way (urine antibodies decline sooner than serum antibodies), not that urine immune response is slower to resolve than serum. Because there is no direct head-to-head kinetic comparison showing urine declines more slowly than serum, and the provided statements indicate serum persistence is longer, the option's claim is not supported.

299.Option: The persistence of anti-Leishmania antibodies in serum for several years after treatment makes serum-based tests unsuitable for monitoring treatment response, but urine-based tests overcome this limitation by detecting antibodies only during active infection.
Evidence: Leishmania-specific antibodies remain within the infected blood for years after treatment. Thus serum-based antibody detection tests cannot serve as a test of cure [12].
Out of 18 urine samples, 16 showed a significant decrease (p<0.0001) in the urine IgG titre only after six months (day > 180) since the treatment started (Fig 5).

Out of 18 follow-up samples, 16 were entirely negative on and after six months of the treatment initiation (day >180). Two samples remained positive after 6 months of treatment till the data reported (S1 Table).
Our results confirmed the potential of urine samples in ELISA and dipstick test for the noninvasive diagnosis of VL and PKDL and as a test of clinical cure for human VL.
299.Rationale: The passage supports that serum antibody tests are unsuitable as a test of cure because serum antibodies can persist for years. It also shows urine antibodies decline in most patients by six months and that the authors consider urine tests as having potential as a test of clinical cure. However, the statement that urine-based tests 'detect antibodies only during active infection' is too absolute and contradicted by the data showing two patients remained positive at six months and that decline occurs over months rather than immediately. The passage does not demonstrate that urine antibodies are limited strictly to active infection, so the stronger claim in this option is not fully supported.

300.Option: The fact that 72% of dog owners adopted offspring of free-roaming dogs indicates that the rural population is generally accepting of stray dogs, suggesting that community-based dog adoption programs could be implemented without significant resistance.
Evidence: Dog owners who adopted their pets "off the street" were less likely to get their dogs vaccinated than those who either purchased them or were given them (OR 0.08, 95%CI 0.01–0.4, p = 0.001).
300.Rationale: The passage notes that some dog owners adopted pets "off the street," but it does not provide the specific statistic of 72% or any prevalence figure for adoption from free-roaming dogs. The claim that 72% adopted offspring of FRD is unsupported by any verbatim text in the passage. Because the numeric assertion and the broad conclusion about general community acceptance are not substantiated by the provided data, this option cannot be derived from the passage.

300.Option: Respondents from the high/middle socio-economic level had more positive attitudes towards free-roaming dogs, including being more likely to feed them and take injured dogs to a veterinarian, which could be leveraged to promote responsible dog ownership and population management.
Evidence: Participants from the high/middle socio-economic level considered FRD were useful (OR 3.09, 95%CI 1.06–8.97, p = 0.03), were likely to feed them (OR2.81, 95%CI 1.34–5.88, p = 0.005), and would take an injured stray dog to a veterinarian (OR 2.33 95%CI 1.0–5.48, p = 0.04).
300.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that high/middle socio-economic respondents view FRD as useful, are more likely to feed them, and are more likely to take an injured stray to a veterinarian. From an epidemiological and programmatic perspective, these documented positive attitudes identify a group whose behaviors could be engaged to promote responsible ownership and FRD population management. Therefore the strategic opportunity to leverage this subgroup for interventions is directly supported by the quoted data.

300.Option: Because younger respondents were less likely to view free-roaming dogs as a health threat, public health campaigns should prioritize youth education to reduce fear and promote coexistence with stray dogs.
Evidence: The younger respondents (≤34 years) did not consider FRD a threat to human health (OR 0.2, 95% CI 0.04–0.97, p = 0.05), and were more likely to feed them (OR 2.2, 95%CI 1.1–4.5, p = 0.04) than older participants.
300.Rationale: The passage shows younger respondents already do not consider FRD a threat and are more likely to feed them. The recommended strategic action in this option—to prioritize youth education to reduce fear—is inconsistent with the observed data (younger respondents are already less fearful). The passage does not provide evidence that youth lack appropriate knowledge or that reducing fear is a priority; thus the specific recommendation cannot be derived from the passage.

300.Option: The lack of significant factors influencing favorable practices for rabies elimination suggests that community-wide interventions should focus on improving access to post-exposure prophylaxis rather than changing attitudes toward free-roaming dogs.
Evidence: A stable multivariate logistic regression model with significant p-values could not be generated.
Of this latter group a similar proportion (73%) knew that it could be prevented by administering PEP to dog-bite victims or by vaccinating dogs against rabies.
300.Rationale: While the passage states that a stable multivariable model with significant p-values for attitudes/practices towards rabies could not be generated (indicating limited identified predictors), it does not provide evidence that improving access to post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) is a more appropriate programmatic focus than changing attitudes toward FRD. The passage documents some levels of knowledge about PEP (73% in one subgroup) but does not present data on PEP access, barriers to access, or comparative effectiveness of interventions. Therefore the prescriptive conclusion to prioritize PEP access over attitude change is not directly supported by the presented data.

300.Option: Since poultry owners were more likely to perceive free-roaming dogs as a threat due to attacks on backyard poultry, targeted rabies awareness campaigns should focus on livestock-owning households to improve preventive practices.
Evidence: A significant number of poultry owners reported that FRD attacked their backyard poultry for food (OR 3.2, 95%CI 1.07–12.1, p = 0.02).
households that owned poultry were found less likely to report the presence of a rabid dog to the authorities (OR 0.3, 95%CI 0.13–0.7, p = 0.01).
300.Rationale: The passage documents that poultry owners experience direct negative impacts from FRD (attacks on backyard poultry) and also that poultry-owning households were less likely to report a rabid dog. These findings identify livestock-owning households as a group both affected by FRD and exhibiting potentially suboptimal reporting behavior. From an epidemiological and programmatic standpoint, targeting rabies awareness and preventive interventions to livestock/poultry owners is a reasonable, data-driven strategic opportunity that follows directly from the reported associations.

301.Option: The detection of Rickettsia felis DNA in the cerebrospinal fluid of two fatal cases of acute neurological disease, combined with the absence of other identifiable pathogens and the presence of rickettsial-like CSF findings, suggests that R. felis may be a causative agent of severe neurological disease in humans.
Evidence: Two of 38 CSF samples (patients E-059 and E-063) were, however, positive for Rickettsia DNA as determined by genus-specific 23S rRNA qPCR.
Results were consistent with only Rickettsia felis (Table 1).
Amplicons were confirmed to be R. felis by Sanger sequencing (Figs 1–3).
All CSF and serum samples were negative for Leptospira and O. tsutsugamushi by qPCR.
Retroactive testing for virus was negative for both [12].
Blood cultures were negative for both cases.
Case 1 ... he was found to have CSF mononuclear pleocytosis.
301.Rationale: The passage documents detection and species-level confirmation of R. felis DNA in CSF from two fatal neurologic cases and reports negative test results for other bacterial (Leptospira, O. tsutsugamushi), viral, and blood culture pathogens. The CSF finding of mononuclear pleocytosis is a compatible inflammatory finding. Taken together, these observations support the epidemiologic inference that R. felis may be a causative agent of severe neurological disease (a cautious 'may be' causal statement is appropriate given the available data and testing).

301.Option: The seasonal co-circulation of Rickettsia felis with other rickettsial pathogens in Southeast Asia may compound healthcare system burden, particularly in pediatric intensive care units.
Evidence: R. felis has been increasingly identified worldwide as a cause of disease [6–8].
Thirty-eight CSF and 36 serum samples met volume and condition criteria for PCR testing.
Both patients positive for R. felis DNA were males > 30 years who lived in or near Manado (Coordinates: 1°29’35” N and 124°50’29” E).
301.Rationale: The passage does not present data on seasonality or co-circulation with other rickettsial pathogens, nor does it provide information on healthcare system burden or impacts on pediatric intensive care units. The cited material concerns identification of R. felis in two adult fatal cases and broader detection worldwide, but there is no evidence in the passage to support claims about seasonal co-circulation or specific effects on pediatric ICUs; therefore this inference cannot be derived from the passage.

301.Option: The 100% sequence identity of the 17-kDa and ompB genes between the Indonesian cases and the R. felis reference strain URRWXCal2 confirms that R. felis is a zoonotic pathogen with a stable transmission cycle in human populations.
Evidence: The 17-kDa and ompB sequences were 100% identical to the R. felis reference strain URRWXCal2 [3], and gltA was 99.91% homologous.

It is naturally passaged generationally in fleas and has been found in a variety of other arthropods, including mosquitoes, but the exact mechanisms of transmission, including the possible role of vertebrate reservoirs, are still unclear [4].
301.Rationale: While the sequence identity establishes that the organisms in these CSF samples match the R. felis reference strain (supporting species identification), the passage explicitly states that the mechanisms of transmission and the role of vertebrate reservoirs remain unclear. Sequence identity alone does not 'confirm' zoonotic status or a stable human transmission cycle; those conclusions require epidemiologic and transmission data not provided here. Thus the stronger claim in the option is not supported by the passage.

301.Option: The absence of skin rash, eschar, or regional lymphadenopathy in both cases suggests that Rickettsia felis infection typically presents without classical rickettsial signs.
Evidence: Neither patient was noted on the charts to have skin rash, eschar, or regional lymphadenopathy.
Typically, it presents as a mild, febrile illness that can be syndromically confused with many other undifferentiated fevers, especially in Africa and Asia, where diagnostic resources are limited.
301.Rationale: The passage records that these two patients lacked classical cutaneous rickettsial signs, but from two adult cases (and with the passage noting that typical presentation is a mild febrile illness that is often indistinguishable from other fevers) one cannot generalize that R. felis 'typically' lacks classical signs. The evidence is insufficient to establish typical presentation across infections; absence of such signs in these cases does not prove a general pattern.

301.Option: The failure of standard TB therapy in both cases, combined with negative acid-fast staining, provides definitive evidence that Rickettsia felis is the primary cause of death.
Evidence: TB meningitis was initially suspected for both cases but neither CSF was positive by acid-fast bacilli stain and neither responded to presumptive TB treatment.
Capacity to culture TB at the hospital was not available.
Two of 38 CSF samples (patients E-059 and E-063) were, however, positive for Rickettsia DNA as determined by genus-specific 23S rRNA qPCR.
Insufficient sera were available from these two cases for Rickettsia PCR.
301.Rationale: Although TB was suspected and did not respond to presumptive therapy and acid-fast stains were negative, these findings do not provide definitive proof that R. felis was the primary cause of death. The passage notes limitations (no TB culture capacity, insufficient sera for some tests) and other potential confounders; detection of R. felis DNA in CSF supports a possible role but does not alone constitute definitive causation of death. Therefore the strong causal claim in this option is not supported by the presented evidence.

302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate among elderly patients with severe dengue in this study, despite high-quality ICU care, suggests that age-related physiological decline and comorbidities are major contributors to poor outcomes.
Evidence: The in-hospital CFR was 41.3% (31 patients), and these patients had a median ICU stay of 4 (IQR: 1–8) days.
The mean age of the 75 patients was 72.3±9.3 years (ranging from 50.7 to 88.5 years, Table 1).
The most common comorbidities included hypertension (72.0%), diabetes (43.7%), and chronic kidney disease (22.7%).
The rates of underlying diseases and conditions necessitating blood component transfusions were similar between the survivors and non-survivors.

Old patients with DF are often associated with atypical presentations, longer hospitalization and more underlying diseases. In particular, diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and aging are associated with a higher risk of DHF/DSS and a worse outcome [6, 15–18].
302.Rationale: While the passage documents a high in-hospital CFR (41.3%) and that the cohort was elderly with many comorbidities, the study data do not show that comorbidities were more common among non-survivors: "The rates of underlying diseases ... were similar between the survivors and non-survivors." The introductory citations note that aging and comorbidities are associated with worse outcomes in general, but the cohort-specific data do not support the causal claim that age-related decline and comorbidities are major contributors to the observed mortality in this study. The option also asserts "despite high-quality ICU care," which is not stated or evidenced in the passage. Therefore this conclusion cannot be directly derived from the presented data.

302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate in elderly patients with severe dengue is due to the high prevalence of bloodstream infections, particularly late-onset candidemia, which contributed significantly to mortality.
Evidence: Nineteen bacterial and three fungal pathogens were identified in the bloodstream of 18 (23.7%) patients.
Seven patients experienced bloodstream infections early within 48 hours after ICU admission.
Notably, two patients experienced recurrent fever at the eighth and ninth day after the onset of DF, respectively, which was caused by candidemia.

The leading cause of fatality was multiple organ failure (14/31, 45.2%), and nine of these 14 patients had overt bleeding. Seven (22.6%) patients died of refractory shock and severe metabolic acidosis, six (19.4%) intracranial hemorrhage, three (9.6%) bloodstream infections, and one (3.2%) pneumonia with respiratory failure.
302.Rationale: Although bloodstream infections occurred in 23.7% of patients and there were two late-onset candidemias, only 3 of 31 deaths (9.6%) are attributed to bloodstream infections. The leading causes of death were multiple organ failure, refractory shock/metabolic acidosis, and intracranial hemorrhage. Thus the passage does not support the claim that bloodstream infections, including late candidemia, were the primary reason for the high overall case fatality; bloodstream infections contributed to some deaths but not the majority.

302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate among elderly patients with severe dengue in this study is primarily due to delayed access to medical care and inadequate fluid resuscitation, as evidenced by the high rate of cardiac arrest before ICU admission.
Evidence: Seven (9.3%) patients experienced cardiac arrest before ICU admission.

In the multivariate analysis, cardiac arrest before ICU admission (hazard ratio [HR] 6.26 [95% confidence interval: 1.91–20.54]), AKI at ICU admission (HR 2.48 [1.07–5.74]), and initial APTT longer than 48 s (HR 3.91 [1.69–9.07]) were independent predictive factors for in-hospital fatality after adjusting for gender, cardiac arrest before ICU admission, respiratory failure at ICU admission, presence of AKI at ICU admission and initial APTT longer than 48 s (Table 3).
The primary indications for ICU admission included severe gastrointestinal bleeding (23 cases, 30.7%), acute respiratory failure (18, 24.0%), and shock (10, 13.3%).
302.Rationale: The passage shows that cardiac arrest before ICU admission was an independent predictor of mortality (HR 6.26) and that 9.3% experienced pre-ICU cardiac arrest. However, the manuscript provides no data on timing of presentation relative to symptom onset, measures of pre-hospital delays, or adequacy of fluid resuscitation prior to arrest. While cardiac arrest is associated with higher mortality, attributing the high overall CFR primarily to delayed access to care or inadequate fluid resuscitation is not supported by explicit evidence in the passage.

302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate in this cohort is attributable to the limited availability of invasive life-supporting interventions such as hemodialysis and endotracheal intubation for elderly patients in Taiwan, which restricts their recovery potential.
Evidence: There are eight ICUs with a total of 104 beds at National Cheng Kung University Hospital, a tertiary hospital in southern Taiwan.
Approximately three-quarters (57, 76.0%) of all patients required ventilator support during the ICU stay.
At the ICU admission, 41 (54.7%) patients had AKI, according to the KDIGO criteria and 12 (29.3%) of whom needed renal replacement therapy during the ICU stay.
Four (5.3%) patients had end-stage renal disease and received maintenance dialysis.
302.Rationale: The passage documents substantial use of invasive life-support measures in this cohort: 76% required ventilator support and nearly 30% of those with AKI received renal replacement therapy. The hospital described is a tertiary center with multiple ICUs. There is no evidence presented that invasive interventions were limited or withheld for elderly patients. Therefore the claim that limited availability of life-supporting interventions explains the high CFR is not supported by the data in the passage.

302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate in this elderly cohort is primarily driven by the high APACHE II and SOFA scores, which reflect the severity of organ failure and are the most significant predictors of mortality.
Evidence: Median APACHE II score and SOFA score at the ICU admission was 20 and 11, respectively.

In the univariate Cox model, female gender, cardiac arrest before ICU admission, respiratory failure at ICU admission, high creatinine level, AKI at ICU admission, a high APACHE II or SOFA score, and APTT longer than 48 s were significantly associated with in-hospital fatality.

In the multivariate analysis, cardiac arrest before ICU admission (hazard ratio [HR] 6.26 [95% confidence interval: 1.91–20.54]), AKI at ICU admission (HR 2.48 [1.07–5.74]), and initial APTT longer than 48 s (HR 3.91 [1.69–9.07]) were independent predictive factors for in-hospital fatality after adjusting for gender, cardiac arrest before ICU admission, respiratory failure at ICU admission, presence of AKI at ICU admission and initial APTT longer than 48 s (Table 3).

APACHE II and SOFA scores include many clinical parameters and were not included in the final model of multivariate Cox analysis as they demonstrated collinearity with other variables. They were positively associated with acute respiratory failure, AKI, and cardiac arrest.
The area under the ROC curve (AUC) of APTT was 0.76 (95% confidence interval [CI]: 0.64–0.88), APACHE II score 0.84 (0.75–0.93), and SOFA score 0.85 (0.77–0.94).
302.Rationale: APACHE II and SOFA scores were associated with fatality in univariate analysis and had high AUCs, reflecting that severity-of-illness correlates with mortality. However, the multivariate analysis identified cardiac arrest before ICU admission, AKI at ICU admission, and initial APTT >48 s as independent predictors; APACHE II and SOFA were excluded from the final model because of collinearity with these organ-failure variables. Thus although APACHE II and SOFA reflect severity and are correlated with mortality, the passage does not support the statement that they are the primary independent drivers or "most significant predictors" in the multivariable-adjusted analysis.

303.Option: The availability of miltefosine in East Africa for visceral leishmaniasis treatment creates a strong foundation for immediate implementation of miltefosine as a first-line therapy for L aethiopica without further clinical evaluation.
Evidence: In terms of treatment of L aethiopica, the evidence base remains extremely limited.
It is clear that randomized clinical trials are highly needed and should be undertaken.
Miltefosine has been successfully used in more than 50 CL cases due to L aethiopica in Addis Ababa, but the findings have not been published (personal communication, Asrat Hailu).
The available in vitro data relating to these drugs suggest a good susceptibility of L aethiopica to miltefosine, paromomycin, pentamidine and amphotericin B (Table 5).
303.Rationale: The passage explicitly states the overall evidence base for L. aethiopica treatment is extremely limited and that randomized clinical trials are highly needed. While there are anecdotal/unpublished reports and in vitro data suggesting miltefosine activity ("Miltefosine has been successfully used in more than 50 CL cases..." and good in vitro susceptibility), these do not constitute high-quality clinical evidence sufficient to justify immediate implementation as first-line therapy without further clinical evaluation. Therefore the strong claim of immediate implementation based on current availability is not supported by the passage.

303.Option: The national and WHO guidelines recommend intramuscular paromomycin as the preferential first-line treatment for LCL caused by L aethiopica, supported by strong clinical evidence from well-designed trials.
Evidence: In terms of treatment of L aethiopica, the evidence base remains extremely limited.
A Cochrane review published in 2008 identified not a single randomized clinical trial dedicated to CL in Ethiopia [14].
Paromomycin ointment was highly effective in three Ethiopians treated in Israel.

We identified a total of 17 clinical studies reporting on treatment of CL or MCL (presumably) due to L aethiopica, relating to 384 treatment episodes. There were two small clinical trials, two prospective non-randomized studies and 13 case reports/series.
303.Rationale: The passage emphasizes the limited evidence base and paucity of randomized clinical trials specific to L. aethiopica ("not a single randomized clinical trial dedicated to CL in Ethiopia" according to the Cochrane review). Although paromomycin ointment showed efficacy in a small number of treated individuals, there is no statement about national or WHO guideline recommendations or robust, well-designed trial evidence supporting paromomycin as preferential first-line therapy. Thus the option's claims are not supported by the passage.

303.Option: The high genetic diversity of L aethiopica and its frequent association with mucocutaneous and diffuse forms suggest that treatment outcomes are inherently unpredictable and that no standardized therapy can be developed.
Evidence: Clinical manifestations of L aethiopica are particularly diverse and pleotropic, and a high genetic diversity has been documented as well [12].
Localized CL (LCL) is the most frequent manifestation, while mucocutaneous (MCL) and diffuse CL (DCL) is relatively common [7].
DCL is notorious for its chronic and progressive course and non-responsiveness to the common antileishmanial drugs.
303.Rationale: The passage documents high genetic diversity and a spectrum of clinical forms, including forms that are difficult to treat (DCL non-responsive to common drugs). These facts support variability in clinical behavior and challenges in treatment. However, the stronger claim that outcomes are inherently unpredictable and that no standardized therapy can be developed is not supported by the passage. The authors call for randomized trials and evaluation of interventions ("randomized clinical trials are highly needed and should be undertaken"), implying that standardized therapies could be identified with proper study, rather than asserting that standardization is impossible. Therefore the absolute conclusion in the option cannot be derived from the passage.

303.Option: Despite the lack of high-quality clinical trials, the consistent use of antimonials in clinical practice in Ethiopia indicates that their efficacy and safety have been well-established through long-term observational use.
Evidence: Most commonly used drugs were antimonials (n = 201), pentamidine (n = 150) and cryotherapy (n = 103).
In vitro data generally suggested a low susceptibility of L aethiopica to antimonials (Table 5).
Outcomes with systemic antimonials were reported in 38 cases, of which only nine demonstrated clinical response.

In a study from Tigray, clinical non-response was observed in 23 (15%) of the 154 treated (intralesional or systemic SSG). Failure rates were particularly high in MCL and DCL cases and all of the HIV cases relapsed.
303.Rationale: Although antimonials are commonly used in practice ("Most commonly used drugs were antimonials (n = 201)"), the passage provides evidence of limited clinical effectiveness and poor in vitro susceptibility for L. aethiopica, and notable rates of non-response in observational series. These data contradict the assertion that efficacy and safety have been well-established by long-term observational use. The passage instead highlights uncertainty and limited effectiveness in some contexts, so the option's conclusion is not supported.

303.Option: The evidence base for treatment of cutaneous leishmaniasis due to L aethiopica remains extremely limited, with most studies being small, non-randomized, and lacking rigorous methodology, despite the availability of certain drugs in clinical practice.
Evidence: In terms of treatment of L aethiopica, the evidence base remains extremely limited.
A Cochrane review published in 2008 identified not a single randomized clinical trial dedicated to CL in Ethiopia [14].

Out of a total of 95 studies or reports identified and screened, only 27 provided information relevant to our topic (Fig 1). This comprised 13 case series and seven case reports, generally clinical information collected as part of clinical practice. Most of these were reported over 30 to 40 years ago.

We identified a total of 17 clinical studies reporting on treatment of CL or MCL (presumably) due to L aethiopica, relating to 384 treatment episodes. There were two small clinical trials, two prospective non-randomized studies and 13 case reports/series.

Most studies had obvious limitations, beyond the descriptive nature. Definitions of treatment outcomes varied across studies and were often not clearly defined. Sample size was often small, follow-up short and information on patient characteristics often limited. In formal quality assessment, the non-randomized studies performed poorly, with a median score of three (maximum score is eight).
303.Rationale: The passage repeatedly states that the treatment evidence base is limited and documents the predominance of small case series, case reports, and few small/non-randomized trials, with methodological shortcomings (variable outcome definitions, small sample sizes, short follow-up, poor quality assessment scores). It also describes multiple drugs being used in clinical practice and some in vitro or anecdotal clinical activity, but emphasizes that rigorous clinical evidence is lacking. Thus the option accurately summarizes the state of the published clinical research.

305.Option: The lack of association between IL18 haplotypes and infection risk suggests that the functional effects of the gene are not relevant in the immune response to T. cruzi.
Evidence: Five possible haplotypes were observed (rs2043055|rs187238|rs1946518|rs360719: TCAC, CGCT, TGCT, TGAT and CGAT), with the haplotypes TCAC and CGCT showing the higher frequencies (37.30% and 37.40%, respectively).

In relation to the seronegative vs seropositive analysis, the frequency of TCAC was increased in the former individuals, being this difference statistically significant (P = 1.50E-03, PFDR = 7.50E-03, OR = 0.76, CI = 0.65–0.90).
On the other hand, the frequency of CGCT was significantly lower in seronegative individuals compared with seropositive individuals (P = 0.0116, PFDR = 0.0290, OR = 1.25, CI = 1.05–1.47),

The haplotype model did not better explain the IL18 association to risk of infection than the model considering the SNPs independently (likelihood P-value = 0.1454), indicating no additive effects (that is, the associated haplotypes were a consequence of the independent associations of the considered variants).

rs187238*C and rs360719*C were significantly increased in seronegative individuals compared to the seropositive subset (P = 2.18E-03, PFDR = 5.04E-03, OR = 0.77, CI = 0.65–0.91; and P = 1.49E-03, PFDR = 5.04E-03, OR = 0.76, CI = 0.64–0.90; respectively)...

the frequencies of rs2043055*C and rs1946518*C were reduced in the seronegative sample set in comparison with the seropositive one (P = 2.52E-03, PFDR = 5.04E-03, OR = 1.29, CI = 1.10–1.53; and P = 0.0162, PFDR = 0.0243, OR1.22, CI = 1.04–1.44; respectively), indicating that they are associated with a higher infection risk.
305.Rationale: The passage reports statistically significant haplotype differences between seronegative and seropositive individuals (e.g., TCAC and CGCT) and also significant individual SNP associations with infection risk. Although the haplotype model did not add explanatory power beyond individual SNPs (no additive effect), this does not imply that IL18 functional effects are irrelevant. On the contrary, multiple SNPs and haplotypes show association with susceptibility, so concluding irrelevance of IL18 function is not supported by the data.

305.Option: The promoter polymorphism rs360719 is likely the causal variant underlying the association between IL18 gene variation and susceptibility to infection by T. cruzi in the Colombian population.
Evidence: The results of these analyses pointed to rs360719 as the most likely causative variant of the IL18 association with Chagas disease, as the statistical significance of both rs2043055 and rs1946518 were clearly lost after conditioning to it, and a trend was maintained when conditioning rs360719 on these two latter variants (Table 2).

rs187238*C and rs360719*C were significantly increased in seronegative individuals compared to the seropositive subset (P = 2.18E-03, PFDR = 5.04E-03, OR = 0.77, CI = 0.65–0.91; and P = 1.49E-03, PFDR = 5.04E-03, OR = 0.76, CI = 0.64–0.90; respectively), thus suggesting that these variants are associated to protection against infection by T. cruzi.
305.Rationale: The study performed conditional logistic regression and explicitly states that these analyses pointed to rs360719 as the most likely causative variant, because conditioning on rs360719 removed the statistical significance of other associated SNPs. The allele-level association of rs360719 with serostatus (protective association in seronegatives) further supports this inference. Therefore the passage supports rs360719 as the probable causal variant in this cohort.

305.Option: The observed association between IL18 variants and infection susceptibility is likely due to population stratification in the Colombian cohort.
Evidence: The population in this region of Colombia is a homogeneous mixture, with no specific concentration of any ethnicity.
The six IL18 SNPs were in Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium in all the analyzed subgroups (P>0.01), suggesting that a possible inbreed in Guanentina and Comunera provinces is not likely.

The marked difference of the average age between the different subgroups of patients (i.e., seronegative and seropositive individuals) could represent a limitation in this study... To control for this possible confounding factor, we decided to perform a logistic regression analysis accordingly with the serological status using age as covariate. The statistical significance was maintained in this analysis thus supporting the consistency of our results (Table B in S1 Text).
305.Rationale: The passage provides statements indicating the sampled population is homogeneous and that genotypes are in Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, arguing against significant population structure or inbreeding effects. The authors also adjusted for age (a potential confounder) and retained significance. The passage does not present evidence that population stratification explains the associations; therefore asserting that the associations are likely due to stratification is not supported by the study text.

305.Option: The functional role of rs360719 in IL-18 expression is likely to be independent of the transcription factor OCT-1, based on the observed genetic associations.
Evidence: an additional promoter variant (rs360719) that has been described to interact with the transcription factor OCT-1 [28];

The results of these analyses pointed to rs360719 as the most likely causative variant of the IL18 association with Chagas disease, as the statistical significance of both rs2043055 and rs1946518 were clearly lost after conditioning to it, and a trend was maintained when conditioning rs360719 on these two latter variants (Table 2).
305.Rationale: The passage explicitly notes that rs360719 "has been described to interact with the transcription factor OCT-1," so claiming independence from OCT-1 is contrary to the quoted background. Moreover, the genetic association data do not provide mechanistic evidence to infer the transcription factor dependence or independence of rs360719’s effect on IL-18 expression. Therefore the statement that rs360719’s functional role is independent of OCT-1 is not supported by the provided text.

305.Option: The IL18 gene variants rs2043055 and rs1946518 are independently associated with increased risk of chronic Chagas cardiomyopathy in the Colombian cohort.
Evidence: the frequencies of rs2043055*C and rs1946518*C were reduced in the seronegative sample set in comparison with the seropositive one (P = 2.52E-03, PFDR = 5.04E-03, OR = 1.29, CI = 1.10–1.53; and P = 0.0162, PFDR = 0.0243, OR1.22, CI = 1.04–1.44; respectively), indicating that they are associated with a higher infection risk.
No statistically significant differences were observed between asymptomatic individuals and chronic Chagasic cardiomyopathy patients for any of the analyzed polymorphisms.

In addition, to further evaluate the possible association between IL18 and progression of cardiomyopathy, we compared IL18 allelic and genotypic frequencies by grouping asymptomatic + CII individuals and CIII + CIV individuals; however, similar to that observed in the previous analysis, no statistically significant differences were yielded (Table C in S1 Text).

The results of these analyses pointed to rs360719 as the most likely causative variant of the IL18 association with Chagas disease, as the statistical significance of both rs2043055 and rs1946518 were clearly lost after conditioning to it...
305.Rationale: While rs2043055 and rs1946518 showed association with infection status (seropositive vs seronegative), the passage states explicitly that there were no statistically significant differences between asymptomatic and chronic cardiomyopathy patients for any polymorphisms, and no association with cardiomyopathy progression. Additionally, conditional analysis indicates these variants are not independent causal signals (their significance was lost when conditioned on rs360719). Therefore they cannot be concluded to be independently associated with increased risk of chronic Chagas cardiomyopathy based on the presented data.

306.Option: Most of the few remaining infected individuals in Kyela district had been positive for circulating filarial antigen before the mass drug administration programme was conducted, indicating that the treatment was effective in reducing the adult worm burden but not fully eliminating persistent infections.
Evidence: Of the 22 individuals with positive CFA results in 2019, 18 had high antigen levels during the EMINI 2009 and 2010/11 surveys and there were 4 new infections of previously uninfected participants (Fig 4B).
In 2019 infected individuals had low positive ODs indicating a low number of adult worms.
In 2009, the prevalence of LF among the EMINI/RHINO participants who were then aged 14 to 65 years was 35.1% (392 of 1116).

In 2019, 1299 of previous EMINI subjects who participated in the RHINO study were screened for the presence of CFA as a marker of an active filarial infection. The overall LF prevalence among these participants was 1.7% (22/1299) which was significantly lower than the measurement pre- and two years post-introduction of MDA (p<0.0001).
The mean OD of CFA positive individuals was 1.55 (SD 0.70) in 2009, 1.11 (SD 0.56), in 2010/11 and 0.49 (SD 0.10) in 2019.
The follow-up in 2019 revealed no high or medium positive values, but rather all 22 CFA positive individuals had low positive results (Fig 3).
306.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that 18 of the 22 individuals who were CFA-positive in 2019 had been antigen-positive (and often high-positive) in earlier surveys, showing that most remaining infections were persistent rather than new. Concurrently, antigen levels (OD) among positives declined markedly from high values in 2009 to low positives in 2019, which indicates reduction in adult worm burden. Because some individuals remain antigen-positive (though at low levels), the programme reduced worm burden but did not fully eliminate all persistent infections.

306.Option: The four new infections observed in 2019 indicate that the mass drug administration programme in Kyela district was ineffective in preventing new Wuchereria bancrofti infections over the ten-year period.
Evidence: Of the 22 individuals with positive CFA results in 2019, 18 had high antigen levels during the EMINI 2009 and 2010/11 surveys and there were 4 new infections of previously uninfected participants (Fig 4B).
From 2011 to 2019, four new infections occurred among 947 previously uninfected individuals, leading to an incidence of 0.05 per 100 person years, showing a 27-fold reduction in new infections.
306.Rationale: The presence of four new infections is reported, but the passage also quantifies a very low incidence (0.05 per 100 person-years) and explicitly states this represents a 27-fold reduction in new infections compared to the pre-MDA period. That evidence shows the MDA substantially reduced, not failed to prevent, new infections. Therefore the option's claim of ineffectiveness is not supported by the data presented.

306.Option: The higher prevalence of LF in males and older individuals in 2019 indicates that the mass drug administration programme disproportionately failed to reach these demographic groups.
Evidence: When the prevalence was compared by sex, LF was less prevalent in female than male participants in the 2009 EMINI survey (32.0% vs 38.9%, p = 0.016), in the 2010/11 follow-up (26.5% vs 29.1%, p = 0.442) and the RHINO study (0.8% vs 2.6%, p = 0.008, Fig 1).

Stratification by age showed that in the 2009 and 2011 EMINI surveys, LF prevalence was lower in young individuals within the age of 14 to <25 years. Similarly, during the RHINO survey in 2019, LF prevalence was the lowest within the same group (14 to <25 years) while the 45 to <65 years group had the highest prevalence (Fig 2).
Multi-variable analysis confirmed that W. bancrofti infection was associated with increasing age, male sex and filarial infection during the previous study (Table 3).
306.Rationale: The passage documents higher prevalence in males and older age groups and that infection is associated with male sex and increasing age. However, it does not provide evidence that this pattern is due to the MDA 'disproportionately failing to reach' those groups (e.g., differential coverage, compliance, or program delivery). Alternative explanations (prior higher exposure, occupational risk, or residual infections among previously infected older individuals) are compatible with the data. Thus the causal claim that MDA disproportionately failed to reach these demographics is not directly supported by the provided evidence.

306.Option: The significant reduction in the mean OD values of CFA-positive individuals over time suggests that the mass drug administration programme has led to a complete eradication of adult Wuchereria bancrofti worms in the Kyela district population.
Evidence: The mean OD of CFA positive individuals was 1.55 (SD 0.70) in 2009, 1.11 (SD 0.56), in 2010/11 and 0.49 (SD 0.10) in 2019.
In 2019 infected individuals had low positive ODs indicating a low number of adult worms.

In 2019, 1299 of previous EMINI subjects who participated in the RHINO study were screened for the presence of CFA as a marker of an active filarial infection. The overall LF prevalence among these participants was 1.7% (22/1299) which was significantly lower than the measurement pre- and two years post-introduction of MDA (p<0.0001).
The follow-up in 2019 revealed no high or medium positive values, but rather all 22 CFA positive individuals had low positive results (Fig 3).
306.Rationale: While the data show a marked reduction in antigen levels and prevalence, they also show that infections persist (1.7% prevalence; 22 individuals still CFA-positive in 2019). The presence of antigen-positive individuals indicates that adult worms are not completely eradicated. Therefore the claim of complete eradication is not supported by the passage.

306.Option: The persistence of CFA positivity in 18 individuals who were previously infected suggests that the mass drug administration programme failed to reduce adult worm burden in the Kyela district population.
Evidence: Of the 22 individuals with positive CFA results in 2019, 18 had high antigen levels during the EMINI 2009 and 2010/11 surveys and there were 4 new infections of previously uninfected participants (Fig 4B).
In 2019 infected individuals had low positive ODs indicating a low number of adult worms.
The mean OD of CFA positive individuals was 1.55 (SD 0.70) in 2009, 1.11 (SD 0.56), in 2010/11 and 0.49 (SD 0.10) in 2019.
The follow-up in 2019 revealed no high or medium positive values, but rather all 22 CFA positive individuals had low positive results (Fig 3).
