306.Rationale: Although 18 of the 22 individuals positive in 2019 had been positive earlier, the antigen (OD) levels among positives have declined substantially from high values to low positives. These data indicate that the adult worm burden decreased among previously infected individuals. Thus persistence of low-level CFA positivity does not imply failure to reduce worm burden; rather it shows partial reduction with some residual infection remaining. The option's assertion that the programme failed to reduce adult worm burden is contradicted by the measured OD declines.

307.Option: The high seroprevalence of Leptospira IgM in urban areas of the DRC suggests that leptospirosis is primarily an urban zoonotic disease driven by poor sanitation and overcrowding, with transmission linked to flooding and exposure to contaminated water.
Evidence: 52 of the positive leptospiral IgM cases (59%) lived in an urban setting; and the odds of leptospiral IgM cases were higher in those living in urban compared to rural areas (Crude OR 3.39; 95% CI 2.18–5.28).

Male sex (Adjusted OR 2.70; 95% CI 1.57–4.65), 20–29 years age group (Adjusted OR 2.85; 95% CI 1.54–5.30) and living in an urban area, albeit less strongly, (Adjusted OR 1.91; 95% CI 1.04–3.48) remained statistically significant following adjustment for other factors
African countries have favorable environmental characteristics for the spread of leptospirosis (rainfall, animal exposure, flooding, rapid urbanization).
307.Rationale: The passage shows that a majority (59%) of seropositive cases were from urban settings and that urban residence was associated with higher odds (Crude and Adjusted ORs). It also notes environmental factors such as flooding that favor leptospirosis spread. However, the option asserts leptospirosis is "primarily an urban zoonotic disease driven by poor sanitation and overcrowding," and explicitly attributes transmission to overcrowding/poor sanitation and frames the disease as primarily urban. The passage does not provide direct evidence about poor sanitation or overcrowding as causal drivers, nor does it establish that leptospirosis is primarily urban (the overall data include rural cases and the urban association is present but not proof that the disease is primarily urban). Therefore the statement overreaches the data and cannot be fully derived from the passage.

307.Option: The seasonal distribution of leptospiral IgM positivity, with 79% of cases occurring during the rainy season, indicates that leptospirosis transmission in the DRC is strongly tied to climatic patterns and should be prioritized during rainy season surveillance campaigns.
Evidence: Seventy-nine percent of the IgM positive cases occurred during the rainy season.

The seasonal distribution of leptospiral IgM positive indicated greater odds of positive cases among samples received during the rainy season compared with the dry season, although these were not statistically significant (Crude OR 1.46; 95% CI 0.83–2.37).
Multivariable analysis ... the association with the seasonal distribution of case group became stronger in effect size as well as statistically significant (Adjusted OR 1.94; 95% CI 1.03–3.65).
In both urban and rural areas, most of the cases occurred during the rainy season.
307.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that most (79%) IgM-positive cases occurred in the rainy season and that multivariable analysis found a statistically significant association (Adjusted OR 1.94) between season and positivity. These findings support the epidemiologic inference that transmission is tied to climatic patterns and therefore justify prioritizing surveillance during the rainy season. Recommendation to prioritize rainy-season surveillance follows logically from the observed seasonal concentration in cases.

307.Option: Leptospira spp. are a probable cause of fever with jaundice in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, as evidenced by the detection of IgM antibodies in 7% of suspected yellow fever cases with negative serology, particularly among young adult males in urban areas.
Evidence: The overall prevalence of Leptospira IgM in this study was 7% (88 patients).
We used samples and data of suspected cases of yellow fever that had negative yellow fever serology, and which had available data and sufficient sample volume for biological analysis.
According to the WHO, a probable leptospirosis case is defined as a clinically compatible case with laboratory findings such as a positive result of an IgM ELISA or other rapid screening test[20].

Male sex (Adjusted OR 2.70; 95% CI 1.57–4.65), 20–29 years age group (Adjusted OR 2.85; 95% CI 1.54–5.30) and living in an urban area, albeit less strongly, (Adjusted OR 1.91; 95% CI 1.04–3.48) remained statistically significant following adjustment for other factors
307.Rationale: The study tested suspected yellow fever cases that were negative for yellow fever and found Leptospira IgM in 7% of them. WHO guidance defines IgM ELISA positivity as compatible with a probable leptospirosis diagnosis. The demographic analysis shows higher odds in young adults (20–29) and males and an association with urban residence. Thus the passage supports the inference that Leptospira spp. are a probable cause of fever with jaundice in the DRC and that cases were concentrated among young adult males in urban areas.

307.Option: The finding that 7% of suspected yellow fever cases were seropositive for Leptospira IgM suggests that yellow fever surveillance systems in the DRC are effective at identifying co-infections with other pathogens, including leptospirosis.
Evidence: Since 2003, the DRC Ministry of Health, in collaboration with WHO, has set up a yellow fever surveillance system. In this system, case notification is based on a standard case definition and reporting by health districts.

Suspected yellow fever cases are defined as any person with acute fever with jaundice occurring within 14 days of the onset of symptoms and not responding to antimalarial drugs or who tests negative for malaria by thick blood smear[19].

Between 2017 and 2018, 1,562 suspected yellow fever cases were reported to INRB. Of these 1,562 cases, 70 were confirmed as yellow fever cases by serological analyses and 1,492 cases had negative serological results.

We retrospectively analyzed data and serum samples ... for IgM presence against Leptospira spp. We used samples and data of suspected cases of yellow fever that had negative yellow fever serology ... A total of 1,300 subjects ... were included in this study.
The overall prevalence of Leptospira IgM in this study was 7% (88 patients).
307.Rationale: While the surveillance system provided specimens that enabled a retrospective study to detect Leptospira IgM in 7% of yellow-fever-suspected, yellow-fever-negative samples, the passage does not state that the surveillance system routinely identifies co-infections or was designed or validated for that purpose. The detection resulted from a retrospective analysis of stored samples, not from routine co-infection surveillance. Therefore the claim that the surveillance system is effective at identifying co-infections is not directly supported by the passage.

307.Option: The high proportion of positive cases among young children (0–9 years) in rural areas suggests that leptospirosis in the DRC is primarily a rural disease affecting children due to exposure to contaminated water sources during agricultural activities.
Evidence: The highest positivity rate of Leptospira IgM was found in the 20–29 years age group (42%) in the urban areas, while the highest positivity rate in the rural areas was found in the 0–9 years age group (36%).

52 of the positive leptospiral IgM cases (59%) lived in an urban setting; and the odds of leptospiral IgM cases were higher in those living in urban compared to rural areas (Crude OR 3.39; 95% CI 2.18–5.28).
African countries have favorable environmental characteristics for the spread of leptospirosis (rainfall, animal exposure, flooding, rapid urbanization).
307.Rationale: Although the passage notes that in rural areas the highest positivity rate was in the 0–9 age group (36%), overall more seropositive cases were found in urban areas (59%) and urban residence was associated with higher odds. The passage does not provide evidence that leptospirosis is 'primarily a rural disease' or that children's infections are due to agricultural-water exposure. While environmental drivers like rainfall and flooding are mentioned, specific attribution to agricultural activities or a conclusion that the disease is primarily rural is not supported by the data presented. Therefore this option overstates what can be inferred.

308.Option: The widespread use of insecticide-treated curtains (ITCs) in the intervention arm led to a significant reduction in the number of adult female Aedes aegypti mosquitoes, which likely contributed to the lower seroconversion rates observed in the control arm.
Evidence: There was a significant difference in overall seroconversion rates (seroconversion to any individual serotype or multiple serotypes; p<0.0001) between the intervention and control arms.

The intervention arm had an average seroconversion rate of 50.6 per 100 person-years (PY) (CI: 29.9–71.9) and those in the control arm had an average seroconversion rate of 37.4 per 100 PY (CI: 15.2–51.7). This represents a statistically significant mean difference of 13.2 (CI: 12.0–14.4), with higher incidence in the intervention arm, or a difference equivalent to 35% of the average rate in the control arm.

Overall, entomological indices were similar across treatment and control arms over the course of the study (Table 3). There were no significant differences detected between the intervention and control arms for any of the adult or immature Ae. aegypti indices that were measured.
308.Rationale: The option claims ITCs led to a significant reduction in adult female Ae. aegypti and that this reduction somehow contributed to lower seroconversion in the control arm. The passage explicitly reports no significant differences in entomological indices between arms, contradicting the claim of a reduction in adult females in the intervention arm. Moreover, seroconversion was higher in the intervention arm, not lower in the control arm. Therefore the causal chain asserted by the option is not supported by the passage.

308.Option: The lack of sustained insecticidal efficacy of insecticide-treated curtains (ITCs) and the emergence of deltamethrin resistance in the local Aedes aegypti population likely contributed to the failure of ITCs to reduce dengue seroconversion in the intervention arm.
Evidence: Results were highly variable and ranged from 34%-100% mortality, with 8 of the curtains falling below the 80% mortality threshold, with no discernible pattern attributable to curtain color, sun exposure, or washing frequency.
Results showed further declines in bioefficacy, with 13 of the curtains falling below the 80% mortality threshold (range: 14%-100%).

After hanging for 11 months (October 2010), the same process was repeated and all except for 1 curtain fell below the 80% mortality threshold (range: 32%-98%). Therefore, to ensure an effective intervention was present in the treated households for the remaining period of the study, curtains were re-treated with deltamethrin in the form of KO Tab 123 (Bayer) during November 2010, with a total of 3,886 curtains (91.9%) re-treated.

Cone bioassays after re-treatment with KO-Tab 123 showed that the curtains did not immediately recover to the 100% bioefficacy observed at baseline. At 1 month following re-treatment (January 2011), average mortality for 36 ITCs was 74.5% (range: 48%-94%). By 9-months following re-treatment (August 2011) average mortality from 9 tested ITCs was 97.2% (range: 87.5%-100%).

Insecticide susceptibility data from CDC bottle bioassays demonstrated that the local Ae. aegypti population was fully susceptible to deltamethrin at baseline and remained fully susceptible when tested during May 2010 and July 2010. Resistance to deltamethrin was first detected during February 2011, when 24-hour mortality using the WHO bioassay dropped to 79.7% and was at a similar level (74.8%) during April 2011. Mortality fell further to 68.3% (using the CDC bottle bioassay) during August 2011.

There was a significant difference in overall seroconversion rates ... The intervention arm had an average seroconversion rate of 50.6 per 100 person-years (PY) ... and those in the control arm had an average seroconversion rate of 37.4 per 100 PY ... with higher incidence in the intervention arm.
308.Rationale: The passage documents a pronounced decline in ITC bioefficacy over time (many curtains falling below the 80% mortality threshold by 6–11 months) and only partial recovery immediately after re-treatment, indicating lack of sustained insecticidal activity. It also reports the emergence of deltamethrin resistance in the local Ae. aegypti population beginning February 2011. At the same time, the intervention arm experienced higher seroconversion rates and there were no significant entomological differences between arms. Together, these facts support the epidemiological inference that waning curtain efficacy and rising insecticide resistance likely undermined the intervention’s ability to reduce dengue seroconversion in the intervention arm.

308.Option: The higher seroconversion rates in the intervention arm were primarily due to increased human mobility and cross-cluster transmission, which overwhelmed the protective effect of the insecticide-treated curtains.
Evidence: Clusters were geographically contiguous in the same region of the city.
There was a significant difference in overall seroconversion rates (seroconversion to any individual serotype or multiple serotypes; p<0.0001) between the intervention and control arms.

Overall, entomological indices were similar across treatment and control arms over the course of the study (Table 3). There were no significant differences detected between the intervention and control arms for any of the adult or immature Ae. aegypti indices that were measured.
308.Rationale: The passage reports the unexpected higher seroconversion in the intervention arm but provides no data or discussion about human mobility, movement patterns, or cross-cluster transmission as causal factors. While clusters were geographically contiguous, that fact alone does not establish that increased human mobility was the primary cause of higher seroconversion in the intervention arm. Because the passage contains no evidence measuring or attributing effects to human movement, this causal attribution cannot be derived solely from the provided text.

308.Option: The initial detection of deltamethrin resistance in the local Aedes aegypti population was likely due to the use of ULV spraying in surrounding areas, which created selection pressure on the mosquito population.
Evidence: ULV spraying does not offer any residual insecticidal effect, and studies indicate that ULV spraying is ineffective unless repeated frequently at closely timed intervals [4]. Hence, it is most practical when employed for outbreak response rather than for routine dengue control [4–6].

Routine Ae. aegypti control in Iquitos consisted of larviciding and health education activities ... In response to increases in reported dengue cases or elevated Ae. aegypti indices, emergency measures, including ULV spraying and city-wide cleanup campaigns (collection of water-holding containers), were employed [19, 21, 22, 26–30].

Resistance to deltamethrin was first detected during February 2011, when 24-hour mortality using the WHO bioassay dropped to 79.7% and was at a similar level (74.8%) during April 2011. Mortality fell further to 68.3% (using the CDC bottle bioassay) during August 2011.
308.Rationale: Although the passage notes that ULV spraying has been used historically in outbreak responses in Iquitos, it does not provide any direct evidence linking ULV spraying in surrounding areas to the timing or emergence of deltamethrin resistance observed in this trial. The passage does not document frequency, timing, or geographic extent of ULV applications concurrent with the resistance detection, nor does it present genetic or ecological evidence that ULV spraying created the selection pressure. Therefore attributing the initial detection of resistance to ULV spraying is speculative and not directly supported by the provided text.

308.Option: The perceived effectiveness of insecticide-treated curtains (ITCs) led to increased use of other protective measures in the intervention arm, which likely contributed to the higher seroconversion rates observed.
Evidence: They are also well accepted by communities [17], because their perceived efficacy is reinforced by the reduction in other biting insects, cockroaches, houseflies and other insect pests [11].
There was a significant difference in overall seroconversion rates (seroconversion to any individual serotype or multiple serotypes; p<0.0001) between the intervention and control arms.

Overall, entomological indices were similar across treatment and control arms over the course of the study (Table 3). There were no significant differences detected between the intervention and control arms for any of the adult or immature Ae. aegypti indices that were measured.
308.Rationale: The passage indicates that ITCs were well accepted and their perceived efficacy was reinforced by reductions in other pests, but it does not present any data indicating that households in the intervention arm changed their use of other protective measures as a result. Moreover, the option claims that increased use of other protective measures contributed to higher seroconversion — a direction of effect that is counterintuitive (additional protective measures would be expected to lower, not raise, seroconversion). Because there is no evidence in the passage documenting behavioral changes in additional protective measures or linking such changes to increased seroconversion, this inference cannot be drawn from the provided information.

309.Option: The short treatment duration and lower cost compared with other treatment options may make PMIM a preferred drug to include as part of a combination therapy regimen for VL patients in Bangladesh.
Evidence: Paromomycin is a low-cost drug that was demonstrated to be efficacious and generally safe and well tolerated for the treatment of VL in a Phase III trial conducted in endemic areas of India [4].

A clinical trial on different treatment regimens for VL completed in four East African countries showed that the combination of sodium stibogluconate (SSG) and PMIM administered over 17 days had efficacy and safety comparable with a 30-day course of SSG alone. Thus, adding PMIM to the treatment regimen lowered both the cost and duration of treatment.

This Phase IIIb study was designed to confirm the effectiveness of PMIM to treat VL in Bangladeshi patients in an outpatient setting to support registration of this low-cost treatment option in Bangladesh.

The final clinical response rate 6 months after EOT was 94.2% (95% CI 90.0–98.4); 94.9% in pediatric (56/59) and 93.4% in adult (57/59) subgroups; and 94.3% (66/70) in males and 94.0% (47/50) in females.
309.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that adding PMIM to SSG reduced both treatment duration and cost and that paromomycin is a low-cost drug with demonstrated efficacy in prior trials. The current Phase IIIb trial also shows high final clinical response rates for PMIM in Bangladeshi patients. Together, these statements support the epidemiological inference that the short duration and lower cost make PMIM an attractive candidate to include in combination regimens for VL in Bangladesh.

309.Option: Paromomycin belongs to the aminoglycoside class of agents, which are known to cause nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity. Patients with clinically significant renal dysfunction or history of hearing loss were excluded from this study. Nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity were not formally studied in this trial.
Evidence: history of hypersensitivity to aminoglycosides or sulfite;
Exclusion criteria included ... clinically significant renal or hepatic dysfunction; ... history of hearing loss;
Four subjects had 6 serious adverse events during the study (tinnitus, hearing impairment, and renal impairment all in one subject; ...).
tinnitus, hearing impairment, and renal impairment, all considered possibly related to study drug, were reported in one adult male after EOT.
309.Rationale: The passage refers to 'hypersensitivity to aminoglycosides' in the exclusion criteria, implicitly linking paromomycin to the aminoglycoside class. The study explicitly excluded patients with clinically significant renal dysfunction and those with a history of hearing loss, indicating concern about renal and auditory toxicity. During the trial, tinnitus, hearing impairment, and renal impairment were observed and considered possibly related to the study drug, demonstrating these toxicities emerged as safety signals. The protocol describes general safety assessment by recording adverse events but does not describe dedicated/formal nephrotoxicity or ototoxicity testing as a distinct study endpoint, so it is reasonable to infer these specific toxicities were not formally studied beyond adverse-event monitoring.

309.Option: The evaluation based on clinical outcome at 6 months was planned for several reasons. This study was conducted with limited resources and was designed similar to a phase 4 study to generate evidence that PMIM works in Bangladesh similar to India, where the etiological agent and patient populations are thought to be very similar.
Evidence: Six months after EOT (approximately Study Day 202), patients were asked to return for an assessment of final clinical response.

The planned sample size of 120 patients with VL was not based on statistical calculations but was considered a sufficient size to determine effectiveness and safety of PMIM in the Bangladeshi population based on high efficacy rates reported in large-scale studies of PMIM in Indian populations [4,5].

This Phase IIIb study was designed to confirm the effectiveness of PMIM to treat VL in Bangladeshi patients in an outpatient setting to support registration of this low-cost treatment option in Bangladesh.
309.Rationale: While the passage states that a 6-month final clinical response assessment was part of the protocol and that the sample size was chosen based on prior Indian studies, it does not state that the study was 'conducted with limited resources' nor that it was 'designed similar to a phase 4 study.' The study is described as Phase IIIb and intended to support registration in Bangladesh; the passage does not assert that etiological agents and patient populations are assumed to be 'very similar' to India, nor does it provide justification phrased as in the option. Those additional claims are not supported by the provided text.

309.Option: The combination of sodium stibogluconate (SSG) and PMIM administered over 17 days had efficacy and safety comparable with a 30-day course of SSG alone, suggesting that PMIM could be used as a standalone treatment for VL in rural Bangladesh.
Evidence: A clinical trial on different treatment regimens for VL completed in four East African countries showed that the combination of sodium stibogluconate (SSG) and PMIM administered over 17 days had efficacy and safety comparable with a 30-day course of SSG alone. Thus, adding PMIM to the treatment regimen lowered both the cost and duration of treatment.

Paromomycin IM injection (PMIM) was approved for the treatment of VL in August 2006 by the Drugs Controller General of India and was included in the World Health Organization (WHO) Essential Medicines List in 2007.
Paromomycin is a low-cost drug that was demonstrated to be efficacious and generally safe and well tolerated for the treatment of VL in a Phase III trial conducted in endemic areas of India [4].

This Phase IIIb study was designed to confirm the effectiveness of PMIM to treat VL in Bangladeshi patients in an outpatient setting to support registration of this low-cost treatment option in Bangladesh.
The final clinical response rate 6 months after EOT was 94.2% (95% CI 90.0–98.4); 94.9% in pediatric (56/59) and 93.4% in adult (57/59) subgroups;
309.Rationale: The passage documents that PMIM has demonstrated efficacy and safety as monotherapy in prior Phase III and Phase IV studies in India, that combination regimens including PMIM reduced duration and cost while retaining efficacy, and that this Phase IIIb trial in Bangladesh produced high final clinical response rates with PMIM alone. Taken together, these pieces of evidence support the inference that PMIM could be used as a standalone treatment for VL in rural Bangladesh.

310.Option: The use of speckle-tracking 2D echocardiography (STE) in pediatric Chagas disease patients reveals that subtle myocardial strain abnormalities are common, even in the absence of clinical symptoms.
Evidence: STE results: Mean four-chamber global longitudinal strain was -19% while strain rate gave -1 (seg-1). The median longitudinal strain was 19.19%, while for strain rate was -1 (seg-1). Values were found to be above (i.e., less negative) -16% strain in 8 patients. As regards strain rate, 3 patients presented values above -0.8 (seg-1). These cases were considered normal since the global echocardiographic contractility evaluation was normal.
In conclusion, no disturbances in myocardial contractility were observed by STE and three-dimensional echocardiography.
Furthermore, no clinical symptoms such as ventricular tachycardia, syncope, chest pain or palpitations were observed during the follow-up.
310.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that although some individual strain/strain-rate values were less negative in a subset of patients, these were considered normal in the context of the global echocardiographic evaluation. The authors conclude that no disturbances in myocardial contractility were observed by STE. Therefore the passage does not support the claim that subtle myocardial strain abnormalities are common; rather it supports that STE did not reveal clinically significant abnormalities despite absence of symptoms.

310.Option: The high prevalence of ECG abnormalities in pediatric Chagas disease patients, including cRBBB and LAFB, indicates that early treatment does not prevent cardiac conduction disturbances.
Evidence: During the follow-up period, alterations in routine ECG were observed in 11/234 (4.7%, 95% CI [2–7.4%]).

However, in 4/11 patients these ECG variations were considered normal variations for children. In the remaining 7/11 patients the ECG variations were considered pathological and only 4/7 patients with cRBBB and LAFB were considered to have ECG abnormalities probably related to CD.
Since the standard of care for CD is the treatment of all infected children, it was not possible to include a non-treated control group due to ethical issues.
310.Rationale: The passage documents a low absolute prevalence of ECG alterations (11/234 or 4.7%) and specifies that only 4 patients had ECG abnormalities probably related to Chagas disease. That is not a 'high prevalence.' Furthermore, because there is no untreated control group (ethical constraint), one cannot conclude from these treated-cohort data that early treatment does not prevent conduction disturbances—absence of a comparator and the low observed prevalence do not support the asserted conclusion.

310.Option: The use of sensitive cardiological techniques such as speckle-tracking 2D echocardiography (STE) in pediatric Chagas disease patients treated in the early phase of infection shows no significant long-term regional wall motion abnormalities, suggesting that early treatment may prevent cardiac structural changes.
Evidence: A descriptive study of cardiological, parasitological and serological results in a cohort of pediatric CD patients, with at least 6 years’ post-treatment follow-up, treated with benznidazole (Bz) or nifurtimox (Nf) was conducted...

STE results: Mean four-chamber global longitudinal strain was -19% while strain rate gave -1 (seg-1). ... These cases were considered normal since the global echocardiographic contractility evaluation was normal.
Left ventricular ejection fraction was measured by three-dimensional echocardiography. Mean ejection fraction was 59.2% (45 to 74%).
In conclusion, no disturbances in myocardial contractility were observed by STE and three-dimensional echocardiography.
310.Rationale: The study assessed treated pediatric patients with extended follow-up using sensitive techniques (STE and 3D echocardiography) and found no disturbances in myocardial contractility or regional wall motion. Given that these patients were treated early and demonstrated normal contractility measures and ejection fraction years later, it is epidemiologically reasonable to infer that early treatment may be protective against long-term cardiac structural changes. The wording 'may prevent' is appropriately cautious and consistent with the observed absence of abnormalities in this treated cohort.

310.Option: The lack of an untreated control group due to ethical considerations limits the ability to definitively conclude that early treatment prevents cardiac complications in pediatric Chagas disease patients.
Evidence: Since the standard of care for CD is the treatment of all infected children, it was not possible to include a non-treated control group due to ethical issues.
310.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that no untreated control group was included for ethical reasons. From an epidemiological standpoint, absence of an untreated comparator limits causal inference about treatment effectiveness in preventing outcomes. Therefore one cannot definitively conclude that early treatment is the direct cause of the low incidence of cardiac complications without a control group; the passage itself acknowledges this limitation.

310.Option: The persistence of T. cruzi DNA in blood samples after treatment suggests that parasiticidal treatment may not fully eliminate the parasite, even in pediatric patients with long-term follow-up.
Evidence: Baseline parasitemia data were available for 168/234 patients: 140/168 (83.3%) were initially positive ... 130/140 positive patients were followed up by parasitemia. In 129/130 (98.5%) parasitemia became negative by T. cruzi—qPCR at the end of treatment and remained persistently negative throughout the follow-up.
310.Rationale: The quoted data indicate that while parasitemia cleared in the vast majority of followed patients (129/130), one patient in the followed group did not become qPCR-negative. The existence of at least one persistent positive result after treatment supports the inference that parasiticidal treatment may not fully eliminate T. cruzi in every pediatric patient, even with long-term follow-up. This conclusion is directly supported by the reported parasitological follow-up proportions.

311.Option: The seasonal peak in dengue cases during the monsoon months is likely due to increased human mobility and cross-border travel, rather than mosquito breeding conditions.

However, the specific claim that increased apoptosis is the cause of neutrophil activation and that apoptosis itself directly clears larvae is not supported by the presented evidence.

315.Option: S100A8/A9 inhibits L3-induced inflammation by decreasing chemokine production and neutrophil activation, thereby facilitating larval migration during L. sigmodontis infection.
Evidence: Our data indicates an anti-inflammatory role of S100A8/S100A9 on neutrophil activation, which reduced inflammatory responses and facilitated the migration of infective L3 larvae into the thoracic cavity.
Subcutaneous L. sigmodontis infection in S100A8/A9-/- mice resulted in a significantly reduced worm burden compared to WT infected animals (Fig 1B).
Concentrations of CXCL-1 (Fig 1E), CXCL-2 (Fig 1F), CXCL-5 (Fig 1G), and elastase (Fig 1H) were significantly increased in the thoracic cavity lavage of S100A8/A9-/- mice compared to WT controls.
L. sigmodontis infection led to a significant increase of S100A8/A9 within the BAL compared to naïve WT controls (Fig 3A).

Myeloperoxidase (MPO) as well as elastase ... were increasingly released ... from bone marrow-derived neutrophils of S100A8/A9-/- mice compared to WT neutrophils at baseline conditions in vitro (Fig 4A and 4B).

L3 larvae cultured with S100A8/A9-/- neutrophils showed an earlier inhibition of L3 motility compared to L3 larvae cultured with neutrophils of WT mice (Fig 4E). 315.Rationale: The passage explicitly states an anti-inflammatory role for S100A8/A9 that reduces neutrophil activation and 'facilitated the migration of infective L3 larvae into the thoracic cavity.' Experimentally, mice lacking S100A8/A9 had reduced worm burdens after natural and subcutaneous infection and displayed elevated neutrophil-associated chemokines (CXCL-1/2/5) and neutrophil enzyme release, consistent with greater neutrophil activation in the absence of S100A8/A9. In vitro, S100A8/A9-/- neutrophils more effectively inhibited larval motility via DNA-trap (NET)-dependent mechanisms, and depletion of neutrophils reversed the protective phenotype in knockouts. Together these findings support the inference that S100A8/A9 normally inhibits L3-induced inflammation (chemokine production and neutrophil activation), which in turn facilitates larval migration.

316.Option: Despite the delay in disease progression and the reduction in virus load in blood and organs as evidenced by virus titration and IHC, ribavirin was not able to prevent the lethal pathophysiological cascade.
Evidence: Although one animal survived after treatment, ribavirin did not significantly increase the survival rate (p = 0.4). However, the drug prolonged the time to death (median 3 vs. 6 days for placebo vs. ribavirin, p = 0.0007), reduced the levels of AST (p = 0.001) and ALT (p = 0.006) at day 2, reduced the virus titer in blood at day 2 (p = 0.0007), increased the weight at day 2 and 3 (p = 0.03 and p = 0.002, respectively), and reduced the terminal virus concentration in all organs when compared to placebo at day 3 (p<0.001 separately for each organ).
Virus antigen-positive cells (NP) were significantly reduced in liver and spleen compared to untreated or placebo-treated mice (data not shown).
However, ribavirin-treated mice that succumbed to infection on days 4–9 showed extensive bridging hepatocellular necrosis at the time of death (Fig. 2).

Like in untreated mice, the necrosis was accompanied by presence of numerous Iba-1-positive macrophages (Kupffer cells), showing enlarged cell bodies and focal clustering, and iNOS-expressing activated monocyte-derived cells (Fig. 3). Both alterations are suggestive for strong monocyte/macrophage activation.
However, in contrast to untreated mice, virus antigen was hardly detectable in liver tissue of the treated mice (Fig. 2), consistent with the low virus titer in all organs (Fig. 5, bottom).
Thus, ribavirin reduces CCHFV load and delays disease progression, but it does not prevent terminal liver necrosis, monocyte/macrophage activation, and lethal outcome in the IFNAR−/− mouse model.
In humans, the virus causes a febrile illness that may be associated with hemorrhage, liver necrosis, shock, and multiorgan failure.
316.Rationale: The Passage Body explicitly reports that ribavirin treatment reduced viremia and organ virus concentrations, decreased virus antigen by IHC, lowered liver enzymes, and delayed time to death, indicating antiviral effect and delayed disease progression. Despite these effects, treated mice that eventually died exhibited extensive terminal bridging hepatocellular necrosis and marked signs of monocyte/macrophage activation, and overall survival was not significantly improved. The authors conclude directly that ribavirin "reduces CCHFV load and delays disease progression, but it does not prevent terminal liver necrosis, monocyte/macrophage activation, and lethal outcome in the IFNAR−/− mouse model," which is the same inference stated in the option. Therefore the option is derivable from the Passage Body.

318.Option: The 24-bp insertion in the chitotriosidase gene, which results in impaired enzyme activity, is associated with an increased risk for the development of Madurella mycetomatis mycetoma.
Evidence: It was described elsewhere that chitotriosidase activity is reduced when patients are heterozygous for this insertion and completely absent when they were homozygous for this allele [12, 14].
The insertion containing allele was found significantly more frequently in the patients than in the control group (p = 0.004).
Based on these data, the 24-bp insertion in the chitotriosidase gene does increase the risk for invasive mycetoma disease caused by M. mycetomatis (odds ratio 2.9; 95% CI 1.4–6.1).
318.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the 24-bp insertion reduces or abolishes chitotriosidase activity and reports a statistically significant higher frequency of the insertion allele among patients compared with controls (p = 0.004), with an odds ratio of 2.9. These statements directly support the inference that this insertion (which impairs enzyme activity) is associated with increased risk of M. mycetomatis mycetoma.

318.Option: The high prevalence of M. mycetomatis in Sudanese soil and the presence of antibodies in the population indicate that the 24-bp insertion in chitotriosidase is a primary determinant of disease susceptibility in this population.
Evidence: In endemic areas in Sudan, M. mycetomatis DNA was found in the soil [3] and antibodies against mycetoma causative agents have been detected in the majority of the inhabitants in these areas [4].
However, it is not clear why only a minority of the exposed humans develop overt clinical infection.
Several environmental and patient-related factors have been described to influence the risk for the development of mycetoma, including concurrent schistosomiasis [5].
Based on these data, the 24-bp insertion in the chitotriosidase gene does increase the risk for invasive mycetoma disease caused by M. mycetomatis (odds ratio 2.9; 95% CI 1.4–6.1).
318.Rationale: While the passage documents widespread environmental exposure (DNA in soil, antibodies in majority) and shows that the 24-bp insertion increases risk, it also states that only a minority develop clinical infection and lists multiple environmental and patient-related risk factors. The study demonstrates an association but does not provide evidence that this single genetic variant is the primary determinant of susceptibility at the population level. Therefore the stronger claim that it is the primary determinant is not supported by the passage.

318.Option: The lack of association between AMCase polymorphisms and mycetoma suggests that AMCase is not involved in the host immune response to M. mycetomatis in endemic populations.

Allele frequencies of A50G and A290G in the patients were similar to those in the controls (p = 0.65; odds ratio 1.2; 95% CI 0.6–2.1; and p = 0.72; odds ratio 1.1; 95% CI 0.7–1.8, respectively).
Furthermore, no difference was found in the presence of the 10-bp insertion between patients and controls (p = 0.72; odds ratio 1.1; 95% CI 0.7–1.8).
318.Rationale: The passage reports no association between studied AMCase polymorphisms and disease, but it also provides direct histologic evidence that AMCase is present and concentrated in the fungal grain and can bind hyphae. Absence of an association with the specific genetic variants tested does not logically imply AMCase is not involved in the host immune response; the passage provides contrary evidence of AMCase localization at the infection site. Therefore the categorical conclusion that AMCase is not involved is not supported by the data presented.

318.Option: The 24-bp insertion in the chitotriosidase gene is associated with increased chitinase activity, which may contribute to a stronger immune response against M. mycetomatis in exposed individuals.
Evidence: It was described elsewhere that chitotriosidase activity is reduced when patients are heterozygous for this insertion and completely absent when they were homozygous for this allele [12, 14].
The insertion containing allele was found significantly more frequently in the patients than in the control group (p = 0.004).
Based on these data, the 24-bp insertion in the chitotriosidase gene does increase the risk for invasive mycetoma disease caused by M. mycetomatis (odds ratio 2.9; 95% CI 1.4–6.1).
318.Rationale: This option asserts the insertion increases chitinase activity and thereby enhances immunity, but the passage states the opposite: the 24-bp insertion reduces or abolishes chitotriosidase activity and is associated with increased risk of disease. Thus the direction of effect claimed in the option contradicts the data presented and is not supported.

319.Option: The high rate of positive environmental swabs on household furniture and children’s hands indicates that flies are the dominant vector for trachoma transmission in hyperendemic communities.
Evidence: Locations with the highest proportion of positive swabs were household furniture (18%) and children’s hands (8%).

319.Rationale: Although environmental swabs were positive on furniture and children’s hands, the passage explicitly states that C. trachomatis spreads via ocular and nasal secretions and that there is no animal or insect reservoir. The mention of fly-reduction as an environmental intervention does not establish flies as the dominant vector; rather flies are targeted to reduce access to infected secretions. The passage provides no direct evidence that flies are the primary or dominant vector, so this causal inference is not supported.

319.Option: In high prevalence villages, C. trachomatis DNA was detected on household objects, children's hands, and clothing, suggesting that environmental contamination may contribute to transmission, particularly in settings where hygiene practices such as frequent clothing washing are less common.

Twenty-one of the positive swabs, collected from 11 households, were from the same village, Njoge.
washing clothes <3 days prior to the study visit was protective against obtaining a positive environmental swab (OR 0.17; 95% CI 0.03–0.88).
other possible routes of transmission of infected secretions, such as on hands of caretakers or clothing and bedding, may be present and could serve as targets for interventions.

It also reports that more recent washing of clothes was protective (OR 0.17), supporting the inference that hygiene practices like frequent clothing washing are associated with reduced environmental contamination. The authors explicitly suggest hands, clothing, and bedding may be transmission routes and intervention targets, so concluding environmental contamination may contribute to transmission, especially where hygiene is less common, is directly supported by the data.

319.Option: The lack of positive environmental swabs in two villages with low trachoma prevalence indicates that environmental contamination is not a significant factor in disease transmission.
Evidence: The 23 positive environmental swabs were obtained from 13 households (Table 3). Twenty-one of the positive swabs, collected from 11 households, were from the same village, Njoge. Obtaining at least one positive environmental swab from the household was associated with village of residence (ranging from 0% in Mkutani and Banyi Banyi to 34% in Njoge, p = 0.011) and presence of ocular infection in the index child (percent positive swabs 34% vs. 3%, p = 0.001)
other possible routes of transmission of infected secretions, such as on hands of caretakers or clothing and bedding, may be present and could serve as targets for interventions.

The passage shows significant associations between environmental positivity and village and with presence of ocular infection in the index child, implying environmental contamination is linked to transmission in some settings. Lack of detection in some villages could reflect lower prevalence, sample size, or other local factors; the study does not provide evidence to rule out environmental contamination as a contributor to transmission generally.

319.Option: Frequent face washing alone is sufficient to prevent environmental contamination and interrupt trachoma transmission in high-burden communities.

washing clothes <3 days prior to the study visit was protective against obtaining a positive environmental swab (OR 0.17; 95% CI 0.03–0.88).
other possible routes of transmission of infected secretions, such as on hands of caretakers or clothing and bedding, may be present and could serve as targets for interventions.

The authors also highlight multiple potential routes (hands, clothing, bedding) and endorse a multifaceted intervention strategy, so the claim that face washing alone is sufficient is not supported by the data.

319.Option: The presence of C. trachomatis DNA in the household environment of children with active trachoma suggests that environmental sources are the primary route of transmission, especially in hyperendemic areas.

When we included presence of ocular infection in the index child in the model (Table 5, model A), only that variable was statistically significantly associated with obtaining at least one positive environmental swab from a household (OR 22.0; 95% CI 2.31–209).

These findings do not demonstrate that environmental sources are the primary route of transmission; they show environmental contamination occurs and may facilitate transmission, but do not establish primacy over direct person-to-person transfer of secretions.

320.Option: The model output suggests that upstream sampling locations are ideal for environmental surveillance because they are less affected by environmental flow and pathogen decay.
Evidence: the highest probabilities of detection are concentrated in upstream branches that do not have ‘parent’ branches, and the lowest probabilities of detection are concentrated in downstream branches with large catchment populations, where larger volumes of wastewater flow and environmental flow converge (Fig 4).
dilution and pathogen decay over time appear to play an important role in the downstream concentrations and probability of detection at 09:00 (Fig 4).

a more informative ES plan may include more upstream sites that capture as much of the upstream population as possible, while also maintaining a high probability of detection, rather than relying on a downstream convergence branch like 573 to provide a representative sample for the entire catchment population.

In fact, it notes detection probability is reduced at downstream branches (due to dilution/decay). The statement that downstream sampling is "more reliable because they integrate signals" overstates what the text supports; the text only indicates downstream sites may still detect at high prevalence, not that they are superior or inherently more reliable.

Evidence: When choosing sampling locations, there are multiple competing priorities, including maximizing the total catchment population of all samples, maximizing the probability of detection, minimizing the total number of samples needed to provide an overview of the wastewater network, and the selection of sampling locations that are deemed accessible, suitable, and convenient to a regular sampling schedule.

two branches (573 and 815) prioritize capturing large upstream catchment populations and are located downstream of major convergence points in the wastewater network. However, in this model scenario, both branches have relatively low maximum probability of detection, likely due to dilution factors and decay rates as bacteria travel downstream and are continually diluted as wastewater flows converge.

a more informative ES plan may include more upstream sites that capture as much of the upstream population as possible, while also maintaining a high probability of detection, rather than relying on a downstream convergence branch like 573 to provide a representative sample for the entire catchment population.

320.Option: A negative sampling result at downstream branches does not indicate that there are no infected persons in the wastewater network, and infected persons are likely to be missed by environmental surveillance if these sites are relied upon.
Evidence: In these low prevalence scenarios, large environmental surveillance wastewater channels may be unlikely to yield positive detections and sampling further upstream, at shared toilets or the building level, may be warranted, as suggested in Wang et al., 2020 [26].
It is clear from Fig 6 that, when prevalence is low, under any flow scenario, downstream convergence branches with large upstream populations are not ideal sampling locations.

If targeted upstream sampling at shared toilets or at the individual street level is not feasible, then an environmental surveillance sampling plan aiming to identify any infections within the catchment population would need to include a greater number of sampling locations, with an emphasis on upstream branches, and an increased sampling frequency and volume, to increase detection probability.
320.Rationale: The passage describes scenarios where downstream channels are unlikely to yield positive detections, particularly at low prevalence, and recommends upstream or more intensive sampling to avoid missed infections. This directly supports the inference that negative results from downstream sites do not rule out infection in the population and that relying solely on downstream sampling can miss infected persons.

321.Option: In VL/HIV patients, neutrophil counts, as well as the ability of neutrophils to phagocytose bioparticles and express CD10, are not restored following treatment, suggesting that neutrophils play a key role in the poor prognosis of these patients.

In VL/HIV patients, despite an increase in neutrophil counts at EoT, these numbers stayed significantly lower until the end of follow-up.

321.Rationale: The passage provides direct longitudinal data showing that (a) neutrophil counts in VL/HIV patients remain significantly lower through follow-up, (b) phagocytosis by neutrophils "remained impaired at all time points in VL/HIV patients," and (c) CD10 expression in VL/HIV patients did not normalize after treatment and only increased marginally. The passage also documents markedly higher relapse rates in VL/HIV patients and an association between higher CD10 and non-relapse. Taken together, it is epidemiologically reasonable to infer that persistent neutrophil quantitative and functional defects are present after treatment in VL/HIV patients and that these defects are plausibly implicated in their poorer prognosis. The option is worded as a suggestion (not a definitive causal claim), which is consistent with the associative evidence provided in the passage.

neutrophil counts were significantly lower in both groups as compared to controls at ToD. CD10, a molecule present only on segmented neutrophils [29], that can regulate both inflammatory processes [32] and T cell effector functions [33].

322.Option: The failure of FNAV to neutralize O. hannah venom, despite strong immunoreactivity to its major neurotoxins, suggests that antigenic similarity alone is insufficient to ensure cross-neutralization efficacy.
Evidence: For HPLC-fractionated O. hannah venom, NTX (fractions 9–18), CRISP (fraction 32, 35) and SVMP (fraction 33, 35, 38, 40, 42–44) immunoreacted strongly toward FNAV,
The lethality of O. hannah venom was not neutralized by FNAV, even at maximum dose of 100 µL/mouse (Table 1).
The major protein in these fractions with lower immunereactivity was identified as β-cardiotoxin, a member of the CTX protein family (S5 Table).

322.Option: The high sequence similarity between O. hannah PLA2 and N. atra PLA2 implies that FNAV should effectively neutralize O. hannah venom, but the observed lack of neutralization may be due to differences in venom delivery or absorption in the mouse model.

The major proteins in fractions 19, 21 and 27, identified as PLA2 family proteins, also showed poor immunoreactivity to FNAV.
The lethality of O. hannah venom was not neutralized by FNAV, even at maximum dose of 100 µL/mouse (Table 1).
322.Rationale: The passage does not present any data on sequence similarity between PLA2 of O. hannah and N. atra, so the premise of this option is unsupported. Moreover, PLA2 constitutes only a small fraction (3–4%) of O. hannah venom and PLA2 fractions showed poor immunoreactivity to FNAV, which contradicts the claim that PLA2 similarity should predict neutralization. The passage also provides no information about differences in venom delivery or absorption in the mouse model as an explanation. Thus this chain of inference cannot be derived from the provided text.

323.Option: The persistence of follicles after bacterial clearance in the NHP model mirrors the clinical pattern observed in human trachoma, suggesting that immune-mediated inflammation may continue even after the infectious agent is no longer detectable.
Evidence: Scarring or chronic trachoma develops over multiple years and after recurrent Ct infections [5].
Follicular conjunctivitis was observed in all animals from 2 weeks post-exposure (p.e.) (12/12, p<0.0001), with 8/12 also demonstrating inflammation (median score = 2) (S4A and S4B Fig).

Clinical scores peaked at 4 weeks p.e. and then steadily decreased (Fig 3A). Although at 6 weeks p.e., higher than the average clinical scores were recorded for almost half of the group (n = 5 macaques) for the remainder of the study.

In 4/12 macaques, the bacterial load decreased to undetectable levels at 6 weeks p.e. In the 8 remaining macaques the bacterial load decreased to undetectable levels by week 9, which was 2 weeks p.t. (Fig 3B).

Cytokines VEGF, IL-12/23, IL-13, IL-17α, IL-10, IFN-y, IL-2, IL-15, sCD40L and IL-1β decreased between weeks 4 or 6, or during both timepoints, but rebounded at week 11 p.e., to levels higher than baseline, and this effect was observed in more macaques.

These results demonstrate 4 different conjunctival cytokine response patterns to Ct as follows: no significant change, increase or decrease in correlation to bacterial load, or increase following treatment once the bacterial load was cleared.
323.Rationale: The passage shows that in the NHP model clinical signs (follicles/inflammation) can persist beyond the time when bacterial load becomes undetectable (bacterial clearance by week 6–9 p.e.), and that some cytokines rebound or increase after bacterial clearance. It also emphasizes that chronic scarring in human trachoma develops over years after recurrent infection. Together these facts support the epidemiological inference that immune-mediated inflammation can continue after the infectious agent is no longer detectable, and that the persistence of follicles after bacterial clearance in the NHP model reflects patterns seen in human disease.

323.Option: The delayed increase in IL-17A and other pro-inflammatory cytokines observed in the NHP model suggests that these markers are directly responsible for the persistence of follicular lesions after bacterial clearance.
Evidence: Cytokines VEGF, IL-12/23, IL-13, IL-17α, IL-10, IFN-y, IL-2, IL-15, sCD40L and IL-1β decreased between weeks 4 or 6, or during both timepoints, but rebounded at week 11 p.e., to levels higher than baseline, and this effect was observed in more macaques.
Follicular conjunctivitis was observed in all animals from 2 weeks post-exposure (p.e.) (12/12, p<0.0001), with 8/12 also demonstrating inflammation (median score = 2) (S4A and S4B Fig).

These results demonstrate 4 different conjunctival cytokine response patterns to Ct as follows: no significant change, increase or decrease in correlation to bacterial load, or increase following treatment once the bacterial load was cleared.
323.Rationale: While the passage documents temporal changes in IL-17α and other cytokines, including rebounds after bacterial clearance, it does not provide evidence of a causal role linking these cytokine changes directly to persistence of follicular lesions. The authors report correlations and temporal associations but do not demonstrate direct causation. Therefore the specific claim that these markers are directly responsible for lesion persistence is not supported by the presented longitudinal data.

323.Option: The absence of detectable neutrophils in conjunctival imprints indicates that neutrophils do not play a significant role in the immune response to acute trachoma in the NHP model.
Evidence: Negligible proportions of neutrophils (CD66+ cells) and natural killer (NK, CD159a-NKG2A+ cells) were observed throughout the study period (less than 1% of immune cells).

323.Rationale: Although the study's conjunctival imprint samples detected negligible neutrophils (<1%), the passage also cites prior model infection studies indicating neutrophil-mediated hyperinflammation can cause ocular damage. The lack of neutrophil detection in these imprints may reflect sampling method, timing, or compartmentalization rather than definitive evidence that neutrophils play no significant role. Therefore concluding absence of a significant role for neutrophils over-interprets the data and is not justified by the passage.

323.Option: The use of azithromycin treatment in the NHP model led to a rapid and complete resolution of all clinical signs, indicating that antibiotic intervention is sufficient to fully reverse the immunological changes associated with trachoma.
Evidence: At 7 weeks post-exposure (p.e.), Group 2 received oral azithromycin 20 mg/kg/day the first day then, 10 mg/kg daily for the next 2 days, according to the standard recommendations for the treatment of chlamydia infections.

Clinical scores peaked at 4 weeks p.e. and then steadily decreased (Fig 3A). Although at 6 weeks p.e., higher than the average clinical scores were recorded for almost half of the group (n = 5 macaques) for the remainder of the study. At week 6, the inflammation score decreased for 5/12 animals. However, median clinical scoring decreased post-azithromycin treatment (p.t.), as inflammation decreased in 10/12 macaques (S4B Fig).

In 4/12 macaques, the bacterial load decreased to undetectable levels at 6 weeks p.e. In the 8 remaining macaques the bacterial load decreased to undetectable levels by week 9, which was 2 weeks p.t. (Fig 3B).
Cytokines ... rebounded at week 11 p.e., to levels higher than baseline, and this effect was observed in more macaques.
These results demonstrate 4 different conjunctival cytokine response patterns to Ct as follows: ... increase following treatment once the bacterial load was cleared.
These results demonstrate that conjunctival Ct exposure elicits systemic circulation of Ct-specific IgA and IgG, which remained elevated to the latest time-point tested.
323.Rationale: While azithromycin reduced bacterial load and median clinical scores (inflammation decreased in 10/12 animals), the passage does not show rapid and complete resolution of all clinical signs in all animals. Some animals retained higher-than-average clinical scores for the remainder of the study, cytokine profiles rebounded after treatment, and systemic antibodies remained elevated. Thus the data do not support the stronger claim that antibiotic intervention fully reverses all immunological changes associated with trachoma.

323.Option: The correlation between B cell infiltration and bacterial load in the NHP model implies that B cells are directly responsible for clearing Chlamydia trachomatis in the conjunctiva.
Evidence: Between weeks 3 and 6 p.e., significant proportions of B cells (CD20+ HLADR+-double positive CD45+ cells) were detected with the highest proportion at 6 weeks p.e. (2.14 ± 0.57%, p = 0.0156 for week 6 vs week 1 p.e.).

323.Rationale: The passage documents a correlation between B cell increases and bacterial load (and with IgG levels), but correlation does not prove causation. The authors also highlight the essential role of T cell responses in Ct clearance. There is no direct experimental evidence in the passage demonstrating B cells are the causal mechanism of bacterial clearance in the conjunctiva, so the statement that B cells are directly responsible is not supported by the presented data.

324.Option: The LEC bilayer co-culture system is superior to in vivo models for evaluating the pharmacokinetics of anti-filarial drugs due to its ability to simulate human liver metabolism.
Evidence: A primary human lymphatic endothelial cell co-culture with adult female B. malayi was selected and initially validated as a drug screening system against macrofilaricidal and Wolbachia depleting drugs.

324.Rationale: The passage describes a lymphatic endothelial cell-based co-culture system validated for drug screening, and details the cell types and media used (LEC, HEK293) and how compounds were prepared. There is no description in the Passage Body of liver cells, hepatic metabolism, microfluidic perfusion systems, or any pharmacokinetic modeling. Therefore the specific claim that this co-culture system simulates human liver metabolism and is superior to in vivo models for evaluating pharmacokinetics is not supported by any quoted text and cannot be derived from the passage.

324.Option: The LEC bi-layer co-culture system supports fully viable female B. malayi in culture for three weeks, enabling accurate assessment of drug efficacy over prolonged exposure periods, including for slow-acting agents like doxycycline and flubendazole.
Evidence: Full survival was achieved for the duration of the 21-day culture period in LEC+Mϕ(naïve) or LEC+LEC insert co-cultures, a significant improvement on the other culture conditions tested (P = 0.0027 Mantel-Cox log-rank test).

Full motility was sustained for the 21-day duration with the LEC+Mϕ(naïve) and LEC+LEC insert groups, whilst other groups demonstrated significant declines in motility during the culture period (P<0.0001, Kruskal-Wallis with Dunn’s comparison test).

After 7-day treatment, both treatments significantly reduced Wolbachia levels. Doxycycline depleted Wolbachia on average by 77%, whilst treatment with flubentylosin reduced levels by 85% median depletion (5.26x106 and 3.47x106 median Wolbachia load per female vs 2.3x107 vehicle control, P<0.0001, Kruskal-Wallis with Dunn’s comparison post-hoc test, Fig 5D).

Vehicle control treated female B. malayi retained 100% survival and motility throughout the course of the study, whereas survival declined from 12 days of treatment in both FBZ and SUR treatment groups with 12% surviving after 14 days of SUR treatment and 0% survival with FBZ treatment (P = 0.0019 & 0.0003, respectively, Log-rank Mantel Cox test; Fig 6B).

Wolbachia loads within female B. malayi after one to two weeks in culture, Wolbachia loads were significantly lower, on average by 55%, than in vivo controls ... and by three weeks, levels had declined by 74.4% (0.8 vs 3.12x107 median wsp copies per worm (P<0.005, Kruskal-Wallis with Dunn’s comparisons post-hoc test).
324.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct experimental evidence that the LEC bilayer co-culture (LEC+LEC or LEC+Mϕ(naïve) inserts) sustains 100% survival and full motility of female B. malayi for a 21-day (three-week) period, and that the system was successfully used to evaluate both anti-Wolbachia drugs (e.g. doxycycline/flubentylosin) and direct-acting macrofilaricides (e.g. flubendazole) with measurable, significant effects during multi-day exposures. These points support the inference that the system enables prolonged exposure testing and can detect efficacy of agents over weeks. The passage does note a culture-associated decline in Wolbachia over time, so accurate assessment requires matched vehicle/timepoint controls; nevertheless, the authors validated drug effects above these culture-associated changes, indicating the system is suitable for prolonged efficacy assessment (including slower-acting drugs) when appropriately controlled.

324.Option: The LEC bilayer co-culture system enables the detection of false-positive drug hits by mimicking the in vivo immune environment through the inclusion of polarized macrophages.
Evidence: To determine whether addition of macrophages to the culture system enhanced parasite survival further, a monocyte-derived cell line, THP-1 (ECACC), originally derived from an acute monocytic leukaemia patient were evaluated. To differentiate THP1 cells into macrophages (Mϕ), cells were treated with 10 ng/ml of 12-O-tetradecanoylphorbol-I3-acetate (PMA; Peprotech), as previously described [22]. For polarisation into either a classically- or alternatively-activated phenotype, cells were treated with 50ng/ml recombinant interferon-gamma, (IFN-γ), or 25 ng/ml of recombinant interleukin 4 (IL-4) and interleukin 13 (IL-13), respectively (Peprotech), as previously detailed [23].

Similar survival was apparent with LEC+Mϕ(IFN-γ) or LEC+Mϕ(IL-4/13) inserts, whereby 100% survival was maintained for 15 and 16 days (12/12 female B. malayi), before declining to 67% (8/12) and 83% (10/12), respectively, by day 21.

A primary human lymphatic endothelial cell co-culture with adult female B. malayi was selected and initially validated as a drug screening system against macrofilaricidal and Wolbachia depleting drugs.
324.Rationale: While the passage documents inclusion of THP-1–derived macrophages and their polarization states in transwell inserts and reports effects of different insert conditions on parasite survival and metabolic activity, it does not state or demonstrate that these macrophage conditions replicate the in vivo immune environment sufficiently to identify false-positive drug hits. There is no experimental evidence presented that the macrophage-containing co-cultures specifically reduce false positives (for example by distinguishing host-mediated versus direct drug effects) or that the system mimics the full in vivo immune milieu. Therefore the specific claim about detection of false-positive hits via immune-mimicry cannot be derived from the Passage Body.

324.Option: The use of CB.17 SCID and BALB/c IL-4Rα-/-IL-5-/- mice as animal models for B. malayi infection results in significantly higher microfilariae production compared to gerbils, making them ideal for large-scale drug screening.
Evidence: Both CB.17 SCID and BALB/c IL-4Rα-/-IL-5-/- mice yielded significant, 3-fold higher adult worm burdens compared with yields recovered from gerbils (gerbil median recovery of inoculate = 4%, CB.17 SCID = 13%,BALB/c IL-4Rα-/-/IL-5-/- = 12% (P<0.0001 Kruskal-Wallis with Dunn’s post-hoc test, Fig 1A).

B. malayi mf production in the peritoneum was normalised to number of female B. malayi worms recovered per animal, to allow for accurate comparisons. Significantly higher mf productions were observed in gerbils, which were 5-fold and 20-fold higher than those of CB.17 SCID or BALB/c IL-4Rα-/-/IL-5-/- mice; gerbil median = 7.4x104 mf/female (median absolute mf yields /animal = 856,666, range = 0–5,250,000) vs = 1.5x104 mf/female (median absolute mf yields /animal = 238,700, range = 0–938,925), or 3.5 x103 mf/female, (median absolute mf yields /animal = 61,000, range = 0–520,000), CB.17 SCID or BALB/c IL-4Rα-/-/IL-5-/- respectively, P<0.0001, Kruskal-Wallis with Dunn’s post-hoc test, Fig 1D).
324.Rationale: The Passage Body shows that the immunodeficient mouse strains produce higher adult worm yields than gerbils, but microfilariae (mf) production per female worm is significantly higher in gerbils (5- to 20-fold higher). Thus the statement that the mouse models yield higher mf production than gerbils is contradicted by the data. Because the option's premise about mf production is false, the conclusion about suitability for large-scale screening on that basis cannot be derived from the passage.

324.Option: The decline in Wolbachia content in cultured female B. malayi is due to the absence of male mating partners, indicating that the culture system cannot support reproductive biology.
Evidence: As mf production was not sustained beyond 7 days in vitro (Fig 4A), the cessation of embryogenesis may have impacted on measures of overall metabolic activity and Wolbachia content, compared with freshly isolated, gravid female worms.

To more accurately interpret whether these measures reflected a real decline in viability or cessation of mf production, a sample of 62 adult female worms were isolated from CB.17 SCID mouse infections, and approximately equal batches were either processed immediately for metabolic activity and Wolbachia molecular assays, set up into culture within LEC bilayers, or implanted into naïve CB.17 SCID recipients (Fig 4C). After removal of male worms and 2-week in vivo implantation, female worm mf production had ceased. This was confirmed by overnight culture release assays.

Median Wolbachia titres within female B. malayi ex-implanted worms were 37% significantly lower than those recovered from in vivo mixed-sex controls (2.0 vs 3.3x107; P<0.05, Kruskal-Wallis with Dunn’s comparisons post-hoc test, Fig 4E).

Reductions in metabolic activity and Wolbachia production in female-only explants after two weeks were similar to levels in B. malayi cultured on LEC bilayers, which displayed an average 24% decline in metabolic activity (median 0.5 O.D.), and a 45% reduction in Wolbachia content (1.8x107 vs 3.3x107). Levels of metabolic activity and Wolbachia were adjudged not to vary significantly between female ex-implanted worms and cultured parasites.
324.Rationale: The authors observed that mf release ceased in cultured female worms by day 14 and that Wolbachia titres declined over 1–3 weeks in culture. They then performed an experiment removing males (female-only explants) and found that after two weeks these female-only worms also ceased mf production and had significantly reduced Wolbachia titres and metabolic activity; these reductions were similar to those seen in LEC-bilayer–cultured females. This set of observations supports the inference that the decline in Wolbachia content during culture is attributable, at least in part, to cessation of embryogenesis associated with absence of male mating partners, and indicates that the culture conditions (in which females are maintained without males) do not sustain reproductive biology.

325.Option: The increase in mosquito abundance in oil palm plantations is likely due to higher mean temperatures, which enhance larval development and adult survival.
Evidence: Overall, human landing catches conducted in plantations yielded an average 1.5 times more mosquitoes per session than those at logged forest sites (GLM, RR = 0.42, df = 64, 95% CI: 1.17, 2.0, p = 0.002).

Temperature data indicated that although mean daily temperatures did not differ significantly between land-use types during the sampling period, daily temperature fluctuations were greater in oil palm plantations than in logged forest [55].
This key difference between land-use types was not predicted by commonly used TPCs.

Therefore, the claim that the abundance increase is likely due to higher mean temperatures enhancing development and survival is not supported by the passage.

Evidence: Vectorial capacity was estimated to be greater in oil palm plantation than in forest, largely driven by greater mosquito abundance in oil palm.
This key difference between land-use types was not predicted by commonly used TPCs.

Despite warmer temperatures in plantation sites, none of the parameters estimated using the temperature-dependent responses from Mordecai et al. [22] differed significantly between the land-use types for either sampling year (Wilcox, Z = 0.43, p = 0.663; Fig 1).

It also reports no significant difference in mean daily temperatures and that TPC-derived parameters did not differ between land-use types. Thus the option claiming consistency with TPC predictions and attributing it to higher mean temperature and microclimatic variation is not supported.

325.Option: The similarity in vectorial capacity between land-use types, despite differences in temperature fluctuations, suggests that microclimatic variation has no significant effect on mosquito transmission dynamics.
Evidence: Vectorial capacity was found to be 1.5 times greater in oil palm plantations (1.51 ± 0.03, mean ± SE) than in logged forest (0.99 ± 0.01, mean ± SE; F(1,4) = 61, β = 0.50, p < 0.001).

Temperature data indicated that although mean daily temperatures did not differ significantly between land-use types during the sampling period, daily temperature fluctuations were greater in oil palm plantations than in logged forest [55].

The higher vectorial capacity in oil palm plantations was driven by greater mosquito density (m) at plantation sites, as rVC estimates did not differ significantly between land-use types (Wilcox, W = 6.5, Z = 0.06, p = 0.77).
325.Rationale: The passage reports a clear difference in vectorial capacity (higher in plantations) and notes greater temperature fluctuations in plantations. It does not conclude that microclimatic variation has no effect; rather, it shows that observed transmission potential differences were driven by mosquito abundance and that TPCs did not predict this. Therefore the statement that similarity in vectorial capacity implies no significant microclimatic effect is contradicted by the passage.

325.Option: The higher vectorial capacity in oil palm plantations is primarily due to increased biting rates resulting from greater daily temperature fluctuations in these habitats.
Evidence: The higher vectorial capacity in oil palm plantations was driven by greater mosquito density (m) at plantation sites, as rVC estimates did not differ significantly between land-use types (Wilcox, W = 6.5, Z = 0.06, p = 0.77).
Gonotrophic cycle length did not differ between logged forest and oil palm plantation treatments (Mann-Whitney U, Z = -0.99, n = 83, p = 0.33), averaging 8.24 ± 3.2 (±SD) days.
Biting rate was taken as the inverse of the median gonotrophic cycle length, and set as 0.12 for all sites.

Temperature data indicated that although mean daily temperatures did not differ significantly between land-use types during the sampling period, daily temperature fluctuations were greater in oil palm plantations than in logged forest [55].

When calculated from thermal performance curves, biting rates were estimated to increase by 50% compared to estimates derived from laboratory data on gonotrophic cycle length, and did not differ significantly between land-use types (Wilcox, Z = -1.55, p = 0.13).

Empirical gonotrophic cycle data and the biting rate parameter used were the same across land-use types, and TPC-derived biting rates did not differ significantly between habitats. Thus the claim that increased biting rates driven by temperature fluctuations are the primary cause is not supported by the data presented.

325.Option: The increase in vectorial capacity in oil palm plantations, driven by greater mosquito abundance, was not predicted by commonly used thermal performance curves, despite similar mean temperatures between land-use types.
Evidence: Based on our data, vectorial capacity was estimated to be greater in oil palm plantation than in forest, largely driven by greater mosquito abundance in oil palm.
This key difference between land-use types was not predicted by commonly used TPCs.

Temperature data indicated that although mean daily temperatures did not differ significantly between land-use types during the sampling period, daily temperature fluctuations were greater in oil palm plantations than in logged forest [55].

Overall, human landing catches conducted in plantations yielded an average 1.5 times more mosquitoes per session than those at logged forest sites (GLM, RR = 0.42, df = 64, 95% CI: 1.17, 2.0, p = 0.002).
325.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that vectorial capacity was higher in oil palm plantations and that this was largely driven by greater mosquito abundance measured in the field.
