390.Rationale: Although IFN-γ-secreting T cells are present, the passage explicitly states that even when immune responses control acute infection, T. cruzi persists for decades and that persistence can lead to inflammatory tissue damage. The predominance of monofunctional IFN-γ responses and evidence of degraded/exhausted T cell responses argue against the conclusion that IFN-γ presence alone signifies effective control that prevents further damage. Thus the option overstates what can be inferred from the data.

391.Option: The temporal pattern of dengue in Port Sudan showed a bi-cyclic trend, with two transmission peaks: a short peak in winter (November–December) and a longer peak in summer (June–August), both preceded by peaks in mosquito density.
Evidence: The M-shaped curves have two peaks.
The first peak of P/P (0.51) occured in November followed by a serological peak of IgM in December (7.89%).
The second and largest peak of IgM was in July (8.14%). It was preceded by a higher peak of P/P two months earlier in May (0.66) and by the highest peak of larval ration in April (1.81).
Both the very low P/P in August (0.04) and disappearance of dengue vector in September resulted in zero IgM seropositivity in September and October.
There is a significant correlation between P/P index and IgM sero-prevalence in the next month (Pearson r = 0.71, n = 11, p = 0.015).
391.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes two serological peaks (M-shaped curves) with the first mosquito-density peak (P/P) in November followed by an IgM peak in December, and a second larger IgM peak in July that was preceded by higher mosquito indices in April–May. The significant month-lag correlation between P/P and subsequent IgM seroprevalence supports the statement that transmission peaks were preceded by peaks in mosquito density. The months named in the option (November–December and the summer period centered on July) are consistent with the timing reported in the passage, and the passage indicates the July peak was the larger one, supporting the characterization of a larger summer peak.

391.Option: The low incidence rate in lower and middle class neighborhoods suggests that these areas have higher herd immunity due to earlier exposure to dengue.
Evidence: BI, HI and P/P indices were lower in the upper class neighborhoods (65, 51 and .24), as compared to the other two strata (91, 66 and .38, respectively).

Overall, an incidence rate of 35 new cases in 10,000 (193/54,886) people was shown (Table 5). A significant difference between the high stratum and the other two strata was shown on incidence rates (F = 73.97, d.f. = 2, p < 0.001), 0.0031 versus 0.0036 each, respectively.
391.Rationale: The passage does not report a low incidence rate in lower and middle class neighborhoods; instead it states the incidence rate was lower in the high (upper-class) stratum (0.0031) versus the other two strata (0.0036). The option's claim is therefore inconsistent with the reported spatial pattern. Furthermore, the passage gives no evidence linking any observed incidence differences to higher herd immunity from earlier exposure; that inference is not supported by the presented data.

391.Option: The disappearance of the dengue vector (Ae. aegypti) in September demands further research to define whether there is a true disappearance and if so how and from where the vector is reintroduced.
Evidence: The dengue vector was found throughout the year in Port Sudan, except in September when no larvae or pupae Ae. aegypti was found in the inspected water containers.
It is possible that Ae. aegypti dwells in different breeding containers during September and/or there are desiccated eggs of the mosquito in the breeding containers.
Both the very low P/P in August (0.04) and disappearance of dengue vector in September resulted in zero IgM seropositivity in September and October.
391.Rationale: The passage documents an absence of detected Ae. aegypti larvae/pupae in September and explicitly acknowledges uncertainty about whether the vector truly disappears or persists in undetected forms (different containers or desiccated eggs). Given this stated uncertainty and its epidemiological importance for explaining the observed zero IgM seroprevalence in subsequent months, it is reasonable and directly supported by the passage to infer that further research is required to determine whether a true disappearance occurs and how reintroduction (if any) happens.

391.Option: The bi-cyclic transmission pattern in Port Sudan is driven primarily by seasonal monsoon winds, which facilitate the dispersal of Ae. aegypti from neighboring countries.
Evidence: The global emergence of dengue is linked to increasing travel of viremic people as well as dispersal of its main vectors (i.e. Aedes aegypti and Ae. albopictus) into new locations [3]–[5].

Meteorological parameters (minimum and maximum temperature, relative humidity, precipitation and, evaporation rates, wind speed and direction) were recorded on daily basis in two stations in Port Sudan.
The dengue vector was found throughout the year in Port Sudan, except in September when no larvae or pupae Ae. aegypti was found in the inspected water containers.
391.Rationale: While the introduction notes that vector dispersal and climate variability are related to dengue emergence and the study recorded wind speed and direction, the results and analyses presented do not provide evidence that seasonal monsoon winds drive the observed bi-cyclic transmission pattern or facilitate cross-border dispersal of Ae. aegypti. The passage presents correlations with temperature, humidity and local mosquito indices, but does not link transmission peaks to monsoon wind-driven importation from neighboring countries. Therefore the causal claim in the option is not supported by the study's presented data.

391.Option: The high seroprevalence of IgM in July (8.14%) was likely due to secondary dengue infection in adults aged 18–39, who are more vulnerable to severe disease.
Evidence: The second and largest peak of IgM was in July (8.14%).

There is a significant difference in IgM positive results between age groups :<5, 6–17, 18–39, 40–60 and >60 years (χ2 = 5.05, d.f. = 5, p = 0.03). Among these age groups, higher dengue attack rates were shown in adults aged 18–39 and above 40 years, 2.14% (17/791) and 1.77% (14/791), respectively.
391.Rationale: Although the passage reports a July IgM peak and notes higher overall attack rates in the 18–39 age group, it does not provide any data on whether infections were primary versus secondary, nor does it provide evidence that adults 18–39 were the specific contributors to the July peak or that they experienced more severe disease. The assertion that the July IgM peak was 'likely due to secondary dengue infection' and that adults 18–39 are 'more vulnerable to severe disease' is not supported by the study data presented in the passage.

392.Option: Visiting workers in Al Ahsa exhibit a stronger immune response to sandfly saliva because they are more likely to have been previously infected with L. major.
Evidence: In Al Ahsa, we found that non-local patients (visiting labour) had significantly higher levels (P<0.001) of anti-PpSP32 compared to the local residents (Fig. 5A).
Interestingly, nearly three quarters of the non-local patients developed more than three lesions compared to only 40% in the local group (Fig. 5B).
The presence of IgG antibodies against Ph. papatasi saliva has been associated with a higher risk of being infected with L. major [4,6].
392.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that visiting workers have higher anti-PpSP32 antibody levels and a higher number of lesions, and that anti-saliva IgG is associated with higher risk of infection. However, the option asserts a specific cause — that the stronger immune response is because visiting workers are more likely to have been previously infected with L. major. The Passage Body does not provide evidence distinguishing prior (past) infections from current infection or proving that prior infection prevalence is higher in visiting workers; it only documents higher antibody levels and lesion counts among non-locals. Therefore the causal claim about 'previously infected' is not supported by the provided text.

392.Option: The transient nature of the antibody response to sandfly bites allows for the detection of recent exposure but does not reflect long-term risk of leishmaniasis infection.
Evidence: The transient nature of the antibody response to sandfly bites [6–10] allows for the study of temporal changes in transmission risk and the efficacy of vector control programmes [11].
The presence of IgG antibodies against Ph. papatasi saliva has been associated with a higher risk of being infected with L. major [4,6].
392.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the transient antibody response permits study of temporal (recent) changes in transmission risk, which supports the first half of the option about detecting recent exposure. However, the option's concluding assertion that these transient antibodies 'do not reflect long-term risk of leishmaniasis infection' is not stated or demonstrated in the passage. In fact, the passage also reports that presence of IgG antibodies has been associated with higher risk of infection, implying some relationship to infection risk. Because the Passage Body does not provide evidence that transient antibody measures definitively do not reflect long-term risk, the stronger negative claim cannot be derived solely from the provided text.

392.Option: The higher antibody response to sandfly saliva in visiting workers compared to long-term residents in Al Ahsa suggests that the migrant population is highly exposed to sandfly bites and less immune to cutaneous leishmaniasis, while residents may have developed tolerance due to long-term exposure.
Evidence: In Al Ahsa, we found that non-local patients (visiting labour) had significantly higher levels (P<0.001) of anti-PpSP32 compared to the local residents (Fig. 5A).
Interestingly, nearly three quarters of the non-local patients developed more than three lesions compared to only 40% in the local group (Fig. 5B).
Repeated exposure to sandfly bites produces antibodies against its salivary components in the host, providing an indirect measure of exposure to vectors [5].
392.Rationale: The passage supports that visiting workers have higher anti-PpSP32 levels (indicating greater exposure) and more lesions than local residents. From this, it is reasonable to infer higher exposure among migrants. However, the option goes further to assert that migrants are 'less immune' while residents 'may have developed tolerance due to long-term exposure' — claims about relative immune status or tolerance are not presented or supported in the Passage Body. The study does not provide immunologic or longitudinal data demonstrating tolerance or immunity differences attributable to duration of residence. Thus the speculative interpretation about immunity/tolerance cannot be derived from the provided text.

392.Option: The significantly higher number of lesions in visiting workers compared to local residents in Al Ahsa is primarily due to genetic susceptibility differences among the migrant population.
Evidence: In Al Ahsa, we found that non-local patients (visiting labour) had significantly higher levels (P<0.001) of anti-PpSP32 compared to the local residents (Fig. 5A).
Interestingly, nearly three quarters of the non-local patients developed more than three lesions compared to only 40% in the local group (Fig. 5B).
392.Rationale: While the passage documents that visiting workers have higher antibody levels and a higher number of lesions than local residents, it does not provide any data or discussion about genetic susceptibility or any genetic comparisons between groups. The claim that the difference is 'primarily due to genetic susceptibility' is a causal assertion not supported by the Passage Body. Therefore this option cannot be derived from the provided evidence.

393.Option: Elevated levels of IL-6 in severe visceral leishmaniasis are primarily driven by concurrent bacterial infections, as indicated by high serum levels of LPS and FABP2.
Evidence: IL-6 levels were more significantly elevated in the patients with severe VL (Fig 3A; p = 0.03).

Remarkably, severe VL patients that died had IL-6 serum levels of 11,443 ± 15,388 pg/ml, whereas the surviving severe VL patients had 2,848 ± 7,512 pg/ml (Fig 3B; p = 0.01). All of the patients that died had circulating IL-6 levels ≥ 200 pg/ml, while only 2 patients that survived (1 SVL, 1 classical VL) presented with IL-6 levels above this threshold.
LPS and FABP2 levels were similar in the VL D0, DTH+ and HC groups and were not changed after treatment (VL D30) (Fig 4A and 4B).
393.Rationale: The passage shows IL-6 is elevated in severe VL and markedly higher in fatal cases, but it also reports that LPS and FABP2 levels (measures commonly used to indicate bacterial translocation/infection) were similar between VL patients and controls and did not change with treatment. Therefore the data do not support the claim that elevated IL-6 in severe VL is primarily driven by concurrent bacterial infections indicated by high LPS/FABP2. The passage provides direct evidence against the asserted mechanism.

393.Option: IL-6 levels are elevated in severe visceral leishmaniasis due to a failure in the regulation of IFN-γ production, which leads to uncontrolled inflammation and tissue damage.
Evidence: classical VL patients present an impairment of IL-2, IFN-γ and IL-12 production from T cells specific to leishmania antigens in vitro, that is restored after successful treatment [6–14].
Interestingly, patients with active VL have high levels of IFN-γ and IL-12 in their serum, which theoretically could help to control the infection.
IL-6 levels were more significantly elevated in the patients with severe VL (Fig 3A; p = 0.03).
The levels of particular circulating cytokines during the inflammatory processes observed in active VL disease could be a determinant, or arise as a consequence, of disease severity.
393.Rationale: While the passage documents both altered IFN-γ dynamics and elevated IL-6 in severe disease, it does not present evidence that failure to regulate IFN-γ is the cause of IL-6 elevation or that such a failure leads to uncontrolled inflammation and tissue damage. The authors explicitly note that cytokine elevations "could be a determinant, or arise as a consequence, of disease severity," indicating causality is not established. Thus the specific causal pathway asserted in the option is not supported by the presented data.

393.Option: The presence of sCD14 in serum is a reliable biomarker for bacterial co-infection in visceral leishmaniasis patients, as it is elevated during active disease and decreases after treatment.
Evidence: sCD14 and MIF, molecules associated with TLR recognition, were higher in the active disease group (2,882 ng/ml and 39 ng/ml, respectively) than DTH+ group (1,348 ng/ml and 19 ng/ml, respectively). Furthermore, circulating levels of sCD14 and MIF decreased with treatment (1,820 ng/ml and 17 ng/ml, respectively) (Fig 4C and 4D).
LPS and FABP2 levels were similar in the VL D0, DTH+ and HC groups and were not changed after treatment (VL D30) (Fig 4A and 4B).

Santos-Oliveira et al. suggested the interference of bacterial co-infections in the severity of VL, based on the finding of high serum levels of LPS, sCD14, MIF and Fatty Acid-Binding Protein 2 (FABP2) [20]. This suggestion was based on observation of a few patients, however, and this interesting finding cannot yet be generalized as an explanation for disease severity in all VL patients.
sCD14 levels were not, however, associated with disease severity/ mortality.
393.Rationale: Although sCD14 is elevated during active disease and falls after treatment, the study also found no increase in LPS or FABP2 (other markers of bacterial translocation) and explicitly cautions that prior suggestions linking these markers to bacterial co-infection cannot be generalized. The authors do not claim sCD14 is a reliable marker of bacterial co-infection; moreover, sCD14 was not associated with severity/mortality. Therefore the strong claim that sCD14 is a reliable biomarker for bacterial co-infection is not supported by the presented data.

393.Option: The systemic release of cytokines, particularly IL-6, is associated with disease severity and mortality in visceral leishmaniasis, independent of bacterial co-infection.
Evidence: High levels of IFN-γ, IL-10, IL-6, IL-27 and TNF-α cytokines were observed in sera of VL patients before treatment when compared with healthy control and DTH+ individuals (Fig 1).
IL-6 levels were more significantly elevated in the patients with severe VL (Fig 3A; p = 0.03).

Remarkably, severe VL patients that died had IL-6 serum levels of 11,443 ± 15,388 pg/ml, whereas the surviving severe VL patients had 2,848 ± 7,512 pg/ml (Fig 3B; p = 0.01). All of the patients that died had circulating IL-6 levels ≥ 200 pg/ml, while only 2 patients that survived (1 SVL, 1 classical VL) presented with IL-6 levels above this threshold.
LPS and FABP2 levels were similar in the VL D0, DTH+ and HC groups and were not changed after treatment (VL D30) (Fig 4A and 4B).

Recent reports have indicated a link between bacterial co-infection and VL disease severity [20,23,24]. To examine if bacterial infections could be influencing the release of the inflammatory and anti-inflammatory cytokines, we measured levels of the LPS, FABP2, MIF and sCD14 molecules.
393.Rationale: The study demonstrates that systemic cytokine release, especially IL-6, is higher in severe VL and markedly higher in fatal cases, indicating an association between IL-6 levels and severity/mortality. The authors specifically measured markers of bacterial translocation (LPS, FABP2) to evaluate whether bacterial co-infection could explain cytokine elevations and found these markers were not elevated in VL patients compared with controls. Taken together, the data support an association of cytokine storm/IL-6 with severity and mortality that is not explained by elevated LPS/FABP2 measures of bacterial co-infection in this dataset.

393.Option: The strong correlation between IL-27, IL-10, IL-6, and TNF-α suggests that these cytokines act in a synergistic cascade to drive the progression of visceral leishmaniasis.
Evidence: We observed a strong correlation between IL-27 and IL-6 (r = 0.69), IL-10 (r = 0.85) and TNF-α (r = 0.86) (Fig 2).
The levels of particular circulating cytokines during the inflammatory processes observed in active VL disease could be a determinant, or arise as a consequence, of disease severity.
393.Rationale: The passage documents strong correlations among IL-27, IL-10, IL-6 and TNF-α, but explicitly acknowledges that elevated cytokine levels "could be a determinant, or arise as a consequence, of disease severity." Correlation alone does not establish that these cytokines act in a causal, synergistic cascade driving disease progression. The authors do not provide mechanistic or temporal evidence to support the stronger causal claim in the option, so it cannot be derived from the presented data.

394.Option: The reproduction number in Chile fluctuated around 1.0 from early August 2020 onward, indicating that the epidemic was in a state of equilibrium with no net growth or decline in transmission.
Evidence: This was followed by a decline in the reproduction number to less than 1.0 between June 19th-August 9th, 2020. Since then, the reproduction number has fluctuated around 1.0 with the most recent estimate of R ~ 0.96 (95% CrI: 0.95, 0.98) (Fig 5).
The most recent estimate of R~ 0.96 (95% CrI: 0.95, 0.98) as of November 2nd, 2020.
394.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that after a decline to <1.0 between June 19 and August 9, 2020, the reproduction number 'has fluctuated around 1.0' with a most recent estimate near 0.96. An Rt fluctuating around 1.0 is epidemiologically interpreted as approximate equilibrium with no sustained net growth or decline in transmission. Therefore this inference is directly supported by the passage.

394.Option: The reopening of society in late April 2020 under the 'Safe Return' plan led to a sustained increase in case incidence, which was not mitigated by subsequent localized lockdowns in Santiago and Valparaíso.
Evidence: the government started to ease the COVID-19 restrictions in late April by reopening the economy under the “Safe Return” plan, including the televised opening of some businesses and stores, as new infections had reduced between 350–500 per day by the end of April, implying an only apparent flattening of the COVID-19 curve

This strategy resulted in a new wave of infections; with the virus spreading from more affluent areas of Chile to more impoverished, crowded communities, forcing the government to reimpose lockdown measures in Santiago in mid-May
The case counts continued to increase, averaging ~4943 cases per day in June 2020, and started to decline thereafter.
The reproduction number was estimated at 0.87 (95% CI: 0.84, 0.89) as of July 7th, 2020.
394.Rationale: While the passage indicates that reopening under 'Safe Return' was followed by a new wave of infections and that localized measures initially proved problematic, it also provides evidence that case counts began to decline after the June peak and that the reproduction number was estimated below 1.0 (R = 0.87) by July 7, 2020. Thus the claim that the increase was 'sustained' and 'not mitigated' by subsequent localized lockdowns is not supported by the passage data, which indicate mitigation (declining cases and R<1) following reimposed interventions.

394.Option: The initial sub-exponential growth pattern in Chile (p ~0.8) was primarily due to the country’s high population density and urbanization, which limited the effectiveness of early interventions.
Evidence: The incidence curve displays sub-exponential growth dynamics with the scaling of growth parameter (deceleration of growth parameter), p, estimated at 0.77 (95% CI: 0.73, 0.81) and the intrinsic growth rate, r, estimated at 0.81 (95% CI: 0.67, 1.00).

Chile has employed an agile public health response by announcing a ban on public health gatherings of more than 500 people on March 13th, 2020... the closure of all daycares, schools, and universities on March 16th, 2020... the announcement to close country borders on March 18th, 2020, and the declaration of national emergency on the same date, accompanied by several concrete interventions to further contain the outbreak in the region.
imposing and lifting lockdowns in small geographical areas (municipalities) proved unsuccessful in areas with high interdependencies such as the Greater Santiago.
394.Rationale: The passage documents the observed sub-exponential growth (p ≈ 0.77–0.80) and describes early interventions and some issues with municipality-level lockdowns. However, it does not attribute the observed p value primarily to population density or urbanization. The causal claim that high density/urbanization was the primary reason limiting early interventions is not stated or demonstrated in the passage; therefore this inference cannot be derived solely from the provided text.

394.Option: The implementation of lockdowns and apt social distancing interventions have indeed slowed the spread of the virus.
Evidence: The 20-day ahead forecast suggested that the early social distancing measures significantly slowed down the early spread of the virus in Chile, whose effect is noticeable about two weeks after implementing an intervention, as shown in Fig 6.
The reproduction number was estimated at 0.87 (95% CI: 0.84, 0.89) as of July 7th, 2020.
The incidence curve displays a nearly linear growth trend with the deceleration of growth parameter, p, estimated at 0.51 (95% CI: 0.47, 0.56).
394.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that model forecasts indicate early social distancing measures 'significantly slowed down the early spread' with effects visible after about two weeks. Additionally, subsequent modeling around July 7th shows a reproduction number <1 (0.87) and a shift to a near-linear growth pattern (p~0.51), consistent with slowed transmission. Thus the inference that interventions slowed spread is directly supported by the text.

394.Option: The high positivity rate at the beginning of the epidemic indicates that the government failed to cast a wide enough net to test the masses early in the pandemic, and there were probably many more active cases than those detected by epidemiological surveillance, underestimating the epidemic growth curve.
Evidence: The total number of tests performed for this time period were 4,325,617, amongst which 10.9% (47,597) had positive results.
The average number of daily tests was estimated at ~5,460 for April 2020 and ~12,959 for May 2020, a 137% increase.

The positivity rate (percentage of positive tests among the total number of tests) has fluctuated from a monthly average of ~9.07% (SD: 2.3) in April 2020 to a monthly average of ~4.87% (SD: 0.65) in October 2020.
394.Rationale: The passage provides data showing relatively low daily testing early in the epidemic (~5,460/day in April) and higher positivity (~9.07% in April), and documents expansion of testing over time. While these facts are compatible with possible under-ascertainment early in the epidemic, the passage does not explicitly conclude that the government 'failed' to test widely nor quantify the degree of undetected cases. Inferring government failure and a specific extent of underestimation goes beyond the surveillance and modeling data presented and requires additional assumptions or external data; therefore this stronger causal judgment is not derivable solely from the passage.

395.Option: The level of knowledge about rabies and its control measures is directly associated with the uptake of the 2012 vaccination campaign, indicating that educational campaigns should be prioritized to improve vaccination rates.
Evidence: The vaccination uptake was significantly associated with the knowledge of the dog owners about rabies (p<0.001) and its control measures (p<0.05) (Table 6).
The proportion of dog owners who had vaccinated dogs was higher for those who considered rabies a fatal disease (54%; 225/415) than for those who did not (26%; 9/35).
Similarly, dog owners with a high level of knowledge of rabies control measures (59%; 161/272) tended to vaccinate their dogs compared to their counterparts (46%; 60/131).

For those dog owners who had not vaccinated their dogs (216 of 450 dog owners), the most important reasons for not joining were the lack of information about the schedule of the vaccination campaign (40%) and the difficulty to catch their dogs during the vaccination campaign (37%) (Fig. 3).
395.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports a significant association between dog owners' knowledge about rabies and their uptake of the 2012 vaccination campaign (p<0.001). Higher proportions of vaccination uptake were observed among owners who recognized rabies as fatal and among those with a higher level of knowledge about control measures. Additionally, lack of information about the campaign schedule was the most commonly reported reason for not participating (40%), which supports the inference that improving information and education could increase uptake. Therefore, prioritizing educational campaigns is a reasonable epidemiological implication derivable from the presented data.

395.Option: Geographical accessibility is one of the important predictors of vaccination uptake among dog owners, and targeted interventions in villages with poor accessibility may increase vaccination uptake in the future.
Evidence: Moreover, the uptake of vaccination was higher if the dog owners had a monthly income of more than 1 million Rupiah (OR = 2.39; 95%CI = 1.10–5.20) and lived in a village with a good accessibility (OR = 3.84; 95%CI = 1.92–7.67) compared with those having a yearly income less than Rp500,000 and who lived in a village with poor accessibility.

The odds of having a high level of knowledge of rabies control measures was higher in the following situations: for dog owners who lived in villages with good accessibility (OR = 2.14; 95% CI = 1.07–4.27) compared to poor accessibility, ...

Villages located along the provincial road connecting West to East Flores Island were categorized as villages with ‘good accessibility’ ... Villages that can only be reached by foot or motorcycle, which have no public transportation facility, were categorized as villages with ‘poor accessibility’.

For those dog owners who had not vaccinated their dogs (216 of 450 dog owners), the most important reasons for not joining were the lack of information about the schedule of the vaccination campaign (40%) and the difficulty to catch their dogs during the vaccination campaign (37%) (Fig. 3).
395.Rationale: The multivariable analysis identifies village accessibility as an independent predictor of vaccination uptake (OR = 3.84 for good vs poor accessibility), indicating that owners in better-access villages were substantially more likely to participate. The passage defines accessibility categories and further links good accessibility with higher knowledge levels, which can facilitate uptake. Reported barriers such as difficulty catching dogs and lack of information also plausibly interact with accessibility. Thus, it is epidemiologically supported to infer that targeted strategies addressing poor-access villages (e.g., tailored outreach, logistical support) could increase vaccination uptake.

395.Option: The low vaccination coverage in Flores Island is primarily due to the lack of trust in the vaccine's efficacy, as many dog owners expressed skepticism about its effectiveness.
Evidence: For those dog owners who had not vaccinated their dogs (216 of 450 dog owners), the most important reasons for not joining were the lack of information about the schedule of the vaccination campaign (40%) and the difficulty to catch their dogs during the vaccination campaign (37%) (Fig. 3).
Other reasons, of minor importance, included the lack of belief in the vaccine efficacy (13%), and the young age of the dog at the time of the vaccination campaign (6%).

The uptake rate of the dog vaccination measure has been low, with an average vaccination coverage of around 53% of the registered dogs during the 2000–2011 vaccination campaigns [4]. This value is lower than the 70% coverage of the complete dog population, which is recommended to maintain the control of rabies between annual vaccination campaigns [1].
395.Rationale: The passage lists the primary reasons for not participating in the vaccination campaign as lack of information (40%) and difficulty catching dogs (37%); lack of belief in vaccine efficacy was reported by only 13% of respondents. Therefore, the data do not support the assertion that skepticism about vaccine effectiveness is the primary cause of low coverage. The statement that low coverage is 'primarily due to' lack of trust is not derivable from the presented data.

395.Option: The high uptake of post-exposure treatment among dog-bite victims indicates that the public health system in Flores Island is well-equipped to manage rabies exposure and prevent fatalities.
Evidence: Of the 450 dog owners interviewed, 89 (20%) reported that at least one of their family members had been bitten by a suspected rabid dog during the last fourteen years. Of these 89 bite cases, 75 (84%) cleaned the wound, and 50 (56%) received vaccination.
The level of uptake of these measures did not differ between regencies (p>0.05).
395.Rationale: While wound cleaning was common (84%), only 56% of bite victims received vaccination. The passage does not provide data on completeness/timeliness of post-exposure prophylaxis, availability of immunoglobulin, or outcome (fatalities prevented). A 56% vaccination rate does not clearly indicate a well-equipped system capable of preventing fatalities. Therefore, the strong conclusion that the public health system is well-equipped is not supported by the presented data.

395.Option: The culling of roaming dogs in Manggarai Regency has been effective in reducing rabies transmission and should be expanded to other regencies as a primary control strategy.
Evidence: In response, the Flores Island government have implemented a comprehensive control campaign since 2000. This control campaign is based on a combination of control measures, including mass vaccination of dogs, culling of roaming dogs, placing imported dogs in quarantine, and giving pre- and post-exposure treatment to humans.

Despite the initial control measures applied, which entailed the culling of the entire dog population in and around the affected villages (1998–1999), rabies spread to other regencies of the island [11].
The proportion of dog owners that had culled their dogs was significantly higher in Manggarai (45%) than in Sikka (27%) (p<0.001).
However, this campaign has not yet been successful in eliminating rabies from Flores Island.
395.Rationale: Although culling was used more frequently in Manggarai and contributed to differences in dog numbers, the passage does not present evidence that culling reduced rabies transmission or human cases; in fact, it notes that rabies spread despite earlier culling and that the overall campaign has not eliminated rabies from the island. There is no data demonstrating effectiveness of culling as the primary control measure, so recommending expansion of culling cannot be derived from the presented data.

396.Option: The spatial distribution of dengue cases in Peru is primarily driven by human movement and population density, with weather patterns playing a secondary role in determining transmission intensity.
Evidence: We conclude that a coupling of temperature-humidity is an important and sensitive determinant of DENV transmission-potential regulating the location, timing and intensity of risk.

Out of more than 2 trillion classification tests performed to fully explore Peruvian weather-space, only one set of weather inputs achieved a classification of districts as dengue-positive or negative with 95% sensitivity and specificity.

with 30 million people in Peru, the largest population centers are mostly clustered along the western coast. Highest dengue incidence rates developed in a different spatial pattern across northeastern Peru.
Human movement across the border may provide a dengue entry point into Peru.
396.Rationale: The passage repeatedly identifies temperature-humidity coupling as the primary, sensitive determinant of where, when, and how intense transmission is, and demonstrates that weather thresholds alone can classify transmission status with high sensitivity/specificity. Although human movement and population distribution are mentioned (e.g., as possible entry points), the authors do not state that human movement or population density primarily drive spatial distribution; in fact they note highest incidence developed in a spatial pattern different from largest population centers. Therefore the claim that spatial distribution is primarily driven by human movement/population density with weather secondary is not supported by the passage.

396.Option: The coupling of temperature and humidity is a critical determinant of dengue transmission potential, with higher humidity levels amplifying transmission magnitude when temperature is optimal, and transmission intensity increasing exponentially with longer duration in optimal weather-space.
Evidence: We conclude that a coupling of temperature-humidity is an important and sensitive determinant of DENV transmission-potential regulating the location, timing and intensity of risk.

Group 5, the highest transmission group, was distinguished by annual periods of concurrent high mean and minimum temperature and high mean humidity > 82%. The most important observation from level 2 classifications was that although temperature alone identified positive vs. negative districts in level 1, mean humidity in conjunction with temperature was necessary for differentiating groups by transmission intensity, indicating a coupling of temperature-humidity in the dynamics of large epidemics.
Impact was highest as humidity increased above 80% when mean temperature was 25–29°C.
Annual duration above 22°C was the best predictor of incidence magnitude in lower incidence districts; duration above 25°C was the best predictor in higher incidence districts.
producing the largest epidemics when humidity >80% for >6 weeks per year or >75% for >19 weeks per year, while concurrently mean temperature >25°C.

The natural log of cumulative incidence rate was used because the increase in incidence as temperatures rise within the optimal range and as humidity rises amidst temperatures optimal for transmission is exponential in nature.
396.Rationale: The passage explicitly concludes temperature-humidity coupling is an important determinant and provides empirical findings that higher humidity (e.g., >80%) amplifies impact when mean temperature is in the 25–29°C range. It also documents that longer annual duration in these optimal conditions predicts higher cumulative incidence and that incidence increases exponentially (log-transformation used because of exponential relationship). Thus this overarching relationship is directly supported by the evidence presented.

396.Option: The relationship between weather and dengue transmission is linear and predictable, with each degree increase in temperature directly proportional to a corresponding increase in case counts.
Evidence: Weather-dengue dynamics are complex, multi-factorial, and non-linear and traditional statistical methods have produced diverse and conflicting perspectives regarding the weather-disease connection [3–5,7,8,11–18].

The natural log of cumulative incidence rate was used because the increase in incidence as temperatures rise within the optimal range and as humidity rises amidst temperatures optimal for transmission is exponential in nature.
The dramatic contrast between where time is spent in weather-space and where transmission occurs indicates that weather necessary to support transmission is very specific.
396.Rationale: The passage explicitly characterizes weather-dengue dynamics as non-linear and complex, and uses a log transformation because incidence increases exponentially (not linearly) with optimal weather conditions. It also emphasizes that transmission occurs only in a specific weather-space rather than showing a simple proportional increase per degree. Therefore the linear proportional claim is not supported.

396.Option: Rainfall is a more significant predictor of dengue transmission in Peru than temperature and humidity, as it directly influences mosquito breeding sites and seasonal onset of epidemics.
Evidence: Temperature and humidity are emerging as weather components with greatest impact on DENV transmission, yet the separate and combined effects of this coupling across a range of ecological conditions remain ill-defined [3,4,7,11].

Weather was conceived as a 2-dimensional “weather-space” composed of temperature vs. humidity, inclusive of the full range of conditions observed locally across Peru, within which DENV dynamics are observed (portrayed for low and high transmission areas in S1 Fig).
We conclude that a coupling of temperature-humidity is an important and sensitive determinant of DENV transmission-potential regulating the location, timing and intensity of risk.
396.Rationale: The passage focuses on temperature and humidity as the primary weather components affecting transmission and explicitly frames weather-space as temperature vs. humidity. Rainfall is not presented or analyzed as a primary predictor in this study. Therefore the claim that rainfall is a more significant predictor is not supported by the passage.

396.Option: The largest dengue epidemics in Peru occurred in areas with the highest population density, indicating that human population size is the primary determinant of epidemic intensity regardless of weather conditions.
Evidence: with 30 million people in Peru, the largest population centers are mostly clustered along the western coast. Highest dengue incidence rates developed in a different spatial pattern across northeastern Peru.
We conclude that a coupling of temperature-humidity is an important and sensitive determinant of DENV transmission-potential regulating the location, timing and intensity of risk.
Together these areas represent 11% of the Peruvian population and 70% of cases reported during 1994–2012.

Maynas has a limited weather-space that supports dengue transmission nearly year round in the lower end of the optimal temperature range but with very high humidity >80% most of the year. The large population, high incidence rates, and year-round transmission season, make Maynas an important reservoir of cases for the region.
396.Rationale: The passage shows that the largest epidemics were concentrated in geographically specific elevated-risk areas that together account for a disproportionate share of cases (70%) but only 11% of the population, and it emphasizes that weather (temperature-humidity coupling and duration in optimal weather-space) is the key regulator of where high transmission occurs. While population size can contribute (e.g., Maynas combines large population and favorable weather), the passage does not support the claim that population density alone, regardless of weather, is the primary determinant of epidemic intensity.

397.Option: The structural similarity of Ov-TSP-1 to Sm-TSP-2 from S. mansoni implies that Ov-tsp-1 is essential for tegument formation in both species due to conserved function.
Evidence: Sm-TSP-2 is a tetraspanin from the tegument of S. mansoni [15], [16] that is essential for proper tegument formation, and silencing of the Sm-tsp-2 gene proved lethal for schistosomula in vivo [17].

Ov-TSP-1 showed the conserved characteristics of the TSP family, notably the signature cysteine–cysteine-glycine (CCG) motif in the LEL, which is the location for the formation of three disulfide bonds within the LEL and influences interactions with other molecules (Figure 1).
Ov-TSP-1 showed ... 74% identity with Sm-TSP-1 of Schistosoma mansoni (XP_002580456.1) (Figure 1).

Suppression of Ov-tsp-1 in adult flukes impacts on proper tegument formation and results in increased vacuolation, implying that this protein is essential for fluke development and survival and is therefore worthy of consideration as a vaccine and/or drug target.

This RNAi procedure knocked down expression of Ov-tsp-1 by 67% (p = 0.06), 72% (p = 0.01) and 55% (p = 0.02) on days 6, 10 and 16 in vitro, respectively, compared to the negative control group that received luciferase dsRNA (Figure 6).

The tegument of Ov-tsp-1 dsRNA treated worms (Figure 7C, E and F) was more highly vacuolated than firefly luciferease dsRNA and non dsRNA controls (Figure 7B, A and D), with extensive and enlarged vacuoles throughout the surface layer.
397.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that a schistosome tetraspanin (Sm-TSP-2) is essential for tegument formation and lethal when silenced. It also shows that Ov-TSP-1 shares substantial sequence identity with schistosome tetraspanins and has the conserved structural features of the TSP family. Independent experimental knockdown of Ov-tsp-1 produced tegument malformation in O. viverrini. Together these facts (known essential function in S. mansoni, high sequence/structural similarity, and functional loss in O. viverrini upon knockdown) support the inference that the tetraspanins perform a conserved, essential role in tegument formation across these trematode species.

397.Option: Suppression of Ov-tsp-1 expression in adult O. viverrini results in tegument malformation, including increased vacuolation and a thinner tegument, indicating that Ov-TSP-1 is essential for proper tegument formation and structural integrity.
Evidence: Suppression of Ov-tsp-1 in adult flukes impacts on proper tegument formation and results in increased vacuolation, implying that this protein is essential for fluke development and survival and is therefore worthy of consideration as a vaccine and/or drug target.

This RNAi procedure knocked down expression of Ov-tsp-1 by 67% (p = 0.06), 72% (p = 0.01) and 55% (p = 0.02) on days 6, 10 and 16 in vitro, respectively, compared to the negative control group that received luciferase dsRNA (Figure 6).

The tegument of Ov-tsp-1 dsRNA treated worms (Figure 7C, E and F) was more highly vacuolated than firefly luciferease dsRNA and non dsRNA controls (Figure 7B, A and D), with extensive and enlarged vacuoles throughout the surface layer.
The tegument of knocked down tsp-1 worm is more highly vacuolated (indicated by arrows) and thinner compared with controls.
397.Rationale: The experimental data show that targeted RNAi reduced Ov-tsp-1 transcript levels and that the resulting phenotype observed by TEM was marked tegumental pathology — increased vacuolation and thinning compared with controls. The authors explicitly interpret these findings as implying that Ov-TSP-1 is essential for proper tegument formation and survival. Therefore the option is directly supported by the study results.

397.Option: The recognition of Ov-TSP-1 by sera from infected humans and hamsters indicates that the protein is a promising candidate for serodiagnosis of opisthorchiasis.
Evidence: Immunoblot analysis showed that sera from humans and hamsters infected with O. viverrini both reacted with rOv-TSP-1 (Figure 5).
Non-infected hamsters served as negative controls.
397.Rationale: The passage documents that recombinant Ov-TSP-1 is recognized by antibodies in sera from infected humans and experimentally infected hamsters, while non-infected hamster sera did not react (negative controls). Seroreactivity in infected hosts and absence in negatives is the fundamental evidence required to consider an antigen as a candidate for serodiagnostic assays. While further validation (sensitivity, specificity, cross-reactivity) would be required before clinical use, the immediate experimental outcome supports the inference that Ov-TSP-1 is a promising serodiagnostic candidate.

397.Option: The expression of Ov-tsp-1 in all developmental stages of O. viverrini suggests that it plays a role in the initial excystation of metacercariae from fish hosts.
Evidence: Ov-tsp-1 is expressed in all stages; expression was highest in metacercariae followed by two-week old juveniles, adults and eggs in descending order (Figure 3).

The tegument of O. viverrini metacercariae, juvenile and adult flukes is exposed to the mammalian host tissues; indeed the tegument of the adult fluke forms an intimate contact with the host biliary epithelium [20], resulting in chronic cell proliferation, immunopathology and ultimately tumorigenesis [21], [22].
397.Rationale: While the passage shows that Ov-tsp-1 is expressed at all life stages and is relatively abundant in metacercariae, there is no experimental data addressing the process of excystation from fish hosts or a mechanistic link between Ov-tsp-1 and excystation. Expression in a stage is not sufficient evidence to infer a specific role in excystation. Therefore the specific functional claim about initial excystation cannot be derived from the presented data.

397.Option: The high sequence identity between Ov-TSP-1 and C. sinensis TSP suggests that Ov-tsp-1 may function as a receptor for host bile components, facilitating parasite survival in the biliary tree.
Evidence: Ov-TSP-1 showed the conserved characteristics of the TSP family, notably the signature cysteine–cysteine-glycine (CCG) motif in the LEL, which is the location for the formation of three disulfide bonds within the LEL and influences interactions with other molecules (Figure 1).
Ov-TSP-1 shares 97% identity with a TSP of its close relative, Clonorchis sinensis (GAA49954.1) calculated from the alignment over 229 amino acid ...

Tetraspanins contain 4 transmembrane domains and are frequently expressed at the cell surface in association with each other and with other molecules, such as integrins, where they function to regulate cell adhesion, migration, proliferation, and differentiation [12], [13]. Tetraspanins have also been shown to act as receptors for viruses, most notably CD81 binding to hepatitis C [14].
the tegument of the adult fluke forms an intimate contact with the host biliary epithelium [20], resulting in chronic cell proliferation, immunopathology and ultimately tumorigenesis [21], [22].
397.Rationale: The passage establishes high sequence identity between Ov-TSP-1 and C. sinensis TSP and notes that tetraspanins are surface proteins that interact with other molecules and can act as receptors in some contexts. It also notes the intimate contact of the tegument with the biliary epithelium. However, there are no experimental data in this study demonstrating that Ov-TSP-1 binds host bile components or acts as a receptor for such molecules. The suggestion that it 'may function as a receptor for host bile components' is speculative and not directly supported by the experimental outcomes described; thus this specific functional inference cannot be derived from the passage.

398.Option: Since FZT infections involve multiple reservoir hosts including domestic animals and the transmission cycle includes environmental contamination, preventive chemotherapy targeting only humans may not be sufficient to reduce long-term transmission.
Evidence: Studies from the same area have revealed high prevalence of different MIF-species, especially Haplorchis spp., in dogs, cats and pigs, suggesting that domestic animals could play an important role as reservoir hosts and in the transmission of FZT [9], [10].
FZT infects animals and humans after consumption of raw or insufficiently cooked freshwater fish, in the case of humans often as a traditional dish [2].

Nissen et al. found that half of the North Vietnamese farm dogs that had been given a single dose of praziquantel were reinfected with FZT within three months [24]. Hence they could not recommend anthelmintic treatment as the only intervention to control FZT in dogs.
The prevalence two, 16, 29 and 60 weeks after preventive chemotherapy were 2.3% (CI 0.8–3.7), 10.9% (CI 7.8–13.9), 18.2% (CI 14.4–22.0) and 29.8% (CI 25.3–34.3), respectively.
76/118 (65%) of those people egg-positive after 60 weeks were also positive before treatment, suggesting a relative high degree of reinfection in these people.
398.Rationale: The passage documents high prevalence of FZT in domestic animals and explicitly suggests they "could play an important role as reservoir hosts and in the transmission of FZT." It also documents rapid reinfection in both animals (dogs reinfected within three months) and humans (prevalence rising to 29.8% by 60 weeks, with 65% of positives at 60 weeks previously positive). These observations support the epidemiological inference that interventions targeting humans alone (preventive chemotherapy) are unlikely to achieve sustained reduction of transmission when animal reservoirs and environmental/fish-mediated transmission remain unaddressed.

398.Option: The high reinfection rate observed within one year after preventive chemotherapy suggests that the current strategy of selective treatment based on self-reported raw fish consumption is ineffective and should be replaced with universal treatment in all endemic areas.
Evidence: Preventive chemotherapy is here given as selective chemotherapy to people confirming that they eat raw fish.
The prevalence two, 16, 29 and 60 weeks after preventive chemotherapy were 2.3% (CI 0.8–3.7), 10.9% (CI 7.8–13.9), 18.2% (CI 14.4–22.0) and 29.8% (CI 25.3–34.3), respectively.
The increase in prevalence after preventive chemotherapy is significant between all time points, as seen by the repeated measure ANOVA analysis (table 1).

WHO recommends preventive chemotherapy, that is large-scale distribution of anthelminthic drugs to populations at risk, as the main intervention strategy against liver flukes [12], [13]. If the prevalence in a district is >20%, universal treatment of all individuals in the district is recommended once a year. If the prevalence is <20%, the recommendation is either a universal treatment every second year, or targeted treatment once a year of all those who report habitually eating raw fish [12].
398.Rationale: The passage supports that selective treatment (based on self-reported raw fish consumption) was the program used and that reinfection rates rose substantially within a year. This suggests selective treatment may be inadequate in these studied communities. However, the specific policy recommendation to "replace with universal treatment in all endemic areas" is broader than the evidence supports. The study provides data from two communes and documents WHO guidance that universal treatment is recommended when district prevalence is >20% — but it does not provide evidence that universal treatment would be superior in all endemic settings, nor does it evaluate universal versus selective strategies across diverse contexts. Therefore the strong, universal policy conclusion cannot be strictly derived from the passage alone.

398.Option: The fact that C. sinensis infections are less common in fish than in humans suggests that human-to-human transmission of this species is more likely than transmission via contaminated fish.
Evidence: FZT infects animals and humans after consumption of raw or insufficiently cooked freshwater fish, in the case of humans often as a traditional dish [2].
The morphological characteristics of eggs belonging to different species are difficult to differentiate by microscopy, and this may lead to unreliable data of their epidemiological distribution [6].
In Northern Vietnam, there is a mix of C. sinensis and MIF, especially Haplorchis spp., in humans [7], [8].

Studies from the same area have revealed high prevalence of different MIF-species, especially Haplorchis spp., in dogs, cats and pigs, suggesting that domestic animals could play an important role as reservoir hosts and in the transmission of FZT [9], [10].
398.Rationale: The passage consistently describes fish consumption as the transmission route for FZT and documents animal reservoirs (dogs, cats, pigs). It does not state that C. sinensis is less common in fish than in humans, nor does it provide any evidence of human-to-human transmission. In fact, the biology described implies foodborne transmission from infected fish. The note that egg morphology is difficult to differentiate only cautions about species misclassification, not about alternative transmission modes. Therefore the claimed inference about human-to-human transmission is unsupported by the passage.

398.Option: Because MIF infections are less serious and not carcinogenic, preventive chemotherapy should be prioritized for liver fluke infections rather than for minute intestinal fluke infections.
Evidence: The most serious health hazard associated with liver fluke infection is the increased risk of cholangiocarcinoma.
The MIF infections have been less studied, but the clinical manifestations may be less serious and MIF are not considered carcinogenic [5].
The morbidity of the liver trematodes, especially the predisposition for cholangiocarcinoma, is the main reason for establishing control measures [11].
WHO recommends preventive chemotherapy, that is large-scale distribution of anthelminthic drugs to populations at risk, as the main intervention strategy against liver flukes [12], [13].
398.Rationale: The passage explicitly contrasts the greater morbidity and carcinogenic risk associated with liver flukes (e.g., risk of cholangiocarcinoma) against MIF, which are described as likely less serious and not carcinogenic. It also notes that the morbidity of liver trematodes is the primary rationale for control measures and that WHO recommends preventive chemotherapy against liver flukes. From an epidemiological prioritization perspective, this supports the inference that preventive chemotherapy and control efforts should be prioritized toward liver fluke infections over MIF, given their higher long-term health burden.

398.Option: The use of praziquantel in dogs and cats has been shown to be highly effective in reducing FZT transmission, making animal treatment a viable alternative to human-targeted interventions.
Evidence: Nissen et al. found that half of the North Vietnamese farm dogs that had been given a single dose of praziquantel were reinfected with FZT within three months [24]. Hence they could not recommend anthelmintic treatment as the only intervention to control FZT in dogs.

Studies from the same area have revealed high prevalence of different MIF-species, especially Haplorchis spp., in dogs, cats and pigs, suggesting that domestic animals could play an important role as reservoir hosts and in the transmission of FZT [9], [10].
76/118 (65%) of those people egg-positive after 60 weeks were also positive before treatment, suggesting a relative high degree of reinfection in these people.
398.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that praziquantel given to farm dogs resulted in rapid reinfection (50% reinfected within three months) and explicitly states that anthelmintic treatment alone is not recommended as the sole intervention for controlling FZT in dogs. The persistent high prevalence in animals and humans and the documented reinfection undermine the claim that animal praziquantel treatment alone is a highly effective alternative to human-targeted interventions. Therefore the option is not supported by the presented evidence.

399.Option: The presence of specific refractory genes in Ae. aegypti populations from northern Cameroon may explain the lower transmission rates observed in these populations, suggesting that genetic resistance could limit ZIKV spread in certain regions.
Evidence: Similarly, in Ae. aegypti, TR varied significantly (Fisher’s exact test: P = 0.0014) according to the population with lowest TR reported in two populations from northern part of Cameroon, Bénoué (56%) and Maroua (43%) (Fig 3B).
It has been demonstrated that the level of vector competence varies according to mosquito populations and ZIKV strains [28–31].
399.Rationale: The passage documents lower transmission rates in two Ae. aegypti populations from northern Cameroon (Bénoué and Maroua) and notes that vector competence varies by mosquito population and viral strain. However, the specific claim that 'refractory genes' are the explanation is a mechanistic genetic assertion not measured or reported in the study. The passage provides no data on mosquito genetics or identification of refractory genes, so attributing the observed lower transmission rates to specific genetic resistance cannot be derived from the passage.

399.Option: The invasive species Ae. albopictus, which has replaced Ae. aegypti in many areas of Central Africa, is likely to be the primary driver of future ZIKV outbreaks due to its higher population density and broader geographic distribution.
Evidence: it was reported for the first time in Central Africa in early 2000 in Cameroon [21] and has progressively colonized almost all countries in the region where it tends to supplant the resident species Ae. aegypti [22–25].
When all populations of each species were analysed together, Ae. aegypti (56%) exhibited higher TEs than Ae. albopictus (42%) (Fisher’s exact test: P = 0.02).
Overall, ZIKV titres were significantly higher in Ae. aegypti compared to Ae. albopictus (Chi-squared test: χ2 = 6.4527, df = 1, P = 0.01).
399.Rationale: While the passage states that Ae. albopictus has invaded and tends to supplant Ae. aegypti in the region, the experimental data show that Ae. aegypti had higher transmission efficiency and higher ZIKV titres than Ae. albopictus. The option's claim that Ae. albopictus is likely to be the primary driver because of higher density and distribution invokes population density and geographic distribution which were not measured in this study. The experimental findings do not support the conclusion that Ae. albopictus would be the primary driver of outbreaks; in fact, the competence data point the other way.

399.Option: The higher transmission efficiency of Ae. aegypti in Central Africa is primarily due to its greater preference for human blood meals, which enhances its role in sustaining human-to-human transmission cycles.
Evidence: When all populations of each species were analysed together, Ae. aegypti (56%) exhibited higher TEs than Ae. albopictus (42%) (Fisher’s exact test: P = 0.02).
For each population, six batches of 60, 7–10 day-old females were exposed to an infectious blood meal containing 1.4 mL of washed rabbit erythrocytes and 700 μL of viral suspension.
399.Rationale: The passage demonstrates that Ae. aegypti had higher transmission efficiency than Ae. albopictus in these experiments. However, the study used standardized artificial blood meals (washed rabbit erythrocytes) for all mosquitoes, and did not measure or report host (human) feeding preference. Therefore, asserting that greater anthropophily (preference for human blood meals) is the primary reason for higher TE is unsupported by the data presented and cannot be derived from the passage.

399.Option: Despite the invasive nature of Ae. albopictus and its dominance in sympatric areas, Ae. aegypti exhibits higher transmission efficiency and viral titres in saliva compared to Ae. albopictus, suggesting a greater potential for ZIKV transmission in Central Africa.
Evidence: it was reported for the first time in Central Africa in early 2000 in Cameroon [21] and has progressively colonized almost all countries in the region where it tends to supplant the resident species Ae. aegypti [22–25].
When all populations of each species were analysed together, Ae. aegypti (56%) exhibited higher TEs than Ae. albopictus (42%) (Fisher’s exact test: P = 0.02).
Overall, ZIKV titres were significantly higher in Ae. aegypti compared to Ae. albopictus (Chi-squared test: χ2 = 6.4527, df = 1, P = 0.01).
399.Rationale: The passage states that Ae. albopictus has invaded and tends to supplant Ae. aegypti in the region, but the experimental results show Ae. aegypti had significantly higher transmission efficiency and higher ZIKV titres than Ae. albopictus. From an epidemiological perspective, higher transmission efficiency and greater viral titres in saliva are direct indicators of greater vector potential to sustain human transmission. Thus the inference that Ae. aegypti has greater potential for ZIKV transmission in Central Africa is supported by the presented data.

400.Option: The lack of further titer increase after a third vaccination in adult pigs suggests that mRNA vaccines are less effective than traditional vaccines in boosting long-term immunity.
Evidence: The third vaccination at day 49 did not further increase RABV-G specific neutralizing antibody levels.
These experiments, therefore, indicate comparable immunogenicity of the mRNA vaccine with licensed rabies vaccine and the general induction of specific immune response in mice.
400.Rationale: The passage reports that the third vaccination in adult pigs did not further raise titers, but it also states that mRNA vaccine immunogenicity was comparable to licensed vaccines. A single observation of no further titer increase after an additional boost does not support the broad conclusion that mRNA vaccines are less effective than traditional vaccines at boosting long-term immunity. The data do not compare boosting kinetics directly between mRNA and traditional vaccines in the same adult pig boosting schedule, so inferential leap to inferiority is not supported by the passage.

400.Option: The fact that some mice with VN titers above 0.5 IU/ml still died after challenge suggests that the 0.5 IU/ml threshold is invalid for all forms of rabies infection, including natural exposure.
Evidence: After two vaccinations, all RABV-G immunized mice developed relevant virus neutralizing (VN) titers of ≥0.5 IU/ml (range: 5.9 to 70.2 IU/ml), considered as protective in humans, dogs and cats [34].
All RABV-G mRNA and HDC vaccinated mice were protected against lethal rabies virus challenge (Fig 3A).

In the CD4-depleted, mRNA immunized group VN titers were strongly reduced: 3 out of 8 mice showed VN titers below 0.5 IU/ml and the mean titer of the groups was about 3 IU/ml, i.e. more than 15-fold reduced compared to the CD4-intact, mRNA-vaccinated group.

In the CD4-depleted, mRNA vaccinated group, all mice had to be sacrificed within 11 days after infection with similar kinetics as in the negative control group (Fig 5C) in line with the strongly reduced VN titers in this group.
400.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that mice with VN titers >0.5 IU/ml died after challenge under normal immunization conditions; in fact, mice with VN ≥0.5 IU/ml were reported as protected. The CD4-depletion experiment shows that when CD4+ T cells were depleted during immunization the group had reduced VN titers (mean ~3 IU/ml) and all died; however this does not demonstrate that individuals with titers above 0.5 IU/ml necessarily died in general, nor does it justify the broad statement that the 0.5 IU/ml threshold is invalid for all forms of rabies infection including natural exposure. The passage provides no data on natural exposure outcomes to support that broader claim.

400.Option: The observed reduction in TNFα mRNA expression in vaccinated mice indicates that the mRNA vaccine prevents rabies virus replication by suppressing neuroinflammation rather than by direct antiviral mechanisms.
Evidence: In mRNA and Rabipur vaccinated mice TNFα mRNA transcript level was reduced compared to mock vaccinated control group at both analysis time points and different brain sections (Fig 4B).

N-protein mRNA was not normalized to a cellular house-keeping gene ... In contrast, all but one of the analyzed brains of mice immunized with RABV-G mRNA or Rabipur were negative for N-protein mRNA at both time points, demonstrating a strong suppression of viral replication below the detection limit after vaccination (Fig 4A).
400.Rationale: While vaccinated mice showed both reduced TNFα mRNA levels and markedly lower viral N-protein RNA, the passage does not establish the causal direction that suppression of neuroinflammation (TNFα) is the mechanism by which viral replication is prevented. It is equally or more plausible from the data that effective vaccine-induced immune responses limited viral replication, and reduced viral replication then resulted in lower neuroinflammation. The passage does not present mechanistic evidence that suppression of TNFα is the primary protective mechanism rather than a downstream consequence of reduced viral replication; therefore the causal claim in the option is not supported.

400.Option: The mRNA vaccine's ability to induce strong CD4+ T cell responses suggests that cellular immunity is more critical than humoral immunity for protection against rabies virus in experimental models.
Evidence: In contrast, antigen-specific CD4+ T cells were significantly elevated in mRNA immunized mice compared to Rabipur treated animals with a high proportion of IFN-γ/TNFα-double positive CD4+ T cells (mean of 0.48% for IFN-γ/TNFα expressing CD4+ T cells after RABV-G mRNA and 0.091% after Rabipur vaccination) (Fig 2B, right panel).
In the CD4-depleted, mRNA immunized group VN titers were strongly reduced: 3 out of 8 mice showed VN titers below 0.5 IU/ml and the mean titer of the groups was about 3 IU/ml ...
In the CD4-depleted, mRNA vaccinated group, all mice had to be sacrificed within 11 days after infection ... in line with the strongly reduced VN titers in this group.
After two vaccinations, all RABV-G immunized mice developed relevant virus neutralizing (VN) titers of ≥0.5 IU/ml ... considered as protective in humans, dogs and cats [34].
400.Rationale: The passage demonstrates that the mRNA vaccine induces strong CD4+ T cell responses and that CD4+ T cells are required for generating robust virus-neutralizing (VN) antibody titers; CD4 depletion led to markedly reduced VN titers and loss of protection. These data indicate an important role of CD4+ T cells in enabling humoral immunity, and that humoral (VN antibody) responses are correlated with protection. They do not support the inference that cellular immunity alone is more critical than humoral immunity for protection. Rather, the evidence shows that CD4+ T cell help is necessary for protective humoral responses, so humoral immunity remains a critical effector of protection.

400.Option: The titer of 0.5 IU/ml, while considered a correlate of protection for natural rabies infection, may not reliably predict protection against intracerebral challenge in experimental settings.
Evidence: After two vaccinations, all RABV-G immunized mice developed relevant virus neutralizing (VN) titers of ≥0.5 IU/ml (range: 5.9 to 70.2 IU/ml), considered as protective in humans, dogs and cats [34].
Intracerebral infection poses an extremely harsh challenge that is artificial, but accepted by the scientific community as a stringent experimental test.

In the CD4-depleted, mRNA immunized group VN titers were strongly reduced: 3 out of 8 mice showed VN titers below 0.5 IU/ml and the mean titer of the groups was about 3 IU/ml, i.e. more than 15-fold reduced compared to the CD4-intact, mRNA-vaccinated group.

In the CD4-depleted, mRNA vaccinated group, all mice had to be sacrificed within 11 days after infection with similar kinetics as in the negative control group (Fig 5C) in line with the strongly reduced VN titers in this group.
400.Rationale: The passage explicitly notes that 0.5 IU/ml is considered a correlate of protection for natural infections. However, it also describes the harsh intracerebral (i.c.) challenge model and provides an example where vaccine-elicited immunity failed to protect in that experimental setting: after CD4+ T cell depletion during immunization, the group had a reduced antibody response (group mean ~3 IU/ml with 3/8 below 0.5 IU/ml) and all mice died following i.c. challenge. This demonstrates that serological thresholds established for natural exposure may not reliably predict protection in stringent artificial challenge models (i.c. challenge), particularly when other immunological factors (e.g., CD4+ T cell help) are compromised. Thus the cautious statement that the 0.5 IU/ml threshold may not reliably predict protection in experimental intracerebral challenge settings is supported by the data presented.

401.Option: The presence of N. mikurensis in bat-associated ticks that fed on domestic dogs, bats, and other wildlife suggests that these ticks may serve as bridge vectors in the transmission of this zoonotic pathogen between different host species.
Evidence: We detected Neoehrlichia mikurensis in both I. simplex (n = 1, EW16, Romania) associated with M. schreibersii and I. vespertilionis (n = 3, EW54, Romania; EW75, EW159, Hungary), previously fed on C. lupus familiaris, M. schreibersii, and M. myotis (Fig 5).

I. vespertilionis indicated the broadest spectrum of host feeding, including M. schreibersii (n = 13), M. bechsteinii (n = 1), M. myotis (n = 6), Rhinolophus euryale (n = 1), R. ferrumequinum (n = 18), R. hipposideros (n = 3), Rhinolophus sp. (n = 6), and non-bat hosts of C. lupus familiaris (n = 6), Equus caballus (n = 1), and Sus scrofa (n = 1)

these data suggest that bat-associated ticks may play a hitherto neglected link between bats and non-chiropteran hosts, implying their potential role in the zoonotic transmission of bat-derived vector-borne pathogens.
401.Rationale: The passage documents detection of N. mikurensis specifically in bat-associated ticks that had fed on multiple host species including domestic dogs and several bat species. It also explicitly suggests that bat-associated ticks can link bats and non-chiropteran hosts and could play a role in zoonotic transmission. From an epidemiological perspective, detection of a zoonotic pathogen in ticks that feed on both wildlife and domestic animals supports the inference that these ticks could function as bridge vectors facilitating pathogen movement between host groups.

401.Option: The detection of Neoehrlichia mikurensis in bat-associated ticks from both Romania and Hungary indicates that this pathogen is now widely distributed across Europe and may be emerging in new regions.
Evidence: We detected Neoehrlichia mikurensis in both I. simplex (n = 1, EW16, Romania) associated with M. schreibersii and I. vespertilionis (n = 3, EW54, Romania; EW75, EW159, Hungary), previously fed on C. lupus familiaris, M. schreibersii, and M. myotis (Fig 5).
Ticks were collected in caves and mines between 2016 and 2023, at nine locations in Hungary and 12 locations in Romania (S1 Table and S1 Fig).
401.Rationale: While the passage shows N. mikurensis was detected in bat-associated ticks from sampling sites in Romania and Hungary, these findings are limited to the surveyed locations and small sample numbers. The statement that the pathogen is 'now widely distributed across Europe and may be emerging in new regions' is a broader geographic and temporal inference that is not supported by the provided data alone. The passage does not present continental-scale surveillance or temporal trend data that would justify claiming wide distribution or emergence across Europe.

401.Option: The fact that N. mikurensis was detected in ticks associated with both bats and domestic dogs implies that the pathogen is being maintained in a stable reservoir cycle involving bats, dogs, and tick vectors.
Evidence: We detected Neoehrlichia mikurensis in both I. simplex (n = 1, EW16, Romania) associated with M. schreibersii and I. vespertilionis (n = 3, EW54, Romania; EW75, EW159, Hungary), previously fed on C. lupus familiaris, M. schreibersii, and M. myotis (Fig 5).
However, the frequency of such behavior is currently unknown, most importantly because the primary source of host preference data comes from field observation of ticks infesting bats [20,24].
401.Rationale: Detection of pathogen DNA in ticks that had fed on different hosts indicates exposure but does not demonstrate reservoir competence, sustained transmission, or a stable enzootic cycle among bats, dogs, and ticks. Establishing a stable reservoir cycle requires evidence of infection and transmission among host populations (e.g., pathogen presence in hosts, demonstration of infectiousness to ticks, and sustained transmission), which the passage does not provide. The authors also note limitations in host preference data and frequency of cross-host feeding, so maintenance in a stable reservoir cycle cannot be concluded from the presented molecular findings alone.

401.Option: The presence of N. mikurensis in bat-associated ticks that fed on multiple host species suggests that these ticks are capable of maintaining and transmitting the pathogen across all life stages, including from bats to humans.
Evidence: We detected Neoehrlichia mikurensis in both I. simplex (n = 1, EW16, Romania) associated with M. schreibersii and I. vespertilionis (n = 3, EW54, Romania; EW75, EW159, Hungary), previously fed on C. lupus familiaris, M. schreibersii, and M. myotis (Fig 5).
We did not use ticks that were directly collected from bats in this work, therefore this work did not involve handling of live bats.

The latter two ixodid species, as well as soft ticks (Ixodida: Argasidae) of bats, such as Chiropterargas boueti, Carios kelleyi, C. vespertilionis, Secretargas transgariepinus, and Reticulinasus salahi occasionally feed on humans [24–26]
401.Rationale: Although N. mikurensis DNA was found in ticks that had fed on different hosts, the passage does not present evidence of transstadial (across life stages) maintenance or demonstrated transmission to humans. The study did not collect ticks directly from bats or from humans, and occasional feeding on humans is noted but not linked to documented pathogen transmission. Therefore, asserting capability to maintain and transmit the pathogen across all life stages and from bats to humans goes beyond the data provided.

401.Option: Since dogs are susceptible to N. mikurensis and were found to be hosts of bat-associated ticks in this study, these ticks are likely to be a primary vector for the pathogen in domestic dog populations.
Evidence: We detected Neoehrlichia mikurensis in both I. simplex (n = 1, EW16, Romania) associated with M. schreibersii and I. vespertilionis (n = 3, EW54, Romania; EW75, EW159, Hungary), previously fed on C. lupus familiaris, M. schreibersii, and M. myotis (Fig 5).
I. vespertilionis indicated the broadest spectrum of host feeding, including ... C. lupus familiaris (n = 6) ...
However, the frequency of such behavior is currently unknown, most importantly because the primary source of host preference data comes from field observation of ticks infesting bats [20,24].
401.Rationale: The passage demonstrates that some bat-associated ticks had fed on dogs and that N. mikurensis DNA was detected in some ticks, but it does not provide evidence that these bat-associated ticks act as primary vectors for N. mikurensis in dog populations. Establishing a primary vector role would require data showing frequent tick–dog contact, efficient transmission to dogs, and epidemiologic evidence of dog infections attributable to these ticks. The authors explicitly note limitations and unknowns in host-feeding frequency and host spectrum, so claiming these bat-associated ticks are the primary vector for dogs is not supported by the presented data.

402.Option: The lack of strong pre-zygotic barriers between Schistosoma haematobium and S. bovis suggests that hybridization between these species may be common in areas where they co-occur and infect the same definitive hosts.
Evidence: We hypothesize that since these hybrids are frequently encountered in the field [37,37,39] and since parental species are able to pair in the laboratory [27] mate recognition should not constitute a strong barrier to reproduction.

Overall, when analysing all limited choice experiments together (i.e., Exp. 1 to 4) no significant difference was recorded between the number of observed homo- and hetero-specific pairs and those expected under a random mating scenario (χ2 = 21.71, d.f. = 16, P-value = 0.152, Table 2).

When all mating combinations were allowed between S. haematobium and S. bovis, ... Chi-square tests did not reveal significant departure from random mating hypothesis, when the number of each pairing combination was analysed in each hamster separately and also when analysing all replicate together (Table 3).

Schistosoma haematobium x S. bovis hybrids are today the most studied hybrid system of schistosomes. These hybrids were first identified in Niger by Brémont [36] and more recently in Senegal [26] but appeared widely distributed in West Africa [26,27,36–39].

Therefore, the lack of reproductive incompatibility (i.e., isolation by behaviour and physiology) between schistosome species infecting humans and animals may facilitate gene flow if the host isolation barriers are broken down.
