466.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that the single 500 mg mebendazole was among the least efficacious treatments in the model-based estimates (CR 14%). It also states that the model's 'true' cure rate estimates were lower than the observed cure rates. Therefore the claim that accounting for diagnostic error produced a higher cure rate for 500 mg mebendazole (i.e., that it is more effective than previously believed) contradicts the presented results and cannot be derived from the passage.

466.Option: The combination of albendazole, pyrantel pamoate, and oxantel pamoate is the most effective treatment for hookworm infection, with a cure rate of 66% and an egg reduction rate of 98%, which exceeds the efficacy of all other regimens tested.
Evidence: The treatments that achieved the highest ‘true’ CRs and ERRs against hookworm infections were mebendazole (6x100 mg) with a CR of 88% (95% BCI 79–95%) and an ERR of 99.6% (95% BCI 98–100%) and the triple combination treatment (albendazole 400 mg plus oxantel pamoate 20 mg/kg plus pyrantel pamoate 20 mg/kg) with a CR of 66% (95% BCI 59–73%) and an ERR of 98% (95% BCI 97–99%).
The estimated CRs ranged from 10% to 88% and the ERRs from −26% to 100% (Table 4).
466.Rationale: While the passage reports the triple combination (albendazole + oxantel + pyrantel) had a CR of 66% and ERR of 98%, it also reports that another regimen (mebendazole 6x100 mg) achieved a higher CR (88%) and higher ERR (99.6%). Thus the triple combination is highly effective but is not the single most effective regimen among those tested, so the statement that it 'is the most effective ... which exceeds the efficacy of all other regimens' is not supported by the passage.

466.Option: Despite considerably lower cure rates obtained by our modeling framework, our results confirm earlier findings that treatments which consist of a combination of drugs or multiple doses are more efficacious than a single-drug treatment.
Evidence: Fig 2 shows a comparison of the model estimates of the CRs and ERRs to the observed ones, where the ‘true’ estimates of CR were lower than the observed ones.

For instance, Palmeirim et al. (2018) found that six dosages of 100 mg mebendazole are more efficacious against hookworm infections than a single dose of 500 mg [8]. Moser et al. (2018) presented data from a trial conducted in Lao People’s Democratic Republic (Lao PDR) highlighting high efficacy of a combination of albendazole, pyrantel pamoate plus oxantel pamoate against hookworm infections [9].

The treatments that achieved the highest ‘true’ CRs and ERRs against hookworm infections were mebendazole (6x100 mg) with a CR of 88% (95% BCI 79–95%) and an ERR of 99.6% (95% BCI 98–100%) and the triple combination treatment (albendazole 400 mg plus oxantel pamoate 20 mg/kg plus pyrantel pamoate 20 mg/kg) with a CR of 66% (95% BCI 59–73%) and an ERR of 98% (95% BCI 97–99%).

The two least efficacious treatments were mebendazole 500 mg and oxantel pamoate (20 mg/kg) with CRs of 14% (95% BCI 11–18%) and 10% (95% BCI 7–15%) and ERRs of 21% (95% BCI -5–41%) and -26% (95% BCI -8–16%), respectively.
466.Rationale: The passage states that the model produced lower 'true' cure rates than the observed ones. It also cites prior studies showing multi-dose regimens and combinations are more efficacious than single-dose/single-drug regimens, and the model-based results are consistent with that pattern: the highest 'true' CR and ERR were observed for a multiple-dose mebendazole regimen (6x100 mg) and a triple-drug combination, while single-dose mebendazole (500 mg) had among the lowest efficacy. Therefore the inference that, despite lower modeled CRs overall, combination regimens or multiple-dose regimens outperform single-drug single-dose treatments is supported by the passage.

467.Option: The SINE compounds reduce viral titers of New World alphaviruses by interfering with viral assembly and/or budding, likely through sequestering capsid in the nucleus and disrupting capsid/glycoprotein interaction at the plasma membrane.
Evidence: The three active compounds, KPT-185, KPT-335, and KPT-350, confined capsid to the nucleus (Fig 1A and S3A Fig), yielding a localization pattern similar to that seen with treatment by the well-documented CRM1 inhibitor, Leptomycin B [18].
Extracellular viral RNA was reduced upon treatment with Leptomycin B and KPT-185 at 4 and 8 hpi relative to DMSO controls (Fig 3A), with a greater relative reduction at 8 hpi.

In contrast, at 4 hpi, intracellular RNA levels were approximately equal regardless of treatment, with a decrease seen by 8 hpi in both Leptomycin B and KPT-185 treated cells relative to DMSO controls (Fig 3B).
Capsid was present in the supernatants of DMSO and KPT-301 controls, but not in Leptomycin B or KPT-185 samples.

SINE compounds were not inhibiting viral protein synthesis. These data combined with the altered capsid localization and the observed decrease in viral RNA in extracellular supernatants, indicate that SINE compounds can sequester capsid, removing it from the pool of available structural proteins, leading to decrease in virus assembly and/or release of mature virions.
467.Rationale: Multiple direct observations support that SINE compounds sequester capsid in the nucleus and reduce extracellular (virion-associated) RNA and released capsid without blocking protein synthesis. The authors explicitly conclude these data "indicate that SINE compounds can sequester capsid, removing it from the pool of available structural proteins, leading to decrease in virus assembly and/or release of mature virions." This mechanism — interference with late steps (assembly/budding) due to nuclear sequestration of capsid — is the most strongly supported by the experimental findings. The specific phrase about disrupting capsid/glycoprotein interactions at the plasma membrane is an inference consistent with removal of capsid from the cytoplasmic pool required for virion assembly and release; the core, directly supported mechanism is sequestration of capsid in the nucleus → reduced assembly/release.

467.Option: The SINE compounds reduce viral titers by blocking the nuclear import of viral capsid, thereby preventing its interaction with host transcription factors.
Evidence: The three active compounds, KPT-185, KPT-335, and KPT-350, confined capsid to the nucleus (Fig 1A and S3A Fig), yielding a localization pattern similar to that seen with treatment by the well-documented CRM1 inhibitor, Leptomycin B [18].

As a control, TC83 virus with a mutated capsid that is defective in nuclear import (TC83_Cm; [38]) was analyzed. TC83_Cm infected cells treated with DMSO, Leptomycin B, or KPT-185 all displayed capsid cytoplasmic staining (Fig 1A).
By forming a tetrameric complex with the host transport proteins CRM1 and importin α/β, VEEV capsid protein disrupts functioning of the nuclear pore complex [17].
siRNA mediated knockdown of CRM1 resulted in confinement of VEEV capsid to the nucleus, whereas loss of importin α and/or importin β resulted in VEEV capsid being primarily cytoplasmic [18].
467.Rationale: The passage shows the active SINE compounds confine capsid to the nucleus (i.e., they inhibit nuclear export), not block nuclear import. The control TC83_Cm virus defective in nuclear import is cytoplasmic, demonstrating that blocking import yields cytoplasmic localization, the opposite of the SINE effect. Thus the data do not support a mechanism of blocking nuclear import; rather they support blocking export (nuclear retention). The additional claim about preventing interaction with host transcription factors is not demonstrated as the causal basis for reduced titers in the experiments.

467.Option: The SINE compounds reduce viral titers by enhancing the host innate immune response through upregulation of interferon-stimulated genes.
Evidence: All four transcripts were strongly induced following TC83 infection (Fig 7A). Cells treated with KPT-185 also displayed increased levels of all four transcripts, with only IFNβ being significantly reduced compared to DMSO treated cells.
These results indicate that ISGs are capable of being induced in the presence of SINE compounds.
This indicates that SINE compounds likely disrupt a late step in the virus lifecycle such as viral assembly or budding.

SINE compounds were not inhibiting viral protein synthesis. These data combined with the altered capsid localization and the observed decrease in viral RNA in extracellular supernatants, indicate that SINE compounds can sequester capsid, removing it from the pool of available structural proteins, leading to decrease in virus assembly and/or release of mature virions.
467.Rationale: While the passage shows that interferon-stimulated genes (ISGs) can be induced in infected cells treated with KPT-185, the authors explicitly interpret multiple independent lines of evidence (nuclear sequestration of capsid, reduced extracellular RNA, loss of capsid in supernatants, lack of inhibition of protein synthesis) as indicating disruption of a late step (assembly/release). The passage does not present direct evidence that upregulation of ISGs is the primary causal mechanism for reduced viral titers. Therefore, although innate response modulation is observed, it is not the mechanism most strongly supported by the data for the reduction in viral output.

467.Option: The SINE compounds reduce viral titers by directly inhibiting the activity of the viral nonstructural proteins involved in RNA replication.
Evidence: Extracellular viral RNA was reduced upon treatment with Leptomycin B and KPT-185 at 4 and 8 hpi relative to DMSO controls (Fig 3A), with a greater relative reduction at 8 hpi.

In contrast, at 4 hpi, intracellular RNA levels were approximately equal regardless of treatment, with a decrease seen by 8 hpi in both Leptomycin B and KPT-185 treated cells relative to DMSO controls (Fig 3B).
This indicates that SINE compounds likely disrupt a late step in the virus lifecycle such as viral assembly or budding.
SINE compounds were not inhibiting viral protein synthesis.
467.Rationale: The experimental pattern (no early intracellular RNA decrease at 4 hpi, reduction in extracellular RNA and released capsid, and the authors' interpretation that SINEs disrupt a late step like assembly/budding) argues against direct inhibition of nonstructural proteins/replication as the primary mechanism. The data and authors' statements support disruption of assembly/release rather than inhibition of RNA replication machinery.

467.Option: The SINE compounds reduce viral titers by inducing apoptosis in infected cells, leading to premature cell death and reduced viral production.
Evidence: Four SINE compounds (S1A Fig) were first screened for toxicity in two separate cell lines, treated for 24 hours, and determined to be non-toxic at 2.5 μM (S2 Fig, [39, 40]).
The CC50 for all four compounds was greater than 10 μM and therefore 10 μM was used to calculate the selective index (SI) for each compound.
inhibitor treatment also increased cellular viability in infected cells compared to infected cells treated with the vehicle only [18].
467.Rationale: The passage provides explicit toxicity data showing the SINE compounds are non-toxic at active concentrations (2.5 μM) and have CC50 values >10 μM. Additionally, previous inhibitor treatment increased cellular viability in infected cells. There is no experimental evidence of apoptosis induction leading to reduced viral production; on the contrary, the compounds appear not to cause premature cell death at the concentrations used. Thus apoptosis-induced reduction of virus is not supported.

468.Option: The presence of mGMCSF fusion increases the immune response to the LdPxn1 antigen primarily due to its role as a secretory module that enhances antigen delivery to professional antigen-presenting cells.
Evidence: GMCSF possesses a 17 amino acid leader sequence that mediates secretion of the protein [22].
GMCSF also functions as immunomodulator. GMCSF plays important role in activation, maturation, and function of dendritic cells.

Western blotting on culture supernatant and lysate of cells transfected with LdPxn1 plasmid DNA confirmed that the vaccine antigens are expressed in mammalian system. Moreover, GMCSF facilitates the secretion of the vaccine antigen.
Although not statistically significant, the presence of mGMCSF fusion appreciably increased the total IgG response as compared to immunization with the antigen in the absence of GMCSF.

The result clearly shows that immunization with two doses of pcDNA-mGMCSF-LdPxn1 DNA followed by a single booster immunization with rLdPxn1 protein induced significantly higher CD4+ T cell response with high level expression of IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-2 (Fig. 4).
468.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that mGMCSF both (a) contains a leader sequence mediating secretion and (b) functions as an immunomodulatory adjuvant that activates/matures dendritic cells and increases immune responses. It also shows experimentally that GMCSF facilitates secretion and that mGMCSF-containing constructs elicited stronger responses. However, the claim that the increase is 'primarily due to its role as a secretory module that enhances antigen delivery to professional antigen-presenting cells' asserts a single dominant mechanism. The Passage does not establish that secretion-mediated antigen delivery is the primary mechanism rather than GMCSF's immunomodulatory/adjuvant effects (activation/recruitment of APCs). Therefore the stronger causal claim about the primary mechanism is not supported by the provided text.

468.Option: The protective efficacy of the LdPxn1 vaccine is primarily determined by the magnitude of the humoral immune response, as evidenced by high IgG2a titers and sustained antibody levels after infection.
Evidence: At Week-6 post-immunization, mice that were immunized with plasmids expressing peroxidoxin-1 DNA in the presence or absence of mGMCSF fusion induced significantly higher antigen-specific total IgG response than the respective controls, pcDNA or pcDNA-mGMCSF (p<0.05).

LdPxn1-specific antibody response remained high after infection. Mice that received the plasmids expressing LdPxn1 antigen showed high IgG, IgG1 and IgG2a response before infection as well as three and eight weeks after infection. These mice produced a remarkably higher IgG2a response than control mice (Fig. 5C).

The result clearly shows that immunization with two doses of pcDNA-mGMCSF-LdPxn1 DNA followed by a single booster immunization with rLdPxn1 protein induced significantly higher CD4+ T cell response with high level expression of IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-2 (Fig. 4).

In order to evaluate the protective efficacy of the vaccine antigens, footpad swelling was measured... mice that were immunized with vaccine antigens generally developed lower footpad lesion over the entire period of the experiment. Mice primed with the plasmid expressing LdPxn1 in the presence of mGMCSF fusion DNA adjuvant followed by a booster immunization with rLdPxn1 showed the least footpad swelling (Fig.8).
468.Rationale: While the Passage documents sustained antibody responses including IgG2a after infection, it also documents strong antigen-specific CD4+ T cell responses (high IFN-γ, TNF-α, IL-2 and multifunctional Th1 cells) in the immunized mice and links those cellular responses to protection. The Passage does not present evidence that humoral magnitude (antibody titers) is the primary determinant of protection; rather the data emphasize cellular Th1 responses associated with reduced lesion size. Thus the claim that protective efficacy is primarily determined by humoral magnitude is not supported.

468.Option: The induction of high levels of IL-10 by antigen-stimulated CD4+ T cells is a key factor in the protective efficacy of the LdPxn1 vaccine, as it enhances macrophage activation and parasite clearance.
Evidence: spleen cells isolated from mice immunized with pcDNA-mGMCSF-LdPxn1 produced significantly more IL-10 than those from control mice (P<0.05).

The IFN-γ to IL-10 ratio was 7.2 and 29.6 for the mice immunized with pcDNA-LdPxn1 and pcDNA-mGMCSF-Pxn1, respectively. On the other hand, injection of pcDNA and pcDNA-mGMCSF controls resulted in a ratio of only 2.2 and 2.7, respectively.
Unlike, pre-challenge response, no difference was seen between immunized and control mice regarding IL-10 production.
The result clearly shows that immunization ... induced significantly higher CD4+ T cell response with high level expression of IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-2 (Fig. 4).
468.Rationale: The Passage reports some increase in IL-10 in immunized mice pre-challenge but also emphasizes a high IFN-γ to IL-10 ratio and strong Th1 cytokine responses (IFN-γ, TNF-α, IL-2) associated with protection. There is no statement that IL-10 enhances macrophage activation or that IL-10 is the key protective factor; indeed IL-10 is typically regulatory and can inhibit macrophage microbicidal functions. Furthermore, post-challenge IL-10 was not different between groups. Therefore the specific claim that high IL-10 induction is a key protective mechanism enhancing macrophage activation is not supported by the Passage.

468.Option: The partial protection observed in mice immunized with LdPxn1 is due to the antigen's ability to induce a balanced Th1/Th2 immune response, as indicated by the presence of both IgG1 and IgG2a antibodies.
Evidence: Like total IgG response, mice primed with plasmids expressing the vaccine antigens developed a significantly higher IgG1 response than the control mice that received pcDNA or pcDNA-mGMCSF (p<0.05).
At week-9, the mice that were immunized with pcDNA-LdPxn1 or pcDNA-mGMCSF-LdPxn1 showed significantly higher IgG2a response than the respective controls (p<0.05) (Fig. 1C).

LdPxn1-specific antibody response remained high after infection. Mice that received the plasmids expressing LdPxn1 antigen showed high IgG, IgG1 and IgG2a response before infection as well as three and eight weeks after infection.
The result clearly shows that immunization ... induced significantly higher CD4+ T cell response with high level expression of IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-2 (Fig. 4).

In order to evaluate the protective efficacy of the vaccine antigens... mice that were immunized with vaccine antigens generally developed lower footpad lesion... Mice primed with the plasmid expressing LdPxn1 in the presence of mGMCSF fusion DNA adjuvant followed by a booster immunization with rLdPxn1 showed the least footpad swelling (Fig.8).
468.Rationale: The Passage documents induction of both IgG1 and IgG2a isotypes, but it emphasizes strong Th1-type cellular responses (IFN-γ, TNF-α, IL-2, multifunctional CD4+ cells) in the immunized mice and links these Th1 responses to protection. There is no direct evidence that a balanced Th1/Th2 profile is the cause of the observed partial protection. On the contrary, data point toward Th1-biased cellular immunity as an important correlate of protection. Consequently, attributing protection to a balanced Th1/Th2 response is not supported by the provided text.

468.Option: The level of protection conferred by the LdPxn1 antigen is strongly correlated with the induction of a high level expression of IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-2 cytokines by antigen-specific CD4+ T cells.
Evidence: In this study, we have demonstrated that immunization of mice with LdPxn1 antigen in the presence of fusion mGMCSF adjuvant in DNA-protein prime-boost strategy induces a strong immune response with significant increase in the frequency of CD4+ T cells that express IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-2. More importantly, the antigen induced a significantly higher proportion of multipotent CD4+ T cells that simultaneously express all the three Th-1 cytokines and resulted in a significantly increased protection of mice from L. major challenge infection.

The result clearly shows that immunization with two doses of pcDNA-mGMCSF-LdPxn1 DNA followed by a single booster immunization with rLdPxn1 protein induced significantly higher CD4+ T cell response with high level expression of IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-2 (Fig. 4).

In order to evaluate the protective efficacy of the vaccine antigens, footpad swelling was measured every week for eight weeks after challenge infection with Leishmania major... Mice primed with the plasmid expressing LdPxn1 in the presence of mGMCSF fusion DNA adjuvant followed by a booster immunization with rLdPxn1 showed the least footpad swelling (Fig.8).

At Week-6 post challenge, mice immunized with plasmid expressing the antigen with or without mGMCSF adjuvant developed significantly smaller lesion than that of the respective control groups (p<0.05).
468.Rationale: The Passage explicitly links the vaccine-induced increase in multifunctional Th1 CD4+ cells (co-expressing IFN-γ, TNF-α, IL-2) with significantly increased protection against L. major challenge (reduced footpad swelling and smaller lesions). Multiple statements directly connect high-level expression of these Th1 cytokines in antigen-specific CD4+ T cells to improved protective efficacy, supporting the inference that protection correlates strongly with induction of IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-2 by CD4+ T cells.

469.Option: The trained dog's ability to detect nymphs of T. infestans without prior exposure suggests that the odor recognition stimulus is specific to the species and not shared across Triatominae, as the dog also detected T. sordida and T. guasayana.
Evidence: Training was carried out in the trainer's home using the method outlined by the United States Customs Service [43]. First the living triatomines were presented to the dog to stimulate the dog's olfactory memory before being hidden somewhere in a house, and the dog was told to “search”.
The dog consistently marked the location of the nymphs, but did not find the fresh or dry faeces on any occassion.
A total of 70 triatomines was collected during 5 field trips with NERO.
The remaining bugs comprised 18 specimens of Triatoma guasayana, and 30 specimens of Triatoma sordida (Table 2).
469.Rationale: The passage shows the dog was trained using live triatomines (T. infestans) and in experiments the dog consistently marked nymphs. The field captures included multiple triatomine species (T. sordida and T. guasayana) collected during searches with the dog. However, the passage does not provide evidence that the dog's odor recognition is species-specific or that the odor is not shared across Triatominae. It does not test cross-reactivity explicitly nor conclude that detection of other species reflects species-specific cues; detection of multiple species could indicate shared odorants or general triatomine recognition. Therefore the specific inference (odor recognition stimulus is species-specific and not shared across Triatominae) cannot be derived from the Passage Body.

469.Option: The failure of light traps and manual checks to detect sylvatic T. infestans indicates that these conventional methods are ineffective for any triatomine species, regardless of habitat or life stage.
Evidence: the search for this species using light traps and manual checking of fallen trees and burrows had been unsuccessful.
We report here a novel approach using a trained dog, which has revealed several sylvatic populations of T. infestans in the Paraguayan Chaco.
469.Rationale: The Passage Body documents that previous searches specifically for T. infestans using light traps and manual checking were unsuccessful in this region, and that the trained dog approach revealed sylvatic T. infestans where those methods had failed. However, the passage does not claim that conventional methods are ineffective for all triatomine species, in all habitats, or across all life stages. It provides evidence of failure for particular methods against T. infestans in this context, but generalizing to 'any triatomine species, regardless of habitat or life stage' is not supported by the text.

469.Option: The use of trained dogs to detect triatomines reduces sample collection bias and decreases the time spent searching for sylvatic specimens, which are present in very low densities and dispersed over wide areas.
Evidence: search for this species using light traps and manual checking of fallen trees and burrows had been unsuccessful.
We report here a novel approach using a trained dog, which has revealed several sylvatic populations of T. infestans in the Paraguayan Chaco.
This has enabled us to find sylvatic T. infestans in the region of the Paraguayan Chaco through a quick, easy and low-cost procedure.
The dog consistently marked the location of the nymphs, but did not find the fresh or dry faeces on any occassion.
A total of 70 triatomines was collected during 5 field trips with NERO.
sylvatic populations of this vector have only occasionally been reported in Paraguay [9] although nymphs of T. infestans were recently reported amongst vegetation near indigenous dwellings [10].
469.Rationale: The Passage Body contrasts previous unsuccessful conventional searches with the successful use of a trained dog that revealed sylvatic populations. It explicitly states the dog-based method is 'quick, easy and low-cost' and reports collection of 70 triatomines in five field trips, indicating greater efficiency in finding dispersed sylvatic bugs. The dog also consistently marked nymphs in trials, suggesting it can detect life stages (nymphs) that might be missed by other methods, which implies a reduction in sample collection bias toward easier-to-detect stages or habitats. The passage also notes sylvatic populations had 'only occasionally been reported', supporting that these specimens are hard to find (low detectability/densities and dispersed). Together, these statements support the inference that trained dogs can decrease search time and help reduce biases in sampling sparse, dispersed sylvatic triatomine populations.

470.Option: The detection of a single positive case in a child from a previously treated area suggests that the mass drug administration program in Recife was ineffective in interrupting transmission.
Evidence: Only one child in the municipality of Camaragibe was positive for the presence of circulating filarial antigen (Table 2).
This child was a six-year-old girl from the Nova Descoberta district of the municipality of Recife (a post-MDA area).
She was born in Recife and had moved to Camaragibe with her family in 2014.
She was amicrofilaremic, and ultrasound examination did not reveal any adult worm nests in the cervical, axillary, or inguinal lymphatic chains.
Investigations of parasite, antigens, and adult worms in other members of her family (parents and three siblings) were negative.
Recife and Olinda completed three TASs with two-year intervals in 2018, while Jaboatão dos Guararapes will complete theirs in 2020 [1,8].
In all studies, all microfilaremia cases were treated with DEC (6 mg/kg/12 days).
470.Rationale: The passage reports a single antigen-positive child who was amicrofilaremic, had negative ultrasound, and whose family members tested negative; parents had participated in three rounds of MDA. A solitary antigen-positive individual (especially one who migrated from a post-MDA area) is insufficient evidence to conclude that the MDA program in Recife was ineffective at interrupting transmission. The passage also documents completion of TASs and treatment of microfilaremia cases, which do not support a conclusion of program failure. Therefore the claim that MDA was ineffective cannot be derived from the presented data.

470.Option: The lack of physical and administrative barriers between municipalities means that lymphatic filariasis can be eradicated across the entire Metropolitan Region of Recife through coordinated mass drug administration campaigns.
Evidence: Nine other municipalities (Abreu e Lima, Cabo de Santo Agostinho, Camaragibe, Igarassu, Ilha de Itamaracá, Ipojuca, Itapissuma, Moreno, and São Lourenço da Mata) are part of the Metropolitan Region of Recife and are areas that adjoin the endemic sites [10].
Evidence in these municipalities is limited to historical data based on case reports [6,11–17]; thus, the epidemiology of LF in these areas is uncertain [18].

The objective of the Global Program to Eliminate Lymphatic Filariasis (GPELF) is to phase out this endemic disease as a public health problem by 2020. The GPELF is based on two lines of action: reduction of the prevalence of infection and management of morbidity to prevent incapacity [3–5].
470.Rationale: While the passage states that municipalities adjoin endemic sites and that epidemiology in adjacent municipalities is uncertain, it does not state anything about physical or administrative barriers, nor does it provide evidence that eradication across the entire Metropolitan Region is achievable solely by coordinated MDA. The passage does not evaluate feasibility, logistics, or the presence/absence of barriers, so the strong conclusion that eradication 'can be' achieved across the region through coordinated MDA is not supported by the provided text.

470.Option: The historical presence of microfilaremia and vector infection in the nine municipalities indicates that mass drug administration should be reinstated in these areas to prevent further spread of lymphatic filariasis.
Evidence: The review of the literature identified eight epidemiological surveys performed during the study period.
In the remaining eight municipalities, there were cases of microfilaremia, corresponding for the prevalence of <1% [11–15].
The vector infection rate was 1.1% in a single survey conducted in the municipality of São Lourenço da Mata in 1967 [12].
In 2015, an investigation of antigens conducted in the municipality of Ipojuca did not identify any cases of LF [16].
Only one child in the municipality of Camaragibe was positive for the presence of circulating filarial antigen (Table 2).
470.Rationale: Although historical surveys showed low-prevalence microfilaremia and a single historical vector infection report, the contemporary school-based survey found only one antigen-positive child among 17,222 tested. The passage does not present direct evidence that current transmission is ongoing in these nine municipalities, nor does it provide an analysis concluding that reinstating MDA is necessary. Deciding to reinstate MDA requires additional evidence about current transmission dynamics, risk thresholds, and programmatic considerations that are not provided here. Therefore the specific policy implication that MDA 'should be reinstated' cannot be derived solely from the passage.

470.Option: The use of immunochromatographic card tests (ICTs) in schoolchildren is a reliable method for confirming the elimination of lymphatic filariasis in areas with uncertain endemicity, as demonstrated by the absence of positive cases in the nine municipalities studied.
Evidence: Immunochromatographic card tests (ICTs) were used to test for circulating filarial antigen (BinaxNOW Filariasis, Alere Scarborough, Orlando, United States). For these tests, 100 μL of capillary blood was collected.
In 2015, an investigation of antigens conducted in the municipality of Ipojuca did not identify any cases of LF [16].
Only one child in the municipality of Camaragibe was positive for the presence of circulating filarial antigen (Table 2).
470.Rationale: The passage describes use of ICTs for antigen detection in schoolchildren and reports survey results, but it does not claim ICTs alone are a definitive or 'reliable' method to confirm elimination. Moreover, the option incorrectly states 'absence of positive cases in the nine municipalities' whereas the study detected one positive child. Confirmation of elimination typically requires a combination of surveillance approaches and programmatic criteria (e.g., TAS results, molecular xenomonitoring), which the passage references elsewhere. Therefore the strong claim about ICTs being sufficient to confirm elimination is not supported by the provided text.

470.Option: Even in areas with low endemicity that have stopped mass drug administration, ongoing surveillance is necessary to prevent the risk of disease recrudescence due to migration and the presence of vector breeding sites.
Evidence: Nine other municipalities ... are part of the Metropolitan Region of Recife and are areas that adjoin the endemic sites [10].
Evidence in these municipalities is limited to historical data based on case reports [6,11–17]; thus, the epidemiology of LF in these areas is uncertain [18].
In the remaining eight municipalities, there were cases of microfilaremia, corresponding for the prevalence of <1% [11–15].
The vector infection rate was 1.1% in a single survey conducted in the municipality of São Lourenço da Mata in 1967 [12].
Only one child in the municipality of Camaragibe was positive for the presence of circulating filarial antigen (Table 2).
This child was a six-year-old girl from the Nova Descoberta district of the municipality of Recife (a post-MDA area). The child was born in Recife and had moved to Camaragibe with her family in 2014.

Consequently, surveillance and verification of the interruption of transmission started with molecular xenomonitoring (identification of parasite DNA in vector mosquitoes) and thick drop tests in two endemic municipalities of the Metropolitan region of Recife after three TASs were stopped.
470.Rationale: The passage documents low but historical microfilaremia and prior vector infection, uncertainty in epidemiology in adjacent municipalities, and a single antigen-positive child who migrated from a post-MDA area—illustrating how movement can introduce antigen-positive individuals into areas that have ceased MDA. The authors also note ongoing surveillance activities (molecular xenomonitoring and thick drop tests) after stopping TASs. Combining these facts supports the public health implication that continued surveillance is warranted in low-endemicity, post-MDA areas to detect and prevent recrudescence due to human movement and vector presence.

471.Option: Despite high levels of knowledge about schistosomiasis transmission, schoolchildren in Mbita Sub-county continue to engage in high-risk behaviors due to the lack of safe water sources and sanitation infrastructure, indicating a critical gap between knowledge and practice.
Evidence: Nearly 60–70% of pupils in 2015 and 2016 correctly answered “contact with contaminated water” as a transmission mode.
More than 70%, 80%, and 70% of pupils bathed, fetched water and washed clothes/utensils, respectively at Lake Victoria every day or sometimes in both years.

In 2016, 3.3% of households had tap water and according to the Health and Demographic Surveillance System (HDSS) initiated by the NUITM-KEMRI project (Institute of Tropical Medicine, Nagasaki University, Japan and Kenya Medical Research Institute, Kenya), there were nine functional boreholes available in the area.
Of the 93 schools, 71 (76%) depended on Lake Victoria as a water source for daily school life
61 of 93 (66%) schools asked pupils to fetch/bring water for use in schools and of those, 53 schools had Lake Victoria as the nearest water source (Fig 3).
471.Rationale: The passage shows that a majority of pupils correctly identify contact with contaminated water as the transmission mode (evidence of knowledge), yet very large proportions still bathe, fetch water, and wash in Lake Victoria (evidence of ongoing high-risk behaviors). The data also document very limited availability of safe piped water and few boreholes, and that most schools depend on lake water and ask pupils to fetch it. Together these facts support an epidemiological inference that there is a gap between knowledge and practice driven by lack of safe water/sanitation infrastructure, rather than by absence of awareness.

471.Option: The high rate of toilet use at school compared to at home suggests that community-level sanitation infrastructure is more developed than household-level infrastructure in Mbita Sub-county.
Evidence: about 85% of the pupils reported that they regularly use toilet while in school, but the figure statistically decreased to about 67% while at home in both years (p = 0.004 in 2015, p < 0.001 in 2016 by χ2 test).
471.Rationale: The passage documents higher reported toilet use at school (≈85%) than at home (≈67%), but it does not provide direct data on community-level sanitation infrastructure outside schools (e.g., community latrines, sewer systems) or on household infrastructure coverage beyond this behavioral report. Inferring that community-level sanitation infrastructure is more developed than household-level infrastructure goes beyond the evidence: the observed difference could reflect availability of school toilets specifically, school policies enforcing toilet use, or behavioral differences rather than broader community infrastructure development. Therefore the structural inference is not supported by the provided text.

471.Option: The contradiction between school water-fetching policies and health education messages indicates that school administrators are unaware of the risks of schistosomiasis transmission through lake water.
Evidence: 61 of 93 (66%) schools asked pupils to fetch/bring water for use in schools and of those, 53 schools had Lake Victoria as the nearest water source (Fig 3).

Both textbooks covered cause, transmission, and prevention. However, one of the textbooks wrongly described that drinking contaminated water is a mode of transmission, and the other missed the symptoms of intestinal schistosomiasis (S1 Table).
Pupils cited schoolteachers (more than 90%) or hospital/health centre (about 80%) as their main sources of information.
471.Rationale: The passage shows that many schools require pupils to fetch lake water and that textbooks include transmission content (with some inaccuracies). However, it does not provide any direct evidence about school administrators' knowledge or awareness. Administrators may be aware of transmission risks but lack alternative water sources or infrastructure, or other operational constraints may drive the water-fetching policy. The claim that administrators are unaware is a specific attribution of intent/knowledge that cannot be derived from the presented data.

471.Option: The use of school-based health education through science textbooks has been ineffective in improving students' knowledge about schistosomiasis, as evidenced by the high prevalence of misconceptions about transmission and symptoms.
Evidence: Both textbooks covered cause, transmission, and prevention. However, one of the textbooks wrongly described that drinking contaminated water is a mode of transmission, and the other missed the symptoms of intestinal schistosomiasis (S1 Table).
More than half of the pupils wrongly chose “blood in urine” as a main sign of dominant schistosomiasis in Mbita (data is available only for 2016).
Nearly 60–70% of pupils in 2015 and 2016 correctly answered “contact with contaminated water” as a transmission mode.
Pupils cited schoolteachers (more than 90%) or hospital/health centre (about 80%) as their main sources of information.
471.Rationale: The passage indicates that school-based education (science textbooks and teachers) is the main information source for pupils, but textbooks contain inaccuracies and omissions. Although a majority identify contact with contaminated water correctly, substantial misconceptions remain (e.g., many pupils wrongly identified blood in urine as the main sign locally). Given that school materials and instruction are primary sources, the persistence of these misconceptions supports the inference that current school-based health education as implemented (textbooks/content) is not fully effective at improving accurate knowledge among students.

472.Option: The presence of a frame-shift mutation in ML0825c, encoding a zinc-sensing transcriptional regulator, suggests that the recurrent strain in patient 2188 had evolved increased virulence due to functional impairment of the SmtB protein.
Evidence: Analysis of 2188–2007 and 2188–2014 revealed identical genome sequences (Fig 1). Curiously, both strains belong to a new SNP subtype intermediate between subtypes 4N and 4O.

An insertion of 9 nucleotides (GGACATCTA at position 1,219,061) was found in ML1052, a putative PucR-like transcriptional regulator, which leads to a modification of the protein. Interestingly this mutation was present at only 30% frequency in 2188–2007, while it was fixed in 2188–2014.

Another frame-shift arising from a dinucleotide insertion was found in ML0825c, the ortholog of rv2358 in M. tuberculosis that codes for the protein SmtB, a zinc-sensing transcriptional regulator and member of the AsrR/SmtB family.[23, 24].

The C-terminal part of SmtB is essential for the protein dimerization, zinc binding and DNA recognition. Furthermore, a specific histidine residue (H138 in ML0825c and H117 in Synechococcus StmB) is important for the allosteric coupling of the zinc and DNA binding sites in the protein.[25] Modeling of M. leprae StmB in silico (S2 Fig) showed that the frame-shift leads to loss of H138 and should thus impair protein function.
472.Rationale: The passage documents a frame-shift in ML0825c in the 2188 strains and states that modeling indicates loss of an important residue and likely impairment of SmtB function. However, the claim that this impairment implies 'increased virulence' is not supported by the passage. The text does not report any clinical evidence linking this mutation to increased virulence, nor does it state that loss of SmtB function would increase virulence — only that the mutation should impair protein function. Inferring increased virulence from a predicted loss-of-function is not justified by the provided data.

472.Option: The extended observation period allowed identification of relapses/reinfection, and whole-genome sequencing enabled differentiation between relapse and reinfection in a field setting with high resolution.
Evidence: During follow-up, four of the treated patients presented with new symptoms between four and eight years after completion of U-MDT and three of these were re-investigated in this study.

In this study, we investigated three recurrent cases of leprosy from the U-MDT/CT-BR trial to determine whether recurrence was due to drug resistance, bacterial persistence or to reinfection. To achieve this, we compared whole genome sequencing analysis of M. leprae collected from skin lesions at the initial diagnosis and during the recurrence of the disease and correlated the sequence data with the clinical, microbiologic and serologic findings.
Sufficient whole genome read coverage was obtained from the six M. leprae samples for genotyping and comparative genomic analyses (S4 Table).
The recurrent strain 1126–2011 was clearly distinct from the primary strain 1126–2007, and differed in 44 SNPs, 4 InDels and 6 VNTR loci (Fig 1 and S5 Table).

Strains 3208–2007 and 3208–2015 differed in only two SNPs and one VNTR locus (Fig 1 and S5 Table). Both SNPs ... were present in 3208–2015, indicating that 3208–2015 was certainly the direct progeny of 3208–2007.
472.Rationale: The passage shows that patients were followed for multiple years (4–8 years post-treatment) allowing the detection of new episodes. The study explicitly used whole-genome sequencing on paired samples from initial and recurrent episodes and obtained sufficient coverage. The genomic comparisons provided clear evidence distinguishing distinct strains (reinfection) from direct progeny with minimal changes (relapse/persistence), demonstrating that extended temporal follow-up combined with whole-genome sequencing enabled high-resolution differentiation between relapse and reinfection in this field observational setting.

472.Option: The high antibody titers to PGL-1 and LID-1 antigens during and after treatment suggest that patients remained immunologically responsive to M. leprae, which may have contributed to the recurrence of leprosy.
Evidence: In addition, the first diagnosis revealed that the three MB patients showed high IgM and IgG antibody levels to PGL-1 and LID-1 antigens, respectively (S1 Fig).
Overall, the antibody titers gradually declined but remained above the threshold for positivity for at least one of the antigens during the study period except for patient 1126.

This patient showed an antibody titer below the threshold just before the recurrence of the disease (39 months after U-MDT) and then both antibody titers increased by the time of recurrent disease. By contrast, despite oscillating levels of PGL-1 antibody for 3208, antibody titers, especially to LID-1, remained high for 3208 and 2188 during the entire study period.
472.Rationale: The passage demonstrates that antibody titers to PGL-1 and LID-1 were high and often remained positive during follow-up, indicating persistent serologic responses. However, the statement asserts that this immune responsiveness 'may have contributed to the recurrence' — a causal link not supported by the passage. The text does not claim antibodies contributed to recurrence; it uses antibody titers as biomarkers to monitor disease state. There is no evidence in the passage that higher or persistent antibody titers caused or facilitated recurrence, so the causal implication is not derivable from the provided information.

472.Option: The use of a custom-synthesized oligonucleotide array to remove host DNA from libraries improved the sensitivity of whole-genome sequencing in clinical specimens, enabling reliable detection of low-abundance M. leprae strains.
Evidence: To remove host DNA from the libraries, we used a custom-synthesized oligonucleotide array (Agilent) spanning the entire M. leprae genome.[18]

High throughput sequencing is becoming increasingly efficient and cost-effective with purified DNA but is more challenging with clinical specimens such as DNA extracted directly from skin biopsies, especially from formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) samples.
Sufficient whole genome read coverage was obtained from the six M. leprae samples for genotyping and comparative genomic analyses (S4 Table).
472.Rationale: The passage describes the technical challenge of sequencing pathogen genomes from clinical FFPE specimens due to host DNA background and states that a custom oligonucleotide array spanning the M. leprae genome was used to remove host DNA from libraries. The fact that 'sufficient whole genome read coverage was obtained' from these clinical samples supports the inference that the array-based host-DNA removal improved the effective sensitivity of sequencing and permitted reliable recovery of M. leprae genomes (including from low-abundance material) for downstream comparative analyses. Thus the methodological claim is supported by the described methods and outcomes.

473.Option: The high sensitivity of the CareStart G6PD test ensures that all patients with true G6PD deficiency are correctly identified, which supports the safe and effective use of standard daily primaquine in the majority of treated patients.
Evidence: Diagnostic parameters showed 100% sensitivity (4/4; 95%CI 39.8–100.0) and 68.1% specificity (62/91; 95%CI 57.5–77.5), with a prevalence of 4.2% (95%CI 1.2–10.4). Negative and positive predictive values were 100% (95%CI 94.2–100.0) and 12.1% (95%CI 3.4–28.2), respectively.
During implementation (February 2019 to early January 2020), 72 G6PD deficient and 267 G6PD normal cases were detected using the CareStart RDT.
473.Rationale: The passage reports a measured sensitivity of 100% and a negative predictive value of 100% in the field subset, meaning that within this sample the test identified all true deficient patients and RDT-normal results reliably corresponded to true normal enzyme activity. Additionally, the majority of detected cases during implementation were RDT-normal (267 normal vs 72 deficient), indicating most patients would be eligible for standard daily primaquine. Therefore, from the study data one can infer that the high sensitivity and excellent NPV support safely treating the majority (those testing normal) with standard daily primaquine, since true deficiencies were detected and normals were reliably identified.

473.Option: The implementation of the CareStart G6PD test in the Brazilian Amazon has led to improved patient adherence to weekly primaquine regimens due to increased trust in the diagnostic process.
Evidence: Eleven patients said that performing the test helped them have greater confidence in the treatment of malaria, since many had a negative experience with the disease (Fig 4).
Treatment supervision was solely dependent on municipality’s capability.
473.Rationale: While some patients reported increased confidence in treatment after testing (supporting improved trust), the passage provides no quantitative data or stated outcomes demonstrating improved adherence to weekly primaquine regimens. The text explicitly notes that treatment supervision depends on municipal capability, and the study did not present adherence measurements or pre/post adherence comparisons. Therefore the specific inference that implementation led to improved adherence to weekly primaquine cannot be derived from the provided passage.

473.Option: The lack of a control line in the CareStart G6PD test prevents the identification of invalid results, which may lead to misinterpretation of test outcomes and compromise the reliability of G6PD screening in field conditions.
Evidence: The test does not have a control line.
An invalid result was considered when blood did not run correctly through the testing window.
As a positive point, respondents reported that the test was easy to perform, but many mentioned challenges in interpreting the test color results.
473.Rationale: The passage states that the test lacks a control line and that an invalid result was defined based on observable blood flow in the device; it also documents HCP difficulties interpreting faint colors. However, it does not state that the absence of a control line 'prevents the identification of invalid results' or directly attributes compromised field reliability to the missing control line. The data instead show specific performance metrics (sensitivity/specificity) and interpretation challenges. The causal claim that lack of a control line prevents identification of invalid tests and thereby compromises reliability is not explicitly supported by the passage and therefore cannot be definitively derived.

473.Option: Although the CareStart G6PD rapid diagnostic test showed 100% sensitivity, its poor specificity led to a high number of false deficient results, resulting in many patients being incorrectly classified as unable to receive standard daily primaquine treatment.
Evidence: Diagnostic parameters showed 100% sensitivity (4/4; 95%CI 39.8–100.0) and 68.1% specificity (62/91; 95%CI 57.5–77.5), with a prevalence of 4.2% (95%CI 1.2–10.4). Negative and positive predictive values were 100% (95%CI 94.2–100.0) and 12.1% (95%CI 3.4–28.2), respectively.
In this scenario, 29 (87%) false deficient patients out of 33 diagnosed in the RDT missed the chance of radical cure due to low specificity.
473.Rationale: The passage reports that although sensitivity was measured at 100%, specificity was only 68.1%, and explicitly quantifies that 29 of 33 RDT-deficient diagnoses were false deficient in the assessed subset—leading those patients to miss the chance for radical cure. This directly supports the inference that low specificity produced many false deficient classifications and thus caused patients to be incorrectly deemed unable to receive standard daily primaquine.

473.Option: The use of the CareStart G6PD test in routine malaria treatment units has been shown to significantly reduce hospitalization rates related to PQ-induced hemolysis in the Brazilian Amazon.
Evidence: Although AHA can lead to severe anemia and acute kidney injury [4,5], with an estimated impact on hospitalization costs of US$ 6 million per year to the Brazilian public health budget [6], there is currently no routine G6PDd screening performed in vivax malaria patients prior to treatment in Brazil, where all patients are given standard short-course PQ treatment (0.5 mg/kg/day for 7 days) [7].

The present study aimed at evaluating the routine implementation of the CareStart G6PD test at malaria treatment units (MTUs) of a municipality in the countryside of the Brazilian Amazon, assessing the reliability of interpretation by health care professionals (HCPs), the test performance in the field, and the perception about the test by HCPs and users pre- and post-implementation.
473.Rationale: The passage describes the implementation, test performance, and perceptions, and notes the clinical consequences of AHA and associated hospital costs, but it does not provide any data on hospitalization rates before versus after implementation or demonstrate a significant reduction in hospitalizations related to PQ-induced hemolysis. Therefore the claim that routine use of the test has been shown to significantly reduce such hospitalization rates is not supported by the information in the passage.

474.Option: Higher FHP coverage may be associated with increased leprosy transmission due to greater contact between individuals in primary care settings, leading to more frequent exposure and infection.
Evidence: FHP is a nationwide program, aimed at broadening access to public health services, especially in deprived areas, by offering free community based primary care" [32].
FHP contributes to leprosy control by supporting early detection and treatment of cases, contact tracing, control of disabilities and other preventive measures [34].
The analysis shows a significant increase in NCDR of leprosy as FHP coverage increases.
474.Rationale: The passage describes the Family Health Program (FHP) as expanding access to community-based primary care and explicitly states that FHP "contributes to leprosy control by supporting early detection and treatment of cases, contact tracing, control of disabilities and other preventive measures." There is no evidence in the passage that primary care settings increase transmission; instead, the passage frames FHP as a control measure. Therefore the claim that higher FHP coverage increases transmission through greater contact in clinics is not supported by the Passage Body and cannot be derived from the provided evidence.

474.Option: The rise in new case detection rates with higher FHP coverage is likely due to increased reporting bias, as health professionals in well-covered areas are more likely to diagnose and report leprosy cases for performance evaluation purposes.
Evidence: FHP contributes to leprosy control by supporting early detection and treatment of cases, contact tracing, control of disabilities and other preventive measures [34].
Increased access to primary care (PHC) achieved in Brazil mainly by FHP implementation has been shown to increase new case detection rate of leprosy [35].

The analysis shows a significant increase in NCDR of leprosy as FHP coverage increases. In the adjusted model, compared with the low tertile of FHP coverage, in the medium tertile of FHP coverage (72.03–95.08%) there was an increase of 5% over the period (RR  = 1.05 95% CI  = 1.02–1.09) and for the higher tertile and increase of 12% over the period (RR  = 1.12 95% CI  = 1.08–1.17).
474.Rationale: While reporting bias (differential reporting) is a conceivable alternative explanation, the passage provides a specific, documented mechanism: increased access to primary care via FHP has been shown to increase new case detection of leprosy. The authors present empirical adjusted results linking greater FHP coverage with higher NCDR, and they describe FHP activities (early detection, contact tracing) that would produce true increases in detected cases. The passage does not mention performance-evaluation-driven reporting or other evidence of reporting bias. Thus the specific explanation of reporting for performance evaluation is not supported by the Passage Body and cannot be derived solely from the provided text.

474.Option: The increase in new case detection rate of leprosy with higher FHP coverage reflects a failure of the Family Health Program to effectively manage infectious diseases, leading to delayed diagnosis and treatment.
Evidence: FHP contributes to leprosy control by supporting early detection and treatment of cases, contact tracing, control of disabilities and other preventive measures [34].
Increased access to primary care (PHC) achieved in Brazil mainly by FHP implementation has been shown to increase new case detection rate of leprosy [35].

The selected 1,358 municipalities originate over 50% of the new leprosy cases detected each year in Brazil and the annual NCDR of leprosy decreased from 74.8 to 45.6 per 100,000 people over the study period from 2004 to 2011.
474.Rationale: The passage portrays the FHP as a program that expands access to primary care and "supports early detection and treatment of cases, contact tracing"—actions that are contrary to 'failure' leading to delayed diagnosis. Moreover, the overall annual NCDR declined substantially over the study period, arguing against a programmatic failure producing more disease. The passage rather supports improved detection as an explanation for higher NCDR in areas with greater FHP coverage. Therefore the assertion that the increase reflects FHP failure is not supported by the Passage Body.

474.Option: The increase in new case detection rate of leprosy associated with higher Family Health Program coverage likely reflects improved case detection rather than a true increase in incidence.
Evidence: FHP contributes to leprosy control by supporting early detection and treatment of cases, contact tracing, control of disabilities and other preventive measures [34].
Increased access to primary care (PHC) achieved in Brazil mainly by FHP implementation has been shown to increase new case detection rate of leprosy [35].

The analysis shows a significant increase in NCDR of leprosy as FHP coverage increases. In the adjusted model, compared with the low tertile of FHP coverage, in the medium tertile of FHP coverage (72.03–95.08%) there was an increase of 5% over the period (RR  = 1.05 95% CI  = 1.02–1.09) and for the higher tertile and increase of 12% over the period (RR  = 1.12 95% CI  = 1.08–1.17).
The annual new case detection rate of leprosy (NCDR), was calculated as the number of reported new cases of leprosy ... per 100,000 people [33].

Hidden prevalence includes undiagnosed cases (which are mainly responsible for transmission of the leprosy). The NCDR of leprosy which reflects the incidence is calculated from the number of new cases detected in a given population [7], [8].
474.Rationale: The Passage Body explicitly states that FHP activities include early detection and that increased access to primary care via FHP "has been shown to increase new case detection rate of leprosy." The statistical findings in the study show higher NCDR where FHP coverage is greater (adjusted RRs >1). The passage also defines NCDR as derived from the number of new cases detected and notes the role of "hidden prevalence" (undiagnosed cases). Taken together, these statements directly support the epidemiological inference that the observed association most plausibly reflects improved case detection (reduced hidden prevalence) rather than a genuine rise in incidence.

474.Option: The observed increase in new case detection rate of leprosy with higher FHP coverage may be due to a genuine rise in leprosy incidence caused by increased population mobility and urbanization in Brazilian municipalities.
Evidence: In the selected municipalities, during the study period, there was a marked expansion of the average municipal BFP coverage both in all population (from 24.6 to 44.7%) and in the target population (from 57.1 to 96.4%). There was also an increase in the mean municipality FHP coverage reaching 79.7% in 2011.

Marked improvements in the socioeconomic conditions was observed in the selected municipalities during the study period. The mean urbanization rate reached 61.3% in 2011. There were reductions in percentage of poor people in the municipality (from 43.8 to 29.8%), Gini Index (from 0.56 to 0.53), illiteracy rate (from 23.1 to 19.6%), unemployment rate (from 9.0 to 6.9%), average number of residents per household (from 3.9 to 3.5) and mean percentage population aged less than 15 years (from 34.7 to 28.3%).

474.Rationale: The passage documents concurrent socioeconomic improvements (including urbanization) but presents no evidence that these changes produced a genuine rise in leprosy incidence; in fact the overall annual NCDR declined substantially during the study period. The authors attribute increased NCDR in higher-FHP areas to improved access and case detection rather than to increased transmission from mobility/urbanization. Therefore the specific claim that population mobility and urbanization caused a real increase in incidence is not supported by the Passage Body.

475.Option: The fact that only one study demonstrated a significant impact of outdoor fogging on vector populations indicates that space-spraying is ineffective and should be abandoned in dengue control programs.

475.Rationale: The passage does report limited and mixed evidence regarding space-spraying (reviews concluding no evidence to support its use) but also documents at least one study showing significant reduction in adult mosquito numbers after nocturnal fogging. 475.Option: The lack of robust evidence from randomized controlled trials evaluating the effectiveness of vector control interventions, particularly space-spraying, means that the true impact of these methods on dengue transmission remains unknown.

Nineteen studies reported dengue incidence, 17 studies reported BI, 16 studies reported HI, 11 studies reported CI, 1 study reported tank positivity, 3 studies reported number of mosquito adults, 6 studies reported pupal indices, 3 studies reported ovitrap data.

In fact the passage reports very high local estimates in Kathmandu (up to 50% in some grid cells), and provides cross-validation metrics for model performance. vaccinees with serologic evidence of recent past exposure had a significantly increased rapid Ogawa-IgG antibody response compared to individuals without evidence of prior exposure after the first vaccine dose on day 7 (p = 0.04).

V. cholerae Ogawa-OSP antigen-specific MBCs demonstrated the most robust increase over baseline. The majority of vaccinees had a V. cholerae Ogawa-OSP IgG MBC response ... Therefore, it is a reasonable epidemiological inference from the Passage Body that persistent OSP-specific MBCs induced by BivWC may underlie its longer-term protection compared to WC-rBS.

While natural infection with V. cholerae induces long-term memory B cell (MBC) responses that are correlated with protection against V. cholerae infection[5], the WC-rBS vaccine does not appear to induce significant MBC responses, despite a similar initial plasma antibody response[6,7].

The suggestion that CTB has a suppressive effect is not stated or supported in the Passage Body, so this causal inference cannot be derived from the provided text.

481.Option: A higher FPG level on admission was an independent predictor of in-hospital death and severe disease type in individuals with SFTS without preexisting diabetes.
Evidence: After adjusting for some clinical variables, age (≥60 years), tremor, and high PCT and FPG levels (≥11.1 mmol/L) were independently associated with in-hospital death among these patients (Fig 4A).

481.Option: The routine use of antibiotics to prevent sepsis in patients with severe SFTS may have masked the association between high FPG levels and secondary infections, suggesting that glucose control is less critical in this population.
Evidence: Logistic regression analysis did not identify high FPG level as a risk factor for the occurrence of secondary infection among these patients (OR1 = 2.286, 95%CI:0.529–9.874, P = 0.268; OR2 = 3.066, 95%CI:0.692–13.575, P = 0.14) (Table 4).
One possible explanation for this finding might be the routine use of antibiotics for prevention in our medical center in individuals with severe SFTS.
plasma glucose control is critical in the management of patients with SFTS and may improve their prognosis.
481.Rationale: The passage does state that routine preventative antibiotic use could explain why high FPG was not associated with secondary infection in their analysis, which supports the first clause (masking). However, the additional assertion that this implies glucose control is less critical is not supported and contradicts an earlier statement that "plasma glucose control is critical" for management and prognosis. The passage does not conclude that glucose control is less important; thus the option overreaches beyond the evidence.

481.Option: Since HbA1c levels are not routinely measured upon admission, the observed association between FPG and clinical outcomes may be due to undiagnosed preexisting diabetes in some patients.
Evidence: The inclusion criteria were patients with SFTS: (1) confirmed via reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR), (2) with FPG data on admission, (3) aged >18 years, and (4) without preexisting diabetes or current trauma.
The exclusion criteria were patients with SFTSV (4) patients with a confirmed diagnosis of diabetes or use of anti-diabetic agents.
FPG levels were measured within 24 h of admission and blood samples were obtained after an overnight fast of ≥8 h.
481.Rationale: The passage describes that the study included only patients without preexisting diabetes (by history/records) and that FPG was measured on admission. The passage does not state that HbA1c was or was not measured, nor does it provide data about undiagnosed diabetes. The claim that the association may be due to undiagnosed diabetes (because HbA1c was not routinely measured) is speculative and not supported by explicit information in the passage, so it cannot be derived from the provided text.

481.Option: Acute hyperglycemia upon admission is a hallmark of insulin insufficiency and is associated with increased levels of free fatty acids and lipolysis, which may explain the worse survival outcomes in patients with SFTS.
Evidence: Acute hyperglycemia is often observed in individuals with SARS-2 with no history of diabetes or glucocorticoid use [9]. This phenomenon most likely occurs due to stress-induced hyperglycemia during an acute inflammatory response [10].
The possible reason might be that high FPG is usually the consequence of an infection due to the release of counter-regulators.
481.Rationale: The passage attributes acute hyperglycemia to stress-induced hyperglycemia and release of counter-regulatory hormones during infection, not specifically to insulin insufficiency or to increased free fatty acids and lipolysis. The detailed metabolic mechanisms proposed in the option (insulin insufficiency, elevated free fatty acids, lipolysis) are not discussed or evidenced in the passage; therefore this causal mechanistic inference cannot be derived from the provided text.

481.Option: The high prevalence of acute hyperglycemia in patients with SFTS without preexisting diabetes suggests that the SFTS virus directly impairs glucose metabolism, leading to secondary infection and worse outcomes.
Evidence: Moreover, whether SFTS virus (SFTSV) influences glucose metabolism in individuals without preexisting diabetes, which further causes secondary infection or even exacerbates the condition, remains unclear.
FPG levels were comparable between patients with SFTS with and without secondary infection (6.689±0.1923 mmol/L vs. 6.876±0.1227 mmol/L, P = 0.5521).

Logistic regression analysis did not identify high FPG level as a risk factor for the occurrence of secondary infection among these patients (OR1 = 2.286, 95%CI:0.529–9.874, P = 0.268; OR2 = 3.066, 95%CI:0.692–13.575, P = 0.14) (Table 4).
481.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that whether SFTSV influences glucose metabolism remains unclear, so asserting that the virus directly impairs glucose metabolism is not supported. Furthermore, the data do not show an association between high FPG and secondary infection, undermining the claim that virus-induced hyperglycemia leads to secondary infection. Thus the causal chain asserted in the option is not derivable from the study text.

482.Option: Since Bartonella was detected in mites from both M. musculus and M. booduga, and both species are synanthropic, mites in human habitations are likely to be the main source of zoonotic Bartonella infections in this region.
Evidence: Of the seven mite pools screened, one pool from M. musculus (n = 3) and another one from M. booduga (n = 1) was positive.
Synanthropic species M. musculus had a prevalence of 58.33% (n = 24), followed by M. booduga at 2.85% (n = 35), a species abundant in grasslands but often present in forests and human habitations.
R. satarae was captured in forests and abandoned plantations, while R. rattus (n = 10) was only captured in human habitation.
482.Rationale: The passage does report Bartonella-positive mite pools from M. musculus and M. booduga, and explicitly labels M. musculus as synanthropic. However, M. booduga is described as "abundant in grasslands" and only "often present in forests and human habitations," not clearly characterized as synanthropic in the same way. Further, the passage does not provide any evidence that mites in human habitations are the "main source" of zoonotic infections — no data on transmission to humans, infection incidence in humans, or comparative contribution of different reservoirs/vectors is presented. The available data (only two positive mite pools among seven, host habitat descriptions, and host prevalence data) are insufficient to support the strong causal claim that mites in human habitations are the main source of zoonotic Bartonella infections.

482.Option: The haplotype accumulation curve suggests that mites in this ecosystem are likely to carry a higher diversity of Bartonella haplotypes than their host species, indicating a greater role in pathogen diversification.
Evidence: Analysis of 657 bp of these rpoB sequences revealed 13 haplotypes, and haplotype diversity of 0.9 (Fig 3A).
All of the haplotypes were host-specific, and within two host species, multiple haplotypes were observed.
The sequence from the pooled mites from M. musculus also belonged to this central haplotype.
The sequence obtained from mites collected from M. booduga also belonged to the MB1 haplotype.
The haplotype accumulation curve (solid line) shows the cumulative haplotype richness (rpoB) sampled across 63 positive small mammals.
482.Rationale: The haplotype analyses in the passage indicate that haplotypes were host-specific and that the sequences recovered from mites matched the haplotypes of their corresponding hosts (mites from M. musculus and M. booduga belonged to the host haplotypes). The haplotype accumulation curve is based on host-derived rpoB sequences and indicates overall haplotype richness across sampled small mammals. There is no evidence in the passage that mites carry a higher diversity of haplotypes than hosts; in fact, mite sequences matched host haplotypes in the samples obtained. Therefore the claim that mites likely carry greater haplotype diversity and play a greater role in pathogen diversification is not supported by the presented data.

482.Option: The presence of Bartonella in mites from M. musculus and M. booduga, along with the detection of zoonotic lineages in these hosts, confirms that mites are competent vectors capable of transmitting Bartonella to humans in this ecosystem.
Evidence: Of the seven mite pools screened, one pool from M. musculus (n = 3) and another one from M. booduga (n = 1) was positive.
hematophagous arthropods, such as ticks, mites, and fleas transmit these pathogens between reservoirs and incidental hosts [4].
Sequences from this study (GenBank accession no. MT787669-MT787733) were polyphyletic and phylogenetically related to multiple Bartonella species.
These three lineages formed a monophyletic clade and were nested between B. tribocorum and B. elizabethae (PP = 1).
482.Rationale: While the passage documents PCR detection of Bartonella DNA in two mite pools and describes general background that hematophagous arthropods can transmit Bartonella, it does not provide direct evidence that mites in this study are competent vectors (i.e., capable of acquiring, maintaining, and transmitting live infectious bacteria to new hosts). Detection of Bartonella DNA in pooled mites is consistent with possible vector involvement but does not confirm vector competence or human transmission. The phylogenetic relatedness of host-associated sequences to known zoonotic species suggests potential relevance to human health, but does not "confirm" mite competency or actual transmission to humans. Therefore the definitive claim of confirmed vector competence and transmission to humans is not supported by the presented data.

482.Option: The high prevalence of Bartonella in R. satarae and M. musculus, combined with their synanthropic nature, indicates that these species are the primary drivers of Bartonella transmission to humans in the Western Ghats.
Evidence: Bartonella was present in 52.48% of the small mammals tested (Table 1).
Among the most common four species, R. satarae had the highest prevalence of Bartonella at 85.07% (n = 67).
Synanthropic species M. musculus had a prevalence of 58.33% (n = 24), followed by M. booduga at 2.85% (n = 35), a species abundant in grasslands but often present in forests and human habitations.
R. satarae was captured in forests and abandoned plantations, while R. rattus (n = 10) was only captured in human habitation.
482.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence that R. satarae and M. musculus have high Bartonella prevalence; it explicitly identifies M. musculus as synanthropic. However, R. satarae was captured primarily in forests and abandoned plantations, not necessarily synanthropic, so the statement that both are synanthropic is not fully supported. More importantly, the study does not present data on actual transmission to humans, incidence of human infection, or relative contributions of different host species to human spillover. Inferring that these species are the "primary drivers" of transmission to humans is a causal claim that goes beyond the observational host prevalence and ecological descriptions provided. Thus this strong inference is not justified solely by the passage.

482.Option: The detection of mites carrying Bartonella haplotypes phylogenetically related to known zoonotic species suggests the potential for mite-mediated transmission of these pathogens to incidental hosts, including humans.
Evidence: hematophagous arthropods, such as ticks, mites, and fleas transmit these pathogens between reservoirs and incidental hosts [4].
Of the seven mite pools screened, one pool from M. musculus (n = 3) and another one from M. booduga (n = 1) was positive.
The sequence from the pooled mites from M. musculus also belonged to this central haplotype.
These three lineages formed a monophyletic clade and were nested between B. tribocorum and B. elizabethae (PP = 1).
Sequences from this study (GenBank accession no. MT787669-MT787733) were polyphyletic and phylogenetically related to multiple Bartonella species.
482.Rationale: The passage documents (a) that hematophagous mites are known vectors of Bartonella generally, (b) detection of Bartonella DNA in mite pools collected from M. musculus and M. booduga in this study, (c) that mite-derived sequences matched host haplotypes, and (d) that several host-associated lineages are phylogenetically related to known zoonotic Bartonella species. Together these observations reasonably support the inference that mite-mediated transmission to incidental hosts, including humans, is possible (i.e., suggests potential). The option is appropriately cautious ('suggests the potential') rather than asserting confirmed transmission or vector competence, and thus can be derived from the evidence presented in the passage.

483.Option: The inflammatory environment in acute dengue infection contributes to endothelial glycocalyx dysfunction, as evidenced by strong positive correlations between plasma syndecan-1 concentrations and multiple inflammatory proteins.
Evidence: disruption of the endothelial glycocalyx integrity is increasingly recognized as a key process in plasma leakage and, therefore, we determined associations of DEPs with plasma syndecan-1 concentrations, which is a surrogate marker of endothelial glycocalyx degradation.

plasma concentrations of syndecan-1 (SDC1), a surrogate marker of endothelial glycocalyx degradation [59], correlated positively with a large number of inflammatory proteins (Fig 4) and fluid accumulation during hospitalization (S5B Fig).
The strongest associations were observed for HGF (rs = 0.74; FDR 1.18x10-6) and IL18R1 (rs = 0.71; FDR 6.59x10-6).
Most of the shared DEPs/DETs correlated with syndecan-1 (SDC1) concentrations or clinical parameters (S2 Table).
483.Rationale: The passage explicitly defines syndecan-1 (SDC1) as a surrogate marker of endothelial glycocalyx degradation and reports that SDC1 "correlated positively with a large number of inflammatory proteins" with strong specific associations (HGF, IL18R1). From an epidemiological perspective, these observed positive correlations between systemic inflammatory proteins and a marker of glycocalyx degradation support the inference that the inflammatory environment in acute dengue is associated with — and likely contributes to — endothelial glycocalyx dysfunction. (Note: the passage reports correlations, which support an inference of contribution/association; causal proof is not established within the Passage Body.)

483.Option: The lack of significant associations between inflammatory proteins and clinical signs of dengue severity, such as hematocrit and fluid accumulation, indicates that systemic inflammation does not play a role in disease progression.
Evidence: Overall, there were only few significant associations between DEPs and the following markers of dengue severity: platelet number, hematocrit, plasma albumin, gallbladder wall thickness and the presence of ascites or pleural fluid.

plasma concentrations of syndecan-1 (SDC1), a surrogate marker of endothelial glycocalyx degradation [59], correlated positively with a large number of inflammatory proteins (Fig 4) and fluid accumulation during hospitalization (S5B Fig).
483.Rationale: The passage does state that there were only few significant associations between DEPs and certain clinical severity markers. However, it also documents that syndecan-1 (a marker of glycocalyx degradation and fluid accumulation) correlated positively with many inflammatory proteins and with fluid accumulation. The absence of widespread associations with specific clinical markers in this dataset does not justify the definitive conclusion that systemic inflammation plays no role in disease progression. The evidence supports association between inflammation and vascular integrity (through SDC1), so the blanket statement that inflammation does not play a role is not supported by the Passage Body.
